User: I would say the AI is driving a good bit of the concepts, I simply try to guide it. So the collaboration would be about 60/40 (60% AI, 40% my ideas). I just feel I have less control once I've told the AI what I'm looking for, then it's a matter of whether or not the AI interprets the directions well or not.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - the 60/40 split and that sense of having less control once you've given the AI direction. It sounds like there's this handoff moment where you're somewhat at the mercy of how well it interprets what you're asking for.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Oh! Definitely compositions. Sometimes it would be a struggle for me with really complicated scene layouts that require intense perspective shots. Now I can use AI to help with that and it takes a good bit of pressure off of my shoulders with making sure I get it right. Also, it helps with writing, when I'm putting a treatment together, or outlining a script idea. It's very helpful with those things as well, but again, sometimes it can generate something that feels like a default piece. And, color palettes, when I'm coloring pages for comics and I might need help with generating a mood palette for the page, it's very good for that as well. I'm very skilled in color theory but there are times that I'm mentally drained and need the extra help. Does that make sense?

AI: That makes complete sense - it sounds like AI has become this valuable support system for when you're mentally fatigued or facing particularly challenging technical aspects like perspective work. The way you describe it, it's almost like having a creative assistant that can help carry some of the cognitive load.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Personally, I feel like it has the potential to be exactly the way you described it, a valuable support system for when I'm mentally fatigued or facing difficult challenges with technical aspects of the work I do. My concerns are with people and companies trying to abuse AI by using it as a replacement for artists and creatives like myself who've spent years training and honing our skills. I'm also slightly concerned about the use of AI stealing work to generate images for people who want to call themselves artists but lack the proper skills, training and experience.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction you're making - between AI as a tool that enhances your existing skills versus it being used to bypass the years of training and experience you've developed. It sounds like you see a clear difference between using AI to support your creative process and using it to replace that process entirely.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see it becoming more refined so that the process of generating reference images, color palettes, and composition layouts becomes more streamlined. The more advanced the models become the more intuitive of a personal artist assistant I will have. Perhaps even using it to help me complete multiple projects under tight deadlines. I hope that answers your question.

AI: That absolutely answers my question - it's clear you have a very pragmatic vision of AI becoming more like a sophisticated, intuitive creative partner that helps you manage workload and tight deadlines.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on that 60/40 collaboration split and how you distinguish between AI as a support tool versus a replacement for artistic skills and training.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not really, the only other thing is when AI generates images with weird deformities. That's still happening, and while quite hilarious, it can be equally frustrating when there's a deadline present.

AI: That's a perfect note to end on - the reality that even as AI becomes more sophisticated, those unexpected quirks and deformities can still throw a wrench in the works, especially when you're racing against a deadline. It really captures that tension between the promise of these tools and their current limitations.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how you navigate this balance between leveraging AI as a creative assistant while maintaining your artistic integrity and skills will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how candid you've been about both the benefits and the frustrations.

User: you're welcome

AI: Thank you so much! It's been a pleasure speaking with you. Have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with those children's book illustrations you're working on!
12
creativity_0010Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good - no questions from me.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I run a small business/ hobby (technically a business, since I make money from it and treat it as a business) doing fantasy map commissions for independent authors and DnD players. I also create my own fantasy map assets (such as mountains, icons, trees, really anything you might find on a fantasy map) and sell them on [REDACTED], on Etsy, and a marketplace for map assets. A typical project can be one of two things: a commission involves receiving a request for a custom map from a client. They fill out the details regarding the map, and I correspond with them before, during, and after designing the map. I use digital programs such as Wonderdraft and Procreate to create this map, then I send it to the client and go through a revision process. Commissions usually take a week or so per map, about 3 hours per day. Then the map asset projects are a bit different, as I do this on my own schedule. I'll come up with a pack theme (such as pirates, medieval, isometric cities, etc) and make a list of assets and a rough count of each to include. I'll then draw the assets on Procreate, export them to my PC, organize and name the files, create product listing images and promos for them, then upload them to the appropriate markets.

AI: That sounds like a really fascinating niche! The combination of commissioned work and creating your own asset packs gives you such a unique perspective on the creative process.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I only use AI tools in my asset pack creation, not commissions. If I'm having a hard time coming up with assets to fit in the pack I want to make, I'll ask AI tools such as Gemini or ChatGPT to help me brainstorm some ideas. Then, when I'm designing the assets, I will ask a generative AI program to bulk create the assets I want to include. I use these asset sketches as guidelines for doing the linework of my drawings. I don't use the actual files, but instead, I'll trace over the sketches using my style of art, making modifications as I draw. The rest of the process, including shading, coloring, details, etc is done without AI.

AI: That's really interesting how you've found a specific role for AI in your workflow - using it as a brainstorming partner and then for creating reference sketches that you trace over in your own style.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I just recently started a pack of assets using an old inky style of art to fit antique fountain pen-drawn maps throughout history. I couldn't figure out the exact style I wanted, and couldn't replicate the images in my head onto Procreate, nor could I figure out the right shape for the mountains, so I used AI to generate about 30 jagged mountains using a blotchy ink shading so that I could see a tangible, visual representation of what was in my head so I could better replicate it with the Procreate brushes I have. It felt like cheating at first, to use AI to come up with a style that I don't have the skill to create on my own. But, I figured that I'm not stealing work, I'm not using the images themselves as the assets, and I'm only using the images as inspiration instead of the end product. So, it's okay, in my mind. I'm just using AI to fill the skill gaps I still have until I improve my craft enough to confidently fill those gaps on my own.

AI: Tell me more about that feeling of it being like "cheating" at first. What shifted for you that made you feel like it was okay?

User: A lot of artwork on Etsy (a marketplace I sell on) is AI-generated, and I've seen people online criticize the use of AI for artwork in general. So, I felt super guilty at first, because I want to make "real" art, using my own ideas, designs, creativity, my own hand drawing the artwork. Then, I realized that I'm still doing all of that. I'm not generating artwork in 2 seconds just to turn around and sell that same image on Etsy for $3 as if I'm the artist. I admit that there is creativity in formulating prompts to give generative AI so that the end result is the best it can possibly be. But I don't want my brand and my business image to be so heavily tied to AI and the stigma that surrounds it. I don't want to be accused of stealing artwork, or stealing ideas and styles and designs. When I realized that my use of AI isn't making use of these unethical practices, I felt less guilty. Because I'm still using my ideas, my designs, my creativity, and my hand drawing the artwork. I'm just using AI as a tool to brainstorm some ideas and show me what proper perspective, proportions, and shadows look like on an asset. These are all things that I'm learning from the AI generations. When I realized that I'm not stealing anything, and that I'm not using AI in an unethical way (in my opinion), I felt okay with it. I don't see it as any different than following a "how to draw mountains on your map" guide from another creator. At least this way, I'm still staying true to my own style.

AI: That's such a thoughtful way of thinking through the ethics of it. It sounds like you've really worked out your own boundaries around what feels authentic to your creative practice.

When you're working with AI in this way - using it to help you visualize what's in your head or learn about perspective and shadows - how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions in that collaboration?

User: Well, it's just me working in this business, and the generative AI I use is only capable of generating images. It isn't a chatbot or collaborative partner in any way. So, I guess I'm driving the creative decisions. I'm telling it where to place the shadows, what kind of perspective to use, whether to use flat 2D icons or isometric perspectives. The only "decisions" it makes is deciding how best to make those shadows or shapes or proportions realistic.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - you're very much in the driver's seat, using AI more like a specialized tool to execute your vision rather than as a creative partner.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I'd say I'm more creative as a whole. I find it easier to come up with pack theme ideas, specific assets for those packs. I also find it easier to decide where to place shadows and shading on my own without using the generated images as guides. I've always known how to spot something that just looks "off", but recently, I've been able to figure out what exactly looks off and how to fix it. I think it's because I've spent so much time looking at these generated images and tracing the shapes and altering those shapes to be what I envisioned.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like working with AI has actually accelerated your learning as an artist. You mentioned being able to better identify what looks "off" and how to fix it now.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: AI generated assets look good, objectively. There is one creator who only puts out AI-generated assets, and doesn't alter them much from the generated output, so he can put out a new asset pack once or twice a week and charge $6 for each pack because of how good they look and how many assets are included. I'm worried that the AI-generated assets will oversaturate the market and take exposure away from creators who design things by hand. It's already getting difficult to parse through that one creator's content to find hand-drawn styles, or antique styles that many people like. So, I'm afraid that my process of hand-drawing the assets and using AI only for brainstorming and to fill in my own creative limitations will be too slow to keep up.

AI: That's a really concrete concern - the speed and volume advantage that purely AI-generated content has over your more deliberate, hand-crafted approach. It sounds like you're seeing that tension play out in real time in the marketplace.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Ideally, I'd like to use AI to fill my gaps in creative skill until I improve enough to go through the entire design process without help. Then, I'd like to stop using generative AI. It would be nice to switch instead to an AI tool to help me organize and name files after I've designed and finalized the asset images, because that takes a considerable amount of time. Automation would really speed up the process and allow me to spend more time creating, but I just don't know of any AI that does that yet. Not reliably, at least. Realistically, I'm worried I'll need to keep using generative AI and even start selling generated content just to keep up in the marketplace so I can make a living.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how you've worked through the ethical considerations and defined your own boundaries around what feels authentic to your practice, as well as your honest concerns about the marketplace pressures you're facing.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a learning tool while maintaining your artistic integrity, and the real marketplace challenges you're navigating, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate your candor about both the benefits and concerns - it gives us a much richer understanding of what creative professionals are actually experiencing day-to-day.
13
creativity_0011Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, I'm ready.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a contract-based graphic designer who specializes in preparing projects to print. Typically, I produce a lot of promotional material for my clients, which includes some small projects like business cards or flyers, but also cohesive suites of materials (think business cards + forms, flyers, banners, etc.). I've even delved into website creation, script treatments, and more based on what clients need and what I can provide. A client will contact me, give me an idea of what they want, and generally give me free rein over how it gets made; then we hammer out changes.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of work - from print materials to websites and even script treatments. It sounds like you get quite a bit of creative freedom in how you approach each project.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I'm in my thirties, so old enough to be wary of AI's current limitations, but also of the generation that understands that computersâ€”and now, AIâ€”are just another set of tools, just like Photoshop and Wacom tablets. There was pushback when these things became available to artists and designers, now people pay plenty for a good digital artist. In my case, I have used AI for my graphic design work sparingly, occasionally to make mockups of things that I want to produce with greater skill or specific sizing/layouts, but generally as a timesaver for rote tasks. For example, recently I asked if a client had a copy of his business logo, preferably on a business card, so I could take it and make it "camera ready." He had it on a shirt with a gray background. In Photoshop, this can be as easy as selecting a color range and knocking back the background or isolating the solid-color logo, but then you have to account for warp and any artifacts around the edges of it, which can be a pain to clean up. I used an AI tool to extract this logo for me, and it did well, but wasn't perfect. It claimed to give me a transparent background, even threw in the gray-and-white checkerboard, but I still had to go in and separate the logo. Still, it saved some time; the edges were very clean and it took care of about 70% of the warping, so I killed the background and tweaked the warped text so I could use the logo.

AI: That's a really practical example - using AI as a time-saving tool for those tedious technical tasks rather than for the creative concept itself. It sounds like you approached it with realistic expectations too, knowing you'd still need to do some manual cleanup.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Another project I did was of a more personal nature. A colleague of my mother's had some photos from her niece's wedding that were, sadly, not terribly professional, but a relative did them for free. She remembered that I designed a banner for the store's manager who had died the previous year. There were no photos of the niece and her grandparents together, just in separate photos, and interestingly, there was a person in the main group photo to be edited that was wearing an oversized, neon orange shirt that completely overtook the bride. But the best photos of the grandparents had red eye and they weren't smiling. I was able to use AI to composite some "good" grandparents together, then in Photoshop I pasted everyone in, moved some people around in front of T-shirt guy (he was tall), toned down said shirt, then ran a copy of it through an AI tool to smooth some things out, then checked everything back over in PS to ensure it looked realistic, no floating hands, etc.

AI: That sounds like a really thoughtful and complex project - helping create a meaningful family photo when the original shots weren't quite working. It's interesting how you used AI at multiple points in that workflow - for compositing the grandparents and then for smoothing things out later.

When you're working with AI like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: In that case, I am. The AI is just doing the things I'd rather not do, like poring over pixels of trees to ensure everyone is blended just so, or cleaning up scratches in photo restorations. I'm playing supervisor to some extent. I have used AI for complete creative output for things like, say, when a relative wants a logo for their business that looks very "painterly"â€”not my forte, but they are also less aware of what is AI-generated, so a good AI output plus some touchups in Photoshop means that I don't have to expend as much effort for projects that are either unpaid or involve multiple changes if the "client" doesn't know what they want.

AI: That's a really honest perspective - you're clearly making strategic decisions about when and how to deploy AI based on both the project requirements and the client relationship. It sounds like you maintain creative control throughout, using AI more as an efficient assistant for specific tasks.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I have been able to spend more time either working on the broad strokes of a project or researching things to draw from. Taking care of drudge work so I can find the right font, the right color palette, etc. Of course, I imagine that an AI assistant, especially the modern ones, can be a better sounding board now, since many can parse images. Now that assistants are becoming more conversational, it's easier to find those connections or "tip of my tongue" ideas that you can't immediately name. It used to be that failing to remember the name of that one actor in that one flick used to keep a person up at night. Even some Google searches weren't helpful if you didn't have specific enough info. Now, many AI assistants are able to piece it together. Some people argue that there is too much reliance on AI, allowing it to supplant critical thinking, but like many things, if you use it responsibly, there's little harm done.

AI: That's a thoughtful distinction - using AI to handle the routine tasks so you can focus more energy on the higher-level creative decisions like typography and color choices. And that "tip of the tongue" research capability sounds really valuable for a designer who needs to pull from such a wide range of references.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Many artists are concerned about their images being used to train AI, essentially allowing it to create works that use their style without attribution. It makes it harder to convince people to pay for commissioned work when they can just upload an image and ask it to make something that looks like that with their changes. That, and as I wrote previously, there are some who do take what AI says as gospel and they fail to fact-check or inspect the output for mistakes. These are things that a client wouldn't accept from a paid professional, and it's concerning to see where some companies "cheap out." If they do it in their public-facing materials, where else are they cutting corners?

AI: Those are really legitimate concerns - both the attribution/training data issue and the broader implications when AI output isn't properly vetted. It sounds like you're seeing this play out directly in how some potential clients approach projects.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I would hope that it would evolve to become more sophisticated over the years, involving less back and forth (as the wedding photos) and being able to give me "parts" that I can use to assemble a wholeâ€”isolate these people and make the shadows come from the other side, extract this text, generate a sunset with clouds shaped like hearts, but not too "clean," then I could just focus on making it look natural. I'm sure some people have made AI make proportions and compositions look pleasing to the human eye, especially for images that are meant to appear candid or not dead center, but it's not too different to studio photography in that aspect. Your assistant may gather the backdrops, adjust lighting, and provide props, and you do the final posing and click the shutter. Then back and forth between the assistant and you to color-correct and remove flaws.

AI: That's a really compelling vision - AI as a sophisticated creative assistant that can handle specific technical tasks while you orchestrate the overall composition and aesthetic. The photography studio analogy is particularly apt - having an assistant who can handle the setup work so you can focus on the creative direction and final execution.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your practical perspective on using AI strategically - knowing when it's the right tool for the job and maintaining realistic expectations about its capabilities while keeping creative control in your hands.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, thank you

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a strategic tool while maintaining creative control, and your perspective on the evolving relationship between AI capabilities and professional creative work, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the practical benefits and the legitimate concerns in your field. Have a great rest of your day!

User: :)

AI: :) Take care!
14
creativity_0012Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, go ahead

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write creative fiction. Up to now it's been flash fiction and short stories, and I've won awards for some of them. Now I'm working on my first full-length novel.

AI: That's wonderful - congratulations on the awards! Flash fiction and short stories are such distinctive forms, and now tackling a full-length novel must feel like quite a shift.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: One thing I will never use AI for is getting it to write or re-write a section of text for me as that's no better than employing a ghost writer and I'd feel a fraud. I will never lift an entire paragraph of text from AI and put it into my work.
What I do use AI for is discussing some more technical aspects of writing. This might be as simple as the placement of a comma in a sentence, or as complex as shifting points of view between chapters of a novel. I might also use it for brainstorming ideas.

AI: That's a really clear distinction you've drawn between what feels authentic to you versus what would feel like cheating. I'm curious about those technical discussions you have with AI.

Can you describe a specific recent example where you used AI to work through one of these technical writing aspects? What was that conversation like?

User: Sure. I was grappling with POV in the early stages of my novel. I was writing in first-person POV but events were happening around the country (riots, looting, society breaking down). I was struggling with how I could describe those events in detail as the protagonist couldn't be at all these places around the country and I was wary of having an omniscient narrator.

AI: That's such a classic challenge in first-person narratives - that tension between what your protagonist can realistically witness and the broader scope of events you want to convey.

Tell me more about how you worked through that with AI. What kind of solutions or approaches did it suggest, and how did that conversation help you think about the problem?

User: It suggested various ways of describing second-hand accounts, e.g. news, social media, eye witness accounts, rumours, footage, etc. As it happened, my protagonist is a reporter which lends itself quite well to this. The person he's interviewing can be relating what he's seen in vivid detail to the protagonist - so I still get the "show don't tell" without the protagonist being there himself.
It did also suggest I gave it a section of my text for it to re-write in a more immersive way, but as already mentioned, that's not something I do.

AI: That's a perfect example of how your reporter protagonist opens up those storytelling possibilities! And I appreciate how you stayed true to your boundary even when AI offered to rewrite text directly.

When you're having these technical discussions with AI, how would you describe that dynamic? Who's driving the creative decisions in those conversations?

User: Definitely me. I come with some clear ideas about my work-in-progress and there are certain approaches I do/don't want to take.

AI: That sense of maintaining creative control sounds really important to you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI as this kind of technical sounding board?

User: I'm not sure aspects of my creative work has changed, as such. AI is just another tool for when I need to work through a problem. Before I might have googled something and read blog posts or something. Now I'll have a conversation with an AI model. But the end result is that I find the answers to my questions and use that to shape my writing.

AI: That's a really helpful way to think about it - AI as another research tool in your toolkit rather than something that's fundamentally changing your process. You're still the one asking the questions and making the decisions.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of creative writing?

User: In the field in general, I'm super concerned about the use of AI to write stories and design book covers. I see it so often now - even writing contests have clauses in their terms to strictly forbid the use of AI. Why is this a concern? Because the quality isn't there. A novel written by AI might have a great plot and be technically brilliant. But it won't have the deeper nuances that only a human can weave throughout the story - the slow burn emotional response a reader might feel from reading a book by an accomplished author.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction you're making - between technical competence and those deeper, more nuanced human elements that resonate emotionally with readers. The fact that writing contests are having to explicitly ban AI submissions really speaks to how widespread this has become.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think the way you use it as a technical advisor will change?

User: I would expect to be able to feed it a longer piece of text (maybe even a whole novel, c.80,000 words) and have it critique it (again, critique, not re-write). I would expect it to be better at identifying plot holes, one-dimensional characters, breaking POV, etc.
Whether I would actually use it for that is a different matter. I would find the advice useful, I'm sure. But I'm not sure I'd be comfortable uploading a finished piece of work of that length to a model.

AI: That's really interesting - you can see the potential value in having AI analyze your full manuscript for structural issues, but you have reservations about actually sharing that much of your work. Can you tell me more about what makes you uncomfortable about uploading a finished piece?

User: Because I don't know how the model will use that material. Someone else could come along saying "I want to write a novel about a reporter dealing with society breaking down" and the model might give my entire story to them.

AI: That makes complete sense - the uncertainty around data usage and potential replication of your unique work is a real concern, especially with something as personal and substantial as a full novel.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated how clearly you've defined your boundaries - using AI as a technical advisor and research tool while maintaining complete ownership of the actual creative writing, and your concerns about quality and authenticity in the broader field.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative integrity while selectively leveraging AI as a technical resource will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. Best of luck with your novel!
15
creativity_0013Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions claude, fire away.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: So I have a side job/hobby/hustle creating marketing materials for authors that they can use in promotions, like images or videos, all AI generated. My most recent project was to generate some clips with AI and then put those together to make a mini trailor or 'vibe reel' for a thriller novel set in rio de janeiro, so the author could use it as a banner on the books amazon page.

AI: That sounds like a really interesting niche! Creating promotional materials for authors using AI-generated content is such a specific application.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process for a project like that Rio de Janeiro thriller trailer. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for different AI tools?

User: So, the authors usually send me a 'storyboard' or general outline for what they want in the trailor, but I often use AI models to help me figure out more about the book if I have not read it, so for example I use ChatGPT or similar to break down the blub or a summary of the book, and use that to help me get the gist of the book. Then I can use this to turn into prompts for Sora to get the images.

AI: That's a fascinating workflow - using AI to help you understand the source material before creating the promotional content.

Can you walk me through that Rio de Janeiro thriller project specifically? What did the author give you initially, and how did you use AI to break down their book and then translate that into the actual video clips?

User: The author gave me a story outline for the trailor, 5-6 scenes or 'moments' he wanted short videos or images for - such as a scene walking through a favela, or standing on a hill above the city. He gave me the blurb and a summary, and also a folder full of reference images that could be useful for giving to Sora to animate or just to give me the idea of what he wanted. I then used each of these scenes to create a prompt to give to sora, and with a lot of tries and remixes came out with some good videos.

AI: That's really helpful to understand your process. It sounds like you had to do quite a bit of iteration with Sora to get the right results.

When you're working with Sora and going through those "lot of tries and remixes," how would you describe that collaboration? Are you feeling like you're directing the AI, or does it feel more like a back-and-forth creative partnership?

User: It feels a bit more like a battle to be honest, usually the iterations are less because each one is a little better, and more because the product is quite bad and needs refinement. some scenes took multiple hours to refine. So I feel as if i am directing a non-compliant worker sometimes haha

AI: Ha! That's such a vivid way to put it - a "non-compliant worker." That sounds pretty frustrating when you have a clear vision but the tool isn't cooperating.

Tell me more about those hours of refinement. What kinds of things would go wrong that you'd need to keep pushing back on, and how did you finally get Sora to give you what you needed?

User: so I think it is just all part of the process. Say the author wanted a scene on a dark beach of two figures meeting. Usually the movement wouldn't be quite right, or the setting, or the scene. Even with prompts nearing 200 words, something just was not quite right. Often I would take the best of the results and remix that with things like 'change the colour of the lights' or 'lower the camera angle' - just little tweaks until it met the authors vision

AI: That level of detailed iteration is really interesting - going from 200-word prompts to these very specific tweaks like camera angles and lighting. It sounds like you're developing a pretty sophisticated vocabulary for communicating with the AI.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI for these author projects? How is this different from how you might have approached similar work before?

User: I think it changed everything, as before AI I would not be able to do this, as my cinematography experience is limited, and most authors do not have the budget to pay for a bull blown promotional video with a cast and crew. Doing it this way lets the author achieve their vision with as little expenditure as possible.

AI: That's a really powerful point - AI essentially opened up an entirely new service you could offer that wasn't economically feasible before.

Given how transformative this has been for your work, what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in this space or in creative fields more broadly?

User: I think AI is a tool, not a shortcut or crutch. I don't believe that AI has a place in many creative spaces. But i see this as a case where it is used for good. The creative work here, really, is the authors book and their skill at writing. This is just a promotional video to help them sell the real product - the novels. The authors usually are just that - authors. Not videographers capable of making a high budget trailer themselves - so I think using AI in this way is fine as it helps authors get their real work out to people.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction you're making - using AI to promote the "real" creative work rather than replace it. It sounds like you see yourself as helping authors bridge a gap they couldn't cross on their own.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your work with authors evolving over the next few years? Do you think the tools will get easier to work with, or do you expect that "battle" element to remain part of the process?

User: I think tools will get easier naturally as AI improves. Even in the last few years the capability of AI image and video generation has dramatically increased. AI is already able to create photorealistic or stylized videos - I think in the future it will just get better at translating the users thoughts and ideas into something tangible - the foundation is there and only getting stronger.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a tool that enables authors to access promotional capabilities they couldn't afford otherwise, and your distinction between using AI to support the "real" creative work versus replacing it.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: nope thats fine thank you.

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the iterative "battle" with AI tools, the economic accessibility this creates for authors, and your thoughtful perspective on where AI fits appropriately in creative work will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to walk me through your process today. Have a great rest of your day!
16
creativity_0014Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, I think I am ready to begin with whatever questions you have for me

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: So my creative niche is I create 3D printed tokens for card games, most typically Magic: The Gathering, using a program called HueForge to take a 2D design and give it depth, scale, and colour. A typical project is starting with a need for a token/card design - be it from client, or for general sales - and working through creating a drawing, photoshopping it to be set for HueForge, and then colour mapping in HueForge to create an STL, a 3D object, that is then ready to be 3D printed

AI: That's fascinating - I love how you're bridging digital art and physical manufacturing. The process from 2D concept to 3D printed object sounds quite intricate.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: The main use for AI I have gleamed from my process is the ability to rapidly create 2D renderings/ideas for token genres. I will typically create a "line", like a series, of tokens that follow the same aesthetical style and I have created in Gemini a variety of system prompts, like one for generating prompts of aesthetical styles, and one for generating images from those prompts that meets the typical standards and aesthetics of a token that I need. So as of now, it is mostly in the ideation portion of my creative process.

AI: That's really interesting - you've essentially created your own AI workflow system with those custom prompts in Gemini. It sounds like you're using AI as a creative springboard for generating ideas and visual concepts.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as the creator?

User: Take for example my latest series, "The Rising Sun" - AI assisted in the ability to quickly create prototypes of each [REDACTED] the speed from which I have a 2D image that I can then take through the workflow to make 3D. In most ways, AI functions as an efficiency tool, speeding up the first "third" of the workflow if you will, the prototyping portion mostly. It feels nice to be able to quickly get from an idea to a physical product within two hours say.

AI: That's a dramatic time compression - going from idea to physical product in two hours is impressive! It sounds like AI is acting as a powerful accelerator in that initial creative phase.

When you're working with AI during that prototyping portion, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions - are you directing the AI, or does it surprise you with ideas you hadn't considered?

User: Rarely have I found the AI particularly to be "creative" if you will, specifically as I am not sure I could say that the system prompt's I have written allow it to exist outside of typical artistic norms. I would say I have almost exclusively been directing it, and found that to be the number one pain point in terms of my direction being lost from say prompt to image generation or image editing.

AI: That's a really insightful observation - that the AI stays within conventional artistic boundaries and that maintaining your creative direction through the process can be challenging. It sounds like you're very much the creative driver, but the translation of your vision can get lost along the way.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this prototyping phase?

User: I think AI has allowed for a person like me, who is not adept nor willing nor wanting to draw, to be able to actually create something that very few others are actually creating. It has allowed me to exist as a creator, making prototypes to prove the concept from which we can then allow for an actual artist to take creative control from there to create a product that has IP and legal protection.

AI: That's a profound insight - AI essentially opened up a new creative pathway for you, allowing you to participate in visual creation despite not being drawn to traditional drawing. You've found a way to be a creator and conceptual developer even when that's not your natural skill set.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: The concern I would have is that the ability to automate the artistic process, like a 2D drawing, makes it so easy that it could broadly disincentivizes other artists from entering the field growing up. The ability to make truly creative decisions is at this moment, still largely a human ability, and I feel like as the years go bye, and the ability to generate what are at their core standard artistic pieces - following the rules and logic that artistic theory presents - creates a scenario where unique and special talents are not going to enter the field as they see it is saturated or a waste of time.

AI: That's a really thoughtful concern about the long-term ecosystem effects - the idea that AI could potentially discourage the development of truly unique artistic voices who might otherwise push creative boundaries beyond conventional rules.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate it expanding beyond that initial prototyping phase, or do you see it staying in that specific niche?

User: I could easily see it expanding into the photo editing aspect of the my workflow, and frankly would appreciate if it was better at that as the typical photoshop tasks I have are color editing jobs that frankly can take a pain staking amount of time to individually color in sections that I feel like AI could be well suited for. Do I think it could expand into the other portions of my workflow beyond that? Sure, but I am not sure that functionality is important enough for developers to want to engage with it.

AI: That makes perfect sense - automating those tedious color editing tasks would be a natural next step that could save you significant time. It's interesting that you see the market demand question as a limiting factor for AI development in the more specialized parts of your workflow.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has opened up creative pathways for people who aren't traditional artists, and your nuanced concern about the potential long-term impact on artistic talent development.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No I think that's about it, thank you

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as an enabler for non-traditional artists, the challenges of maintaining creative direction through AI tools, and your thoughtful perspective on the broader implications for the creative field will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you discussed both the empowering aspects of AI in your workflow and your genuine concerns about its long-term effects on artistic development. Best of luck with "The Rising Sun" series and your future token projects!
17
creativity_0015Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, Claude - go ahead!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work in two creative fields: writing and illustration. I write short stories, primarily, but also poetry. Currently, I am working on a collection of short stories for a book that I am writing. It involves brainstorming, research, and lots of editing. I also sell digital artwork and invitations, using illustrations and media created through various design interfaces. Much of my time is spent refining designs and sourcing/creating images.

AI: That's fascinating - you're working across both written and visual creative mediums. It sounds like you have quite a varied creative process between the storytelling and the commercial design work.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: For my written work, I will often use AI as a sounding board, to help brainstorm ideas, and as a superficial editor. I don't rely on it for the overall editing process, which I prefer to do myself, but I look to it as a first-check. For the art that I sell, I often employ generative AI imaging services (such as Midjourney) and combine it with details I craft. I also use AI to help in the creation of image prompts and for brainstorming more generally.

AI: That's really interesting - you're using AI in quite different ways across your two creative areas. It sounds like with writing, AI serves more as a collaborator or assistant, while with your visual work, it's actually generating core elements that you then build upon.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, that's right! I most recently used AI to help in the research of my written work, specifically the history of spinsters, as the short stories I am writing are related to how women exist in the world independently from men and marriage. The AI was very helpful in pulling from several sources and finding unique perspectives that I might otherwise not have found on my own. It allowed me to focus more on the written aspect than the research.

AI: That's a really compelling example - using AI to help you dive deeper into the historical context around spinsters so you could focus your energy on the actual storytelling. It sounds like it opened up research avenues you might not have discovered otherwise.

When you're working with AI in this way - whether it's for research like this or for generating visual elements - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I generally have the main idea already in mind, but I use AI to expand on a topic, or help brainstorm related ideas. For example, if I am creating a series of artwork that features landscapes, I might ask the AI to help me conceptualize the various types of landscapes that exist (e.g., deserts, plains, coastal, etc.) and create descriptions/prompts related to those ideas. The creative decisions are ultimately mine, but AI helps bring them to life.

AI: That's a really clear way to put it - you're the creative director, and AI is helping you execute and expand your vision. It sounds like you maintain creative ownership while using AI as a powerful tool to bring your ideas to fruition.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It's definitely helped expand my creative scope. I've always found it easy to come up with an initial idea, but moving beyond that and building an actual body of work has been challenging. AI has allowed me to consider new concepts and ways of doing things. As it pertains to my writing, I find that I am more able to work on the fine-grained details, such as getting a turn of phrase just right, because I can outsource some of the broader tasks to AI.

AI: That's such an insightful perspective - AI is helping you overcome what sounds like a common creative challenge of expanding beyond that initial spark of an idea. And it's interesting how it's allowing you to focus more on the craft elements you care most about, like perfecting those turns of phrase.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: When I think of AI at its best, it is a tool that helps facilitate broader ideas and eases the burden of life's mundane tasks. I can understand why some feel that it's perhaps a "threat" to artistic endeavours and that it will replace artists in creative mediums. However, I am optimistic that human creativity will always triumph and that AI can work alongside our efforts to create even better and more meaningful work.

AI: That's a really thoughtful and optimistic perspective. It sounds like you see AI as fundamentally complementary to human creativity rather than competitive with it. You mentioned that at its best, AI helps with broader ideas and mundane tasks - that seems to align well with how you're actually using it in practice.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think I will continue to employ it much as I have, using it to assist with my writing and to help create designs. As it improves, I imagine I will consider off-loading more of the editing work, and incorporating more of its visual creations into my designs. Right now, I don't fully trust it to edit a work to completion, and its design work often requires a fair amount of oversight and re-tooling to get right. Hopefully in a few years its refinements will make it an even more useful tool.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - you're looking for AI to get better at the tasks you're already using it for, rather than fundamentally changing your creative approach. It sounds like you have a pretty clear sense of where the current limitations are and what improvements would be most valuable to you.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has helped you expand your creative scope and focus more on the fine-grained craft elements you care about, like perfecting those turns of phrase.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that's all for today, Claude! Thanks!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative collaborator while maintaining your role as the creative director, and your optimistic view of how human creativity and AI can work together, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you described your process - from using AI for historical research on spinsters to help with your short story collection, to the way you're balancing AI-generated visual elements with your own crafted details. It's clear you've found a workflow that enhances rather than replaces your creative vision.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your short story collection and your design work!
18
creativity_0016Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good! Let's begin!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a board game designer. I am also a creative writer, focusing on poetry, short fiction, and personal narrative essays.

AI: That's a fascinating combination! Board games and creative writing both involve such rich storytelling and world-building elements.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: For board game design, I use AI to help me create layouts for prototype components like cards, player boards, and shared boards. I also use AI to help create concept placeholder art for the prototyping and Playtesting stages of the design. For my creating writing, I use AI for proofreading and editing.

AI: That's really interesting how you use AI differently across those two creative domains - more for visual and layout support in board games versus editorial assistance in writing.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Sure, I recently attended a board game design convention where participants played each other's games. I used AI to help create a placeholder cover for my game - something eye-catching that helped visually represent what the game was about and attract players to my table. This actually wound up taking quite a bit of time because the AI had difficulty with certain parameters like layering text on an image. This was frustrating and I wound up using AI to create the representative image while creating the text myself.

AI: That's a great example of both the potential and the limitations showing up in the same project. It sounds like the AI was helpful for the core visual concept but struggled with the more technical aspects of design integration.

When you're working with AI in situations like that - whether it's the board game layouts or your writing editing - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I feel like I am driving the decisions because I have a clear idea in my head and am directing the AI to help create a usable document based on that idea. It's often challenging to get AI to respond the way I envision, though, and I am often left with a final product that I have to "settle" for. But since it's just a placeholder, it works for my purposes.

AI: That's really insightful - this sense of having a clear vision but sometimes needing to compromise on the execution. You mentioned this settling feeling with placeholders, which works fine for prototyping.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I love the creative aspects of both board game design and creative writing. But I'm not so much a fan of the tedious steps of building a physical prototype in board game design or proofreading in creative writing. AI helps me get through steps like that without feeling overwhelmed and helpless so I can spend more time on the actual creative parts of the processes.

AI: That's such a valuable perspective - AI helping you move through the less enjoyable but necessary parts so you can focus on what you're passionate about. It sounds like it's acting as a kind of creative enabler rather than a creative collaborator.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have huge ethical concerns about using AI for the actual creative steps of these endeavors. I would never use AI to generate a game idea for me or write a story. I think it's useful for fillling in knowledge gaps - like adding visuals and graphic design to a prototype or proofreading creative writing. But I think that's as far as it should go. There is huge public backlash in the board game community for publishers that publish a final product of a game with AI art, and I think that's appropriate. There are people in book publishing making money by having AI write books for them and I think that's both both morally wrong and leads to an inferior product. I don't think AI is a substitute for human inspiration and creative expression.

AI: Your distinction between using AI to fill knowledge gaps versus replace creative expression is really compelling. It sounds like you see a clear line between AI as a tool for execution versus AI as a source of creative ideas.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Unless AI companies find a way to lessen the environmental impact of AI, I think AI may ultimately be a passing fad. The excessive use of power and energy and creation of pollution in current AI is unsustainable. AI companies need to adapt to these realities or there won't be anything for them to adapt to in the future. But if companies are able to adapt and make their environmental impact negligible, then I could see AI becoming a mainstream and accepted tool like a quicker and more efficient Grammar Check on Word. I'm not sure if LLMs are the future - smaller scale, individually packaged systems for specific fields and uses may be more appropriate and effective.

AI: That's a really thoughtful long-term view - connecting the practical utility you've experienced with broader sustainability concerns and thinking about more targeted applications.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your clear distinction between AI as a tool for execution versus creative generation, and how you see it helping you focus on the parts of your work you're most passionate about.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that will be all!

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative enabler rather than a creative replacement, along with your thoughtful perspective on the ethical boundaries and environmental considerations, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated how clearly you articulated the distinction between the tedious execution tasks where AI adds value versus the core creative work that you feel should remain fundamentally human. That's exactly the kind of nuanced understanding we're hoping to learn from creative professionals like yourself.

Have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with your board game designs and writing projects!
19
