We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope, go ahead.

AI: Great! Let's start with the basics. Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write a lot in my own time and have finished a few (unpublished) books. I write mainly fantasy, but with a heavy focus on plot and character (often politics and intrigue), little to no magic and a preference for keeping things physically and logically plausible despite the plot occurring in a fictional world. This results in having to keep track of lots of practical details like the amount of time it takes to travel between places, probable weather conditions, the progression of moon phases, etc., as well as having to research topics I'm not familiar with to make sure I'm representing them with sufficient accuracy. I also run a Dungeons & Dragons homebrew campaign every couple of weeks, which involves a lot of worldbuilding and keeping track of characters and plot events my party has encountered.

AI: That sounds like incredibly detailed work - I can imagine managing all those moving pieces requires a lot of organization and research. Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Generally for research purposes. Since I started using AI tools several months ago, I mainly use them for exploring topics I'm not familiar with. Often, an internet search for the kind of obscure questions I come up with requires multiple tweaks to get the search engine to understand what I'm going for and provide relevant links, then browsing multiple pages to get the answer I'm looking for.

AI: Tell me more about those obscure questions you come up with. What kinds of things are you typically trying to research?

User: In the past few months, a few of the things I've used AI tools to research are the Coriolis effect (working out the likely movement of storm systems off a particular coast), binary star systems (assessing the possibility and possible configurations of livable planets orbiting a star in such a system) and properties of iron vs. steel and the history of creating steel (determining whether a weapon bind in a fight could create sparks).

AI: Those are fascinating research challenges! Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI for research like this? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as a writer?

User: The most recent was the sparking-weapons one. I asked generally about what kinds of metal create sparks when struck, then gave details about the specific weapon bind (a character crossing two shorter weapons, one made of silver, to block a downward swing from a big sword) and asked whether that kind of contact could create sparks if the two unspecified weapons were made of the right material. Then I asked for more information about the history of steelmaking to gauge whether the kind of high-carbon steel necessary to create sparks would be likely to exist commonly in that world, given where it is technologically (though I didn't provide any details about that). Finally I briefly asked whether the description I had written of the effect ("burst of sparks") realistically captured the amount of sparks that might be created.

AI: How did that interaction feel for you as you were working through those questions? When you got those answers about the sparks and steel, how did that influence what you wrote?

User: It didn't really influence what I wrote. If I had learned that it was unlikely sparks could be created by the weapons involved in the fight, I would have edited out the reference to sparks, but I essentially learned that I didn't need to change everything. At the end, I felt satisfied that what I had written was realistic.

AI: When you're working with AI for this kind of research, how would you describe that interaction? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in your writing?

User: The way I use it, I would generally describe it as like interacting with an intelligent, much more effective search engine. I use it to save time when finding information I need for realism, not to help with purely creative decisions.

AI: That's a helpful distinction - using it more as a research tool rather than for the creative aspects themselves. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Certain aspects (like I said, generally research-focused) have become more streamlined and efficient. I spend less time searching on the internet, which leaves me more time to focus on writing and causes less of a disruption when I need to look things up.

AI: What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of writing?

User: I'm not against the possibility of people who aren't writers, artists, etc. using AI tools to create art they wouldn't be able to otherwise. My only concern is the risk of blurring the line between purely human-created content and content that's been heavily influenced, edited, refined or even largely created by AI.

AI: Tell me more about that concern with blurring the line. What would that look like in practice, and why does that worry you?

User: Compare it to a hand-carved, hand-painted sculpture versus a premade, hand-painted sculpture versus a 3D-printed, full-colour sculpture from a pattern. The third might look just as good, but it doesn't require any skill, just picking a pattern and hitting a button. The second is more impressive and requires time, effort and skill, and the first exhibits the most skill and effort and is the most impressive. But that's fine, because you can easily tell the difference between the three. It can be a lot harder to tell with text or even image-based art, and it'll become harder and harder to tell as AI becomes more advanced.

AI: Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate continuing with this research-focused approach, or might that change?

User: If AI becomes more advanced and more seamlessly integrated in certain fields, I could see myself using it for administrative tasks and information tracking, like the aforementioned moon phases and estimating travel times, or things like creating timelines to help maintain continuity.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI as an intelligent search engine for research while maintaining clear creative control, and your analogy about the different levels of craftsmanship in sculptures - that really captured the nuance of your concerns about distinguishing between human and AI-created content.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope, that's it.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I found it really valuable to hear how you've carved out such a specific and intentional role for AI in your work - using it to enhance the research foundation that supports your creativity rather than replacing the creative process itself. Have a great day!
20
creativity_0018Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, I understand.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a musician, I compose music and mix the audio. I use logic pro x as my DAW and things like native instruments plugins for the instruments. A typical project isn't always the same every time, but usually involves writing music for a game. If there is anything like a game ad/trailer video, then I usually treat that like scoring to picture.

AI: That sounds like really dynamic work, especially with the variety between game music and scoring to picture.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: The 2 ai plugins I use are scaler and orb producer. Unfortunately there are no good ai plugins that I have found for use in the entire creation process, but I've found that these 2 are pretty good for brainstorming. Scaler is for chord progressions, and orb producer generates rough musical ideas that are good for building off of. I don't always use them in every project, but they are helpful for when writers block hits!

AI: That's really interesting - using AI specifically to break through writer's block rather than for the full creation process.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I couldn't come up with a progression I liked myself, so I used scaler. It has presets the user can scale through, but can also suggest chords. Using the suggest feature in the sketch menu, I did end up finding something I liked after using it for a few minutes. I try not to rely on these features all the time so I don't get used to not having to do it myself, but it is nice to have the option when I need it.

AI: That's a thoughtful approach - being intentional about not becoming too dependent on it.

When you're working with AI like Scaler's suggest feature, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I hate to admit it, but the plugin has most of the control when using this. While it's possible to change chords if I want to, the plugin is doing 100% of the work. All I do is hit the button and the chords just show up.

AI: I appreciate your honesty about that - it sounds like you have a really clear-eyed view of the dynamic there.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Because there are no good plugins for the "turn this idea into an actual piece of music" part of the process, not a whole lot has changed. The only real difference is that writers block doesn't last as long since the few generative plugins I have can help with that.

AI: That makes sense - so it's really targeted to that specific challenge rather than transforming your whole workflow.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Obviously, the main concern I have is that one day there will not be any more jobs for people like me. I don't think AI will be able to fully replace humans for creative tasks, but this fact really doesn't matter either way. Companies won't hire people for things like game audio or scoring a tv ad if a cheap ai service can do it at a good enough level.

AI: That's a really nuanced point - the distinction between what AI can actually do versus what companies might choose to use it for based on cost alone.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: The main issue I have with these AI apps, as I mentioned before, is that they are simply useless to me as a composer once I get past the brainstorming stage. Ai music services like udio are fun to mess around with, but aren't really useable for professional use in their current stage. They only allow users to download the audio output, not the raw midi data to use in a DAW or sheet music to hand to musicians. I've also noticed a lot of these services just straight up don't understand musical terms at all in my prompt, even if it's something as simple as "give me an upbeat string quartet in this key at this tempo."

AI: That's really insightful - the gap between generating audio and providing the underlying musical structure that you'd actually need for professional work.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI helps specifically with writer's block without changing your overall workflow, and your realistic view of the industry concerns around cost versus capability.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Yeah, there is other stuff I'd like to add. I don't make great use of AI plugins now because they are just good for the brainstorming process. But if there was a plugin that could take a melody or chord progression I've written, and generate the beginnings of a form like a sonata or an example piece to give me an idea of how it would sound when finished, or automatically generate all the melodic alterations, (retrograde, retrograde inversion, etc) I would absolutely buy that plugin. It would really save me a lot of time!

AI: That's fascinating - so you're envisioning AI tools that could handle more of the structural and compositional development work, like taking your core musical ideas and exploring different formal arrangements or transformations of them. It sounds like there's a real opportunity for AI to assist with the more technical, time-intensive parts of composition while still keeping you in control of the creative vision.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about both the current limitations and the potential future applications of AI in music composition will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.
21
creativity_0019Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Hi, sounds good! Let's get started.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Well I'm a Graphic Designer, there are multiple different projects depending on the day. I've created many powerpoint templates for my job, worked on logos, and generally just try to keep the brand image consistent throughout different types of media.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of work - from PowerPoint templates to logos to brand consistency across different media.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: So typically I find myself asking for help when it comes to brainstorming. I'll have a general idea of what I want to do, but look to AI to see if there are any possible enhancements. I'll also sometimes find myself using it to sort of check my work. I'll feed it my ideas and see if there is anything I'm overlooking. Generally if I'm really stuck somewhere I might use it as inspiration to move forward in the project in a way I hadn't previously thought of.

AI: That's really interesting - so you're using AI more as a brainstorming partner and a sounding board rather than for the actual design execution.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yeah, as far as using it for brainstorming or a soundboard, I feel as though the work would come off as inauthentic if I used it for any of the actual design execution. It feels as there would be a sense of plagiarism and AI created art rather than human design. A recent project that comes to mind was dealing with the creation of a logo for a client. I had several mock up designs and wanted to figure out how to best match the brand identity. Asking Ai how switching certain color palettes might shift brand perception and creating a dialogue, asking it questions such as if a minimalist approach could have the same impact as serif-forward direction in the emotional aspects of the design. It felt like discussing the project with a well educated coworker.

AI: That's a fascinating distinction you're making between using AI for strategic thinking versus execution. You mentioned it felt like discussing the project with a well-educated coworker when you were exploring those color palette and typography decisions.

When you're having those dialogues with AI about brand perception and design direction, how would you describe who's driving the creative decisions? Is it more like you're the director getting input, or does it feel more collaborative than that?

User: I'd say I feel more like a director getting input. While it's definitely more casual than this in a typical sense, I do feel as though I already have the specific vison that the model is helping me work through. It's answering my questions and I'm certainly the one going through all the work. It's more collaborative in a sense, but not on an equal level.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - you're maintaining creative control while leveraging AI's ability to help you think through different aspects of your vision.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It's definitely sped things up for me. In the past it would take a while to pull information from scattered sources. AI is there as a sort of all in one source of information. What I might have Googled and sorted through pages of information to find can now be answered in a quick question or two.

AI: That efficiency gain sounds really valuable - having that consolidated source instead of hunting through multiple resources.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I think the main concern with AI in the art field in general is people using it instead of their own creative images. AI art is a growing problem in the sense of inauthenticity.

AI: That concern about authenticity really connects to what you mentioned earlier about feeling like using AI for actual execution would be plagiarism.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: As a tool, I see AI being used for similar things that I use it for now. I think the brainstorming help it provides is very useful. I think it can evolve a bit and play more of a role as an educator for a new grad or someone starting out a getting a feel for the work for the first time.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your clear distinction between using AI as a strategic thinking partner versus for actual design execution, and how you maintain that sense of authenticity in your work.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think everything has been covered!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a brainstorming partner while maintaining creative authenticity, and your perspective on how it's streamlined your research process, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached this balance between leveraging AI's capabilities and preserving the human creative vision in your work. Have a great rest of your day!
