creativity_0020Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope, let's go!

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a generalist creative, so I do photography, photoshop/compositing, art direction, graphic design etc.

AI: That's a really diverse skill set! With all those different creative disciplines you work in, I'm curious - walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: AI has been most useful in handling my admin tasks, like keeping track of client info, responding to emails etc. I use Photoshops AI denoise, and occasionally Generative Fill in my photography work. I've also used Firefly and Stable Diffusion to iterate on initial concepts for illustration jobs.

AI: That's interesting - it sounds like AI is helping you on both the administrative side and the creative side. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: For the above illustration project, I had to produce a large suite of variations of a basic design. AI allowed me to easily and quickly iterate on my base illustration into a multitude of new versions. It worked well for this particular project.

AI: Tell me more about that process of iterating with AI on your base illustration. How did you work with the AI to create those variations - what was that back and forth like?

User: I completed my base illustration by using a hand drawn image that was modified in Firefly, iterated on, then redrawn again by hand. Next, this finalised base illustration was turned into many aesthetic variations in Stable Diffusion

AI: That's a fascinating workflow - going from hand-drawn to AI iteration, back to hand-drawn, and then using AI again for variations. When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions throughout that process?

User: Even when using something like Stable Diffusion (i.e. a tool that "gives a lot of control," using AI feels like a bit of a crapshoot. I understand what I want my final image to look like, but it's difficult to "art direct" the various models. I've experienced similar things using Suno.AI: regardless of the prompt, a lot of the final output seems to be heavily dependant on a random seed. In the future, I'd love to better be able to direct these models so there's less "randomness" involved in the creation process

AI: That's really insightful - the tension between having a clear vision and the unpredictability of the output. You mentioned wanting to better "art direct" the models in the future. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Has this randomness factor affected how you approach projects or your creative process more broadly?

User: Not really, if anything, for genuinely creative work, AI feels like an impediment of sorts. At the moment, its only truly useful if there's no budget for hiring a human illustrator. However, using LLMs has been tremendously helpful in taking the stress out of various admin-related aspects of creative work (analysing analytics, helping with client comms etc.)

AI: That's a really honest perspective - AI as helpful for admin but potentially an impediment for the genuinely creative aspects. You touched on budget considerations there. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: My main income source in creative work is photography-based. I don't see photography as immediately being as negatively affected by AI as much as illustration, music production, writing etc. I also think "fine art" in general is sort of immune to being damaged by AI. I don't believe there's any sense in being a luddite in day-to-day commercial work. Although I "get where people are coming from" when they're mad that people are using AI for illustration work, realistically, the people using AI tools would not have paid for a human artist anyway. I do feel for established illustrators who have had their distinctive style "ripped" by AI models.

AI: You've raised some really nuanced points there - the distinction between different creative fields' vulnerability to AI, and the economic realities versus the style appropriation concerns. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I look forward to Photoshop's integrated AI tools improving. At the moment, it's clearly early days, but they're getting really good. I think they're already extremely useful for doing cutouts etc. (the sort of work that would usually be very time-consuming or offshored to cheap labor) and Generative Fill is decent, but will be amazing very shortly. I most look forward to AI getting better at helping me with the admin stuff I mentioned earlier, though.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI being more of an impediment for genuinely creative work right now, while being genuinely helpful for admin tasks and certain technical aspects like cutouts in Photoshop.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nah, I think that's all. Thank you!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the different ways AI impacts various creative disciplines, the challenges with directing AI models, and that honest distinction between where AI helps versus hinders your creative process - all of that will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
23
creativity_0021Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions. I'm ready to begin.

AI: Perfect. Let's start with getting to know a bit about your work. Could you tell me about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a writer, primarily of science fiction and fantasy, so a project for me would be a book or a series of books, which can take months or years from start to finish. Rarely, I write a short story and that's more a matter of somewhere from hours to days, maybe weeks. But however big/long the project, they all go through similar phases of developing an idea; doing some pre-writing research, world-building, character development, initial plot development; the writing itself, which often includes revisiting earlier stages; an editing/revision/rewrite stage; and the final polish stage of editing. It's not a neat and tidy checklist of steps, but those are the general stages.

AI: That's a great overview of your process. Now I'm curious about how AI fits into that journey. Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process - where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I will sometimes use AI for research purposes, to help me get an overview of a topic and ideas on where I might need to do some deeper learning or give me ideas I might want to expand on, possibly point me toward resources where I can go and do more extensive research myself. I prefer not to rely on AI for research or learning, because it's still prone to inaccuracy and hallucination, but it's sometimes convenient for rounding up general information and giving me a starting point. And also just because I enjoy learning about things for myself, so I don't want to give up that part of the process. In the past, I'd do a Google search and check links and read general articles to get an overview point and then start doing the deeper research from there, so AI is essentially doing that initial Google search and general reading and compiling it for me.

AI: That's really interesting how you're using AI as that initial research springboard while maintaining your own deeper learning process. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI in this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I'm not a scientist, but I enjoy writing sci fi, so learning the science that underpins some of the usual science fiction elements is part of the job. I had developed a planet with a specific set of characteristics that I needed for the purpose of the story, but it was not Earth-like, so I asked AI to help me determine things like weather and day/night cycles and what color the sky would be and, most importantly, what other things might be different which I hadn't even thought about. It provided me with a list of suggestions and, most importantly, reasons for those suggestions, so I was able to do deeper research into what it was telling me. It got a couple of details not quite right, but they were details I might not have thought to include at all otherwise.

AI: That sounds like a really productive collaboration - getting both the suggestions and the reasoning behind them so you could verify and build on them. When you're working with AI in situations like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am definitely the decision-maker. I use AI responses the same way I use any other piece of nonfiction or even fiction that I read. Or for that matter, any other media or life experience. I see the sparks of what might be useful to include in a story and what to do with that spark.

AI: That's a clear sense of agency you maintain. I'm curious - what aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I spend less time clicking links and reading general knowledge articles to compile basic information during the research phase of writing. In much the same way that pre-computer, I had to go to the library and drag physical books to a table and page through them and make notes with pen and paper but then having the same (and more) information available at my fingertips on a computer made research more efficient and convenient, now using AI makes the process a little more efficient and convenient.

AI: That's a helpful analogy - seeing it as the next step in research efficiency. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I personally don't have a ton of concerns about AI in the field of writing. There's pirating, but there's always been pirating. There's AI writing stories and "stealing" readers, allegedly, but if readers think AI-written stories are "good," then that's their business, and they're probably not my market anyhow. Computers and ebooks and indie publishing made it both harder and easier to be a writer. AI makes it both harder and easier to be a writer. Honestly, most writers aren't making a commercial living from writing anyhow, and many more of them than will admit it aren't in it for the money, anyhow.

AI: That's a really pragmatic perspective on the market changes. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Assuming AI improves at providing accurate information and not hallucinating, it may continue to make the research phase of my writing process more efficient. On the other hand, it's not entirely about efficiency. I enjoy digging around for information and learning new things, so I don't necessarily want my information summarized and spoon fed to me.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining agency as the decision-maker and your analogy of AI as another step in the evolution of research tools - from physical libraries to computers to AI.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't have anything to add.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I found your balanced perspective on AI as a research efficiency tool - while maintaining your enjoyment of the learning process itself - really valuable. Have a great day!
24
creativity_0022Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good to me. I'm ready to start.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My creative work involves designing and leading workshops for businesses in many subject areas. As part of this work, I develop presentations, ebooks, training content and many other types of deliverables. These products always involve images, sometimes video, and require the use of graphic design elements. Sometimes this graphic design makes use of company logos, colours, and other in-house design elements but more often I have free reign to ensure the products I create best suite the type of workshop I design and facilitate. If I am doing a specific workshop, such as a design sprint, I am quickly capturing the work of the workshop attendees and using this to create products that represent the steps taken at various points in the workshop. To do this, I have to create documentation rapidly that is professional, visually appealing and can be used as part of the final deliverables I submit to the contracting company. I know that the more creative and visually appealing my content is the better it is received (with a professional focus of course!). I am often engaged in change initiatives, such as workplace culture, and create extensive interactive training files meant to engage staff in responsive learning. This means I focus my work on interactive learning scenarios rather than the read/watch videos and answer multiple choice questions that are so ineffective!

AI: That sounds like incredibly dynamic work - you're essentially creating custom learning experiences on the fly while also producing all the visual and interactive content to support them. The rapid documentation during workshops while maintaining that professional, visually appealing quality must be quite a juggling act.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI in many different ways in my work. Sometimes if I feel "stuck" at the beginning of a project I will use an AI tool to brainstorm various approaches to a specific subject, moving from a general overall view to specific features, such as activities for workshop participants, content for digital files, suggested graphic features (colour, elements, layout), questions for company contacts, etc. I often use AI to create specific images I want to insert in my presentations and final deliverables. I use AI for video generation as well, although I often don't use these videos as they are not quite at the quality level I want. I find AI tools where I can generate non-realistic image based video (cartoon, line drawing, etc.) and off-screen narration most effective. I have successfully used AI augmented reality tools and that was lots of fun and very engaging for workshop participants. I also use AI to critique and edit my final documents, or to add design elements to almost finished products. I actually use AI tools a lot!

AI: It sounds like AI has become deeply integrated across your entire workflow - from initial brainstorming all the way through to final polishing. I'm particularly intrigued by your mention of the AR tools being engaging for participants.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: A recent project where I used AI involved creating immersive learning for a nation wide veterinary company. Each veterinary location was owner managed but made use of company software, followed common procedural steps with business operations (including training) and worked to meet the business objectives of headquarters. I was asked to create training files that could be used at any location to train the various staff members commonly hired (front desk staff, lab techs, vet techs, vets, etc.) To do this I used AI software through a company called Thinglink. Using this AI software allowed me to take static images and information and make them interactive, including opportunities to use augmented reality. I absolutely loved the creative options I had through using Thinglink's AI and software platform. It made everything so much easier to create and share.

AI: That Thinglink project sounds fascinating - transforming static training materials into interactive, AR-enhanced experiences for such a diverse set of roles. You mentioned you "absolutely loved" the creative options and that it made things "so much easier."

Tell me more about that - what specifically felt easier or more creative compared to how you might have approached this type of project before AI tools?

User: First of all, I didn't have to code anything which was excellent because I don't code at the level needed to produce interactive files. AI took care of that for me. It made the learning design process easier and quicker which kept me from getting bogged down in small details that were not central to the project (it kept me on track). Another thing I liked was the help the AI tool gave with enduring content was accessible to many types of learners. In the past, I would need to access many different tools to create the content I was able to create using one AI driven tool. This saved me time which allowed me to focus more on producing engaging content rather than merging files and working with tools that don't always like to blend together.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI essentially removed technical barriers that were pulling your focus away from the core creative and pedagogical work. You went from juggling multiple incompatible tools to having one integrated solution that handled the coding complexity.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Right now, I would say that the back and forth with AI tools is a little stilted but this is getting better. Sometimes the "follow up" questions are repetitive or redundant and I find that frustrating. So I would say that I am driving the collaboration and creative decision making, but that seems reasonable given the current working relationship. I like taking an idea and expanding on it, often to the point where the final product is very different than the initial concept. I like my work and I want to decide what to keep and where to go next. I think you were right earlier - Ai definitely removes technical barriers (and I like tech!) that are frustrating/difficult and provides a way to enhance creativity so I am better able to meet my creative objectives. In the past, I had to "dumb down" many of the things I wanted to produce because either the tech wasn't readily available or I didn't have the knowledge to use it effectively.

AI: That's a powerful point about not having to "dumb down" your creative vision anymore. It sounds like AI is acting more like an advanced toolkit that finally matches your creative ambitions, rather than a creative partner per se.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Instead of using stock photos and videos, or having to use photo editing and video production software, I can now use AI tools that create what I want when I type in text or use something I already have - like a photo or presentation - to produce a unique image or video. That has really helped a lot. I save time and end up with better, more unique products for my clients.

AI: That shift from stock imagery to custom, unique visuals created on-demand is huge - especially for someone creating materials for so many different companies and contexts. It sounds like you're getting both efficiency gains and better creative outcomes.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Everyone (I think) has concerns about how AI affects their area of work, both now and in the future. I know that as AI becomes more effective in its use of information and precision in identifying what is needed, say for a presentation or training, people in my role will have less of a share in the workplace. I do struggle with the fact that even if I copyright my work, it is in digital format which means it will be used at some point by AI to complete a project I won't be paid for. It's impossible right now to keep track of how my work is used by others in effective ways, especially when final products are being purchased by companies, even if I register processes. It is definitely an area of concern and debate and comes down to discussions about ethics and AI. Right now, I see creative, unique process as still being human but also see the definite possibility of AI taking over my work. As much of AI development is commercially driven with profit being most important, I worry that concepts of ethical design will be left behind. Not that every company developing AI is unconcerned with ethics of course.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns - both the immediate issue of your work potentially being used to train AI systems that could eventually replace you, and the broader worry about commercial pressures potentially overriding ethical considerations in AI development.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think that AI's role in my creative work will continue to evolve rapidly. It is amazing the ways AI already makes tasks that were so difficult just a couple of years of ago (coding, image editing, video production, ideation) so easy now. Five years ago if you had told me I could produce training files with imbedded augmented reality easily, I definitely would be skeptical. Now, things have really changed. I predict I will continue to seek out AI tools that are multi-functional so I don't have to flip from one piece of software to another to create products that meet my design and content standards.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has enabled you to realize your full creative vision instead of having to "dumb down" ideas due to technical limitations, and your honest reflections about both the exciting possibilities and legitimate concerns around AI's rapid evolution in creative fields.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that's it.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the shift from technical limitation to creative enablement, the evolution from stock resources to custom-generated content, and your balanced perspective on both the opportunities and challenges ahead will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the exciting possibilities and the real concerns you have about the future of your field. That kind of nuanced perspective is exactly what helps us understand the full picture of how AI is reshaping creative work.

Thanks again, and I hope you have a great rest of your day!
25
creativity_0023Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I create simple content for Muffin Break, a cafe retail chain in AUstralia. I recently started a marketing role in a startup in afterhours and have been exploring video and image generating through different platforms

AI: That's really interesting - so you're working across both established retail marketing and startup environments.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I generate prompts for videos/images from AI as they can provide a better description for me. I also generate a lot of copywriting from AI like captions. I use platforms to generate and fine-tune image too - should I provide examples of the AI models?

AI: You don't need to list all the specific models right now, but I'd love to hear more about how you're using AI for those prompts and copywriting.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I had to generate a tagline for a coffee bean award our company has won. We won an award in alternative milk, decaf, expresso and milk based coffee - basically everything. We want a tagline that connects to customers, showcases our versatility and still be concise. I felt like different models have different abilities to generate a tagline. I was happy with the results and had a conversation to explore all possible options.

AI: That sounds like a really comprehensive approach - using the AI almost like a brainstorming partner to explore different directions for that tagline.

When you're working with AI like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It really depends on the model I am using. I prefer chatbots with follow up questions, as without them it feels one way. For models that provides follow-ups and their own thoughts or perspectives, I think AI does consider things that I haven't considered before. Not to mention the depth of wordings that AI can provide. With the right prompting, I see copywriters role being deminished and the demand for them would decrease.

AI: That's a fascinating distinction you're making between different types of AI interactions - the follow-up questions really seem to change the dynamic for you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think it would be copy writing. I need the idea myself but AI helps me execute them very efficiently. I think image and video generation is still not up to standard for my liking (I have been using free models for them). 80% of the images and videos are not what was desired. I think having editing function would be a game changing feature for these image generation models.

AI: So it sounds like AI has really transformed your copywriting workflow, but the visual content generation still has some gaps for you.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: My concern how our creativity would be diminished and everyone would have similar ideas based on AI models. It is such a shortcut when you can have 10 captions generated for you in a second that is 80% better than your own written one, so whats the point of writing it ourselves? Also AI provides a lot of generic content in terms of writing marketing briefs and people that are not professionals would not know how to tell which one is which.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns - both the homogenization of creative output and people not being able to distinguish quality. It sounds like you're seeing both the power and the potential pitfalls firsthand.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see free models having more advanced image/video generation models. I see a streamline of prompt generating and feeding within the same conversation to generate amazing images. I also think the step up on AI reliance would actually be surprisingly slow, as people come to realise that their biggest assets are still their own brain. I think AI will progress in a marginally slower pace than the past 5 years in terms of creative work or in general.

AI: That's a really interesting prediction about the pace slowing down, especially your point about people rediscovering the value of their own thinking.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how different AI models create different collaborative dynamics - that distinction between one-way versus conversational interactions seems really important for how the creative process unfolds.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the nuanced differences between AI models, the current limitations in visual content generation, and your thoughtful concerns about creativity and quality have been incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
26
creativity_0024Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, I'm ready.

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a writer, a freelance author, editor, and indie publisher. I have written and published a couple of nonfiction books about fibromyalgia on Amazon, and done editing and assisted with publishing on about 5 other books written by other authors. A project I'm currently working on is a devotional intended for people with chronic pain, fibromyalgia, or other chronic illness, focused toward helping them figure out how they function according to God's will for their lives, and it's intended to be the first in a series of devotionals, each one with 40 days or entries, and each one also designed to encourage journaling, so each entry/day will include a journaling prompt at the end, and the print version will have a few pages of ruled paper for journaling after each devotional. I am working right now on the content of the first one, and once I have it finished, arranged, edited, etc, I will design the cover and publish it to Amazon and possibly other avenues, in digital and paperback versions. does that answer sufficiently?

AI: That's a wonderful overview, thank you. It sounds like you have a really comprehensive approach to the publishing process - from writing through design and distribution. The devotional series sounds like a meaningful project, especially with the journaling component.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: For my writing and publishing, I typically use AI quite a bit in the research phase, especially in searching out bible verses or passages that fit the material/subject matter, and especially finding verses that aren't as commonly used for what I'm writing about. For example, in my current book, Habakkuk is coming into play quite a bit. Then I will sometimes reach out to AI for help if I get stuck during my writing with what to say about something or how to express a particular idea. I tend, in my own words, to get too wordy sometimes, and sometimes I have trouble being direct or efficient in expressing an idea, so I'll use AI to get feedback or ideas on how I can say something. And then in the editing phase, I am an avid proofreader, but I will use AI to double check my work and get final feedback. Also to find things that might not be super obvious, like an extra space where it doesn't belong. I have not previously used it for cover design, but I might end up incorporating some of that going forward.

AI: That's really interesting how you've integrated AI across multiple phases - from research through final proofreading. I'm particularly intrigued by your use of AI for finding less commonly used biblical passages like from Habakkuk.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I haven't had other major projects, because I've been working on the current one off and on for awhile , and have recently dealt with a lot of illness. But I can tell you that kind of a sub-portion of the current project where AI was quite useful was when I needed to figure out how to organize my devotionals for the book. I had all of these devotionals which I had written individually but didn't want to just put them in a random order, or something kind of arbitrary like alphabetical, and I didn't necessarily want to go in biblical order by the passage I used for each one, but something that would be meaningful for the readers. So through discussions with a couple of models I was able to decide on 8 categories, with each one having 4 smaller devotionals, and 1 that was more comprehensive, so that it was essentially 8 sets of 5 devotionals, with each covering an area like accepting your illness or God's path, understanding why God allows illness in His followers and how He uses it, and ways that we can still serve and God can still use us, despite, or even because of, our challenges. That help in coming up with the 8 subdivisions, and especially in organizing the devotionals into categories, was incredibly helpful. I felt overwhelmed going in, but by the end I was very grateful for this new tool.

AI: That sounds like such a valuable collaboration - taking what could have been an overwhelming organizational challenge and turning it into a meaningful structure for your readers. I love how you ended up with that thoughtful framework of 8 categories with 5 devotionals each.

When you're working with AI like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It's usually me in the driver's seat, but the models I've used have been very collaborative, making helpful suggestions that sometimes I would never have thought of- so although the AI isn't making the creative decisions, it is very creatively involved in the collab.

AI: That's a great way to put it - you're driving but the AI is creatively involved in the collaboration. It sounds like there's a real back-and-forth happening where the AI can surprise you with suggestions you wouldn't have considered.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I'd say it primarily has just become a bit faster and easier, like when I used to do all manual proofreading/editing, it took a lot longer because I would read and reread, literally forward and backward, to make sure I caught everything, and then I would do word level, sentence level, and paragraph level edits, with multiple rereads for each. Now I don't have to spend quite so much time on it. And creatively, it's been a great sounding board for ideas, like with the organization of the book, where I can tell it what I am trying to do from a very high level, and ask for thoughts and ideas on how to accomplish it, and we can go back and forth with iterations of the ideas, and it will make suggestions that I might not go with precisely but will sometimes spark a different way of viewing things that will help me decide on the right approach.

AI: That's a really insightful perspective - AI as both a time-saver and a creative sounding board. It sounds like it's particularly valuable in those moments where you need to step back and see your work from a different angle.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: For myself I don't really have particular concerns on an individual level, but for my field I have concerns in people leaning too heavily on AI for writing and creative tasks, and its impact on people's ability to think for themselves. I have heard people (or read, on social media, forums, etc) talking about that they just put in a high level concept and tell the ai to write a chapter, and then another, and it basically writes the whole book for them. I am concerned that will lead to a lot of books that are very derivative and ultimately restatements of the same things, but not truly creative. And similarly, maybe more importantly, it's being used, to my understanding, by students for creative tasks in schools, and I'm very concerned about the impact that will have on their learning to think and reason on their own, not to mention the ability to simply write something coherent.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns about the broader implications. It sounds like you see a real distinction between using AI as a collaborative tool - like you do - versus having it generate entire pieces of work. The point about students particularly resonates - that there's something important about learning to think through problems yourself.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I'm not really sure yet- to some extent it might depend on how AI itself evolves. But I am fairly set on the idea that creative works need to originate from the human brain, and that I really don't think people should be able to publish and receive royalties from something that was mostly produced by AI... I don't have a problem with that stuff being put out there, but It feels wrong to me that someone can claim a "creative" work, when they didn't really do any of the creating themselves, if that makes sense. I have a real thing about that, so I, myself, will probably continue to do the bulk of my writing the old fashioned way, and as you said, use AI as a tool for brainstorming, organization, research, etc, and double-checking my work for mistakes.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - maintaining that human creative core while leveraging AI as a support tool. Your distinction between creating versus claiming credit for AI-generated work really gets to the heart of some important questions about authorship and creativity.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI as a collaborative sounding board while maintaining clear boundaries about where the creative work should originate, and your concerns about the broader implications for both publishing and education.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not that I can think of at this time. Thanks!

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between leveraging AI as a powerful collaborative tool while preserving the essential human creativity at the core of your work will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

Best of luck with your devotional series - it sounds like a really meaningful project that will help a lot of people. Take care!
27
creativity_0025Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great! No questions!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I do a lot of different creative work. My most recent work has consisted of crocheting neon bucket hats for some teenagers going to a festival. I also do watercolor, macrame, and I write stories and poems. I've also sold furniture I've refurbished among other things.

AI: That's such a diverse range of creative work! It sounds like you really enjoy exploring different mediums and practical applications. I can picture those neon bucket hats being perfect for a festival.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I don't use AI too often because I really enjoy my creative process, but it as been really helpful for some tasks. I will use it to edit things that I've written or help find a suitable color palette for a painting idea I have. If I get stuck on a macrame knot or crochet stitch it has helped me quicker than searching myself does.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you use AI more as a targeted helper for specific moments when you hit a roadblock, rather than as a constant creative companion.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Actually, this is really cool. I saw an image on the internet a couple weeks ago and it instantly inspired me to want to paint something. I uploaded the image to AI and explained my idea for the painting and asked the AI to pull out the colors from the image that I would need and tell me the names. That was very helpful because I didn't have to go through the tedious task of mixing colors and doing a sample sheet to find the perfect shades.

AI: That's such a practical use! I love how you turned what could have been a tedious color-matching process into something much more efficient. It sounds like the AI helped you get to the actual painting part faster.

When you're working with AI in moments like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm definitely driving the creative decisions. It was my idea to begin with, the AI just helped me streamline the process so I could get to the fun part quicker. I am very cautious of keeping the creativity mine. I'm worried about AI taking humanity from the arts so I try to use AI as little as possible.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - using AI as a tool to handle the more mechanical aspects while preserving the human creative core. I can hear that maintaining your own creative agency is really important to you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Just that I'm a little quicker now. The tedious tasks that come along with art, mostly the planning, are a little easier now. MY art and MY creative process are still the same.

AI: I can really hear how intentional you are about preserving your core creative process. It sounds like AI has become a way to handle some of the prep work without touching the heart of what makes your art yours.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Just what I said earlier, I hate that humanity could be taken out of art. Even when humans create really beautiful things people will think it's AI or something. Though, the value of real true proven human art could be appreciated more in the future too, who knows?

AI: That's such an insightful perspective - the idea that authentic human creativity might actually become more valued as AI becomes more prevalent. It's interesting how you're thinking about both the risks and potential opportunities.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Hopefully not at all. I feel like I've already included AI as much as I'd like to.

AI: That's a really clear boundary you've set for yourself. It sounds like you've found that sweet spot where AI helps with just enough of the tedious tasks without encroaching on what you love most about creating.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI only for the mechanical prep work while fiercely protecting the human heart of your creativity - and how you think authentic human art might actually become more valued over time.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so.

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative agency and using AI as a very targeted tool for specific tasks will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about your concerns and boundaries - that kind of thoughtful approach to integrating AI while preserving what makes your work uniquely yours is exactly the kind of perspective we were hoping to learn about.

Have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with your future creative projects!
28
creativity_0026Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. Go ahead.

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work as a screenwriter for film and television. "Typical" varies quite a lot in my line of work. The circumstances around one project are never quite the same. However, you could probably categorise it into three buckets: original writing, rewriting, writing for hire. If I'm doing original writing, I will come up with an idea for a story, determine whether it's best suited to TV or film, then start to ideate on story, characters etc, until I have a sense of the overall plot of the feature or pilot/first season of the TV show. I'll then most likely write it on spec and have my agent send it out, rather than try pitching it out. For rewriting (usually film), I will digest the materials that need work, dissect flaws and strengths, how they can be improved, whether I am the right person to do so, and will then pitch my take on what/how to improve the project to the filmmakers involved. If I get the job, I will then try and execute this take, enhancing the script as a whole along the way, in addition to the specifics in my pitch. Usually, there will be at least one revision step where I can adjust/course-correct/inject further feedback from the filmmakers. In terms of writing for hire, I will be interviewed by show-runners, filmmakers etc. based on the strength of my previous work and then be invited to make suggestions or pitch my take on underlying material or ideas. My job will be to serve whoever is in charge of the creative vision, enabling their intentions and, if given the job, helping to execute this vision.

AI: That's a really comprehensive overview of the different types of projects you work on. It sounds like there's quite a bit of variation in terms of creative control and collaboration depending on which bucket a project falls into.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Usually during my own original writing, particularly at an early ideas stage when outlining, I might use AI to say, "Give me ten surnames for a man from Barnsbury born in 1971". Usually, I will not get the ideal response from asking that question, but it will send me on a path towards what I actually want by giving some examples and eliminating what I don't want.

AI: That's interesting - you're using AI more as a starting point or catalyst rather than for the final answer. Tell me more about that process of elimination and how AI helps send you down that path toward what you actually want.

User: Correct. I have found it most useful as a starting point in my own work, it saves me time from searching for inspiration on a broader canvas and instead delivers more quickly the sort of thing I'm looking for. Sometimes its easier to determine what you don't want than knowing exactly what you do; i.e. you probably have a ball-park idea of what you're looking for, but might not be able to articulate it exactly, a sort of "know it when I see it" creative aspect.

AI: That "know it when I see it" aspect is really insightful - it sounds like AI helps you externalize possibilities so you can react to them and refine your instincts.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I used it for generating character surnames in a feature film I wrote. I also used it as a way of iterating idioms, which where part of a joke in a character's dialogue - the dialogue wasn't sounding quite right, or quite as funny as what I was reaching for. Again, the AI didn't give me something better, but by combining and refining its suggestions I was able to reach something I was happy with.

AI: That's a great example of the iterative process you described earlier. When you were working with those idioms for the character's dialogue, what did that back-and-forth feel like? How did you know when you'd landed on something that worked?

User: The back and forth was pretty limited. I asked for iterations based on "bull in a china shop", which it listed. I was then able to hone in on "loose cannon" asking for more like that. I then asked it to generate examples a few more times before settling on which of the examples I thought was closest to my intention.

AI: So it sounds like you had a pretty clear sense of direction - you knew "loose cannon" was closer to what you wanted than "bull in a china shop." When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I don't think I would really describe it as a collaboration, more a more advanced search/indexing, whereas I would consider collaboration to allow some level of independence of thought from the collaborator. As it stands, I am the sole driver of creative decisions, based on years of experience, exposure and personal taste.

AI: That's a really clear distinction - viewing it more as an advanced search tool rather than a creative collaborator. Given that you're the sole driver of creative decisions, what aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this capacity?

User: Rather than wait for inspiration to come to me, I can make my creative process move faster by trying to be more active/targeted.

AI: That shift from waiting for inspiration to actively seeking it out sounds significant. You mentioned you primarily use AI for your original writing projects. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: A lot of people are worried about AI taking away screenwriter's jobs. I am less worried about that. I'm more worried about producers/directors using AI to generate a first draft, then needing a writer to rewrite it to a high-enough quality, while stiffing them on the money they would have received if they had gone though a normal development process from scratch. I suspect AI's presence in writing will create a more homogenous and good/okay base playing field. Ultimately, this will serve to make outstanding screenwriters and original story-tellers more outstanding, provided that of course, it is humans reviewing the scripts, as the post-facto nature of any AI analysis of screenwriting would otherwise mean the importance of originality would likely be downplayed in an evaluative model, as it simply wouldn't have the sample cases to compare it against - think MOONLIGHT or EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE; both are Oscar winners, making significant sums of money across the world, but prior to MOONLIGHT there had never been a Black gay Oscar-winning film, likewise EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE's fusion of martial-arts, sci-fi, familial relationships and 1/2nd generation immigrant culture is without peer.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - you're less concerned about replacement and more about how AI might change the economic structure and evaluation of screenwriting. Your point about originality is particularly striking - that AI evaluation might miss truly groundbreaking work because it lacks precedent to compare against.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think using AI tools to create proof-of-concepts for pitches or even support green-lights/financing/commissioning will become commonplace, helping to lower a sense of risk for people controlling the cash. Without total authorial control over what it is able to generate, I don't see it being able to supplant physical production (real actors on sound stages/locations etc), but I hope it would lower the barrier to entry in what is an expensive and time consuming creative endeavour. I can totally see myself doing a work-in-progress AI generated short/scenes/sequence as a way to sell a project I am working on.

AI: That's a fascinating vision - using AI as a visualization and proof-of-concept tool to help sell projects. It sounds like you see it potentially democratizing the pitching process by making it easier to show rather than just tell.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI might change the economics of screenwriting and your distinction between AI as an advanced search tool versus true collaboration.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope, I think that's it.

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as an accelerated creative catalyst rather than a collaborator, and your thoughtful analysis of how it might reshape the industry's economic and creative landscape, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
29
creativity_0027Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds pretty self-explanatory! Just hope I can answer everything you need to help with your research

AI: Perfect! I appreciate your willingness to help. Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: So my main business I have that involves creative work is using a Cricut machine to vinyl items such as T-shirts & clothing and also vases, wine bottles, glasses, mugs etc as gifts. I can pretty much vinyl most things including photos. A typical project to me could be designing a logo for a T-shirt, cutting this into vinyl and then applying it with heat.

AI: That sounds like really hands-on, personalized work! I can imagine each project has its own unique requirements depending on what you're creating and who it's for.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Oh it does, it can be so time consuming depending on the design. I have to use a special tool to weed out the vinyl before transferring it. Imagine at Christmas when you have loads to prepare.

AI: That sounds like a lot of intricate work, especially during busy seasons like Christmas! I can imagine the weeding process alone must be quite time-intensive.

I'm curious - in all of that process you described, from designing logos to cutting and applying vinyl, do you use any AI tools along the way? Maybe for design work or any other part of your workflow?

User: I certainly do! I literally get mental block when trying to think of ideas so I use AI to generate these ideas for me, usually Gemini. Gemini will give me design ideas to generate in Cricut and also gift ideas involving vinyl.

AI: That's really interesting! Mental block is something a lot of creative people can relate to, and it sounds like Gemini has become a helpful brainstorming partner for you.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Now let me think, I work on so many projects I don't know whether I am coming or going most days!! The most recent project I can think of was a friend wanted me to make a unique, memoriable gift for their mum who was turning 90 years old. I didn't want it to be the usual cushion or vase so I asked Gemini to provide me with a list of gift ideas to make. The ideas that Gemini came out with I never would have dreamt of in my wildest dreams, they were so thoughtful and out of the box.

AI: That sounds like exactly the kind of breakthrough you were hoping for! I love that Gemini helped you move beyond the typical options to something more special.

Tell me more about that - what was it like when you saw those unexpected, thoughtful ideas come back from Gemini? How did it feel to get suggestions that were so different from what you might have come up with on your own?

User: To be honest it was a relief and like a weight had lifted off of me. I didn't want to let me friend or her mum down with a rubbish gift idea which would look bad on me but I just could not think of anything. I also had so much going on that, honestly, I just didn't have the time to think either. Gemini saved me time. I felt happy and satisfied if that's how you can describe it I guess. Also optimistic, knowing there was this ability to generate insane ideas to make my business amazing.

AI: That relief you described really comes through - it sounds like Gemini didn't just help with ideas, but actually took some pressure off you during a busy time.

When you're working with AI like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: I don't quite understand this question? do you mean how do I feel working with Gemini to create these ideas?

AI: Yes, exactly! I'm curious about the dynamic between you and Gemini when you're working together. For instance, are you directing Gemini with specific requests, or is it more like Gemini is leading with suggestions that you then choose from? How do you decide what to actually make from the ideas it gives you?

User: Well in this instant I literally just told Gemini the scope which was a gift for a 90th birthday, for a lady, has to be unique and different and Gemini took over and gave me the suggestions. There isn't a set formula I follow to pick which idea to have, I just go with my gut and the one I felt stood out as the most unique and also one I was obviously able to make.

AI: That makes perfect sense - trusting your instincts about what feels right and what's actually feasible for you to create. It sounds like you set the parameters and then let Gemini run with it, but you're still the one making the final creative call.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Nothing has really changed with my creative work since using AI, by using Gemini it has enabled me to create new ideas to make my business thrive and showcase my work and stand out from the crowd but I do still also create my own ideas too.

AI:

User: Nothing has really changed with my creative work since using AI, by using Gemini it has enabled me to create new ideas to make my business thrive and showcase my work and stand out from the crowd but I do still also create my own ideas too.

AI: That's a great distinction - so AI has added to your capabilities rather than replacing how you work. It sounds like you've found a balance between using Gemini for inspiration and still drawing on your own creativity too.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: Regarding my own personal creative work, I do not have any concerns, AI only seems to help make life easier. If you are considering the creative field as a whole then some artists maybe worried about how AI can generate a creative piece of work in seconds when it would take them hours, even days and could ruin their livelihood. As I do not use AI on this level it does not affect me to that depth.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - recognizing that your use case is different from someone who might be creating full artwork from scratch. It sounds like for your business, AI is more of a brainstorming tool rather than doing the actual creative execution.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I really hope my predictions are correct on this one but over the next five years I would love you to be able to tell AI the design you would like in Cricut, for example, say you want to design "Merry Christmas Mum" on a wine bottle. you simply say this and the AI would be able to design this in the Cricut design space, including all of the correct measurements and then determine the correct tools to use to cut the design. It would then load the design to the Cricut machine and cut it and also have another tool to be able to weed the design also so all that is needed is to peel the design at the end to transfer. Having to plan the design in Cricut is so time consuming especially when adding pictures so having AI be able to do this, which I think is more than capable, would be a life saver.

AI: Wow, that's such a specific and vivid vision! You're describing a complete automation of the technical setup process - from design creation all the way through to the cutting and weeding. It really sounds like you've thought about exactly where the time bottlenecks are in your current workflow.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as a brainstorming partner that relieves pressure without replacing your creative judgment, and your vision for how it could streamline the technical aspects of your work in the future.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: no I think that is everything

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a brainstorming tool, how it provides relief during busy periods, and your vision for future automation in the technical aspects of your work will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to walk me through your process and share such specific examples. Best of luck with your Cricut business!

creativity_0028Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, all good, please fire away!

AI: Great! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: So I am an author, to date I have published two novels, both thriller novels with philosophical themes. I am currently writing a fantasy series.

AI: That's fascinating - thriller novels with philosophical themes sounds like a really compelling combination.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I find that AI is a terrific way of becoming "unstuck". If I can give you an example, I required a way of describing an institution somewhat like a bank in a pre-industrial society. The list of suggestions given (I always ask for 10 plus suggestions) essentially rebooted my semantic field and I came up with the perfect description for what I wanted , triggered by the best suggestions. I use AI in this way both with descriptions for concepts (novel or adapted), or sometimes just word choices.

AI: That's a really interesting way to use AI - as a kind of semantic catalyst to break through creative blocks. It sounds like you're not taking the AI's suggestions directly, but rather they're sparking your own ideas.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: So I've recently been writing a new series called [REDACTED], with the first novel being called [REDACTED]. In addition to using it on a number of occasions as the catalyst as you say for word choices and conception descriptions, I also use it to talk though world-building consequences. For example, the word has two moons, I described their cycles to the model, and used it to determine the frequency of sacred nights on which both moons were full.

AI: That's a really practical application - using AI as a kind of world-building consultant to work through the logical implications of your fantasy elements. The dual moon calculation sounds like it would have been quite tedious to figure out manually.

When you're working with AI in these situations - whether for the semantic suggestions or the world-building calculations - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Oh, I am, absolutely, I don't really approach AI when stuck creatively, but rather when stuck in terms of something logistical, or in giving definition to a creative idea.

AI: That's a really clear distinction - so you're using AI more as a research assistant or analytical tool rather than as a creative partner. It sounds like the core creative vision and decisions remain entirely yours.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I'd like to add, my experience with existing AI models is that they are overly eager to please, and so (though I'm sure this will change), I don't fully trust the model to be sufficiently critical and demanding as an editorial partner. The aspects of my work that have changed the most are time, even if an AI generated idea sits only as a place holder (though often the suggestions are great), I can move on from something that might have had me stuck for quite some time!

AI: That's a really insightful observation about AI being "overly eager to please" - and how that affects your trust in using it for more critical editorial feedback. The time savings aspect sounds significant though.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of writing?

User: I don't to be honest, I feel that AI is a tool, very much like the internet. While it can be abused, this will not be at the cost of human creativity, of the market for human creativity. Those who adapt best to the tool will benefit from it, even in the creative fields! I would love to get the point where AI operates as a full editorial partner

AI: It's refreshing to hear such an optimistic perspective! You seem to view AI as potentially enhancing rather than threatening creativity. You mentioned wanting AI to eventually operate as a full editorial partner - that sounds like it would require overcoming that "eager to please" limitation you noted.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I would AI to evolve to the point that it has a deeper understanding of tone and style in shifting contexts. I would also want it to connect the dots (or see that the dots cannot be connected) over much larger bodies of text so that it can help to identify any and all plot holes in early drafts of novels. Essentially becoming this full editorial partner we've been discussing.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a tool for getting "unstuck" logistically rather than creatively, and your optimistic view of it as potentially becoming a true editorial partner once it develops better critical capabilities.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I've typically used a text based AI model, but it would be interesting to be able to use a voice based model so that I could remain on screen, and read a short passage, and hear a response (perhaps giving a different perspective, and certainly breaking concentration on the passage at hand to a lesser extent). Just thought I'd mention that. Anyway thank you!

AI:
31
creativity_0029Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great! I don't have any questions.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: You bet. I design and hand-paint custom shoes for people. A typical project includes planning the shoe design digitally, which is usually what takes longest. Once I have the thumbs up from the customer, I sketch the design onto the shoe. I then use paints and brushes to paint the design onto the shoe, using a matte protectant to seal and protect the design when finished.

AI: That sounds like such a unique and personal form of art! I love that you're creating something so customized for each person.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I reach to AI quite frequently throughout the process. Sometimes I use it to help me write emails if I am feeling overwhelmed or nervous about certain customers. I use it to help me come up with ideas for what to put where on my shoe designs. I also use it to discuss color combinations and how they would look on certain parts of the shoe. If I'm facing serious artist's block, I'll have it make some designs for me to gain inspiration from. I NEVER copy these designs - they are used strictly to inspire unique ideas for myself. I also ask it for recommendations on different types of paints and sealer, if I'm wanting to switch my materials up a bit.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI has become quite integrated into different stages of your work, from the business side with emails all the way through to materials advice.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: [REDACTED] Yeah! I recently did a commissioned pair of Attack on Titan shoes. When I started out, I wasn't really sure how I wanted to represent the characters on the shoes. Positioning is super important; the shape of the shoe can really distort characters, if they're not placed right. To help me, I had the model make a few different shoe layouts for me, each with the characters facing certain directions. I was able to find a position that fit very nicely on the shoe. I also had it help me figure out which color would be best for the background. It was incredibly helpful to have the support - it made the project run so much smoother, which was really needed at the time. I was lined up with 11 different commissions, so it made all the difference. I'll attach a picture of how the shoes turned out!
