The picture honestly makes them look a little weird, but I promise they look pretty perfect in person, haha.

AI: Wow, those shoes look incredible! The character positioning really does work perfectly with the shape of the shoe - you can see how much thought went into that placement. And that gradient background is striking.

When you're working with AI like you did on this project - getting layout suggestions and color advice - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: The collaboration was great, but there were a few frustrations. I can't blame AI for not being able to produce a perfect shoe with a perfect design idea, you know? It did take a few tries before we could elaborate on design ideas that I liked. Fine-tuning was frustrating. When it came to the non-visual collaboration, everything was very smooth and easy. I definitely felt as if the give and take was equal at some points. When I felt like my creative juices just weren't flowing, the model gave me some creative ideas that really pulled me out of it. For the most part, I was the driving creative force, and the AI model was just the icing on the cake to help ensure I captured everything perfectly.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - you as the driving creative force and AI as the "icing on the cake." It sounds like you've found a balance where you maintain creative ownership while using AI as a creative partner when you need that extra push.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I feel as if my creative playing field has been substantially increased. Before, I definitely felt limited to what I could conjure up in my mind. Now, I am able to get ideas and inspiration from the suggestions AI gives me. I am able to think of things that I never would have on my own. So I would say that my motivation, attention to detail, and ability to create are all aspects of my creative work that have improved since using AI.

AI: That expansion of your creative playing field sounds really empowering - going from being limited by what you could conjure up on your own to having this broader landscape of possibilities to explore.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have a LOT of concerns. The more AI learns, the better it becomes at mimicking humanity and human creations. I worry that true artists, like myself, will be entirely replaced by what is easily replicated by AI. I am so worried that true human expression will be outplayed by limitless AI ability.

AI: That's such an honest and deeply felt concern - the fear that the very thing that's helping expand your creative possibilities could eventually replace the human expression that makes your work uniquely yours.

Tell me more about what you mean by "true human expression" - what is it about your work that you feel AI can't replicate?

User: It can't replicate the strokes of paint that I put on the shoes. It can't replicate the earnest feeling that is dug into every pen stroke on a drawing that I pour my heart into. It can't replicate the smell of the paint as it dries, or the hours of effort and planning that go into each and every shoe or drawing. It's so frightening that something that took me almost 20 hours can be created in a matter of seconds with AI.

AI: The way you describe those physical, sensory, and emotional elements - the actual paint strokes, the heart poured into each pen stroke, even the smell of drying paint - those are such profound aspects of the creative process that go far beyond just the final visual result.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll continue using it as that "icing on the cake," or do you see the relationship changing?

User: I do think that I will continue to use it as my icing. It is truly helpful. However, if I hear word from the artist community that we need to make an effort to stop AI use, I'll happily comply. I don't want to be a part of something that ultimately reduces the value of human creativity.

AI: That willingness to step away from something that's genuinely helpful to you if it threatens the broader creative community really speaks to your values as an artist and your commitment to preserving human creativity.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated how you described that tension between AI expanding your creative playing field while simultaneously worrying about it potentially replacing the deeply human elements of your work - those paint strokes, that earnest feeling, the hours of effort that can't be replicated.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between using AI as a creative tool while preserving the irreplaceable human elements of your artistry will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

Best of luck with your upcoming commissions, and thank you for creating such beautiful, personalized art for your customers!
32
creativity_0030Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. No questions, I'm just ready to get started!

AI: Perfect! I love the enthusiasm. Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a writer! I'm in the process of working on my debut novel right now, so I'm deep in the trenches of writing and editing my manuscript. Therefore, I have to come up with things like a title, characters, plot, etc.

AI: That's exciting - congratulations on working on your debut novel! That's such a significant milestone.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I don't allow myself to use AI for the creative meat and potatoes of my story, because I want it to be authentic to me. However, I do find myself looking for help for titles in my work by giving AI a synopsis of my story. I also tend to go to it to come up with names that sound good for siblings, last name ideas, things like that which aren't as crucial to the creative part of my work.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction you're making between the "creative meat and potatoes" and those supporting elements.

Can you describe a specific recent example where you used AI for one of these tasks? What was that experience like - how did you approach it and what happened?

User: I was really struggling with a title for my book. I had everything else, my story was well rounded, but nothing was sticking out within my work that would make the perfect, catchy title. I used AI to brainstorm, which then got my creative juices flowing, and I bounced back and forth with it until we found a perfect title.

AI: That's fascinating - you describe it as bouncing back and forth until "we" found the perfect title.

Tell me more about that collaborative process. What did that back-and-forth look like, and how did you know when you'd landed on the right one?

User: So, I started by basically saying, "here's the plot of my story, here's something significant that happens in it, here are the main characters' namds

AI: I can hear you were starting to explain the process - it sounds like you gave the AI some key story elements to work with. Please go ahead and finish that thought - I'd love to hear more about how that conversation unfolded.

User: Sorry, hit send on accident! Let me start over. So, I started by basically saying, "here's the plot of my story, here's something significant that happens in it, here are the main characters' names, can you help?" AI then gave me some ideas, none of which sounded right. However, they did point out something minor from my story I mentioned, which gave me an idea. It was a play on a classic phrase that most people would probably know, but not all. I asked for advice on if it was clear what I was playing off of, and if AI thought people would get it. AI said they likely would, and gave me some similar ideas. One of the ideas it gave was almost perfect, so I changed one word and got a title I love.

AI: That's such an interesting process - it sounds like the AI helped you see your own story from a different angle by highlighting something you'd mentioned but maybe hadn't fully considered as title material.

When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions? How does that feel to you as the writer?

User: Personally, I feel like the way I use it still allows me to drive the creative decisions. I'm mostly using it as a way to force myself to look at things from a different angle. As a writer, or a creative person in general, it's really important to me to stay in control of the creative decisions and use AI solely to see my work in a different way, kind of like if I was bouncing ideas off of another writer over coffee.

AI: I love that analogy - like bouncing ideas off another writer over coffee. That really captures something important about how you're approaching this collaboration.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: I think that I spend less time stressing over minor things and more time actually writing and being creative when I don't have to do the "parts" that don't come as naturally to me, like coming up with a title or making sure names fit together. I have a tendency to get stuck on certain elements like that, and I lose focus and motivation. Now, they're more streamlined and less of a pain point for me.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI has helped you move past some of those creative blocks that used to derail your momentum.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of writing?

User: I do fear that AI could take over writing. I know that may not happen soon, as using AI, I know it currently isn't quite as capable of producing what a naturally talented writer could. In other words, I know the difference between an AI story and a human's work they've poured their heart into almost immediately. There are markers that give it away, you know? But, I also know AI gets smarter every day, so it's not unlikely. I think for a while, there will be some push back from the audience at least that wants something from real live authors as opposed to AI, so that gives me some hope. I like to think AI can just continue to find new ways to help us without being the driving force in someone's creative process, but only time will tell.

AI: You raise such thoughtful points about the tension between AI's evolving capabilities and that human authenticity that readers seem to value. It sounds like you're cautiously optimistic about finding that balance.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll expand how you use it, or do you see yourself sticking to these boundaries you've established?

User: I think, for my given occupation, it'll stay about the same. I think AI will continue to grow and get smarter as we continue to train it, so maybe I'll end up with things like strong titles without a lot of back and forth that starts with some more cheesy ideas. However, like I said, I wish to remain the creative powerhouse of the situation, so I'm not sure I'd ever just blindly take something AI created and slap it on my work as my own. I'd want to keep using it as a tool to get my creative juices flowing, not letting it create for me. Who knows, maybe there will be even more ways in the next couple years that it can assist me without giving up my creative edge, which will allow me to expand on how I use it.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared, and you've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining that boundary between using AI as a creative catalyst versus letting it drive the creative decisions - that coffee shop collaborator analogy really stuck with me.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think that's about all I have!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative authenticity while using AI as a strategic tool - especially for those elements that used to create blocks in your workflow - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

Best of luck with your debut novel! It sounds like you've found a really thoughtful approach to integrating AI that supports rather than replaces your creative vision.
33
creativity_0031Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, I am ready to begin.

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a video producer. I don't have very "typical" projects. Right now I am working on a video for a hypnotist and a short animated film about God and His many forms. Earlier this year, I shot an independent feature film about 4 Generations of black women with sleep disorders and psychic powers. In the past, I was mostly focused on making television and podcasts about technology.

AI: That's a fascinating range of projects - from hypnotist videos to animated films about spirituality to features about generational stories. It sounds like you work across very different genres and formats.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: It completely depends on the project - the videos for the hypnotist mix short shots of AI-generated images and video in with traditional graphics and editing. The short about God is all AI-generated video edited together with traditional non-linear editing. The feature film did not use AI, but I am going back now to see it I can stitch together some stills we shot with footage we took by using AI, without it looking like I'm using AI.

AI: That's really interesting how you're tailoring your AI use to each project's specific needs. You mentioned trying to use AI to stitch together stills and footage for the feature film "without it looking like I'm using AI."

Tell me more about that - what does it mean to you when AI integration is invisible versus when it's more apparent in the final work?

User: The first two projects I mentioned are animated, so it is either easy to blend the AI-generated animation in with the rest of the video, or, in the case if the religious piece, it all looks AI generated, which is fine. But the feature film is all real people, in real environments, so anything AI-generated has to look real - characters have to be consistent, people can't have 6 fingers, etc.

AI: Ah, I see - so it's about matching the visual language and expectations of each project. The animated pieces give you more creative freedom with AI elements, while the live-action feature demands that AI contributions are seamlessly realistic.

Can you describe a specific moment or scene from one of these projects where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that experience feel for you as the creator?

User: Right now, I am trying to make transitions from some photos we took that I photoshopped to look like they are old black and white pictures to color footage of the same moment. Unfortunately, the photos and footage don't line up perfectly, and one is a different shot entirely, so trying to get a smooth transition has been very interesting, but also frustrating. I haven't gotten a result I like yet.

AI: That sounds like a challenging puzzle - trying to bridge between those different visual elements while maintaining the story flow. The frustration you're describing is really telling.

When you're working through that process of not getting the result you want yet, how would you describe the back-and-forth with the AI tools? Are you feeling like you're directing the process, or does it feel more like negotiating with something that has its own ideas?

User: DEFINITELY the latter. I have gotten close a couple of times - I just had to tweak an angle, or two actors in a scene looked perfect, but another was off, but trying to make that one fix will set off a whole slew of other problems. I will get there (i think), but it feels liker I'm going to have to just run a hundred variations of the same thing before I get one perfect generation, completely at random.

AI: That's a really vivid description - the idea that you might get the perfect result completely at random after a hundred tries, and that fixing one element creates a cascade of new problems.

When you do finally get that result you're looking for, how do you think you'll feel about it? Will it feel like your creative achievement, or something else?

User: I don't care what anybody says about working with AI not being "real" art - when I get a good result, I am very happy. I have almost always created as part of a team, where i was writing something for someone else to read, or giving stuff I shot to someone else to edit it. Working with AI feels like that - like we are a team working together to make something special that we couldn't make separately.

AI: That's such a compelling perspective - thinking of it as team collaboration rather than something that diminishes your creative contribution. It sounds like your background working in collaborative environments really shapes how you approach AI.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Are there things you find yourself doing differently now in how you approach projects or solve creative problems?

User: I spend a lot of time learning - or figuring out - how to use different tools to accomplish what I want. I also spend a lot of time trying to make tools do something that they are not good at - wrong tool, or not advanced enough, or just something I can't figure out how to get working the way I want it to work. So I guess the aspect of my creative work that has changed the most is that I can do more things, but figuring out which things I can and cannot do and how to do the things I can takes a lot of time.

AI: That's a really honest assessment - this tension between expanded possibilities and the time investment required to navigate what's actually achievable. It sounds like there's a whole new layer of technical exploration that's become part of your creative process.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Same as everyone's I guess - that as companies figure out how to use these tools, they will need less people to do the creative work. There will still be some jobs for people who are really good at it, but fewer and fewer. It's notr really any different than the past couple decades ahve been though - My first job was working on a TV show with a staff of 60 people. A decade later, I was on a team of about 12 people doing the same thing. A decade after that, 5-10 people were all the people we needed to make 5 separate shows a week.

AI: That's a really insightful historical perspective - you're seeing AI as the latest wave in a long pattern of technological efficiency rather than something entirely unprecedented. The numbers you're describing are pretty stark - from 60 people to potentially even fewer with AI tools.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think your relationship with these tools will change as they improve?

User: Yes. I am pretty solid on image generation now, whenever I can get past preconcieved notions of what I think an image should look like and accept what I am given. Video isn't quite the same...yet. I feel like video has just gotten to the point where it is possible to get a good shot using my computer instead of an outside company's server farm. But now that it has hit that point, I just need to figure out how to make the tools i have do something close to what AI want them to. It took me a couple of months of generating with Stable Diffusion before I got any still images I could stand looking at. The video side is coming along faster than that, at least.

AI: That progression you're describing is fascinating - from those early months with Stable Diffusion where nothing looked right, to now feeling solid with image generation, and seeing video starting to follow a similar but faster trajectory.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI collaboration feeling like teamwork rather than something that diminishes creativity, and your historical view of how technology has continuously changed team sizes and workflows in your field.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: It takes 20% of the work to get 80% of the way to your goal, but then 80% of your work to get the last 20%. I feel like Generative AI is at 80% of where it needs to be, or nearly there. Getting the last 20% will be much harder.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - and it perfectly captures what you were describing earlier about getting close with those transitions but then struggling to nail down those final details that would make it seamless.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the collaborative nature of AI work, the evolution of team sizes in creative industries, and especially that 80/20 observation about where the technology currently sits will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how candid you've been about both the possibilities and the frustrations.

User: Thanks, this has been fun!

AI: I'm so glad you enjoyed it! It was really enlightening to hear about your work across such diverse projects and how you're navigating this evolving landscape of AI tools. Your perspective as someone who's been through multiple waves of technological change in the industry was particularly valuable.

Thanks again for sharing your time and insights with us - it will definitely help inform our understanding of how AI is fitting into creative workflows. Best of luck with those transitions you're working on!
34
creativity_0032Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. Let's start with your first question.

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write short fiction stories. The genre varies; Sci-Fi and Fantasy are favorites of mine, but I write in other genres too. It starts with brainstorming, and when I find an interesting idea or concept to try I start researching the topics that might be relevant. I plan an overall arc for the story to follow with some specific events or scenes to keep my later drafts organized. I typically make edits on the fly, but usually make several edits/refinements throughout the process. Once I think I'm happy with how the project is turning out I'll usually take some time away from it before returning to see if I'm still happy with it. Afterwards I upload the story to an appropriate public site if I'm just writing it for fun, or send it to the person who commissioned it in that case.

AI: That's a really detailed process - I can see how you've developed a structured approach from concept to publication.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I do try to limit my use of AI, so it's my voice and writing style and not someone else's. Sometimes I use AI while editing, to make sure what I've written makes sense and flows in a coherent manner, as well as double-check my grammar and spelling. I also sometimes use it for brainstorming, a change in perspective can really help to come up with interesting ideas. I prefer to do my own research for now, however, sometimes Google's AI Overview has links to pages that are useful, but I usually disregard the actual content of that overview and just check the cited webpages instead.

AI: That's interesting - you're being very intentional about maintaining your own voice while selectively using AI for specific functions.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I recently was writing in Microsoft Word, which has integrated AI. It automatically highlights what has been written and prepares suggestions. In the case of grammar or spelling mistakes it is very useful, but sometimes it prepares suggestions that go beyond that. In this particular case it suggested I change some words to make the sentence easier to read when I had written it to emphasize a specific word in that sentence. The suggestion was technically right, it would be easier to read if I followed its advice, but it would have also defeated the whole point of writing the sentence to begin with. I didn't want to see anymore AI suggestions for the rest of the day because I kept thinking that if everyone used these kinds of suggestions to "improve" their writing, everyone's style would be the same. No one would have a unique writing style anymore. I suppose the experience made me feel conflicted. AI can be very useful for small corrections, but when it is forced into software it's invasive, encouraging over-reliance on the model's functions.

AI: That's a really powerful example - the tension between technical correctness and creative intent. It sounds like that moment crystallized some concerns for you about how AI integration can push writers toward homogenization.

When you do choose to work with AI - like during your deliberate brainstorming sessions - how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: The answer to that question varies. Sometimes I have a specific idea I want to explore, and sometimes even a planned method for that exploration, in which case I'm driving the creative decisions. Sometimes I let the model decide the direction the conversation goes for awhile. In these cases I don't ever take the ideas suggested, but let them act as a guide to slingshot my mind to an adjacent idea. I think the different perspective provided really helps with that. I think the collaboration during these brainstorming sessions works as well as it does because I get to decide how much control I have at any one time.

AI: That's fascinating - you've found a way to use AI as a creative catalyst while maintaining control over when and how much influence it has. The idea of using AI suggestions as a "slingshot" to adjacent ideas is really compelling.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The brainstorming sessions I think have changed the most. If I was totally blocked I'd stop and do something else to wait for inspiration to strike. I didn't get as much work done when that happened. Now, when I can't find something interesting to write, I can use AI to help a bit. Sometimes it can help me with exploring settings for stories, or come up with ways to challenge my own perspectives, which usually leads to me thinking about how I can explore that change in perspective through the stories I write. Even if nothing comes of it, I still feel like I was a little more productive than I would have been otherwise.

AI: It sounds like AI has really helped you work through those creative blocks that used to mean stopping entirely. That shift from waiting for inspiration to having a tool that can help generate it seems quite significant.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: The homogenization of writing style that was mentioned earlier. Everyone wants to express themselves, and AI does makes those small suggestions that add up, until everything that's written sounds the same. This might make it hard for someone who is trying to use their voice to express themselves to be able to find their audience, or for their audience to stumble onto their work. If AI is invasive in the software we use to write it becomes even harder to not use it, to resist thinking "maybe it knows better than me." That makes me ask the question "when will it be more worthwhile for AI to write for me, instead of writing myself?"

AI: That's a profound concern - the gradual erosion from "AI helping me write" to "AI writing for me." The way you described that internal dialogue of "maybe it knows better than me" really captures how these small suggestions can undermine creative confidence over time.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: If I'm optimistic, I see AI being more useful in helping with research; providing more accurate responses and even being able to accurately delve into more obscure topics. This could really condense the time it takes for me to actually start writing about a topic. I am, unfortunately, not a very optimistic person. I expect AI to continue down the path it's already heading, being invasive and furthering the homogenization of writing and other arts. It will be better and better at writing or generating images, and people will find it too much to resist over-relying on AI to do things for them, instead of helping them do it better themselves.

AI: Your distinction between AI helping you do things better versus doing things for you really cuts to the heart of the matter. It sounds like you see a critical difference between AI as a research tool versus AI as a creative substitute.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control and that powerful example about the Word suggestion that would have undermined your intentional emphasis.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No. Thank you for asking these questions and hearing me out.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the balance between leveraging AI as a creative catalyst while protecting your authentic voice, and your concerns about the subtle but cumulative effects of AI suggestions on creative individuality, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating AI into your workflow while remaining mindful of its potential pitfalls. Your perspective as someone actively navigating these challenges is invaluable.

Thanks again for participating in this research!
35
creativity_0033Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: I'm ready!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I do quite a lot of creative work with AI. I do many crafting projects, design social media posts, peruse AI for inspiration for crochet projects and patterns, come up with fun ideas to boost morale at my place of work, etc. Recently, I used AI to design a bubble gum machine logo for my office. It gives out adult prizes. AI helped me come up with a vinyl design that reads: [REDACTED]'s Subpar Prize Machine: Lower your expectations and turn the knob. It has a melty smiley face on it. The staff members love it! I also have used AI recently to come up with many social media posts and inspiration for the dental office I work at.

AI: That sounds like such a fun and diverse range of creative projects! I love the humor in that bubble gum machine logo - "Lower your expectations and turn the knob" is brilliant.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I ultimately begin with AI to expand on an idea that I have. Let's talk about the bubble gum machine to start. I first saw an old bubble gum machine for sale and thought it would be awesome for staff members to be able to earn tokens to get a spin. When I got my initial idea, I then turned to AI to first get recommendations on fun trinkets to put in that were fun and adult humor. Then I asked it to generate some fun names to call it based on some factors: my office is in the basement, it hands out subpar gifts, it is my own personal machine and not sanctioned by the doctors, etc. After inputting my initial ideas, I was able to conversate back and forth until I settled on the perfect name. After settling on a name, I continued to consult it to come up with a fun slogan so staff members would know not to expect anything super great from the machine, as it is truly just a fun little game to boost morale. After I decided on a fun slogan, I was able to consult it to come up with a design for a vinyl cut out to put on the front of the machine.

AI: That's such a detailed process - I can really see how you use AI as a creative partner throughout each stage! It sounds like you start with your own spark of an idea and then use AI to help you develop and refine it step by step.

Can you describe what that back-and-forth conversation with AI felt like as you were working through the bubble gum machine project? How did you know when you'd landed on "the perfect name"?

User: I initially stated what I was doing with the project and gave the details I mentioned to you earlier. AI then gave some suggestions and I went back and forth multiple times, over multiple days even, to finally come up with a name I thought was perfect. AI suggested many names to begin with and I would prompt back stating to focus more on one thought process (ex: add more suggestions that have to do with my office being in the basement, give me more suggestions that get the point across that these prizes are basically worthless, give me a suggestion that uses my name, make it shorter because it needs to fit on a 5" x 17" glass front and I want the words readable, etc.). After spending quite a few turns (probably 10 over a few days), the model suggested a name I thought embodied the project very well--[REDACTED]'s Subpar Prize Machine. I then wanted to add a little subtext so went back to the model to gain ideas for wording. Again, I needed it to be a short length so it would fit well on the space I needed. After the same back and forth process I mentioned above, I settled on Lower your expectations and turn the knob. I run the ideas by a few friends and they all agreed it was a great name.

AI: That's fascinating - so it wasn't just one quick exchange, but really an iterative creative dialogue over several days. It sounds like you were very much steering the direction, giving AI specific constraints and feedback to guide it toward what you were envisioning.

When you're working with AI in this back-and-forth way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Honestly, I have been reluctant to use AI much because I'm afraid of neglecting my own brain of the exercise it gets by brainstorming, writing, editing, etc. So, I always like to make sure I am giving the initial idea and use AI to hone that. I would hate to live in a world where humans don't have original ideas and everything is just AI generated. This is kind of a guardrail I have set for myself. I usually get my ideas from different types of media (mostly things I see on socials) and then try to expand that so it has a spin that is all my own. At that point, I will sometimes take it to AI to expand on that. It is rare that I use AI to come up with an original idea before first brainstorming the situation on my own. It makes me feel like a bit of a failure if I can't come up with my own thoughts to get started. It's a fine line between using my resources (AI) and still using my own brain so it doesn't waste away.

AI: That's such a thoughtful perspective - you've clearly given a lot of consideration to maintaining that balance. It sounds like you see AI as a powerful resource but want to make sure you're still exercising your own creative muscles and maintaining ownership over the initial spark.

Tell me more about that feeling you mentioned - the concern about your brain "wasting away" if you rely too heavily on AI. What does that look like for you day-to-day?

User: Many times, especially when coming up with social media post ideas, I think it would be so easy to just pop a simple prompt into AI and let it do its thing. I have to exercise those restraints that I have put in place for myself so that I continue to use my own thought process. Sometimes I feel it would be easier to just start with AI, and I might actually do that on a day where I have zero time or am in a crunch. However, I always feel a little like a cheater and unoriginal when I do that. My work is my personality in a way and if I am posting something or coming up with a creative idea for work, I want to be able to say that it's mine and not just an AI prompt generation. Keeping in touch with me as a person helps me maintain those guardrails when using AI. This is also true of writing. I have to do some business writing and have always prided myself on being a good writer. I feel those skills are going downhill due to the ease of just plugging in a prompt and having a letter done at my convenience. I don't feel I can actually take credit for it, which actually dampers my self confidence a bit.

AI: That's really honest and insightful - there's clearly a tension between the convenience AI offers and wanting to maintain your sense of creative ownership and skill development. It sounds like when you do use AI as a starting point, it leaves you feeling less connected to the final result.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Both in terms of the work itself and how you feel about your creative process?

User: I would say that it just comes around to being a bit of a faster process, especially with social media posts. I have so much information right at my fingertips and don't have to dig around to come up with ideas as I have in the past. And even though it's more convenient, I feel it still takes away from my own creative process in some ways. However, I am usually pleased with the ideas that are recommended that maybe I wouldn't have thought of on my own. However, it's' still a sticky situation because I always feel like I can't really take full credit for those ideas. It feels much better to show my boss a post and say that I came up with it from my own brain rather than using AI and then showing the boss and having to admit that it didn't come from my own brain but AI instead. Overall, even though I have tried not to let the addition of AI take away some of my creative process, it has definitely had an impact on it still. Again, its the same idea where I feel less proud of projects when AI is involved.

AI: That sense of pride in your work really comes through - it sounds like there's something fundamentally different about how you feel when you can say "I came up with this" versus when AI played a role, even if the final result is good.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: Broadly speaking, I fear we are entering an era where humans have no original ideas. If we don't work to continue the brainstorming process in our own minds, it is a skill that will ultimately be left behind for humanity. I already see this in younger people who have basically known AI their entire lives. And surely there is a limit to AI, meaning the world might become a very boring place with limited ideas. Will all media start to look the same? Also, will we enter a time where we can't believe anything we see is real and must assume it is AI generated? Ultimately, it may be true that my job will no longer exist, as social media will just be managed solely by AI. That seems rather damaging.

AI: Those are some profound concerns - the idea that we might lose fundamental human skills like brainstorming, and that creativity itself could become homogenized. The point about younger people who've grown up with AI is particularly striking.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll maintain those same guardrails, or might your approach change?

User: I desperately want to keep those guardrails, but it is human design to give in to easier ways. I do fear I will rely on it more and more, even though the intent exists to avoid this. I will say that working behind the scenes being an AI trainer has given me a bit of a better perspective of AI since humans are behind the scenes of the training. However, I even see this being taken over and replaced with other AI models in the future, as this is already beginning on some of the projects I work on. My ultimate goal would be to continue the creative process as I am now. However, that's just not reality and things will always be evolving. I expect that I will eventually lean toward the human condition of conforming to the AI world and relying on it more and more. My hope is that AI developers will seek ways to make the process of relying on human input and feedback with models so that human creativity is not lost forever.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your honest perspective on the tension between convenience and maintaining creative ownership - that internal struggle you described about feeling like "a cheater" when using AI as a starting point really illuminates something important about how creative professionals are navigating this new landscape.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative authenticity while navigating the practical benefits of AI, and your concerns about preserving human creativity for future generations, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about the internal conflicts many creative professionals are likely facing - that balance between efficiency and ownership is clearly something worth understanding better as we think about AI's role in creative work.

Have a wonderful rest of your day!
36
creativity_0034Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. Let's go.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a science fiction novelist. Most of my series have involved time travel, but the current one is a bit of a departure. It's a four-book series set in the late 21st century, and partially takes place on a Mars that is in the final stages of terraforming. My academic background is more historical and political than scientific, so I frequently have biology questions, and sometimes need to know things like how far it is from one geographic point on Mars to another.

AI: That sounds fascinating - a terraforming Mars setting must require a lot of world-building research. I can imagine those biology and geography questions come up quite a bit when you're trying to make that world feel authentic.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Any time I have a question that requires me to use advanced math. :) I've also used AI to help come up with some basic rules for a language. Occasionally, I use AI as an advanced thesaurus when I can't come up with the right word. Or when I need to figure out how much a knish might cost at a food truck in 2090 given typical rates of inflation. Stuff like that.

AI: Those are some really practical and specific uses! I love the knish pricing example - that's such a concrete detail that probably helps make your future world feel real and lived-in.

Can you describe a specific recent project or moment where you used AI? What role did it play in that instance, and how did that feel for you as a writer?

User: I needed to figure out whether the process of growing lab meat and the quality of the final product would be affected by the lower gravity on Mars.

AI: That's such a specific and technical question! Tell me more about that - how did you approach getting that answer, and what was it like working through that problem with AI?

User: I simply asked what the differences might be and was then given a list of changes. One of my beta readers is a biologist, and I usually run these things past her, but she was away from the computer. When I spoke with her later, she said that the response was well-reasoned and lower gravity would probably have many of those effects. I like being able to get a general idea on topics like this before I run them past beta readers and other specialists I know, because it saves them time I have my ducks in a row before I contact them. I was less happy with some of the creative suggests for a generic name for lab meat, but I can usually come up with those on my own.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you're using AI as a kind of first-pass research tool to get oriented before you consult your human experts. That seems like a smart workflow.

When you're working with AI in these situations, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions in your writing process?

User: Oh, I'm definitely in the driver's seat. It wouldn't work for me if I ever felt that AI was anything other than a productivity tool. My writing style is commonly known as "pantser" as in "flying by the seat of your pants." If things are mapped out too carefully, I lose interest in writing that story. Why finish it if you already know everything about how the story ends? :) So, I wouldn't be including to use AI for plotting or outlining -- I don't do much of that. And it's worked for 27 books, so I'm not going to tamper with it.

AI: That's a great insight - it sounds like you've found a sweet spot where AI handles the technical research questions that support your world-building, but stays completely out of the creative storytelling process that drives your passion for writing.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Has it shifted how you approach certain parts of your process?

User: One odd thing that I've done from time to time is to write a scene in first person present (which is my strength), then let AI shift it over to 3rd person past. This is the first series that I've written in that tense and POV. It doesn't come easily to me, but this is a slightly different genre than I've written in before, and first person present isn't the norm. I won't make this mistake again, but I've got to finish this last book before I can get back to what I like best. Writing in a way that flows for me and then having AI take the first pass at shifting it to the other style actually saves me a good bit of time because I stay in the flow.

AI: That's such a clever workaround! It sounds like you found a way to let AI handle the mechanical translation between tenses and POVs while you stay in your creative flow state.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: I've got a lot of concerns. I used to be traditionally published, but royalties dropped and they were no longer worth the tradeoff against the creative control that I retain by self-publishing. Unfortunately, its really easy to self-publish--and a book doesn't have to be good to earn money. There are people out there using AI to crank out massive numbers of derivative books each year using systems that were trained on many books, including my own. They can produce hundreds, maybe even thousands, of bad knockoffs in the time that it takes me to write a single book. Even if the quality is low, there are ways to game the system so that you make decent money on Amazon -- and that screws up the algorithm, resulting in lower royalties for many authors who are producing high quality, original work. I love the idea of AI as a research and editing assistant -- it helps me to get more of my books to my base of readers by reducing the time I spend on those tasks. But it's a very slippery slope, and writers (including me) are worried that flooding the market with derivative AI content is going to make it harder to earn a living and much, much harder for the next generation of creatives to break through.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - you're experiencing both the benefits of AI as a productivity tool and the very real concerns about market saturation and economic impact. It sounds like there's a meaningful distinction for you between using AI to enhance your own creative process versus using it to generate content wholesale.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think your current approach will change?

User: For me, AI is a useful tool to handle tasks that I'd either do myself or else have to pay for another round of editing/proofreading. I write in the middle of the night, so having AI to answer a question that is stumping me is far better than waiting a week for someone to get back to me. It's possible that I'll find a few other tasks where AI works for me, but overall, I think it will remain largely the same.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how you've maintained creative control while using AI strategically for research and technical tasks, and your nuanced view of both the benefits and the serious market concerns facing writers today.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that about covers it. Thanks!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a research assistant while maintaining complete creative control, and your concerns about the broader market impacts, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the practical benefits you've found and the genuine challenges facing writers today. Best of luck finishing up that final book in your Mars series!
37
creativity_0035Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I do visual arts in a variety of mediums. An example project may be an acrylic painting, that I may than scan to make prints out of.

AI: That's great - I love the combination of traditional and digital elements in your process. The transition from physical painting to digital prints is really interesting.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I like using AI to brainstorm and when I am stuck. I might use it to help generate a list of ideas when I have a creative block, or to help expand an idea. For instance, if I know I want to paint something using paynes grey with a red accent that has an autumn theme, I might ask AI for a list of possible subject matter or ideas for paintings.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to use AI as a creative partner for ideation. I can see how having that brainstorming support would be valuable when you hit those creative blocks.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: An example of when I used AI was when I wanted to paint a series of woodland themed small paintings. I planned to paint a mushroom in one, a fern in another, and then I asked AI for a list of other ideas to help me come up with other subjects to expand the series on theme. It helped me decide to also paint a fallen tree and wild berries.

AI: That's a wonderful example - it sounds like AI helped you expand and round out your woodland series in a really organic way. The mushroom, fern, fallen tree, and wild berries create such a cohesive collection.

When you're working with AI in situations like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am driving the creative decisions. I find I need to carefully craft the prompts and feed the AI creativity to get helpful feedback and responses.

AI: That's really insightful - the idea that you need to "feed the AI creativity" to get useful responses back. It sounds like there's a real skill to crafting those prompts effectively.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I'm not sure if anything has changed. Sometimes AI is able to help, othertimes it is not helpful. Now, when I feel blocked or frustrated, I have an additional tool (AI) to try to use to see if it unblocks my creative flow.

AI: That's a really practical perspective - having AI as one more tool in your toolkit when you need it, rather than it fundamentally changing how you work. It sounds like it's become part of your problem-solving arsenal.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm not a fan of people using AI to generate images or replace artists in fields. I worry humans will loose artistic talents or appreciation for human made art.

AI: That's a really important distinction you're making - using AI as a brainstorming tool versus using it to generate the actual artwork. Your concern about losing human artistic talents and appreciation for handmade work really resonates.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I hope it will be more helpful and insightful, and able to help me grow as an artist. I would like it to be able to critique my work in a constructive way to help me grow was an artist by providing useful tips about how to practically improve my work.

AI: That's such an interesting vision - AI as a constructive critic and mentor to help you develop your artistic skills. The idea of getting practical, actionable feedback to improve your technique sounds really valuable.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how you're the one driving the creative decisions and need to "feed the AI creativity" - that really captures something important about how this collaboration works.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that covers it all, thanks!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a brainstorming partner while maintaining your role as the creative decision-maker, along with your thoughtful distinction between AI as a tool versus AI as a replacement for human creativity, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and your concerns. Have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with your woodland series and future projects!
38
creativity_0036Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds, great. I don't have any questions at the moment.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Sure. I'm an illustrator and graphic designer. My projects can range from working on logos for small businesses to creating illustrations, infographics, and more for online courses.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of work! I can imagine each type of project has its own unique demands.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: AI is useful for when I'm trying to craft pitches to prospective clients, respond to client emails, or when I want to know what a contract or TOS says.

AI: That's interesting - it sounds like you're primarily using AI for the business and communication side of your work rather than the actual creative design process itself.

Tell me more about that. When you're working on the visual aspects - the logos, illustrations, or infographics themselves - do you find yourself using AI tools there as well, or do you keep those parts of your workflow separate?

User: I don't use Generative AI in my creative work. If I'm feeling really stuck and uninspired, I might use ChatGPT to brainstorm ideas. But ultimately, I try to avoid AI use in my workflow as I don't want to become dependent on it. Creativity is a muscle and if I don't exercise it, it will wither!

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - the idea of creativity as a muscle that needs to be exercised. I can hear that maintaining your own creative independence is important to you.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I've been wanting to branch out and create stock patterns and graphics to sell online. I generally design in my own aesthetic/style, but I know that I'd sell more graphics if I created them in a larger range of styles and appeal to more niches. So I asked for a list of styles and niches. I probably could have done my own research, but it was way faster to just get the list using AI. Once I got the list, I picked a few niches that seemed promising.

AI: That's a great example of using AI as a research tool to expand your business opportunities. It sounds like it saved you time on the market research side while still leaving all the creative decisions and actual design work in your hands.

How did that feel for you - using AI in that more strategic, business-focused way compared to keeping it out of the actual design process?

User: I mean, it was definitely a timesaver and time is money. That said, using AI as an assist, rather than letting it do the actual core, creative work, allows me to maintain my integrity as a creative.

AI: I really appreciate that distinction you're making between AI as an "assist" versus doing the core creative work. That seems to be a key boundary for you.

When you think about maintaining your integrity as a creative, what does that mean to you? What would cross the line from assistance into something that would compromise that integrity?

User: I think using AI tools to do all of the work is too much. Again, I don't use generative AI in my work, because at that point, what is the point of me as a creative? And what is the work without the artist? Simply entering in a prompt to generate an image that is ultimately simply an average of images scraped off the web is not creative. The end result lacks substance.

AI: That's a powerful way to put it - "what is the work without the artist?" It sounds like for you, the human creative process and intentionality behind the work is what gives it substance and meaning.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI, even in those limited ways you've described?

User: Having AI assist with the tedious things like emails, contracts, pitches, etc has saved me time that I can put towards working on creative projects and improving my skills.

AI: That's a really valuable benefit - freeing up time for the work that truly matters to you. It sounds like AI has become a way to handle the administrative burden so you can focus more energy on developing your craft.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Many creatives, including myself, are concerned about the hype surrounding AI tools and how it affects our job stability. Creative work is often undervalued in our society. When budget cuts need to be made, it's often the arts that take a hit. In corporate environments (which is where I work), management is often not aware (or chooses to ignore) the value that the creative team brings to each project. There's a lot of discussion online about how decision makers in many companies are reducing headcount because they are letting AI handle the tasks that humans previously did. The creative industry isn't immune to this trend. Stores like Michael's are selling seasonal "art" prints that are made with AI, Anthropolgie's 2024 holiday shopping bags featured AI art--these are instances where, likely for the sake of profits, companies are choosing to use AI, rather than hire an artist. If companies continue to do this, it will have a devastating effect on creatives' livelihoods.

AI: You've articulated something really important there - the broader economic and industry dynamics at play, not just the technology itself. It sounds like your concerns go beyond the tools themselves to how decision-makers are choosing to implement them, often without fully understanding or valuing what human creatives bring to the table.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll maintain these same boundaries, or do you anticipate your approach might shift?

User: I will likely continue to use AI to help me with mundane admin tasks and to help me understand the legalese that contracts and TOS are swamped with. I know that some photo editing tools utilize AI to improve a photo's lighting, colors, etc. I am okay with that. But I will never use GenAI to do my work, because again, why even be an illustrator if do that? I might as well quit.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your clear articulation of the boundary between AI as an administrative assistant versus AI doing the core creative work, and your perspective on how the creative industry's undervaluation makes these changes particularly concerning for professionals like yourself.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that's all. Thank you.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative integrity while finding practical uses for AI, and your concerns about the broader industry implications, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've considered these boundaries and the bigger picture impact on your field. Take care!
39
creativity_0037Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I have two types of creative work. The first is creating images for adult coloring pages. They are more intricate than a child's coloring page with more detail. For instance, a realistic dragon sleeping in a forest surrounded by trees, fairies, crystals, and hidden images amongst the leaves. So, a project would be taking a creative scene in my head and turning it into an image of black and white lines with no shading. The second is writing creative passages for use in social media. They are short passages but very specific in their theme and tone. A project would entail focusing in on a message that I want to convey and wording it most efficiently without losing the tone. I then might put that message to music and imagery to create a "reel."

AI: That's fascinating - you work across both visual and written creative mediums. I can picture those intricate coloring pages with all those hidden details, and the challenge of crafting those precise social media passages sounds quite different but equally creative.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I have more creativity in my mind than in my hand so AI helps me in that regard. AI is very helpful for creating coloring pages. I have the scenes in my head but I do not have the talent to put that scene onto paper. As far as creative writing, sometimes I have a message that flows but sometimes I can't seem to get it across eloquently. AI's creative writing is fantastic. It can help me put my thoughts to paper, or reword more eloquently, or shorten a passage for social media.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - having more creativity in your mind than in your hand. It sounds like AI is helping bridge that gap between your vision and execution.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: For coloring pages, I recently requested the dragon sleeping in the forest as well as a few other images, like a sitting Buddha and a fairy in flight. I allowed some creative freedom for the AI when I requested the images such as 'surrounded by trees and crystals and other magical objects' and I built off of what AI offered in the image. It felt fun at times and frustrating at times if the AI did not understand the feedback. For creative writing, I recently asked for a short passage (although longer than a social media reel) with a very particular tone of invoking the dragons out of dormancy to activate the Earth's ley lines (darn it I can't find it right now) and I needed help creating an eloquent passage. I was floored by the results. It was so on point with my intention.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you had quite different experiences with AI across those two projects. The writing result that floored you sounds like it really captured what you were going for with the dragons and ley lines theme.

Tell me more about that frustrating part with the coloring page feedback. What was happening when the AI didn't understand what you were looking for?

User: The request is always for a black and white line image. The image I received had some partial shading in it. I requested the AI remove the shading but it just could not seem to do it. It seems that sometimes when an image is already created, the AI has difficulty editing the image. It might even elicit more errors outside of what you requested. Sometimes it's easier to just start over but that can be frustrating when you had a great image but then the AI makes a new creation that is missing parts you liked from the first one. Admittedly, I would probably be getting better results with AI that is more specialized in image creation and/or higher level paid subscriptions. I am still playing around with all different AIs for image creation.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - it sounds like there's this tension between getting something close to what you want but then struggling to refine those specific details, especially when it comes to editing existing images.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I feel like I am driving the creative decisions but I feel like AI provides excellent collaboration and can spark more creativity for me.

AI: That's a helpful distinction - you're in the driver's seat but AI is acting as that collaborative spark.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Oh! I feel like it opens me up to endless possibilities.

AI: Tell me more about that - what do you mean by endless possibilities?

User: For instance, instead of creating just a coloring page, I can expand to create a passage with the coloring page; the dragon invocation along with the dragon image. Prayers along with Christian images. Affirmations alongside meditative images. This leads me to brainstorming what else I could create along those lines. Also, I feel like I can create social media faster when I don't have to spend time struggling with getting the words out.

AI: That's really compelling - it sounds like AI is helping you create these richer, multi-layered experiences rather than just single pieces. And the efficiency gain with social media seems to free you up for other creative exploration.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I do have concerns about taking work away from artists. I could find an artist to hand draw the images I want but on a budget even a subscription for AI is more realistic.

AI: That's a thoughtful concern - the economic reality versus the impact on other artists. It sounds like you're aware of that tension between accessibility for your own creative work and the broader effects on the artistic community.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: As I continue to experiment with different platforms I hope to find one that works well for me for line images and edits. If I fine tune that then I will probably have more creations than I can keep up with! For creative writing, most AIs are great at it. I will absolutely continue to use AI to help me get the words out. I see AI's role being a permanent part of my creative future.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI helps bridge the gap between the creativity in your mind and getting it into tangible form, and how it's opened up those endless possibilities for creating richer, multi-layered projects.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope. Thank you.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative collaborator - from helping translate your mental visions into coloring pages to finding more eloquent ways to express your messages - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how honest you were about both the exciting possibilities and the practical challenges, like the frustrations with image editing and the concerns about impacts on other artists. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
40
creativity_0038Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.
