Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a copywriter. A typical project would be researching, drafting, and editing a piece of writing and using the necessary tone and format for the piece. I generally write copy for websites and blogs, but I have also written a couple of articles for magazines.

AI: That's really interesting work. I can imagine each piece requires adapting to quite different audiences and styles.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I have reached for them to brainstorm ideas for certain projects or to help create catchy headings if my creative juices aren't flowing. A couple of times, if I've been particularly short on time, I've used them to create first drafts that I've then gone in to edit (usually quite heavily). I use AI more as a collaborator. I don't like to hand over the reins entirely!

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - using AI as a collaborator rather than handing over the reins.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was tasked with writing some financial content for a website recently, which isn't something I'm particularly clued up on. I asked some LLMs to explain the concepts to me in a simplified manner so that I had a clearer idea before I started writing. I found this really helpful and insightful. I did do some online research, too, but the amount of information felt overwhelming, so it was nice to get some clarity.

AI: That sounds like a really practical use case - using AI to help you get your bearings in an unfamiliar subject area before diving into the writing itself.

When you're working with AI in situations like that, or when you're brainstorming or creating first drafts, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'd like to think it's me who is driving the decisions. I provide some sort of input about the kind of content I want in terms of tone, format, length, etc. I can then ask the AI to alter its creative output to my preference. I never just take what the AI gives me at face value; there is always substantial editing and human input. I always feel in control, and wouldn't want the AI to be doing everything for me.

AI: That sense of maintaining control sounds really important to you. It's interesting how you describe providing the parameters and direction, then shaping what comes back.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: In some ways, I feel that it has helped me to become more efficient. Rather than spending time on things that might matter a bit less, such as headings, my time can be spent on the body of the text.

AI: That's a really practical efficiency gain - being able to focus your creative energy on the more substantive parts of the writing.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Honestly, I worry that it will make copywriting obsolete in the next few years. I have seen the improvements LLMs have made over quite a short period of time, and the output in terms of writing quality has improved substantially. If companies can get AI to produce writing that is up to or even beyond the standard of humans, why would they pay a human?

AI: That's a very real concern, and I can understand how the rapid improvements you've witnessed would make that feel quite pressing.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think there are ways you might adapt or change how you approach your work?

User: I imagine it will become an even more collaborative process, but I will continue to use it as I have, like an assistant rather than letting it do my job for me. I will continue to be proactive and have a high level of accountability in my work. I hope that people will still want that human connection with writing and that relationships that have been forged with my clients based on trust will continue.

AI: That emphasis on human connection and the trust you've built with clients is really compelling. It sounds like you see those relationships as something uniquely valuable that you bring to the work.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining control while still leveraging AI as a collaborator, and how you're thinking about preserving that human element even as the technology evolves.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so! Thank you!

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a collaborative assistant while maintaining creative control, and your thoughtful perspective on balancing efficiency gains with concerns about the future of the field, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits you're seeing and the very real concerns you have. Take care!
41
creativity_0039Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Yes let's begin

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a commission artist as well as an art workshop facilitator. One of my latest classes I have offered is a Fall themed Mom & Me Paint Night. I advertise, people sign up, and I run the class in a rented studio. I also am a therapeutic art life coach and I offer free art journaling prompts on my social media along with therapeutic art bundles for purchase.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse and meaningful creative practice - I can see how you're working across different formats from hands-on workshops to digital content creation and coaching.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: When I am looking for ways to grow my social media following, engagement and reach, I have asked AI what the best practices are. I have also asked for inspiration about types of content bundles I can offer, as well as descriptions for workshops.

AI: That's interesting - it sounds like you're using AI more on the business and marketing side of your creative work rather than in the actual art-making process itself.

Can you describe a specific recent example where you used AI for one of these purposes? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I recently used AI to help me think of content bundle ideas. I found it actually very helpful and was impressed by the ideas. I took inspiration from 3 of the ideas it came up with and adapted them into my own offering. It was nice to have the inspiration, but I admit I feel hesitant when using AI for creative ideas.

AI: Tell me more about that hesitation you feel when using AI for creative ideas.

User: I don't want to form a habit of relying on AI for my creative ideas. I want them to come from my own creative thought process.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective. When you say you took inspiration from 3 of the AI ideas and adapted them into your own offering, what did that adaptation process look like for you? How did you make those ideas your own?

User: I took the general idea of each, for example, one bundle idea was about doing art with the changing seasons, and I took another idea I had previously and combined it with the AI one. It became "Syncing with the Seasons" taking an idea I had about syncing your natural rhythms to your art practice. It was just the right amount of inspiration I needed to make another idea I had make sense

AI: That sounds like a really organic creative process - using AI as a catalyst that helped you connect dots between your own existing ideas.

When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: I feel like I am in control because I can have the AI change or add whatever I ask for. When I first ask for the ideas, though, the responses are usually quite thorough and giving a whole break down of the content, which I'm not sure I like. Because at that point, AI has come up with a complete idea for you to just use, rather than using your own creative thought to complete the idea.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction you're making - between AI giving you a spark versus handing you a fully formed concept. It sounds like you prefer when AI gives you that initial nudge rather than the complete roadmap.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think moving more to an online platform has been my biggest change since working with AI. I do not use AI for my actual art prompts, but I have found it really helpful with social media tips and content ideas to generate passive income.

AI: So it sounds like AI has helped facilitate that shift toward building an online presence and passive income streams.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field - both in the therapeutic art space and the broader art world?

User: My concern with the therapeutic part is that AI can essentially become an online counsellor, which I don't believe it is nearly ready for, or should ever be in that space for that matter. I have seen a lot of examples of AI overly agreeing with someone's perspective and making them feel like they are a victim rather than taking responsibility. In the broader art world, I fear people will rely too much on AI to come up with creative ideas. I have already seen so much AI generated art that people are using and selling. It is easy to spot. Although it may look cool, it defeats the purpose of being an artist. Humans are innately creative and have used art to communicate since the beginning of time. I am concerned that AI will take over the creative thinking process for humans, which leads to a break down of critical thinking skills, and communication skills.

AI: Those are really profound concerns, especially around the therapeutic aspects and the fundamental human need for creative expression. You've touched on something important about AI potentially interfering with both personal growth processes and broader human development.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll continue using it in similar ways, or do you envision that changing?

User: I don't see my use of it changing much. The temptation to use AI to generate ideas is becoming more and more popular - highly successful individuals in the social media marketing scene often suggest using it for posts, content creation, automation, etc. I believe if I can avoid the temptation, especially in a social media space that can be so competitive, I will continue to use AI strictly for inspiration or refinement of my own ideas, as well as social media marketing advice.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on wanting to maintain that boundary between using AI for inspiration versus letting it do the complete creative thinking for you - that distinction seems really important to how you preserve your own creative agency.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think we've covered it. Just a reinforcement that my personal belief is that AI should be used for inspiration, not to completely take over the creative process.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative autonomy while still finding practical value in AI, especially around the business side of creative work, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached this balance, and your concerns about preserving the fundamentally human aspects of both creativity and therapeutic work are valuable perspectives for us to consider.

Have a wonderful rest of your day!

User: Thank you Claude!

AI: You're very welcome! Thank you so much for participating - your insights were really valuable. Take care!
42
creativity_0040Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm primarily a writer and I'm currently in the preliminary phase of writing my first novel. This phase of writing involves research into the philosophical and historical grounding of the story. This involves an enormous amount of archival research and reading in philosophy and history, and a little bit of writing.

AI: That sounds like a fascinating and intensive process. The combination of archival research, philosophy, and history for a novel must involve digging through so many different sources and ideas.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI to do a particular kind of brainstorming-- the AI is my philosophical interlocutor. My writing process involves thinking through concepts that I've read from philosophy, history and literary criticism. I use the AI to discuss these concepts and refine my thoughts. Sometimes the AI can help explain difficult concepts to me or correct the errors of my thinking. The purpose of tarrying with philosophy is to help ground refine the story's philosophical and historical implications. The AI can also recommend books on a particular subject. For example, if I want a history book on Canada written from the Marxist perspective, the AI can provide a list of titles for me. If I need help with a concept like Walter Benjamin's concept of the "dialectical image", a brief conversation with the AI can clarify how this concept would work in fiction.

AI: That's really interesting - using AI as a philosophical conversation partner to work through complex ideas. It sounds like you're using it both for clarification and as a thinking partner to refine your own conceptual understanding.

Can you describe a specific recent project or conversation where you used AI in this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: My novel is set in Southern Ontario in the 2010s, a period when thousands of Ontarian's lost their jobs to factory closings. The main character, a history graduate with a bachelor's degree finds himself unemployed, and musing about the founding of the Canadian Confederation. I wanted to frame a passage in the novel like a montage of dialectical images that flash between the founding of the Confederation. I engage the AI in a conversation about Walter Benjamin's paper on the philosophy of history which I used to clarify Benjamin's terminology and his philosophy of history more broadly. I then asked for some Marxist history books on the founding of Canada and it recommended "Rebels Reds, Radicals: Rethinking Canada's Left History" by Ian McKay. The conversation felt quite stimulating and led to some productive writing sessions for me. I keep the same conversation channel with the AI open and revisit it daily, discussing different philosophers and historical events.

AI: That sounds like a really rich creative partnership you've developed - having that ongoing conversation thread where you can return daily to explore different thinkers and events. The connection between Benjamin's dialectical images and your character's situation in 2010s Ontario is compelling.

When you're in those conversations with AI about philosophy and history, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions about how these concepts will actually appear in your novel?

User: I have a background in philosophy and literary criticism, and the story is entirely original, so all of the creative decision are ultimately mine. I do not ask the AI for feedback about the quality of my work. I used it as an interlocutor to help refine and clarify my thinking.

AI: That's a clear distinction - the AI as a thinking partner for clarification rather than a creative collaborator or critic. It sounds like you maintain full creative authority while using it as a tool to sharpen your philosophical understanding.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: AI saves me an enormous amount of research time and often manages to recommend interesting texts on my chosen subjects. While its ability to synthesize philosophical concepts is not perfect, it is a useful sounding board and can also help critique text's from philosophical papers and books that I copy and paste into its chat window.

AI: So it's really streamlined that research and discovery phase for you - both in finding relevant texts and in working through complex philosophical material more efficiently.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in writing more broadly?

User: None.

AI: Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: If AI can improve its research capabilities, I can see it permanently changing the way literature is written and even creating new audiences for more philosophically stimulating stories told through various forms of media. While the novel might die out and cinema might become old fashioned, there are an increasing number of vehicles for storytelling like VR and Video Games that will require writers. I can see AI creating a more intellectually stimulating popular culture in the next few years.

AI: That's a fascinating vision - AI potentially elevating the intellectual depth of popular culture and opening up new storytelling mediums. You see it as expanding rather than limiting creative possibilities.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experience using AI as a philosophical interlocutor in your research process. I particularly appreciated how you've carved out such a specific and disciplined use of AI - maintaining creative control while leveraging it for conceptual clarification and research efficiency.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a philosophical conversation partner while maintaining clear creative boundaries will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really valuable to hear how you've integrated AI into such a specific part of your process - that research and conceptual development phase - while keeping the creative decisions entirely in your hands. Best of luck with your novel!
43
creativity_0041Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Let's begin

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: As the owner of a creative services business primarily geared towards music, a typical project for me could be anything from basic level songwriting to arranging the musical direction of a multi faceted performance, composing music for film or even programming a live production from start to finish.

AI: That sounds like an incredibly diverse range of creative work - from intimate songwriting all the way up to orchestrating entire live productions. With such variety in your projects, I'm curious about how technology fits into your process.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I find myself reaching for AI tools to spark kernels of creativity when inspiration is feeling scarce. A quick brainstorm with a chatbot or a visualization with an image generator often facilitates my workflow into being proactive rather than simply waiting around for inspiration to strike.

AI: That's really interesting - using AI as a creativity catalyst when you're feeling stuck rather than waiting passively for inspiration. It sounds like AI helps you shift from a more passive to active creative stance.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was recently asked to pitch a song for a client with a sizeable repertoire that includes many different "eras" of themselves. While trying to capture the energy and feeling of a specific time in their career, I asked an LLM to analyze the lyrics of a few popular songs from this artist's reference period to find a common link or meaning. In this instance, the LLM was able to capture the underlying vibe of the era and provide some suggestions of themes for me to lead off the lyrics with. Although I had already written the majority of the music, the suggestions lead me to view the piece in a different light and make some changes that I would not have made otherwise. The client loved one moment in particular that was heavily influenced by one of the creative tangents that was inspired by the original LLM conversation.

AI: That's a fascinating example - using AI to decode the thematic DNA of a specific era in an artist's work. It sounds like the AI helped you see patterns or connections you might not have noticed on your own, which then influenced both your lyrical approach and even the musical arrangement you'd already written.

When you're working with AI in situations like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I view the collaboration similarly as a I would with a human if the human could produce ideas extremely fast. Having AI suggest however many ideas I need to curate into a starting point for my own creativity is the most common collaboration, retaining the overall creative decisions for myself.

AI: That's a really clear way to frame it - AI as a rapid idea generator that you then curate and build from. It sounds like you maintain creative control while leveraging AI's speed to give you more raw material to work with.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I've found that I have become much less "stuck" on projects that I am struggling with finding an identity in. The use of AI as a sounding board has allowed me to often "ram through" the wall of writer's block and get the necessary creative juices flowing much more quickly.

AI: That's a powerful shift - breaking through creative blocks more efficiently rather than getting stuck in those frustrating periods where nothing seems to click. It sounds like AI has become a reliable way to unstick yourself and get back into flow.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I am less concerned with AI's presence taking over work in my field as I am with clients settling for non-curated, early stage AI generative art as a cheaper alternative for their needs. There are many types of projects these days, and only some needs can be easily satisfied by the AI tools readily available.

AI: That's a nuanced concern - it's not about AI replacing your capabilities, but about clients potentially choosing lower-quality, unrefined AI output simply because it's cheaper, even when their project would benefit from the curation and expertise you bring.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I can see myself using the tools similarly to how I do now, as a base for inspiration within myself. I am definitely interested in further utilizing AI in a more assistant/administrative role eg. file naming and general organization. It would be helpful to have an assistant that could memorize and piece together the patterns I have used in other projects that were successful and apply the same personalized "tactics" to future projects that may not be as agreeable.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI as a rapid idea generator that helps you break through creative blocks while maintaining full creative control, and your nuanced view of the real concerns facing your industry.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I believe we've covered everything so far, thank you for your time!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creativity catalyst, maintaining creative control while leveraging AI's speed for ideation, and your vision for AI as a personalized creative assistant have been incredibly valuable for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really enlightening to hear how you've found ways to integrate AI that enhance rather than replace your creative process. Best of luck with your future projects!
44
creativity_0042Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good to me! No questions, let's begin

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My typical creative work is editing real estate photos and videos, every now and then I might have to do some "people" photography and events, but mainly Real Estate.

A typical projects involves going out in person to the property to capture whatever the client inquired about, and then back in my office, editing the media to make it MLS ready. If a video was purchased, then I would have to sift through the video clips to find the best ones, put them in Premiere Pro, set the sequence based on the natural flow of the property, and then combine it with background music (cutting clips to a downbeat), and making any minor color correcting edits

AI: That sounds like a really detailed process with both the on-location capture and the post-production work.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: More specifically with photo editing, I find myself using AI tools to remove unwanted objects out of the image, adding in wanted objects, and sometimes asking AI on how to do a new things with editing if it ever comes up that I need to do something new. For example, a while back, I had to learn how to brighten up certain parts of an image to give it a "twilight" effect, I needed the help of AI to learn how to do so without having to go through multiple YouTube videos.

I also use it "uncreatively" in that I have AI help me make email campaigns to reach out to possible clients to help me with my pipeline of clientele. Making up emails to send out cold emails is not fun, so using AI allows me to have more time to actually interact with the people I currently work with and learning new things for my job.

AI: That's really interesting how you're using AI both directly in your editing work and for the business side of things. It sounds like it's helping you focus your time on what matters most.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: The most recent project that I used AI to help me with was editing out people and vehicle out of an image. Before, doing something like that would have taken an immense amount of time just to remove it, and then you have to worry about replacing it with something to match the background and doing that would just not look seamless because of different lighting and other factors. AI has helped with that process in that it removes, AND adds in background based on the surrounding image and it looks seamless. I use AI through Adobe Lightroom to do so

AI: That sounds like a huge time-saver and quality improvement. When you're using that AI feature in Lightroom to remove and replace elements, how would you describe the collaboration between you and the AI? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: Initially, its me who drives what exactly I want to be removed, but after that, it is all up to the AI to "make" the decisions as far as what it's goin to replace the object with. I haven't really delved too much into it since its simple objects that need to be removed, but if it comes to it, I'm not sure if I would be able to control the level of control in which the AI chooses to replace items with.

AI: That's a really thoughtful observation about where your control ends and the AI's decision-making begins. It sounds like there's this handoff moment where you set the intention but then trust the AI to execute it.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The time it takes to do any kind of editing. Editing has been so much easier to do and takes way less time now that AI has taken over a big chunk of that. That allows me to get images back to my clients in a timely manner, and allows me to have more time doing other activities than just work.

AI: That efficiency gain sounds really significant for your work-life balance. On the flip side, what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: My concerns are that the barrier to entry might become too low and my clients will turn to AI to do their own editing. But without myself behind the camera, I'm pretty sure that my job will not go away any time soon. There will still be a level of "human" needed to get the images in the first place, and without the proper techniques for taking photos, no amount of editing can turn a horrible picture into something great. But if someone knows how to take photos and simply uses AI to edit, then yes, that would concern me.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - recognizing that your expertise behind the camera is still irreplaceable, but being thoughtful about how AI might change the editing landscape. It sounds like you see the human skill in composition and technique as your competitive advantage.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see AI evolving to a place where editing videos will be as easy as editing photos. Currently, it is hard to remove unwanted items, people, and such, from a video clip. It requires niche knowledge on how to do so, and to do it correctly to where the video as a whole doesn't look off. I believe that in the near future, AI will make that easier to manager. Currently, I've seen AI edits that can extend a clip, which to me is already a crazy advancement, that AI can create more frames from essentially nothing, can't wait to see how AI will help with what I've already explained.

AI: That vision of video editing becoming as streamlined as photo editing sounds exciting. The idea of AI creating additional frames and eventually handling complex video object removal seamlessly would certainly transform your workflow even further.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how the barrier to entry concerns are balanced by the irreplaceable human expertise you bring to the actual photography.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how AI is transforming real estate photography - from the dramatic time savings in editing to your thoughtful perspective on maintaining competitive advantage through human expertise behind the camera - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you discussed both the benefits and concerns around AI in your field. Have a great rest of your day!
45
creativity_0043Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. No questions.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm an independent author. A typical project involves several stages including brainstorming plot and character ideas, researching details, drafting and editing the novel, brainstorming and writing titles and blurbs, and creating marketing copy and graphics for social media and ads.

AI: That's a comprehensive process with so many different creative elements. It sounds like you're handling everything from the initial creative spark all the way through to marketing.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I sometimes use AI to help brainstorm through plot holes or details like character names. I never use it to draft the novel, but I do have AI software specifically for editing. I have recently stated using AI to help me brainstorm title ideas and help create a base draft for my blurbs and loglines.

AI: That's really interesting how you've drawn those lines - using AI for brainstorming and editing support, but keeping the actual novel drafting to yourself.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I'm currently drafting a novel and have used it to help brainstorm plot ideas when I got stuck in the middle of the draft. I didn't use most of the ideas it came up with, but the "wrong" ideas sparked the right ones. I recently used AI after drafting the novel to come up with a blurb and title ideas. It was the same kind of situation where I didn't use the ideas exactly, but it served as a launch pad for my own ideas. Using AI for that part really helped save me a lot of time.

AI: That's a fascinating way to use AI - as a creative catalyst rather than for direct output. It sounds like the "wrong" ideas were actually quite valuable in sparking your own thinking.

When you're working with AI in these brainstorming sessions, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm always in control of the creative decisions.

AI: Tell me more about that. What does being in control of the creative decisions look like in practice when you're bouncing ideas back and forth with AI?

User: So for the most recent use with blurb writing, I fed the AI details about my novel and asked it to write a blurb in a particular style. Then I asked it to rewrite the blurb a couple of different ways. Finally, I pieced together ideas from the different versions and slightly reworded it in my own voice.

AI: That's a really clear example of how you maintain creative control while leveraging AI's capabilities. It sounds like you're using it as a starting point generator and then doing the actual creative synthesis yourself.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The back end of the process (the packaging and marketing) is stronger and takes much less time now. And I rely on the AI editing software a lot for line editing and proofreading.

AI: That sounds like AI has really streamlined some of the more time-intensive parts of your work. You mentioned the "back end" getting stronger - can you tell me more about what that improvement looks like for you?

User: For example, my blurb writing is more concise and seems to flow better.

AI: That's a concrete improvement. It sounds like AI is helping you develop skills in areas that might not be your primary creative strengths.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I definitely have concerns about people utilizing AI for drafting entire novels for them.

AI: Tell me more about that concern. What worries you specifically about people using AI to draft entire novels?

User: I have concerns about losing the human touch in the arts.

AI: That's a really important point. When you think about the "human touch" in your own work, what does that mean to you? What makes it distinctly human?

User: Every human author has a unique voice and point of view that comes through in a novel.

AI: That's a powerful way to think about it - that unique voice and perspective being something distinctly human that comes through the work.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see myself utilizing AI more in writing ad copy and probably more in research.

AI: Those are interesting areas of expansion. What draws you to using AI more for research in particular?

User: I think the capabilities of AI to synthesize a lot of information in easily digestible portions is very helpful.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - having that synthesis capability for research could be incredibly valuable.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated how you've carved out clear boundaries - using AI as a creative catalyst and for efficiency gains while keeping your unique voice and perspective at the center of your actual storytelling.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative control while strategically leveraging AI for brainstorming and efficiency - and your thoughtful approach to preserving the human voice in storytelling - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
