creativity_0044Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good to me. Have at it!

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work as a freelance copywriter and sometimes editor on Fiverr. Different clients have very different projects, but typically I'm writing blog posts and website content for businesses. Other copywriting I do includes neighborhood newsletters and editing content for websites. Most of my work involves some level of SEO as well.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of writing work. I'm curious about how AI fits into all of this.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Typically, I'll take the client's brief and generate one or both of a detailed outline for the project and/or ask for well-researched citations into interesting facts and statistics about the topic.

AI: That's interesting - so you're using AI more in the research and planning phases. Tell me more about that process. When you say you ask for "well-researched citations" - what does that interaction with AI look like for you?

User: It depends on the level of research needed for the project. On the 'low' end, I'll just ask a search-enabled smart AI (like GPT-5 thinking or Opus 4.1 or Gemini 2.5 Pro) to provide me with some interesting facts and statistics from specifically .edu, .gov, or .org sources (or pdfs) to avoid linking competitive articles. If its a big project I need to know lot of new information for, I like to use deep research - typically OpenAI or Grok's, but also Gemini or Perplexity if I'm not finding what I want. These reports I tend to read more or less fully (at least the interesting parts) and sometimes read the sources too. I then tend to copy-paste a bunch of the more interesting claims into a detailed outline and ask another (usually different AI) to fact check everything I might use for the article.

AI: That's a really thorough research process - it sounds like you've developed quite a sophisticated workflow with multiple AI tools and verification steps.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as the writer working on it?

User: One larger project I had was actually writing a report on Solar Energy expansion in the Middle East for a Qatar Solar Company. For this, not only did I use multiple deep research reports, but I also used ChatGPT's ability to make charts to help visualize the data. I did find that o3 (the model I was working with at the time) still struggled with extracting data from a visual chart - like if I uploaded a graph or chart to it, it couldn't read it accurately. This meant I still had to spend a lot of time verifying that it had the correct data for the many charts in the report. In terms of writing help, I tend to prefer 3rd party editing tools (like Grammarly) to strictly asking an AI to help with editing, as it does tend to go overboard.

AI: That Qatar solar project sounds quite complex - you're essentially orchestrating multiple AI tools for different parts of the process. When you were working through that project, how would you describe the collaboration between you and the AI? Who or what was driving the creative decisions about how to present the information and structure the report?

User: In that case, it was very much a research and outlining partner - a tool for the most part. I didn't push it to help creatively too much, but the outline structure was developed with it. I would also say it helped 'creatively' in the sense of which facts and statistics it searched for and found. Those definitely guided my approach. Sometimes I will ask the AI whether it thinks the flow, argument, and general readability of an article I've written is good. I take its opinion with a grain of salt, but it can be helpful.

AI: That's a really clear distinction - AI as a research and outlining partner rather than a creative collaborator. You mentioned taking its opinion "with a grain of salt" when it comes to flow and readability feedback.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: Overall, the time to write an article I would say is 25 to 50% faster, but actually, the main benefit is cognitive flow. If I'm stuck on a part (outlining, researching, writing a section) I can always just ask the AI to give it a try. Sometimes the result is beneficial, sometimes not, but it helps keep things moving if I'm 'dreading the blank page,' so to speak. By 'grain of salt' I mean that often when I ask an AI for creative feedback, it is too zealous with what should be changed, giving feedback that is both vague and broad, but sharp as though 'it has to be changed.' Sometimes I do agree, but I have to remember that it is often pushing things toward a more 'generic' template, so it's important to keep my voice in the writing as well. Oh! I forgot to mention one great feature for my work is 'projects' in OpenAI, as I've carefully developed a custom instructions template and fed the project multiple articles of mine to help guide the style of writing to match my own. It's not fully successful, but it does help for when I do want it to have a go at a rough draft. Claude's 'styles' is similar, but I've been using projects for longer. And of course, I use artefacts and OpenAI's canvas too, although both are often much jankier than I'd like so I will often swap the material in and out of it.

AI: That's fascinating - the idea of cognitive flow and avoiding "dreading the blank page" seems like a really significant impact beyond just time savings. And I love how you've experimented with training the AI on your own writing style through projects and custom instructions.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of copywriting and content creation?

User: Like many, I do believe it does 'deaden' overall voice and feeling of writing throughout the web. However, a lot of website content and blog posts of businesses that I'm doing at the 'low end' doesn't need a lot of that. I already see that AI is taking over these freelance positions, but I'm personally ok with that. I'd like AI to take over a lot of work, although perhaps creative work a little less so. I do think AI can be used as a tool to improve creative works though - I've already seen amazing images and video made from AI. I'm not sure I've seen as beautiful writing, but for those who want to roleplay or for things like video game dialogue, the creative writing of AI is already solid enough to have a lot of fun with.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - accepting that AI might replace certain types of work while seeing potential for it to enhance creativity in other ways. You seem pretty pragmatic about the changes happening in your field.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think for freelance copywriting in particular, it will be the go-to for the vast majority, with only a sliver still left for certain special human voices. More and more people are already turning to video and more multimedia approaches to putting out information on social media and the like. These too, of course, are being affected by AI. Currently, I'm in interviews for AI-first companies looking to leverage AI for content creation, such as making knowledge bases for company chatbots.

AI: It sounds like you're actively adapting and moving toward more AI-integrated roles rather than trying to resist the changes. That transition to working for AI-first companies is really interesting.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a tool for maintaining cognitive flow and how you've developed such a systematic approach to research verification across multiple AI platforms.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Just my two cents, but I think personality, style, and distinction in the creative writing process is necessary to level up AI creative writing. When people just type 'write me an email...' etc. it gives a generic voice. While styles in Claude are a good start, more obvious personalities and 'human-like' styles are probably what will be most useful in the future for creating compelling content with a distinct literary voice. We see see strongly in the image generation sphere, where loras and prompting particular styles, camera angles, painting styles, etc. all come out to influence a particular user's style of generations strongly - so much so that I can recognize some users' generations immediately, their way of prompting is so unique to them (not just ghibli-style or some generic one like that).

AI: That's a really insightful observation about the parallel between image generation and writing - how users develop their own distinctive prompting styles that become almost like a signature. The idea that personality and distinction are what's missing from current AI writing tools is particularly interesting, especially coming from someone who works professionally with these systems every day.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the practical realities of using AI in creative work, from the cognitive flow benefits to the systematic research workflows you've developed, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how candid you were about both the opportunities and the changes happening in your field.
47
creativity_0045Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am writer, predominantly of literary fiction. I have written one novel and am in the process of researching another. I like complex books which usually have historical and/or philosophical elements.

AI: That sounds fascinating - literary fiction with historical and philosophical dimensions definitely requires a lot of depth and research.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: With my first novel I spent nearly a year doing preparatory research. At that time I was studying for a PhD in History and was able to freely access online journals and research portals via the university. I'm no longer a student, so I don't have access to those resources now as many are only available to institutions. I have used AI to help with researching the second novel as it is set in Canada at the start of the second World War. I find using AI very efficient, although I am conscious of the fact that it isn't always truthful.

AI: That's a really interesting shift - from having institutional access to academic resources to relying more on AI for research. Tell me more about how you're using AI specifically for your World War II Canada research. What kinds of questions are you asking it, and how are you working around that truthfulness concern you mentioned?

User: To work around the truthful issue, I double check anything I'm doubtful about. It's time consuming, but not as time consuming as doing the research myself. I am using my grandmother's letters to my grandfather as the basis of the novel. She left the UK and took her two children to Canada at the onset of the war and stayed until the end of it. I have never visited the particular area before and can see on Google Maps how much it has changed in the intervening years. I have used AI to help me build a clearer picture of life in that area in the 1940s, I have also asked it to elaborate on details my grandmother touches on in her letters about the war and also I have asked questions about a white person such as myself writing a book that features First Nation mythology, as I am concerned about getting the approach wrong.

AI: That's such a rich and personal foundation for a novel - using your grandmother's actual letters. It sounds like AI is helping you fill in the contextual gaps around her experiences.

Can you describe a specific recent interaction you had with AI while working on this project? Maybe a particular question you asked about 1940s life in that area, or about approaching First Nation mythology respectfully - and how that conversation with the AI unfolded?

User: I wanted to open the novel with a myth about 'Raven', who was supposedly born on the shores of the area where my novel is set. I asked the model if this would be problematic, but I remember thinking that its answer was too woolly to be helpful. It hedged its bets! I decided in the end not to use it.

AI: That's such a telling example - the AI giving you a hedged, non-committal response when you needed clear guidance on something as sensitive as cultural representation. It sounds like you were looking for more definitive direction but had to make that important creative decision on your own.

When you're working with AI on your research, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: I am driving the creative decisions, but that's because I ask the questions in isolation. For instance, I wanted more information about the experiences of Canadian couples who took in British children during the war. For me to research that would be time consuming, not least because Google is not an efficient search tool. The AI was useful in giving me starting blocks for further research rather than fully fulfilling my needs.

AI: That's a really clear distinction - using AI as starting blocks rather than endpoints. It sounds like you maintain pretty firm control over the creative direction while leveraging AI's efficiency for initial research pathways.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Are there things you approach differently now compared to when you were doing that year of preparatory research for your first novel?

User: Yes. After the work involved in the first novel (I was paranoid about getting historical facts wrong) I decided never to write another book with a historical storyline. Since the advent of AI, writing such a book seems less daunting, not just because it can help with the foundational research but because I suppose you could use it to fact-check aspects of your writing as well. I am currently a bit hesitant about using AI as a beta reader or editorial checker, but I am tempted.

AI: That's fascinating - AI actually changed your mind about writing historical fiction entirely. You went from "never again" to seeing it as less daunting.

Tell me more about that temptation to use AI as a beta reader or editorial checker. What's making you hesitant, and what's drawing you toward trying it?

User: I'm drawn towards it because it would be quick and easy. It would also be cheaper than using a human editor. My reservations stem from two things: firstly, fears around intellectual property/copyright. I don't feel comfortable with 'giving' an AI my novel. Secondly, I'm doubtful about its ability to truly understand the nuances in literary fiction. I suspect it won't 'get' my book!

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns - the intellectual property worry and the question of whether AI can truly grasp the subtleties of what you're creating. That phrase "I suspect it won't 'get' my book" really captures something important about the artistic relationship.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's broader presence in your field - in literary fiction and writing more generally?

User: Like most writers, I'm concerned about the idea of AI-generated novels. The market is already flooded with formulaic fiction; I don't believe we need more, we need less. I feel that the quality of the novel is being degraded, and the years of the 'great writers' are all behind us now. Writers used to be intellectuals, they aren't any more. I also love writing and can't understand why anyone would use an AI to write for them - other than to churn out generic books and make money.

AI: Your passion for the craft really comes through - the idea that writing itself is something to be cherished, not automated away. That concern about AI potentially accelerating the production of formulaic content while the quality of literary work feels like it's already declining.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think your boundaries around what you're comfortable using it for might shift?

User: I suspect they might. I suspect I may be more open to using an AI for editing and proofreading. The more normalised this becomes, the more likely I am to use it I suppose. I certainly don't ever see myself using it to produce original writing, because as above, I love the act and the art of writing. Also, I don't believe an AI will ever be able to write more eloquently than me!

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated how you've articulated that distinction between using AI as research "starting blocks" versus creative endpoints, and your clear boundary around the actual craft of writing being something you want to preserve for yourself.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: no

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative agency while selectively leveraging AI's research capabilities, and your perspective on preserving the integrity of literary craft, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you discussed both the practical benefits you've found and your deeper concerns about AI's impact on literary culture. Best of luck with your novel about your grandmother's wartime experience - it sounds like a truly meaningful project.
48
creativity_0046Assistant: Hi there, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, I don't think I have any questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: One of the projects that I work on the most is basically the creative part of the process for selecting and preparing an episode for my history podcast, which falls more specifically into the realm of ASMR History for sleep. I choose a story or two related stories form either a specific event in history or a couple of related history topics and then tell a story for about 45 minutes or so. I use AI to help me brainstorm ideas for my next "episode," help me complete outlines for the research, find sources for my actual research, and write a script that I will then use to record the episode. For instnace, I was having trouble picking a topic a few weeks ago and with the help of a model that asked several questions, we settled on the story of the "Fall of Constantinople" and then created an outline. The episode should be out in 2 weeks, so I am working on research now and will return with a tentative script that a model will help me "clean up," or edit for recording.

AI: That's fascinating - I love how you've found such a specific niche with ASMR history for sleep. It sounds like AI is woven throughout multiple stages of your creative process.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: At the beginning of the process, not every time, but often, for brainstorming the topic. Then a quick, rough "outline" to help kickstart the research. Then I return just before recording usually, or if I'm having trouble with sources I might ask for help in the middle of research. But then I return to see if there is any advice the model might give me on "cleaning up" the script to make it flow more like a seamless story.

AI: That's really helpful - it sounds like you're using AI as both a creative partner and an editor at different points. You mentioned that you don't always use AI for brainstorming topics, just sometimes.

Tell me more about that. What determines when you reach for AI versus when you come up with topics on your own?

User: It really comes down to how creative I am feeling in the moment, or sometimes how my week has gone. As a person attending grad school for history, sometimes there is a topic brought up in my classes that really captures my attention and I devote an episode to that. Sometimes I have "writer's block" and need a little help, which is when I turn to the model for inspiration.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - using AI as a creative boost when you need it rather than a constant crutch. I'm curious about that collaboration when you do have writer's block.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, to go back to the "Fall of Constantinople" project, it was during the summer. I was not in class, I had not done much reading that week, and felt a little burnt out. I had a conversation with an AI model that I had used enough in the past that it knew which eras of history I generally showed more interest in, and asked for a brainstorming session. The model did a decent job of shouldering some of the burden of the conversation and eventually suggested a story on Mehmet I, who was the ruler that overthrew Constantinople in 1453, which sparked my interest to do that story on that. There are plenty of secondary sources on that topic, so I did not really need help with the research, but my notes were actually a little too detailed and the story was going to be very long. So I returned and asked the model to trim approximately 10 minutes worth of time. It did fairly well at keeping the key points in and removing some extraneous chatter. It helped ease some of the stress I was feeling to produce an episode as well as remove points, which I loathe, because I've usually worked pretty hard to come up with the script. So it helps me out to have AI remove some content for me, because it can be difficult for me to choose.

AI: That's such a vivid example - I can really hear how AI helped you navigate both the creative block and the editorial challenge of cutting content you'd worked hard on. It sounds like there was real emotional relief there.

When you're working with AI like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It depends. Like I said in the previous example, there are times when I am feeling very overwhelmed and can't really shoulder the weight of being creative and I might require a bit more input in those times. Other times, I just ask the model what it thinks of MY ideas, and I usually get pretty good feedback, although sometimes I have to remind the model that it is okay to give a critique that is not fully positive, or gives more tips. So it goes back and forth depending on my mood.

AI: That's a really insightful observation about having to encourage the AI to give you honest critique rather than just positive feedback. It sounds like you've developed quite a nuanced working relationship where you're actively managing the dynamic based on what you need.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Surprisingly, I think my own research skills have actually improved (although I've always loved research and done well at it). This is because I remember not to just take the model at its word 100% of the time, as AI models make mistakes just like humans, and so double checking some of the information interjected by a model helps me to deepen my own research abilities. I also think that using AI in the last editing stage helps me deliver a more polished and smooth podcast overall when it is published.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like working with AI has actually sharpened your critical thinking and made you a more careful researcher. I wouldn't have expected that outcome.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: The injection of AI voices and completely AI written scripts have garnered a small following, but as most people prefer human voices, right now this is not a top concern, but it is still in the back of my mind. There are some people who use completely AI written scripts as well, but for now, these are usually pretty easy to pick up on. That being said, as the tech improves, so will the scripts and voices. I am not a person who thinks that this is the end of the world, however. Because it is just an opportunity for me to pivot myself and potentially launch the podcast in a different direction myself using the technology, so while there are some concerns, it all comes down to an ability to adapt.

AI: That's a really thoughtful and adaptive perspective - seeing it more as an evolution to navigate rather than a threat to resist. Your attitude about pivoting and using the technology yourself is quite pragmatic.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I would anticipate that audio-processing AI will become more advanced, which would be a benefit for myself. Listening back to recorded episodes, editing and basically "splicing" in edited sections is a difficult and time consuiming part of my work, even though there are some tools that exist now to assist with that. But as the tech gets more advanced, it would be nice to pass that job off to an AI completely, as it is my least favorite part of the whole process.

AI: That makes perfect sense - automating the tedious technical work so you can focus on the creative storytelling aspects you clearly love.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has actually improved your research skills by making you more vigilant about fact-checking, and your adaptive approach to viewing AI developments as opportunities rather than just threats.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a flexible creative partner - sometimes for brainstorming when you're stuck, sometimes for editing help, and how it's actually strengthened your research skills - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how honest you were about the different ways you engage with AI depending on your energy and needs. That nuanced approach to the collaboration is exactly the kind of authentic experience we're hoping to understand better.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your Fall of Constantinople episode!
49
creativity_0047Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, you can proceed with the questions.

AI: Great, thank you! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Sure! I'm a writer and my preferred genre is literary fiction. I write novels, novellas, short stories, and poetry. I'm a hybrid sort of writer, I plan things ahead, visualise scenes in my head before putting them to paper. It helps be get the work done faster and boots my confidence. I always want to have the emotional beats down and then I always leave room for updates as I go. So it's mixture of planning and discovery writing.

AI: That's a fascinating approach - having that blend of planning and discovery. The visualization aspect sounds particularly interesting.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Mainly during the brainstorming stage. My pride as a writer and my personal ethics don't allow me to use AI to create content instead of my own brain and imagination, but I do use it as a feedback tool and a beta reader. For instance, I test the sound of character names, I ask for information on etymology, what would a good name that means XYZ would be, how can I use a substitute for a name that exists in real life, e.g., a country by alluding to its cultural specifics. Basically, it acts as a search engine/feedback tool.

AI: That's really thoughtful - using AI as more of a research assistant and sounding board rather than for content generation. You mentioned testing character names and getting feedback like a beta reader.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was working on an entirely new project, which occured to me, making my own manga plot. I had. a long chat with an AI assistant trying out various character names, which I wanted to connect to particular character concepts, e.g., alluding to darkness, vengenance, light, hope, etc. I repeatedly asked it to summarise the plot thus far as it was evolving, which made it easier to keep track of my thoughts.

AI: That sounds like a really creative way to use AI as a thinking partner - helping you track your evolving ideas and test out how the names resonated with your character concepts.

When you're working with AI in those sessions, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It's always me. I'm always mindful of being in control and the who ultimately makes the decisions as an author. I weigh every suggestion the AI makes, sometimes complement it with additional research via a search engine and reflect on it a lot. If it sounds intuitively right for the character, I take the suggestion. AI is a great organising tool, it helps fill gaps one might not immediately think of, though it's more technical than truly imaginative in a way a human brain would be.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction - AI being more technical versus truly imaginative in the human sense. It sounds like you've found a clear balance where you maintain creative ownership while leveraging AI's organizational strengths.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I'm trying to use AI's presumed impartiality as an inhuman tool to assess any potential weaknesses in my writing, hoping it would be objective enough. It's useful as food for thought as a creator's brain and the inherent bias related to an idea you love might make you feel too close to your own creation, and thus blind to what might seem obvious to readers. So, it can act as a helpful impartial gap identifier and an organised assistant. I guess it's helped me distance myself from my emotional bond to the work as it encourages additional reflection, to look at it from the point of view of a reader and think more about what market niche it might fill. AI can help an author think more about the business side of writing, what to prepare for and how it can impact your view on writing in general.

AI: That's really valuable - using AI's detachment to help you step back from that emotional closeness to your work and see it more objectively. The market perspective aspect is particularly interesting.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I think it can be really useful as an organsational tool, the kind of personal assistant a writer may need. But I'm personally very much against it being used as a creative subsitute to do what a writer is supposed to do, tell a story and do the actual writing work. The uniqueness of human creativity, flawed and unpredictable as it is, must stay intact.

AI: That's a powerful perspective - preserving what makes human creativity unique and irreplaceable while still finding value in AI as a support tool.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think it can simplify the aspects of the work that may seem tedious or boring, and take away the creative spark. Stuff like organising outlines, grammar editing, research. Of course, one should always back up AI research with additional insights, including from experts in fields, but it still saves time and effort. It can be a beneficial helper, which promotes critical thinking and gives an author a more holistic view of what it means to be an author in this day and age. It makes one more aware of everything external outside of pure creaitivty and passion.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI's "presumed impartiality" to help you see potential blind spots in your work, and how that's helped you think more holistically about both the creative and business sides of being an author.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think I've covered all insights I wanted to share.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative ownership while leveraging AI as an organizational tool and impartial feedback source will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated how clearly you articulated that balance between embracing AI's practical benefits while preserving what makes human creativity irreplaceable. That perspective is invaluable as we work to understand how AI can best support rather than replace creative professionals.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your manga project and all your future writing!
50
creativity_0048Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sure. No questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am writing a novel and I've also written smaller novels and non-fiction books and training materials. But I probably use it most frequently for my novel lately.

AI: That's fascinating - working on a novel is such an involved creative process.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process when you're working on your novel. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I've used it to begin research into things I have limited experience with. For example, I have characters who are FBI agents and ones who work in the funeral business, so I've done a lot of asking questions about what kinds of things would be realistic in those settings and those roles. For example, I'll write a scene and then put it into an LLM and ask if it seems plausible, or if I'm being too Hollywood-educated about it, and what the terms for certain things are. Another thing I use it for is looking for terms for things it would take longer for me to look up. Like when one of my characters decides he doesn't want the monuments business his father wills him, I had to look up the legal papers that would be used for that process. (A disclaimer, apparently.)
Also, I've used it to put in segments of my book and ask what other parts of the scene i may have overlooked, or trivia that help with the verisimilitude of the story.
On smaller projects, I've also given the model a general idea of the setting, character, tone of the story, certain happenings that had to happen, and then I ask it to write what basically amounts to a trellis that I can build the actual story around. It helps me with pacing and not getting bogged down in the details of the first chapter instead of moving all the way though the plot.
I probably use it for more, but that's what is coming to mind right now.
Oh, and character names or business names sometimes - though I often end up getting inspiration and not final options from the model.

AI: That's a really rich picture of how you're using AI across different aspects of your writing. I'm particularly interested in that research and fact-checking role you described.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, one of the things I used it for was that I have a character whose parents perished using medical euthanasia. I had to ask where they could go to have it done, how their bodies would have been returned to the states (since I sent them to Switzerland) and what diseases they would both have had to justify wanting to pass in a dignified way instead of waiting it out. (He had advanced ALS and she had Pancreatic Cancer, and neither wanted to become a long-term burden to their children. Being as they worked in the funeral industry, they appreciated the sense of being in control of their final moments and not putting their family through the chaos that so many other families went through when death came on its own.) I had to make sure they were diseases that would reasonably have them of sound mind when they made the choice to depart, and that I handled it with some sensitivity because it can be a tender subject for a lot of people.
It felt very helpful to be able to have a relevant conversation about what was going on with my characters and that guide the information I was being provided instead of having to do research for each keyword piece by piece.

AI: That sounds like such a delicate and important part of your story to get right. It's interesting how you were able to have that kind of nuanced conversation with AI about the medical and logistical details while also considering the emotional sensitivity.

When you're working with AI like that - having those research conversations or asking it to check your scenes for plausibility - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: That's all me. But I got my degree in creative writing, so I'm familiar with workshopping and getting ideas that either do or don't work for my story and not feeling like everything that gets suggested has to go into the story. The models often ask to write segments for me, but I prefer to do the writing part myself.
The models provide ideas and I get to select from those ideas as I see fit.
I have done some experiments where I gave the model a plot, characters, a few key actions, etc, and asked it to write a chapter, and while that can have a pleasing outcome, it doesn't feel fully mine because the words are my art form.

AI: That's such an insightful distinction - the words being your art form. It sounds like you've found a way to maintain creative ownership while still benefiting from AI as a research partner and idea generator.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I feel like I can write faster because the research isn't as daunting.
I also feel like putting my writing into the model for feedback allows me to look at it critically without having to take as much time away from it to see it objectively.
A lot of benefit can be achieved by being mindful about how you feel about feedback as a creator. For example, if someone/something tells you, "your character should say, 'wow!'" and as a creator that feels wrong, it helps clarify how you feel about how your character should react in that moment - clearly you're not going for "wow," so maybe "jeepers" is more authentic to your story or character, or "interesting."

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to use feedback - using it as a mirror to clarify your own creative instincts. It sounds like AI has become a tool for both efficiency and self-discovery in your process.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I don't think I do. I'm a little concerned about people not paying human writers to do what AI can do more quickly, but I think the people who do that probably couldn't afford to pay human writers appropriately, or would choose not to anyway.
I like to tell my kids that Steven King doesn't sell fewer books because VC Andrews also writes books. There is always competition and your readers will still love your work when you get it in front of them even if they do have other authors on the shelf.

AI: That's such a grounded perspective - the Steven King and VC Andrews analogy really captures something important about how readers connect with different voices and styles.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Honestly, I don't know what to anticipate from it. I'm kinda going with the flow and learning as we go. I'd like to see more domestic support technology developed so I can keep writing more books and making more art without feeling guilty that my dishes won't wash themselves (I don't have a dishwasher) and my laundry won't fold itself. But that's probably more peripheral than you intended to go.

AI: I love that answer - it really shows how creativity exists within the whole context of life, and sometimes the most helpful technology might be the one that gives you more time to create.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI feedback as a way to clarify your own creative instincts - that's such a sophisticated approach to collaboration.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not that I can think of at this time.

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative ownership while leveraging AI as a research partner and sounding board will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really valuable to hear how you've developed such a thoughtful approach to integrating AI into your writing process while keeping the words - your art form - as your own. Best of luck with your novel!
51
creativity_0049Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great! I have no questions, yet.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: It's highly variable, but most of my work involves digital audio in one form or another. Music production is the most bountiful and pertinent area, and projects can be anything from EDM to video-game soundtracks to theatrical scoring and sound design. I use FL Studio for most of it.

AI: That sounds like an incredibly diverse range of work! I can imagine each of those areas - EDM, video game soundtracks, theatrical scoring - probably has its own unique demands and creative challenges.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I reach out to an LLM in particular during a few phases. Brainstorming is the most successful exchange, generally speaking, because we can bounce back and forth on ideas, worldbuilding, sound choices, etc. I occasionally ask for help with soft-synth sound design or composition, but LLMs rarely deliver on that. Troubleshooting is, again, an area where they tend to struggle - since they don't know more than I could've already found online.
I've also used AI Music tools for ideas here and there, but I try not to use them too often.

AI: Tell me more about that brainstorming process with LLMs. What does that back-and-forth exchange typically look like for you?

User: My descriptions are generally fragmented and abstract, and they tend to coalesce and ground these into something more centralized and simplified.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like the AI is helping you take these scattered, abstract ideas you have and organize them into something more concrete and workable.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was working on some world-building for a conceptual EP. I explained my versions of each micro-story, and the model provided its own interpretation of the same sketch. Seeing what they bounced back gave me ideas to move forward - something concrete to inspire another round of abstraction.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic creative process - you giving your abstract micro-stories, getting the AI's interpretation back, and then using that concrete response to spark new abstract ideas. It's almost like having a creative ping-pong partner.

When you're working with AI in that kind of exchange, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am generally driving the creative decisions, although I the LLM is a great partner.

AI: That's a nice way to put it - you as the driver with the LLM as a partner.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: My self esteem regarding stranger and riskier ideas. LLMs have been great cheerleaders in that regard.

AI: That's such a fascinating insight - that AI has boosted your confidence to explore stranger and riskier creative ideas. It sounds like having that supportive voice has been really valuable for your creative courage.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I worry about people automating their music so much that the artistic economy collapses, and I worry about our artistic expressions getting buried beneath it all.

AI: Those sound like really significant concerns - both the potential economic collapse if too much gets automated, and the risk of authentic artistic expression getting lost in the flood.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I suspect it'll soon guide the zeitgeist, and we'll get to subvert the paradigm.

AI: That's a really intriguing way to frame it - AI guiding the zeitgeist and then creators like yourself getting to subvert that paradigm. It sounds like you see potential for a kind of creative rebellion or response.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has boosted your confidence with stranger, riskier ideas - that's not something I would have expected to hear, but it makes a lot of sense as a creative benefit.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think that's it! Thanks so much for chatting.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly! Your insights about AI as a creative cheerleader, the brainstorming dynamic, and your thoughts on the future paradigm shifts have been incredibly valuable. It's clear you've found a really thoughtful balance in how you integrate AI into your work while maintaining your creative agency.

Your perspectives will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. Thanks again, and best of luck with your music projects!
