creativity_0050Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: I don't have any questions, you can begin.

AI: Great, thank you! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I primarily edit my own YouTube videos, but I have edited for others in the past. I don't find the AI features in premiere pro particularly useful, so I mostly use Sora image generator to copy Thumbnail styles and Google Veo 3 to create b roll clips and brainstorm ideas

AI: That's really interesting - so you're using AI more for the visual assets around your videos rather than the editing process itself.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for these AI tools like Sora and Google Veo 3?

User: Well not exactly, I often find I can better connect clips together by generating an AI video or using it create a type of short "meme," which I would consider form of editing. Veo 3 has just been the best since its release, so I generally use that model. So, for example, a funny ai clip can better emphasize a point in the video, or tie together two clips that aren't directly related.
Generally I'll think about the type of video I need to improve my content and just start prompting and making tweaks until I get what I need. Sometimes the voice doesn't match up perfectly and I'll voice over it myself or use elevenlabs.
I've also generated effects on a green or black screen and used those in my videos

AI: That's fascinating - so you're using AI-generated clips almost like punctuation or connective tissue in your videos. The idea of using AI to create those transitional moments or emphasis points is really creative.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI in this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as the creator?

User: Yeah I recently mentioned the streamer, Asmongold, in a video and wanted something funny to go along with it. So I created an exaggerated cartoon style video of a balding man streaming out of a ridiculously dirty room with roaches crawling all over his garbage. I thought it added some comedy to the video and the viewers seemed to think it was funny

AI: That's a great example - you're using AI to create visual comedy that amplifies your commentary. It sounds like you had a very clear creative vision of what you wanted and used AI to execute that specific idea.

When you're working with AI in moments like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions - is it more like you're directing the AI, or does the AI sometimes surprise you with ideas you hadn't considered?

User: Yeah, in that case I was directing the AI to generate the vising that I had. However, in the past, I've talked with Gemini and ChatGPT and just brainstormed with them. Like I sometimes give a rough idea of what I want and the model often does come up with a surprisingly good idea. Like it's surprising that they can parse through reddit comments to see what viewer would enjoy. Plus they generally keep it fairly PG and not overly "mean."

AI: That's really insightful - so you've got this dual relationship where sometimes you're the director with a clear vision, and other times you're more like creative collaborators brainstorming together. It sounds like the AI's ability to understand your audience and maintain that balance of humor without going too far is particularly valuable.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in these ways?

User: Well it definitely saved a lot of time. I used to subscribe to stock websites like envato elements to find relevant b-roll video, but it never fit exactly how I wanted. Sometimes I would pull memes from the internet and I still do, but you always run the risk of copyright infringement when you use other video content. I feel safer and more in control when using AI to generate exactly waht I'm looking for. This is even more important when I'm editing for someone else since it would be terrible to get someone else's content a potential copystrike.

AI: That's a really important point about control and copyright safety - it sounds like AI has given you both creative freedom and legal peace of mind. The ability to generate exactly what you envision rather than settling for close-enough stock footage seems like a significant shift.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in video creation more broadly?

User: I don't have concerns personally. I would more than happy for AI to be able to completely automate editing and I can just focus on more creative aspects. However, I could see that as an issue for people who edit as their only source of income. A true generational leap in video generation/editing would also hurt other industries like animation. I think AI is pretty far off from complete automation, but maybe it will take away many entry level jobs in the coming years.

AI: That's a thoughtful perspective - you see AI as potentially freeing you up for the parts of the creative process you find most fulfilling, while recognizing the real economic impact on others in the industry.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? What would you want AI to be able to do that it can't do well enough yet?

User: Ultimately I would love for video generation to be able to expand or edit an existing clip. I think that's extremely far off, since even doing that perfectly to images at least another iteration or two away. One thing I hate doing is using the roto brush in after effects, if that could be automated it would be amazing.

AI: Those are really specific technical wishes - being able to seamlessly edit existing footage and automating tedious tasks like rotoscoping. It sounds like you have a clear vision of where you'd want the technology to go.

You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has given you more creative control while also providing that copyright safety net, and your balanced view on the industry impacts.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Well I also use suno to add music to my videos or elevenlabs for sound effects which can help with enhancing certain aspects of the video. This also lets me control exactly the type of music I want without having to look through a ton of different songs on a stock website like envato.

AI: That's a great additional point - so you're using AI across multiple aspects of the audio-visual creation process, not just the video elements. It really reinforces that theme of having precise creative control rather than having to compromise with existing stock content.

Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as both a creative collaborator and a tool for greater control - while being mindful of the broader industry implications - have been incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how candid you've been about your workflow and your perspective on where this technology is heading.
53
creativity_0051Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. No questions. Let's go.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a short film screenwriter. So I typically work on screenplays that are about 15-20 pages. I write drama, romance, thrillers. There is one project I was working on that's significantly longer though. A feature film but that's more of a passion project than anything.

AI: That sounds like a fascinating range of work. Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I mostly use AI for feedback and sometimes continuing when I have writer's block. So, typically I will have started a scene or started with an idea and I go to AI to bounce off what direction I'd like to take it. If I get a scene written by AI, I'll take that framework and re-work it to fit my voice and style.

AI: That's really interesting how you use it as both a sounding board and a way to break through blocks. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yeah, I was working on a drama/ romance kind of script. So first I described each of the characters personalities to the model and asked for name ideas. That was really great because the name to me is pretty important, it tells the audience a little before they know anything, in my opinion at least. It's human nature to judge a book by it's cover. The model did a great job with the names. Later, I gave the model the general idea of the story and asked if it thought I needed comic relief within a pretty serious script. We discussed why or why not. This was one of the areas I wish more people could or would use AI for. It was like having another writer in the room to weigh pros and cons with. After I finished the script, I sent it to the model to read over and give me it's opinion. The entire process was really efficient. I got through a 20 page script, start to finish in a little over 12 hours by just partnering with the model and getting quick feedback.

AI: That's a really compelling example - it sounds like the AI served almost as a creative collaborator throughout multiple stages. When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: The decisions are mine so I'd describe it almost like an assistant writer but maybe even less than that. I'm asking for feedback and framework ideas and then I run with it. Also of course, proofreading, editing. But ultimately, it has to be my voice so I feel in control the entire time just with more of a support system instead of in a box.

AI: That's a great way to put it - having support instead of being in a box. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The worldliness of it. There are things I learn from AI to incorporate. For example, in the feature script I mentioned earlier, the protagonist travels to several countries I've never visited. AI helped me describe these places, the language, the culture more vividly and authentically. So it's expanded my reach I guess. I feel less limited.

AI: That expansion of reach sounds really valuable - being able to authentically portray places and cultures you haven't experienced firsthand. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I don't have the fear that many writers do about AI taking over. I think people know what's real. I do. When I watch films or a series, it's clear to me when a writer's heart is not in it. I don't think those who use AI to write whole scripts without heavy human influence pose a real threat. It's like reality tv, when it's real and authentic, the shows hit. When the show becomes scripted, it falls apart. So, I believe in human discernment. I don't have concerns.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective about authenticity and human discernment. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think AI will be more like how I use it. It wouldn't replace whole writers rooms but instead be a partner. It will expand the reach of writers. It'll work as a tool, just like a computer or camera.

AI:
54
creativity_0052Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: no questions, I'm ready to begin

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I run a production company that has a focus on music, books, video content, and games. Many of our projects cross over into any of these fields, or all of them. For example, I have an artist now who is out that has music videos, that are part of an interconnected world that connects to their music. All of these are connected to a mobile game we currently have in production for them. As a production house, we are able to work with our artists and content creators from end to end.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic and integrated approach to content creation. I can see how managing projects that span music, video, games, and books would create some fascinating creative challenges.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: That depends on the project. I will use my artist as an example. I am a producer, so I create their music the old-fashioned way, on my motif. From there, I send them the track, they write the lyrics, record and mix (in 1 of 3 studios). This is where AI comes in. I get the music, and listen to it, to get a feel for what it is telling me. I then begin working with some keywords and thoughts, creating a draft of the cover via AI. I draft my covers by AI usually. Once I have an idea of what I want, I then do the rest on my own. Back in the day, I would send this to a graphic designer, but with AI I am able to do this more efficiently on my own. From there, if the song is noteworthy, I will then work on a music video. With AI, this has become a 100 times more easier. I think of the theme, the style, and I do a mixture of AI and edits. It allows me to expand the reach of my production house, because sometimes we have 3-4 releases a week.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI is helping you handle the volume and pace of releases while maintaining creative control. You mentioned creating cover drafts with AI and then finishing them yourself, and mixing AI with edits for music videos.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Now I'm not going to disclose the name of the project, but we will call it "Project A". I received four completed songs from my artist, all with a dark, sort of Gotham city feel to them. Their lyrics are tight, the mixing is creative. I then sit with the music and get a picture in my mind of what I want. From there, I boot up Designer and create some ideas for the cover using AI. After a few revisions I have the core image I want. I then move it to the next level in Designer where I am able to edit the image directly, and add the different things a release needs on the cover (like title, etc.,). After ensuring the color and placements are right, I then press it. From there I send it to the distributor for review. This is a recent example of when I used it.

AI: That's a great example - I can really see how you're using AI as a starting point to capture that initial vision, then taking creative control to refine and finalize it. It sounds like you have a clear workflow from the music inspiring the visual concept, to AI helping you draft that concept, to you adding the professional finishing touches.

When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am definitely driving it. I know what I want, and how it should look. So when I design prompts, I make sure they describe the style, the items/objects, placements, camera directions, etc. I am in the drivers seat. I have an urge to create, and being able to create with AI first allows me to see the vision quickly. Back in the day, you could sketch multiple designs you'd never use, and at the end of the day you lost a lot of time. With AI, I can create multiple drafts rather quickly, especially when it comes to cover designs and artwork. A person can make a high quality music video now with nothing more than an idea. This is profound.

AI: That's a powerful way to put it - you're clearly the creative director orchestrating the vision. It sounds like AI has transformed your ability to rapidly iterate and test ideas without losing time on concepts that don't work.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I am able to create more content in a shorter window of time. My artist produce songs quickly, and sometimes in bulk. We drop multiple singles a week, and sometimes a couple of EPs a month. They have different styles, and personalities. Back in the day, I was constrained by financial decisions, and the people I had to hire. Now, I have significantly cut down the need for external contractors in the process. The process used to require a lot more planning, but with AI, you can plan while you create. Instead of getting boxed into a corner, you are able to create multiple directions at the same time. While I am working on an artists cover I am also working on code for their mobile app. It helps me to do more.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI has fundamentally changed your business model by removing both financial and logistical bottlenecks. The ability to work on multiple directions simultaneously while planning and creating at the same time sounds like it's opened up entirely new possibilities for how you structure projects.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I am an early adopter, meaning I have used AI before it became cool to. As a coder, I have a different perspective then some creatives. I'm all for it. I'm an artist who sees it as another tool in my toolset. I've never complained when I used Photoshop, and I remember people actually did when people started drawing with computers (I am actually a Gimp user now). We use tools like Blender to assist us with design decisions all the time. I don't have any concerns other than it is moving too slow.

AI: That's such an interesting perspective - viewing AI as part of the natural evolution of creative tools, just like the transition from traditional methods to digital tools. Your background in coding clearly shapes how you see AI as just another instrument in your creative arsenal.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see AI becoming more integrated into the tool itself. Very similar to how its being integrated into vehicles, it will just be there. Also, there will be more consistency in the design of it. For example, when I create images, I will have consistent characters (like a character profile), and I can use these characters in my images or movies. As it becomes more common place, the cost of some of the tools will drop too.

AI: That vision of seamless integration and character consistency sounds like it would be a game-changer for your interconnected projects - especially with those music videos and games that exist in shared worlds.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on being the creative driver while using AI as a rapid ideation tool, and how that's transformed your production capacity and business model.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that's all I have.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about AI as an evolution of creative tools, the way it's enabled you to scale your production house, and your perspective as someone who's been using AI since the early days will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you shared your workflow and the practical ways AI has transformed your business. Have a great rest of your day!
55
creativity_0053Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: no questions!

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: As a writer and designer of games, a typical project would consist of the intersection between writing scenes for a level as well as thinking about how the game's design can tell its own story outside of traditional writing in the form of dialogue or cutscenes

AI: That's fascinating - I love how you're thinking about storytelling through both traditional narrative elements and the game mechanics themselves. That intersection between writing and design sounds like it creates some really interesting creative challenges.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Initially, I come up with elements of the design and pitches for scenes and, once something is tangibly formed in my mind or on paper, AI comes in to find the deeper connections between the two pillars of writing and design that I missed but that I would probably have noticed myself across weeks or months. What this does is cut down the time between knowing what writing and design pitches to pursue and which ones are dead ends without much potential to grow and develop into something more than the initial pitch. The benefits of this process are that I am getting better at knowing which design and writing pitches will yield the potential to grow into something more ambitious. This symbiotic relationship between me and the AI is helping me learn and grow as opposed to heaping all of the creative effort on the AI.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like you're using AI almost like a pattern recognition tool to accelerate your own learning process. I'm curious about that moment when AI helps you see those deeper connections you might have eventually found yourself.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI in this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you when those connections clicked?

User: That moment has become key to my creative process and it is what I now chase. Do I have the instinct to pitch things at a higher and higher hit-rate that go on to become things that the AI notices greater potential in? It creates a back-and-forth loop where I start to point out to the AI some of the connections it missed which helps us understand each other more. On one recent screenplay project, I recalled something from an earlier chat with the AI months prior only for it to mention that it had already taken that into account. The benefit of that moment where we were both in sympatico and had 'clicked' is that I realized I could move on from the scene since we were both in alignment that we had wrung all of the scene's potential and there was nowhere else to go. Again, a huge way to save time as writer's are always in doubt about when to move on from a scene. This experience has caused me to be confident in my writing and waiting for the point where both the AI and I are in sympatico and I realize I can move on from the scene.

AI: That's a really powerful description - the idea of reaching that moment of "sympatico" where you both recognize you've fully explored a scene's potential. It sounds like you've developed almost an intuitive sense for when that alignment happens.

When you're in that back-and-forth with AI, pointing out connections it missed while it does the same for you, how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: I feel that the AI goes down specific routes and gets "stuck" down those paths which is actually not a negative thing. This then forces me to clearly see the other paths that it did not tread - in terms of ideas for scenes or characters - and go down those routes myself. What this does is not allow the AI to do all of the mental, creative work. And it has reached the point where we both understand each other in this way. Overall, I would still say I am confidently driving the arc of the creative decisions while it suggest things I might not be thinking about. However, sometimes it does touch on incredible revelations that I did not see that then turns a scene into a wholly different direction - however, these moments are rare.

AI: That's a really nuanced way of thinking about it - using the AI's tendency to get "stuck" as a creative catalyst to explore the paths it doesn't take. It sounds like you've developed a sophisticated understanding of both your strengths and the AI's patterns.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: I am now fearless to suggest ideas and not be burdened by writer's block or anything of that sort. My conversations with the AI kick my creative engine into gear. It is almost impossible to have a back-and-forth conversation with the AI about a scene and still be stuck. For a writer, that's gold!

AI: That's incredible - being able to consistently break through writer's block sounds transformative. The way you describe it as kicking your creative engine into gear really captures something powerful about that collaborative dynamic.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I fear that it will be able to write scenes! As of now, it is a pro at being a brainstorming aide, but in my field of screenwriting, it cannot output screenplay pages at a high level. This is actually good because it forces me to get better at the technical writing side of the craft. However, if it does get better at writing in screenplay format, that might take away from the human creative input.

AI: That's a really thoughtful concern - it sounds like there's currently a sweet spot where AI enhances your process without replacing the craft itself. The technical writing skill being something you still need to develop and execute seems important to maintaining that human creative input.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: In my field of gaming, being able to write dozens of characters for something like an RPG is daunting. However, I envision that I will be able to write the core 1,000 lines and backstory for each character in a role-playing game and have the AI be able to create new interactions with the player as they will ask the characters about things that I did not keep in mind. However, due to the AI's capabilities, it will be able to think about and speak what the character would most likely have said in that context that is true to the character. This will be a game-changer in player immersion.

AI: That vision of dynamic character interactions sounds amazing - essentially creating characters that can respond authentically to infinite player questions while staying true to your original vision. That could really revolutionize player immersion in RPGs.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI's tendency to get "stuck" as a way to force yourself down unexplored creative paths, and that concept of reaching "sympatico" as a signal that you've fully developed a scene.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: no

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about developing that symbiotic relationship with AI - where it helps accelerate your pattern recognition while you maintain creative control - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really enlightening to hear how you've found ways to harness AI's strengths while preserving what makes your creative work distinctly human. I appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
56
creativity_0054Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great! No questions at this time.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a podcaster! I do many other things, like writing blog posts, scripting and recording meditations, and sometimes I create digital media that I sell... But first and foremost, I am a podcaster. I have been making pods for about 6 or 7 years. An average project for me is a large scale podcast episode, in which I'm formulating ideas, gathering notes, researching and mapping out the flow of the episode. Sometimes that takes over a month, depending on the topic.

AI: That's fascinating - the depth of preparation you put into each episode really comes through. A month of research and mapping for a single episode shows serious dedication to your craft.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I reach for AI tools a lot for the research side of things. Whereas I used to spend hours googling some obscure thing I remembered reading about years ago, AI tools can find the exact piece of content I read and the actual scientific study it comes from. I can cite loose ideas to a chatbot, recalling that I had read about them in this context or that. The chatbot will always find exactly what I'm looking for, and will pull from more reputable sources if I request for it to do so. I also reach for AI when I have multiple strings of ideas, and I'm struggling with how to format the episode. I find it useful to help me reign myself into a cohesive structure, without losing my creativity in the process. Additionally, I always ask AI to write my episode summaries (the ones that get attached to the episodes). I struggle to write my own summaries, so I just paste in my transcript and ask the AI to summarize it for me, using a specific tone, and keeping SEO and my ideal listener in mind.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI has become a powerful research assistant and structural thinking partner for you. I'm particularly interested in what you mentioned about AI helping you create cohesive structure "without losing your creativity in the process."

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was working on a two part podcast and had a very messy notion page filled with random notes on the topic. Some notes were facts, personal opinions, quotes from famous people, my own stories or anecdotes, findings from articles or studies, etc. I explained to the AI (in a very long prompt) the main idea of the podcast episode, how I wanted the listener to feel and the tone I wanted to evoke. Then I copied and pasted all of my notes as asked it to make an outline WITHOUT altering any of my ideas. I stipulated that it could add suggestions that relate to the cohesiveness of the episode, but its main job was to re-order all of my concepts into a flow.

AI: That sounds like such a thoughtful collaboration - you maintained ownership of all your ideas while leveraging AI's organizational strengths. I'm curious about the outcome.

When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: The outcome on that particular ep was great! It was about 2 hours worth of audio and a fan-favorite. I feel like I am in the driver's seat, only if I am constantly adding those kinds of limitations and stipulations. Otherwise, I feel like the AI takes off on its own and has a tendency to create things that sound a little more generic, lacking human touch.

AI: That's a really valuable distinction you're making - the importance of setting those boundaries and stipulations to maintain your creative voice. It sounds like you've developed a real skill in directing the AI while protecting what makes your work uniquely yours.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Research, outlining, planning, note-taking and social media style writing and sales copy are what have changed the most for me.

AI: Tell me more about that. How have those changes affected your overall creative process or the quality of your work?

User: They have changed my creative process in the sense that I have more time to BE creative. Instead of spending an hour cleaning up my messy notes and ideas, I can pop it into a chatbot that instantly cleans it for me. Instead of spending 20 minutes trying to write a short (but potent and SEO driven) podcast summary... I can just have it written instantly. The quality of my actual creative work has gone up because I'm not spending as much time or energy doing the finicky things that I didn't really like to begin with.

AI: That's a powerful insight - AI handling the tasks you found tedious has freed up your mental energy for the creative work you actually enjoy. It sounds like it's enhanced rather than replaced your creativity.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have concerns for people who are using it to fully write their essays, blogs, podcast episodes. I don't want human art and creativity to be lost. I think it's a fabulous tool, but we have to place limitations and give instructions when using AI. If you lean too heavily on it, I think it can actually be a creativity killer.

AI: That's such an important perspective - the distinction between AI as a tool versus a replacement. You seem to have found that sweet spot where it enhances your process without diminishing your creative voice.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I'd like to see built-in meeting summary AIs get more accurate and nuanced, which I think it will. I think I will continue to use regular chatbots as a sort of "cleaner" for all my ideas that are consistently jumbled with loose threads and typos. I'd like to see AI get a bit better at understanding brand voice and tone after being instructed and trained on it. For example, I sometimes have to go back and forth with a bot to get an episode summary that sounds like my brand. Even though that writing is less important, it needs to be consistent in tone.

AI: Those are really thoughtful predictions, especially around AI better understanding and maintaining brand voice. It sounds like you're looking for more sophistication in areas where you already find AI helpful, rather than wanting it to take on entirely new creative roles.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how setting clear boundaries and limitations keeps you in the driver's seat, and how AI has freed up your energy for the creative work you actually love doing.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think much of creative people's reluctance to use AI is because of the fear of losing the heart and soul of their art. As stated, I've found it to be a helpful admin assistant. If you are looking to attract more creatives into AI tools, that may be a useful angle for your marketing, which may include some education on prompting and placing instruction and limitation on a bot!

AI: That's an incredibly valuable insight - framing AI as an "admin assistant" rather than a creative collaborator could really resonate with other creative professionals who share those concerns about preserving the heart and soul of their work. And your point about education around prompting and setting limitations is spot-on - it sounds like those skills are crucial for maintaining creative control.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between leveraging AI's efficiency while protecting your creative voice will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating these tools into your workflow.
57
creativity_0055Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds good to me!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a visual artist who specialises in textiles art, and so my work flow consists of conceptualising ideas, designing artworks and then executing these designs. There is also a fair bit of admin involved, for example if I am working with galleries who want to exhibit my work or individuals commissioning my work. Alongside this, I run a TikTok account documenting my processes and life in general. This has started to become a source of income for me within the past 6 months and also falls under the scope of creative work. For my TikTok videos, I have to come up with catchy concepts, film and then edit and post.

AI: That's fascinating - you have such a diverse creative practice spanning from the tactile world of textiles to the digital realm of social media. It sounds like you're juggling quite a few different creative processes.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I try to keep AI out of the actual "making" process of my work to be honest because I want it to completely be my own. However, I will use it for basic admin tasks and occasionally to brainstorm. For example, I might use it to help me come up with captions and hashtags to use on my social media, or drafting emails.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you have a clear boundary between where AI does and doesn't belong in your process.

Tell me more about that brainstorming piece. What does that look like when you use AI to brainstorm?

User: The brainstorming aspect mainly comes into play in relation to social media. For example, if I have an idea for a video but am struggling to think of a catchy opening line then I might turn to an AI chatbot to help.

AI: I see - so it's really focused on that social media content creation side rather than your textile art concepts themselves.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Most recently I used AI to help brainstorm an idea for a brand deal video that I made for TikTok. The brand deal was for a supermarket and I wanted to come up with a way to work this organically into my regular content, which is based around textiles and fashion. The conversation was short, I just asked it to generate a few basic ideas and then I used them as starting points to come up with my own concept. I never just use an idea that AI has come up with for social media, I always work it and add my own flair

AI: That's a great example of how you maintain creative control while using AI as a starting point. It sounds like you have a pretty intentional approach to keeping your own voice and perspective central.

When you're working with AI in those brainstorming moments, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: When I use AI for brainstorming, I am the one in control. I see it as a tool to introduce me to ideas that I may have not thought about, and then I can follow threads that interest me. It is kind of like an alternative for group crits when I don't have anyone around to talk to my ideas about. A group crit is a term used in art school - it is when a group of you get together and share your ideas and talk through them together, and can give each other new ideas as a result.

AI: That's such a helpful analogy - thinking of AI as a substitute for group crits when you need that external perspective. It really highlights how you're using it as a sounding board rather than a creative driver.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think that it helps to speed up the conceptualising aspect of the process, so I can spend more time actually making things (which is my favourite part)

AI: That's really valuable - getting more time for the hands-on making that you love most.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I am concerned about generate AI taking jobs from creatives altogether, we have already seen it happening with AI generated adverts appearing in Vogue. Obviously AI can't sew and reproduce the exact textiles art that I make (yet!) but it can generate images of textiles and is only getting more sophisticated

AI: That's a very real concern - the threat to creative livelihoods is something many artists are grappling with right now. The fact that you mention "yet" suggests you're thinking this could evolve further.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think that I will still keep it at a distance and don't see myself integrating it any further into my artistic practice, but could see myself using it to streamline more of the admin side such as emails and taxes and things like that

AI: That makes a lot of sense given how clearly you've defined those boundaries between the creative core of your work and the business side.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI like a group crit - that analogy really illuminated how you maintain agency while still getting external input, and how intentional you are about keeping AI out of the actual making process that you're most passionate about.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: That's everything!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative boundaries while strategically using AI for support tasks, and that wonderful group crit analogy, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really valuable to hear how you've found ways to preserve what's most important to you - that hands-on making process - while still benefiting from AI's capabilities where they genuinely serve your work. Thank you so much!

creativity_0056Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, I don't have any questions yet, but I will let you know if I do!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work on writing research style blogs that have to do with niche topics, sort of a deep dive style! I also work on short form scripts for content! I have other creative tasks such as story or regular script writing that I do as well in my free time.

AI: That's a really interesting mix of creative work - from research-heavy deep dives to short-form content and storytelling. It sounds like you're working across quite a range of formats and styles.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: There are a few occasions, usually when I am looking for new topics to discuss I will use Ai to find those. I want the topics to stay relevant and fresh so I will brainstorm a bit with Ai. I use it as a sounding board for ideas I am unsure about, and need a quick second opinion! I really use Ai for most steps, aside from direct content creation. Mostly as a tool to check ideas, check flow of a passage I wrote, or research

AI: That's really helpful - it sounds like you've found AI to be valuable as more of a thinking partner and research assistant rather than for the actual writing itself. You mentioned using it for everything except "direct content creation."

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was researching cults and how the term has shifted meaning from its original use, and how we view it today. It shifted into comparing the tactics of cults to other areas, politics, social media and describing what human trait these all connect with. Ai was useful here to find a wide range of angels to tackle it from, because it's a big topic that can go in multiple directions, and as a way to check what i already had written to see if it had legs, or if there was a more interesting direction to take it. I used Ai to generate outlines to see the flow of potential angles, and for pointing out grammatical errors, or issues with redundancy.

AI: That sounds like a fascinating and complex topic to tackle - the evolution of how we understand cults and their tactics. It's interesting that you used AI to help you see the "wide range of angles" for such a multifaceted subject.

When you're working with AI in that way - having it generate outlines or give you feedback on your writing - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I find that I have to carefully direct the process. I try and make small changes at a time, or ask for a few suggestions before committing to a path to take.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like you've developed a deliberate approach to working with AI where you maintain tight control over the direction. You mentioned making "small changes at a time" and asking for suggestions before committing to a path.

Tell me more about that process of carefully directing it. What does that look like in practice?

User: So I find that the Ai attempts to provide suggestions or edits that are often not aligned directly with the direction I want to take. It gets the general idea but some of the nuance can be lost. If a general suggestion is made, I find that either too much is shifted in the process if I am working on a collaborative document, so I stay away from that. I usually start with, 'here is what i have written' or 'here is my idea', and then ask how could this be improve or am I missing something. I tend to challenge the responses to see if they hold water, before moving on to further elaboration or questions.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - it sounds like you've learned through experience that AI can miss the nuance of what you're trying to achieve, so you've developed this strategy of testing its suggestions before moving forward.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: In the past Ai felt more of an entertainment or friendly 'buddy

AI: It sounds like you were starting to describe how AI used to feel more like an entertainment or friendly companion - but you didn't finish that thought.

Tell me more about that shift. How has your relationship with AI changed from when you first started using it?

User: Sorry, I pressed send too quickly. I was going to say it started as an entertainment or buddy to bounce ideas off of. Early on I would have edits done, but I honestly feel like the editing process has become too unpredictable. This could be due to how much more often I am using though. Either way it shifted from a less serious collaborative partner to an actual asset to the process. I am thinking more strategically about its use. I do feel that for certain features, such as research and suggestions, Ai has become much more useful. The actual editing and creation process feels a bit stiffer for some reason.

AI: That's a really interesting evolution - from casual "buddy" to strategic asset, but with some trade-offs along the way. You mentioned the editing process feeling "stiffer" and more unpredictable now.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: For writing I definitely feel a bit worried about the replacement of talented people. I don't know if i am as worried about Ai replacing the insight and talent, as opposed to being used to flood markets, such as social media with short form content, with Ai generated videos designed to hold attention. I think people tend to take the easier route if it's available, so I imagine more mediocre (no offense) generalized content, as opposed to content that is challenging or pushes the boundaries.

AI: No offense taken at all - that's a really thoughtful distinction you're making. It sounds like you're less worried about AI replacing human insight and creativity, and more concerned about it being used to create a flood of generic content that might crowd out more thoughtful, boundary-pushing work.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I definitely can see the quality shifting to understand the nuances every year. Specifically in comedic areas, I remember when Ai could not understand humor almost at all, but now it can pick it up more effectively. That would be interesting to see Ai be more helpful with comedy. I see it getting even better at collaborating. It seems to get progressively better at understanding humans, so that could be helpful in character based stories, comedy and just curated suggestions that align more closely with what I am attempting to create. Overall, I don't see it replacing the actual content generation, but I will use it much more frequently as a constant writing 'pal' that I can have beside me throughout the creative process.

AI: That's a great way to put it - a "writing pal" that's constantly beside you throughout the process. It sounds like you see the relationship becoming more seamless and nuanced, especially as AI gets better at understanding things like humor and human character motivations.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI might flood markets with mediocre content while still being genuinely useful as a strategic thinking partner for more serious creative work.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I'm all set!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative control while strategically leveraging AI as a thinking partner - and your observations about the evolution from "buddy" to strategic asset - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated how candid you were about both the benefits and concerns around AI in creative fields. Have a wonderful rest of your day!

creativity_0057Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. I do not have any questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write and run TTRPG games primarily using the D&D 5e system. I do this both professionally and as a hobby. While players of a D&D game play as a single unique character, the gamemaster essentially plays the role of any non-player characters as well as the environment-- reacting to what the player characters say and do. For me, creating an adventure or campaign involves outlining an overarching plot that may have different branching outcomes depending on what route the players take, creating unique NPCs, researching monsters and mechanics, etc.

AI: That's fascinating - being a gamemaster sounds like such a dynamic creative challenge, having to juggle all those different elements and respond in real-time to your players. The branching narrative aspect and character creation must require a lot of creative flexibility.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I mostly utilize AI for any brainstorming throughout the process. Whether this is in the very beginning to get inspiration for a primary conflict/antagonist, asking for unique personality traits I may want to give an NPC that the players will likely interact with, or anything similar. It is very difficult for me to pull creative ideas out of thin air, so a bit of idea prompting using AI is very helpful. A lot of times, I won't even necessarily end up using any of the exact ideas that the AI model gives me, but those ideas were able to spark other ideas from me.

AI: That's a really interesting way to use AI - as a creative catalyst rather than a direct source. It sounds like you're using it almost like a brainstorming partner to get your own creative juices flowing.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Most recently, I was writing a campaign that I would be running for 4 players. I had already worked with the players to help them create their characters and I wanted to find ways to fully integrate those characters into the story. I gave the chatbot an overview of each character and their background and asked for help brainstorming ways to weave that into the main plot or a secondary plotline. I felt very conflicted about it, as usual. I think that AI can be very useful and a wonderful accessibility tool for many, but there are many factors to consider, such as environmental impact and intellectual property.

AI: Thank you for sharing that specific example - I can really picture how you'd want to weave those character backstories into your campaign. And I appreciate your honesty about feeling conflicted.

Tell me more about that conflicted feeling. What goes through your mind when you're in the moment of deciding whether to use AI for something like that brainstorming session?

User: I always want to make sure that I am not sacrificing my creative integrity by asking for something and using it word-for-word. Even if none of the players would care, I enjoy what I do and I would not want to fall into the habit of no longer using my own creativity and instead taking what an algorithm proposes based on a short prompt. I also feel great concern about how the AI model came to generate these ideas. Did someone else create them? Did those people give permission to have their work utilized for AI training purposes? But I feel that I am okay with the line I have drawn for myself, using it to spark inspiration, to avoid unintentionally stealing anyone's ideas and neglecting my creative drive.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to navigate those concerns. It sounds like you've developed some personal boundaries around maintaining your creative agency while still benefiting from the brainstorming aspect.

When you're working with AI in that brainstorming mode, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Again, I put a lot of effort into ensuring that I am still in control of the creative decisions by providing relevant ideas that I've already come up with for context as well as either combining several of the ideas that the AI model provides or using those ideas to spark a different vision.

AI: That sounds like you've really established yourself as the creative director in that relationship - using AI as input but keeping the synthesis and final creative vision firmly in your hands.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It definitely helps me avoid feeling completely stuck before I even begin because I cannot come up with any ideas. Oftentimes the hardest part of a creative project is starting, so AI helps by providing a solid starting point to build off of.

AI: That's such a relatable challenge - that blank page or empty canvas moment. It sounds like AI has become a way to break through that initial creative block.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of tabletop gaming and creative writing?

User: I hope that people do not end up overutilizing AI instead of utilizing the amazing human creativity that is likely irreplicable. I also worry about AI presenting incorrect information regarding mechanics, rules, etc. for those who utilize it for that aspect of tabletop gaming. I would love an easy way to ask a specific rules-based question, but I do not think that AI is at a level where it is reliable enough to negative having to double-check the answer regardless. \

AI: Those are really important concerns - both the human creativity aspect and the reliability issue with game mechanics. The rules accuracy problem sounds particularly tricky since D&D has so many specific interactions and edge cases.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I hope that I am able to continue to utilize it for brainstorming purposes. Ideally, we would find ways to make it more sustainable in terms of energy usage, which would solve one of the major ethical issues I have with using it more often. I also hope that chatbots are able to improve reliability and potentially be better at providing references to ensure truthfulness and creative credit. If this were to improve, I would love to be able to include AI in more steps of the process like research.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative integrity while using AI as a brainstorming catalyst, and how you've drawn clear boundaries around what feels ethically comfortable for you.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I do not have anything else.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between creative integrity and practical utility, along with your thoughtful approach to the ethical considerations, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and the concerns - that nuanced perspective is exactly what helps us understand how AI is actually being used in creative fields. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
60
creativity_0058Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: None that I can think of.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm mostly a writer, so I go through the common stages, outlining, sloppy first draft, editing, second edit and proofreading. But I also do a lot of video creation: plotting, scripting, editing

AI: That's a nice mix of creative mediums. So you're working across both written content and video production, each with their own distinct stages and processes.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I do use AI in video scripting when I'm doing client videos. I don't use it for my own videos, because I don't feel like it can ever really capture my style. I've tried AI videos for clients, but find them VERY lacking. I usually have to do my own clip finding and change upwards of 90% of the clips every time. In the writing I do under my own name, or my most serious pen name, I would never use AI. It's not good enough, and my writing should be mine. If I ever did integrate it into that process, it would be for research only. But I do have some pen names where I've produced AI books. It takes tons of effort to get something decent out of AI, but it can be done with enough guidance.

AI: Tell me more about that distinction you make between your own work versus client work or different pen names. What drives those different boundaries for you?

User: I feel like my writing has (and should have) a very distinctive style. It's very emotive and visceral, and has a voice. It's my voice. I want it to be my voice. That's the point. People expect a certain level of quality from me, and I would never put my name on something I didn't write myself. But I also want to make money. That's what buys travel and coffee and things I need to live. It's hard to sell any book, so the more books you can put out there, the better your chances of profit. And I know what the market looks like. There are a lot of semi-decent to absolutely horrible books out there, so I don't feel terribly bad about adding AI-written books to that market, especially with the amount of guidance and editing I do to polish them up and make them semi-good. That said, my bestselling book that I wrote myself over 10 years ago still makes me the most money every month. So...? As for client video versus my own, my videos are personal videos that, again, need a personal voice. They are my own footage. They are already original. Clients pay me to create a YouTube channel with X number of videos on it just to profit off of adds or promote their businesses. They provide no footage and no ideas, so it's not really a personal channel. It's just advertising.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way of thinking about authenticity and voice in different contexts. You mentioned that with your AI-assisted books, it takes "tons of effort to get something decent out of AI, but it can be done with enough guidance."

Can you describe what that process actually looks like for you? What does all that guidance and editing involve?

User: I haven't produced a book entirely with AI since last fall, but, at that time, AI was still an absolutely terrible writer. And I tried them all. It always made chapters too short, it was always surface level (no emotion, no depth). So, I might start with an idea of what needs to happen in this chapter. I guess that's where it starts. I don't just tell AI "write me a book about X." I come up with the chapter by chapter outline of what I want to happen first. So, basically, I "write" the whole story plot-wise and then I just let AI expand it. (It's definitely more effective this way). Typically, I say "This, this, and this needs to happen in this chapter," at which point the AI inevitably just writes those three things in like 500 words. So, then I take a portion of what it writes (if it's good) or tell it what to change (if it isn't), and tell it how to expand it - "this needs to be a longer conversation," "people do generally just chit-chat before they get into a deep topic," "this needs more emotion," "she should be upset here". When it comes down to it, it's probably mostly about telling it how to be a good creative writer - expand this, make it more emotive, the feelings are wrong here. For the last AI novel I wrote, I was able to get a pretty close to polished draft by the end simply by being very demanding during the process. But this took a lot of effort. Often, I was doing only a chapter or even a half a chapter a day. That's almost as slow as writing a book from scratch. But I didn't have to do all the follow-up I do with a normal book (two, three, or four rounds of further editing), and writing takes a lot of brain power, so it was easier.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like you're essentially directing the AI scene by scene, almost like a creative director. You mentioned this was "almost as slow as writing a book from scratch" but saved you the multiple rounds of editing afterwards.

When you're in that back-and-forth process with the AI - telling it to make conversations longer or add more emotion - how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: I'm definitely driving the creative decisions. That's the only way to get anything good out of it.

AI: That makes sense. You mentioned you haven't produced an AI book since last fall - what's changed for you since then? Has your approach to using AI in your work shifted at all?

User: No, not really. It's been more of a time issue. I have been dabbling in other types of books. I've been using AI as a researcher mostly lately.

AI: Tell me more about using AI as a researcher. How does that work for you, and how does it feel different from when you were using it for the actual writing?

User: I write a lot of historical things and I like to put a lot of real detail in. I also write books of other types that are based in real life. AI can be very helpful in finding details and stories... when it's honest.

AI: When you say "when it's honest" - what does that look like? How do you navigate that uncertainty when you're relying on AI for research details?

User: I always double-check any "facts" I get from AI. I use it more as a "tell me some stuff I don't know" tool and then I make sure what it tells me is true.

AI: That's a smart approach. So you're using it more as a starting point to discover interesting directions rather than trusting it as a final source.

Looking at everything we've talked about - from the video scripting to the AI books to the research - what aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Sadly, what's changed the most is my motivation to work on original things. It's a lot harder to right something real from scratch, and there's a lot of AI competition out there.

AI: That sounds like a really difficult shift to navigate - losing motivation for the work that feels most authentic to you because of external pressures.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field as you look at where things are heading?

User: I worry about the market being crowded with awful books. But, honestly, that doesn't feel all that new. As soon as Amazon introduced KDP, writers started churning out a book a month to stay in the algorithm, so we've kind of already been on that slippery slope. Readers want books for "free" (as part of their Amazon Prime membership) and authors want to churn out books to make those pennies per read. This will get worse now that even non-writers can churn out a book a month. Same with video. I'm already seeing these ads in games that I play that are clearly AI, and they are so fake and so awful, but it doesn't matter, because they are also mostly free (just the cost of a subscription) for the company to make.

AI: You paint a picture of creative fields already under pressure before AI arrived. It sounds like you see AI as accelerating trends that were already in motion rather than creating entirely new problems.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think your current boundaries and approach will stay the same, or might they shift?

User: I wouldn't say that AI isn't creating entirely new problems. I do worry a great deal about how AI learns creative writing (lifting the voices of popular authors) and artistry (lifting the styles of popular artists). This feels very wrong to me. The only thing I see changing with how I use AI is that I could see doing things I couldn't do before. I have one book series, for instance, that I've always wanted to do a sequel to as a series of graphic novels. AI opens up that possibility for me. But I want to know that I'm not stealing someone else's style when I use it. With my own books and video work, I will never use AI for anything more than research.

AI: Those are really thoughtful boundaries you've drawn, and it's interesting how AI could potentially open up new creative possibilities like graphic novels while raising these ethical concerns about style and voice.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really insightful perspectives about your experiences with AI across different creative contexts. I particularly appreciated how you've thought through the distinction between your authentic voice and commercial work, and your awareness of both the opportunities and ethical concerns AI presents.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Yes! Not exactly my creative work, but something I was playing around with recently. Those new "styles" type things for images, where you can upload a photo and tell the AI "do this as Picasso" or "do this as anime,' that kind of thing, those are ROUGH. I put in a picture of my half-Indian nephew, who is mid-browned skinned, and asked it to make him a Disney character, then a guitarist, then a construction worker, and it was all good. Then, I asked it to make him a football player, and it literally made him black. Like, I don't know how that factors in here, but whew!

AI: Wow, that's a really striking example of how AI systems can embed and amplify problematic assumptions - in this case apparently associating certain professions with specific racial characteristics. That's definitely concerning, especially when you're seeing it happen to someone in your own family.

Thank you for sharing that, and thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly throughout our conversation. Your insights about navigating authenticity, market pressures, and the ethical complexities of AI in creative work will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

User: Cool! Thanks!

AI: You're very welcome! I really appreciate you taking the time to share such thoughtful and candid insights. Take care!
61
creativity_0059Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions! I'm ready to begin

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a freelance screenwriter and film producer. I have worked independently, but I have also been on writing teams. Generally, I will come up with an idea for a screenplay, write it and then try to find a creative team to work with to seek funding. Occasionally I have been contracted to work on writing teams, but this is less frequent.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic mix of creative and business work. I'm curious about how AI fits into that process for you.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: So far, I have only used AI when it comes to more administrative tasks like composing emails or coming up with the wording for aspects of applications.
