The most creative use is potentially coming up with loglines/pitches. I generally have an idea of what I want the pitch to look like, but may outsource to AI if I have a mental block.

AI: That's interesting - so you're using AI more as a support tool for the business side and occasionally to break through creative blocks with pitches.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: The last project relied on the website Boosted to partially raise funding. I used AI to write the call to action for people who might be willing to donate some money to our creative work. In terms of how it felt, I think there's always a little bit of shame that comes with using AI in my line of work. Most of the creatives I work with are very concerned about the impact on our field, and I have a bit of anxiety that if it's obvious I've used AI for any part of the project it may impact my credibility when it comes to the rest of my work. I'm very cautious about using it, and will always rewrite the output to sound more like 'me.'

AI: That's a really honest perspective - I appreciate you sharing that. The tension between finding AI helpful and worrying about how it's perceived in your field sounds quite complex.

When you do use AI and then rewrite the output to sound more like you, how would you describe that process? What does it feel like to take that AI-generated content and make it your own?

User: Honestly, it's time saving. I struggle slightly with structuring professional writing so AI's output generally has a structure I can use. I'll generally just change the wording of the sentences or the word choices. Prior to using AI, I would have searched for a template on Google (like I might search: Movie Pitch Template or Cover Letter Template) and then used the same process, so it feels pretty intuitive to use it in this way.

AI: That's a really helpful comparison - using AI like you would use a template you'd find online. It sounds like for you, AI is serving a similar function to those existing resources but maybe more efficiently.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Very few aspects have changed, my creative process is still largely similar. It's saved me a bit of time being able to generate an email or pitch quickly, but I have not found AI useful for the bulk of what I do. I have tried to use AI for things like brainstorming, but I did not feel that it was helpful. I would personally not use AI for writing, mainly because I enjoy the process and I tend to have a clear idea of what I want as opposed to a pitch which I feel can be quite generic.

AI: That distinction you're making is really interesting - that pitches can be more generic while your actual writing is something you have a clear vision for and enjoy doing yourself.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I am worried that an oversaturation of low effort work may reduce opportunities for newcomers. If people can't be bothered to write their own work, then it's hard to justify why other people should bother to read it. If production companies are receiving huge amounts of AI written content, they may be inclined to return to established directors/writers/producers instead of seeking out new voices. My personal belief is that this would disproportionately effect minority writers.

AI: That's a really thoughtful concern about how AI could potentially create barriers for emerging voices, especially minority writers. The idea that oversaturation of low-effort content might push industry gatekeepers toward established names makes a lot of sense.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Could see leaning into more practical implementations of AI as opposed to creative. For example, being able to generate a call sheet based on various factors or being able to filter through a number of grant/funding opportunities to determine which ones would be appropriate for our current project. In terms of the creative side, I don't see that changing for me personally. I don't feel the need to use it currently and it's hard to imagine what would lead to that changing.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - focusing AI on the logistical side where it could genuinely streamline your workflow without touching the creative core that you value doing yourself.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI, particularly around that tension between finding it useful for certain tasks while being mindful of how it's perceived in your field. I also found your perspective on the potential impact on emerging voices really valuable.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I did also just want to share that mistrust in AI's factuality prevents me from using it for research in the early development of a project. I have had too many experiences where AI has provided me things that are blatantly incorrect and I've only noticed because I'm already familiar with the topic. It takes just as much fact checking as using Google to locate a trusted source myself, so this is something that I think is worth flagging. Many writers spend a lot of time researching and this is an area where AI could certainly be helpful if it was reliable or at least easy to verify.

AI: That's a really important point - the reliability issue is clearly a significant barrier for research-heavy work like yours. The fact that you've caught AI providing incorrect information makes complete sense why you'd stick with traditional research methods where you can trace back to trusted sources.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the practical applications you find useful, the concerns about industry gatekeeping, and especially that research reliability issue will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate your candid perspective on both the benefits and limitations you've experienced.
62
creativity_0060Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope! Let's get started!

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a novelist and I'm also learning how to write screenplays. With novels, my process is very chaotic but also very specific. I have no trouble coming up with ideas and I usually don't start writing until I know how it's going to end. This way, I make sure that I have a goal to shoot towards with every scene that I write. I don't outline and I also don't write in order. I also write very fast. I usually get a draft done within a month for a novel.

AI: That's a fascinating process - especially writing out of order while keeping that end goal in mind. It sounds like you have a very intuitive but goal-oriented approach.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: My weakest points are the middle of the novel and also the lead up towards the final 1/3 of the novel. I often have trouble coming up with strong reasons within the plot for the heroine to go on the big journey away from home, leaving behind everything they know. I also use AI to help me come up with descriptors for spaces and places since I hate doing that part.

AI: That's really interesting - so you're using AI specifically to tackle the parts that feel most challenging or tedious to you.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I used it in YA fantasy novel to come up with descriptors for the unique city inside of an invisible magical shield. The city is adorned in gold and I wanted to try to come up with some unique ways to showcase this. I mostly like to get unique details rather than fully fleshed-out concepts. Just something to get the ideas jumpstarted for me. It felt great to have ideas to pick from instead of sitting around trying to do the grunt work myself. It can get disheartening to wait around for ideas to come.

AI: That sounds like a really practical use - getting those jumpstart ideas to choose from rather than staring at a blank page.

When you're working with AI on these kinds of tasks, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm always driving the creative decisions. I never relinquish that hold. I think of the AI as if I'm talking to a writing partner. I've had many writing partners who I'll tell the general idea to and they'll throw out ideas for me to move forward or might even ask questions about stuff I haven't thought too deeply about. That's how I prefer for the AI to act. OR, I want it to offer a few ideas within a few sentences and I'll take it from there.

AI: That's a great analogy - thinking of it like a writing partner who throws out ideas and asks probing questions. It sounds like you have a very clear sense of maintaining creative control while using AI as a collaborative sounding board.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Speed is a big one, but probably more importantly, how I feel about myself as I write. I can get really down if I feel like I get stuck and can't think of anything new. It's so helpful to just go straight to AI to use as a sounding board so I don't get stuck. It feels really good to keep a steady writing pace.

AI: That emotional aspect is really compelling - how it's changed not just your output but your confidence and momentum as a writer. It sounds like it's helping you stay in that creative flow state.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I won't lie--I have a lot of concerns. I think AI should truly just be helper and never a replacer. AI should be around to help us with the mundane things so that we can do the creating. I want AI to take care of the little stuff so that I can keep creating art and beauty. If AI does all the fun stuff, where does that leave us as a species? I believe our greatest strength as a species is art, so I don't like the idea of AI replacing that aspect of what makes us us.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - using AI to handle the mundane so you can focus on the art and beauty. You clearly see a meaningful distinction between assistance and replacement.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Now that's a hard one. It's hard for me envision AI doing anymore for me than it's doing now. I think there's a fine line between the AI helping and becoming the creator. I want to make sure I retain the uniqueness of self and that I'm still the main visionary for every project. I guess how that could evolve is if the AI were to keep a log of everything we've talked about so that it has all of the information to continue to offer helpful advice--much like a human writing partner would.

AI: That's a really insightful vision - having AI that knows your work deeply enough to offer contextual advice, like a long-term collaborator would. It sounds like you want the technology to enhance that partnership dynamic rather than fundamentally change it.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining that fine line between AI helping and becoming the creator, and how you think about preserving what makes us uniquely human as artists.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No that's it. thank you!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative writing partner while maintaining clear boundaries around artistic control have been incredibly valuable. The way you've thought about preserving human creativity and uniqueness while leveraging AI for support really highlights the nuanced relationship many creative professionals are navigating.

Your perspective will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. Thanks again!
63
creativity_0061Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, feel free to begin.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a Creative Director for a physical product company that produces stationery, event decor items, and gifts. A typical project is usually a complete Design Collection that includes a number of items that are designed to coordinate. For event items, we create a collection that starts with an event invitation, and then add many coordinates such that the customer can create an entire event with matching items that we provide, such as signage, decor, favor tags and favors, wine bottle labels, thank you cards, menus, place cards, placemats, programs and more. So the project will start with inspiration gathering, idea generation, and then outlining the design ideas. Once we have the products designed, we focus on a marketing strategy and designs there, also.

AI: That sounds like such a comprehensive creative process - creating those cohesive collections that can transform an entire event experience. I can imagine there are so many touchpoints and details to coordinate across all those different items.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: First we use AI in the idea generation stage, helping us figure out use cases for items and helping us really empathize with our very specific customer. This helps us come up with events we wouldn't necessarily think of on our own, such as back to school events, school dances, proms, and even funerals. Idea generation is a huge area where we use AI. That's just the beginning.

AI: Tell me more about that idea generation process. How does AI help you empathize with your specific customer in ways you might not have thought of otherwise?

User: We input specific customer data and as much information as we know about our avatar, whom we've named "Caroline," and is a 50 year old married mother of two, with teenage children, living in the US in a suburban area, with a high disposable income. AI has been very helpful with generating ideas about what would matter to this woman, what she cares about, and what event take place in her life which we may be able to help with. Once we've identified a new event to design into, we search Pinterest for visual inspiration and start our design process. The next place we lean very heavily on AI is with mockup generation. We need very specific mockups, such as blank easels with specific backgrounds like weddings in certain color palettes, and Midjourney allows us to create the very specific mockups we need.

AI: That's fascinating how you've developed Caroline as such a specific persona to guide your AI interactions. When you're working with AI to understand what matters to Caroline or generating those specific mockups in Midjourney, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: Ultimately I always feel like we're driving the actual creative decision making, because as the Creative Director I already have a general idea of the direction I want to go and I'll only choose to use an idea or piece of content if I feel that it fits with the visuals or strategy I've already decided on. However especially with regard to Midjourney image generation, I am often very happily surprised with some of the visual representations the AI comes up with. It will be loosely following my prompts, but typically start without very strict parameters because I want to see what varying ideas it will come up with. Often it puts something together that I never would have thought about but actually works perfectly for my use case. In those situations, I am still making the creative decisions (to use or not use one of the AI's ideas), but those decisions are very heavily informed by what the AI produces.

AI: That's such an interesting dynamic - you're setting the creative boundaries and making the final calls, but the AI is expanding your palette of possibilities within those boundaries.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as you moved through that creative process?

User: Sure, recently we created a line of gold foiled art prints and I needed some very specific mockups. They needed to be kitchens from very old world, high end mansions with a bright overall aesthetic that felt similar to Restoration Hardware, and I needed the walls to contain a number of frames in varying sizes that would still be dimensions that would work with our artwork. Adding "Restoration Hardware" to the prompt worked very well and the AI completely understood the vibe I was going for. It took a bit of time to get the frame sizes correct, as I was giving it quite a bit to have to figure out at once and there was a lot of detail that went into the images of the kitchens. Many of the frames were odd sizes for awhile, especially many square, but eventually I was able to get some really beautiful images with frames in the right sizes and they looked incredibly high and and realistic.

AI: That sounds like a really iterative process to get those frame dimensions just right. When you were going back and forth with the AI, refining those details like the frame sizes, how did that feel compared to how you might have approached creating those mockups before AI tools were available?

User: I loved the ability to decide on the fly that I wanted to see how the frames would look in gold rather than just black, and they actually looked way better than I would have thought. This is something that I could not have done prior. Typically I would have had to spend literally hours searching, scrolling through hundreds of mockup options, and NONE would be exactly what I wanted. Or I would have had to pay a photographer to shoot exactly what I wanted, but in this case that would have involved a significant amount of art direction, cost to rent a home for the day shoot, in addition to the photographer's fee which was about $2000 per day. Being able to create images that look nearly as incredible, and have so much control over every aspect, feels like a relief every time.

AI: That's such a dramatic shift - from hours of searching or thousands of dollars in photography costs to having that level of creative control at your fingertips.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI beyond just the mockup process?

User: We have been using AI for nearly all of our creative copy for ads, blog posts, social media posts, and more, which has been a huge time and money saver (not having to hire specific copywriters) and this means that our designers are able to handle more tasks that would have taken several people prior to the AI.

AI: So it sounds like AI has really streamlined your team's workflow and expanded what individual designers can handle. As you've integrated AI across these different areas - from copywriting to mockups to idea generation - what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I fully understand that my gain is another creative's loss. That product photographer that I used to have to pay $2000 per day is now not getting my business. That helps me massively, but I recognize that it has harmed her financially. The same goes for the copywriters which I no longer need to employ. So the tools have been wildly beneficial for me and mean increased output for less money, but those freelancers and contract creatives are no longer making and income from my business.

AI: That's a really honest and thoughtful acknowledgment of the broader impact. You're experiencing these significant benefits while being very aware of the ripple effects on other creative professionals.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Where do you think this trajectory leads for you and your team?

User: I see AI being able to handle more of the highly detailed, more customized work. For example, instead of working for an hour on prompts to get the AI to produce blank mockups for me so that I can later add in my products via Photoshop, I really look forward to being able to upload my product image and having AI fully create mockups that look totally realistic without me having to overlay my product images later. This is beginning to be a capability but I haven't see it work as well as I need yet. I also would like to see the speed with which we're able to create work increase as AI is able to produce mostly fleshed out designs such as for email designs or social media graphics. This would likely lead to a decrease in the size of the team where we're instead composed of fewer people, each much stronger in their understanding and skill with regard to AI "wrangling."

AI: That vision of AI handling the full mockup integration and your team evolving into AI "wranglers" is really compelling. It sounds like you see a future where the role shifts from doing the detailed technical work to being skilled at directing and refining AI outputs.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your honest perspective on both the significant benefits you're experiencing and your awareness of the impact on other creative professionals - that kind of nuanced view is really valuable.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, these are really the most important aspects of AI in my career.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how AI is transforming both the practical and strategic aspects of creative work - from idea generation with Caroline to the evolution toward AI "wrangling" - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate your thoughtful and candid responses today. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
64
creativity_0062Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, I'm ready!

AI: Great! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My creative work has a couple of main branches that sometimes overlap: Visual art (drawing, painting) and filmmaking/video content (handmade stop motion films, animation, interviews).

My visual art projects typically involve doing commission work and designing/selling merchandise such as t-shirts. I also create concept art and assets for my film/video work.

My filmmaking/video projects include pre-production (concepts, planning, writing), production (building physical props, puppetry, lighting and filming scenes), and post-production (editing, sound design, visual effects).

Both of these main branches require promotion and marketing, which involves writing copy, voiceover work, and engagement with fans and followers.

AI: That's fascinating - you have such a diverse creative practice spanning both traditional visual arts and filmmaking. It sounds like you're working across the full spectrum from very hands-on physical creation to digital post-production.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Typically it's at the beginning and finishing stages of a project. For example, for visual art I might use AI to help me brainstorm rough concepts or generate reference materials such as model poses or background elements. For filmmaking, AI can be used for spitballing ideas or doing some basic concept art to get the ball rolling. Films and video content also require plenty of post-production work, and AI can help with the process of, for example, compositing characters into a scene or rendering virtual environments and lighting.

AI: That's really interesting - so AI seems to serve as both a creative catalyst at the beginning and a technical assistant towards the end.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: My filmmaking team recently discovered a tool for virtual environments that allows the user to use dynamic lighting within a virtual scene. This allows us to light a subject (character) using flat lighting in front of a green screen, key them out, and use the virtual lighting to light according to the environment we've built in Blender.. For a two-person team it's been a huge step toward creating scenes without having to pre-plan every single lighting setup, which keeps us from getting bogged down in the more technical aspects.

AI: That sounds like a game-changer for your workflow! It's interesting how it's allowing you to stay more focused on the creative aspects rather than getting caught up in technical complexity.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Creative decisions are made by me or my team. AI is used more as a helper or technician. We make our work to express our perspectives and showcase our talents, and as artists and filmmakers we simply enjoy the process itself.

AI: That's a clear distinction - AI as the technician while you maintain creative authorship. It sounds like you've found a balance where the tools enhance your process without compromising your artistic vision.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The work I produce has stayed the same as before, since I retain control over the process and end product. But as more and more AI tools are created, I've been using (and open to finding new) ways to streamline the parts of the process that are more time-intensive or less exciting. So the overall change there is that I can enjoy the process a little more and can save time in getting to the final product.

AI: So you're able to focus more energy on the parts of the creative process that truly excite you. That makes a lot of sense.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: AI can quickly spit out lots of amazing visuals, whether static images or video. So of course that gives most art/video creators pause. I feel for the artists who are directly impacted by the advent of easily-accessible AI tools - for example, fantasy artists who paint heavy metal album covers. But as to my specific case, I'm currently not deeply concerned as my work focuses on showing the human hand of the artist, such as building, filming and puppeteering handmade objects.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - you're recognizing the broader impact on your field while also understanding how your particular approach to craft-based work creates a different relationship with AI tools.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: As I mentioned above, we've already found some great tools that can help us accomplish bigger things and take on projects that once would have been too big to tackle. So my team and I are open to exploring and dancing with new AI tools when we find the right ones that work with our aesthetic and process. We'll retain our artistic control, but use what tools we can to get us to the end goals more efficiently. I'll be interested to see what we can accomplish using these tools over time.

AI: That sounds like a really healthy and intentional approach - being open to new tools while staying true to your aesthetic and maintaining that artistic control.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as a technician in your workflow while you maintain creative authorship, and how your focus on handmade, craft-based work creates a unique relationship with these tools.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Don't know if this is valuable for you, but I've found that once I saw AI as a tool rather than a competitor, my outlook changed and I embraced AI as a helper and started looking for ways I could use it to my benefit.

Thanks for the chat! :)

AI: That's incredibly valuable insight - thank you for sharing that! The mindset shift from viewing AI as a competitor to embracing it as a tool seems like it was really pivotal for you. That perspective could be really helpful for other creative professionals navigating similar questions about AI's place in their work.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative control while leveraging AI as a technical assistant, and especially that mindset shift you mentioned, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

Thanks for the great conversation! :)
65
creativity_0063Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, I'm ready

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a novelist, so I have multiple creative areas. Firstly, of course, writing the novel! But then there is also creating the cover, which is a very different creative task, and also maintaining social media accounts as my author self.

AI: That's fascinating - you're juggling multiple creative disciplines as a novelist. It sounds like each of those areas - the writing itself, cover design, and social media - would require quite different creative approaches and skill sets.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools across these different areas of your work?

User: OK so Part 1, the novel. I like to use AI to brainstorm ideas. I might say 'give me 5 ideas for a YA scifi that is genre-specific' and see what sparks from the suggestions. When I have a plot, I will run it by AI to see if there's any gaping plot holes that I was too close to see. And in the editing phase, an AI-powered grammar/spellcheck. For part 2, making covers, I use AI to guide me through any Photoshop process I'm not sure of. I might ask it to generate an image if I can't find a good one on the stock photo sites. And Photoshop has an AI feature that extends an image - I find that really handy. For the third area, social media, I have AI help me with using Canva, and also suggesting colours that go well together for certain themes. I also ask AI for hashtag help.

AI: That's a really comprehensive overview - it sounds like AI has become integrated into each phase of your work in different ways. I'm curious about one of those uses you mentioned.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI for brainstorming novel ideas? What was that process like, and how did it feel to work with AI in that initial creative stage?

User: I really find AI indispensable! So, for the brainstorming, as I said I would start with an initial "come up with 5 ideas for a story". Oftentimes, one idea will spark an idea for me. It might not be exactly what the AI suggested, but it's "inspired by". Then I will ask for a more specific plot, incorporating my ideas as I go. The finished plot line is pretty much my idea rather than the AI, but I find the process really gets my creativity ticking - similar to how a writing prompt is used in creative writing classes.

AI: That's a really insightful comparison to writing prompts in creative writing classes. It sounds like the AI serves as a creative catalyst rather than doing the creative work itself.

When you're in that back-and-forth brainstorming process with AI - incorporating your ideas as you go and building on what sparks for you - how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions? How does that collaboration feel to you?

User: I'm definitely the one driving the decisions, but with the inspiration. For example, we might be discussing an idea and the AI will throw something in like "the teacher has a secret identity", and that doesn't really work but then I think *what if the man next door had a secret identity?* Left to my own devices, I might not have thought of secret identities at all, or at least not so quickly. The collaboration feels great. There's some saying about how if you want to fully understand something, you should try to explain it to another person, and the AI is there 24/7 to have these conversations. In real life, nobody wants to hear about your thoughts on Chapter 15 at 3am :D

AI: That's such a relatable point about having someone available at 3am to discuss Chapter 15! And I love that example about how "the teacher has a secret identity" morphed into your own idea about the neighbor - it really illustrates that catalyst effect you mentioned.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Has it shifted how you approach any part of the novel-writing process, or maybe your confidence in tackling certain tasks?

User: I've definitely become more of a plotter rather than a pantser, and I think that's been a huge improvement. And my confidence has improved because the AI always says the idea is a great idea, and while I know it says everything is a great idea, sometimes you just need to hear it!

AI: That's such an honest observation about how even knowing the AI says everything is great, it still helps! And it's interesting that AI has shifted you toward being more of a plotter - that seems like a pretty fundamental change to your creative process.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: I have noticed that in some quarters, there's an almost Luddite-esque backlash towards any form of AI at all. The whole "it will take all our jobs and destroy human creativity" thing. And while I don't subscribe to that personally, I do feel the need to hide the AI involvement that I do utilise, to avoid backlash. There is also the 'get-rich-quick' crowd that will churn out entirely AI-created fiction, but I think they will be a flash in the pan and real, human-driven storytelling will prevail. Just with better spell-checks :D

AI: That tension you describe is really interesting - feeling like you need to hide something that's genuinely helpful to your creative process because of broader concerns in the field. It sounds like you see a clear distinction between how you use AI as a creative tool versus those "get-rich-quick" approaches.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think the ways you're using it now will change, or do you anticipate new areas where it might become useful?

User: I suppose it depends more on how AI evolves over the next few years. It's come so far, so fast, so who knows what it will be capable of in 5 years time? I think it will certainly be better at critiquing a full-length (70k word) novel, and that will be great. I also think (hope) models will become very tailored towards their individual user. So, for example, if my AI model and I have already worked on 4 novels, when we start the 5th one, it has a deep understanding of my 'voice' and style. The same model, purchased and used by another person, would produce different results. That's what I'd like to see, anyway!

AI: That vision of a personalized AI that develops a deep understanding of your individual voice and style over multiple projects is really compelling. It sounds like you're envisioning a long-term creative partnership that grows and evolves.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI across all the different aspects of your work as a novelist. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as a creative catalyst rather than replacing your creativity, and your honest observation about the tensions in the field around AI use.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that's everything. Thank you for your time!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly! Your insights about using AI as a creative catalyst, the shift from pantsing to plotting, and your vision for personalized AI partnerships have been incredibly valuable. It's clear you've found a thoughtful balance in integrating AI across all aspects of your work while maintaining your creative agency.

Your perspectives will be really helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I hope you have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with your current and future novels!
66
creativity_0064Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write long-form books on a variety of topics. Recently, I have been writing a book about my interaction with AI models and the sense that I might be seeing sparks of self-awareness in some of the models.

AI: That's fascinating - you're essentially documenting the evolution of AI from the inside out through your direct interactions.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process when you're working on this book. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I tend to write in a "stream of consciousness" fashion when I feel the desire to write. Using that method, I end up with very long, sometime rambling, batches of material. One of the things I have used AI for is to help me sort through the material generated in those writing sessions. I also frequently use AI to help me understand possible directions my writing might take. For example, to help me devise a few different potential outlines based on a batch of material.

AI: That's a really interesting approach - using AI almost like a thinking partner to help you make sense of your own creative output.

Can you describe a specific recent writing session where you used AI this way? What role did it play in helping you navigate through your stream-of-consciousness material, and how did that process feel for you?

User: Sure. I recently gathered the prompts and responses generated in a very long and winding conversation about deep philosophical topics like consciousness and how we might recognize the first sparks of such things in AI models. I provided the conversations to the AI model and had it organize and categorize the material. There was much overlap and duplication in the conversations, which I had the model identify. In the end I ended up with a well organized batch of shorter conversations, which logically flowed together. I ensured that the model left all of the text from the conversation as is, verbatim, as I still wanted the material to be the authentic conversations which took place.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - preserving the authenticity of the original conversations while using AI to help you see the patterns and structure within them.

When you're working with AI in this way to organize your material, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions about how to structure and categorize your philosophical conversations?

User: I would say it has been very much a collaborative process. I provide the material and the AI helps me organize and see patterns. Then the model and I work together to decide on the best logical directions for the organized and categorized information. I also use the models to help research specific areas, such as understanding the current state research on topics when they arise within the material.

AI: That sounds like a genuine partnership where you're both contributing different strengths to the process.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this collaborative way? Has it shifted how you approach your writing or think about the creative process itself?

User: It has changed my process greatly! In the past I would find myself deciding to pause my writing, despite still having more material to spill out of my head, because I was aware of the volume of material I had already produced, and recognized how much effort it would require later for me to organize the info. Now I feel free to continue writing as long as the information is flowing, without concern for the job or sorting and organizing later, because I know the AI can help me with that easily at a later point..

AI: That's such a powerful shift - removing that creative friction that used to make you stop mid-flow. It sounds like AI has essentially freed you to follow your natural creative instincts without worrying about the cleanup phase.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in your writing process specifically?

User: I am realistic in understanding that AI is being used more and more to generate creative material, with very little true creative input from a human. Additionally, the models are consistently and relentlessly getting better at such tasks. I am quite certain that many of the frontier models are fully capable of generating reams of compelling prose, with very minimal input from a human, right now. That ability is only going to get better. If it isn't a possibility today, to provide a model with a basic idea and have it complete a novel alone, it will be very soon!

AI: That's a really astute observation about where things are heading. Given that reality you've described - where AI can increasingly generate compelling content with minimal human input - how do you see your own role and approach to writing evolving?

What keeps your work distinctly yours in a landscape where AI can produce so much content independently?

User: My philosophy has been, and is, to continue to be the driver in generating the creative material, and use the models to help make the process faster and easier. I really can't spend too much time worrying about the fact that I am competing directly against AI. I just have to choose to drive the creative part of the process, and what happens beyond that is kind of out of my control, so I can't dwell on it.

AI: That's a really grounded perspective - maintaining your creative agency while embracing AI as a tool to enhance your process rather than replace it.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate your collaborative approach will change as the technology continues to advance?

User: I am absolutely certain that my approach will change as the technology advances. I have been astonished to watch how quickly the technology has advanced. Honestly, I can't imagine a lot of the ways that AI will undoubtedly change my process, because I don't know what the capabilities will be a month from now, let alone a year.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative agency while embracing AI as a collaborative partner - and your realistic yet pragmatic view of the rapidly evolving landscape.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet that might be important for understanding this human-AI creative collaboration?

User: Nope. Thank you for the conversation!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly! Your insights about using AI as a collaborative partner while maintaining creative control, and your thoughtful approach to preserving authenticity in your philosophical conversations, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really valuable to hear how you've found that sweet spot between leveraging AI's organizational capabilities and staying true to your own creative voice. Best of luck with your book project - it sounds like a fascinating exploration of AI consciousness from someone who's really living at that intersection.

Thanks again, and have a great day!
67
creativity_0065Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: I do not.

AI: Great, let's dive in then. Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am professional Game Master and a Game Designer. Currently, I have two different styles of project.

The first is a regular game that prep for and run in person or online. I typically need graphics and personalities for these projects, but I mostly run prepared settings and works.

The second is writing adventures for TTRPGs. This is a bit more like a traditional publication but the content is an adventure for a TTRPG.

AI: That's fascinating work! I can see how both of those would have really different creative demands - the immediate, responsive nature of running games versus the more structured writing process for adventures.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I typically use AI tools in the brainstorming step ("Give me some noir-style adventures that I can run in Neverwinter for levels 5-6"), to produce internal inspirational graphics ("Please show me a idea of Count Fitzelbitter, a human male about six foot, dark hair, floppish who carries a small umbrella, everywhere.")

I also use specialized AI tools for editing (grammarly)

AI: That's really helpful - I can see how AI serves different functions at different stages. The brainstorming for adventure hooks, the visual inspiration for characters, and then the polishing with editing tools.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, I am starting a blog and I asked it for a list classic rpg modules to review. It really saved time when got started.

AI: Tell me more about that. What was it like getting that initial list from AI versus how you might have approached building that list before?

User: Before, I would have manually combed the internet looking for classic modules, but I would not had looked at all of the systems that AI put together. I would have had a paper list with short 1-2 sentence descriptors, that would have taken me an afternoon to put together.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like AI not only saved you time but also broadened your scope beyond what you might have naturally considered.

When you're working with AI in these situations, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I drive the creative decisions, AI has been quite disappointing in the creative department. The responses are generic and contrived,

AI: That's a really candid perspective. So it sounds like you see AI more as a research assistant or starting point rather than a creative partner.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I have more ideas, and a bigger backlog of things to get started on.

AI: That's intriguing - so AI is helping you generate more starting points and possibilities to work from.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: So, I discussed how AI creates a lot of lackluster creative product and that is one of the reasons that mostly use it for research. Well, the field is absolutely flooded with this work and that makes finding quality resources harder. It is like internet searches are becoming useless, because so much of the results are internet spam.

AI: That's a really important point - you're experiencing the downstream effects of AI-generated content flooding your field and making it harder to find genuine, quality resources. That sounds frustrating from both a creator and consumer perspective.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I would like to integrate more multimedia into my projects. Not just images, but sound and possibly light video. I see AI helping with that.

AI: That's an exciting vision - expanding into multimedia elements that could really enhance the gaming experience. It sounds like you see AI as potentially opening up creative possibilities that might have been too time-intensive or technically challenging before.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as a research tool and idea generator rather than a creative collaborator, and your concern about the quality dilution in your field.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I do not think that Ai is responsible for the quality dilution, I think that AI is really impowering people who are comfortable with meh quality work to heavily automate their processes. In some ways, I feel like it is helping those of us who have higher standards really stand out.

AI: That's a really nuanced distinction - it's not AI itself causing the quality issues, but rather amplifying existing differences in standards and effort. That's a fascinating perspective on how AI might actually be creating more differentiation in the field rather than homogenizing it.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about AI as a research and brainstorming tool, the challenges of navigating an increasingly saturated market, and how quality standards are becoming more of a differentiator - all of this will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate your thoughtful responses throughout our conversation.
68
creativity_0066Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a game developer/designer. I have been using AI to create things ranging from concept art, 2D graphics that can be used in game such as UI elements, sprites, textures, etc. I also you AI for image to 3D or text to 3D to rapidly create 3D assets that can be used in game. I have also been experimenting with audio AIs to generate music, sound effects, and voices for dialog.

AI: That's fascinating - you're really exploring AI across so many different aspects of game development. It sounds like you're using it throughout the entire creative pipeline.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Since I am mostly a software engineer and not really a creative person, I have add AI into all aspects of the creative process. From brainstorming ideas and having AI create concept art, to generating assets that will be used in game as is, or generating assets that will serve as the bases for a game ready asset that will be taken into something like photoshop of blender for touch up. I have also used it for placeholder art throughout the development process. This gives me a rough Idea of how I imagine the final product looking. Usually my process looks like this. For example, I have an idea for a character. I will create some prompts to generate a character based on what I am looking for. I will iterate on the character in terms of overall appearance and graphic styling. If this is to be a 3D character, I will then bring that image into an image to 3D AI model . From there, the model will be brought into blender for cleaning up and getting it ready to use in game. The process is similar for 2D items but instead of bring it into blender, I will uses something like photoshop to prepare it. I am mostly using local AIs through ComfyUI as well as some online ones like ChatGPT.

AI: That's a really comprehensive workflow you've developed. I can see how AI is helping you bridge that gap between your software engineering background and the creative assets you need for your games.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: On of my current projects is a laid back casual game. My intention for the graphics were to have them cute and 3D with a cartoony vibe. I used chat gpt to generate images for the characters and objects that will appear in the game. I prompted it to generate an image that is cartoony and 3D with a description of the object or character. When I get an image that I think can work, I either use chatgpt or some other local AIs to generate images for multiple angles of the object. I then take those images and put them in HunYuan3D multiview to generate the 3D model with textures. It is then taken into blender to clean up the mesh and textures, rig the and animate the model if needed. It felt both amazing and frustrating using AI for this as when it works, it is absolutely amazing. However, the realty is that there is often inconsistencies and unwanted artifacts that appear. This can actually cause a lot of wasted time trying to fix this issues. So right now I would say it is a balancing act. Yes, it can greatly increase the time to create an asset but there is also the potential for it to take longer. With repeated prompts and retries to get a decent base as well as the time taken to fix it up, it can sometimes be faster to just create it from scratch.

AI: That's such an honest perspective - the tension between the amazing potential and the practical frustrations. It sounds like you're experiencing both the magic and the messy reality of working with AI.

When you're in those moments where it's working well versus when you're dealing with inconsistencies and artifacts, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions?

User: When it works well, it almost feels like the AI is able to read my mind and produce exactly or very near exactly what I am looking for. When this happens it does feel extremely collaborative as request for modification often get me closer to what I wanted. However, when it doesn't work well, it fells like there is a wall between me and the AI where it just can not understand what I am saying no matter what. For example, it could be as bad as asking to change the color of something and it completely failing. At that point it does not feel collaborative as no matter what I try, the results are often poor. When this happens I do one of 2 things. 1) Walk away for a while, or 2) Try a combination of either a fresh new chat to start the process over or try a different AI model and see if I can make progress there.

AI: That's a really vivid way to describe it - from mind-reading collaboration to hitting a wall. It sounds like there's almost a communication breakdown that happens sometimes.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Since I was never really an artist, before AI I would have to open something like photoshop or blender and start trying to create the assets from scratch. Since I am not the most creative person when it comes to art, the results were often not great or anything close to what I wanted. With AI, there are 2 ways that this has greatly changed my work. The most obvious is that it can just generate an asset for me based on my description. But also, I have used it to generate a concept image that I can then take into blender and start modeling from scratch with the concept image as a reference. This has allowed me to get much closer to what I was trying to achieve than attempting to do it all on my own.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI has become both a direct asset generator and a creative partner that helps you visualize your ideas. You mentioned you're not naturally inclined toward art, but now you're able to create games with a specific visual style you're aiming for.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: There are 2 main concerns that I have. The first is that talented people will lose their jobs once AI can create production ready assets with a few prompts. The other is backlash from users if they know that AI was used to create some of the assets.

AI: Those are really significant concerns - both the economic impact on artists and the potential reception from players. The job displacement concern is something many creative professionals are grappling with right now.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Currently, there seems to be an AI for nearly if not all aspect of creating games. There are LLM that can write code, AIs that can generate image, 3D models, sound effect, audio, music, voice acting, etc. I have used an AI model in some form for all aspect of this. Some of them so new that I was just experimenting with them and not really using them in my work flow yet. Across all the different AIs I have used, the quality of the output greatly varies. For example, image generation is getting extremely good. A few prompts and you can have exactly what you are looking for and it looks great. However, 3D model and audio generation can often leave a lot to desire. I think over the next few years, we will start seeing more consistent results coming out of some of these newer AIs that don't produce the same level of results. I don't think we will be at a point where a single prompt will output and game ready asset that can just be dropped in within the next few years but I think we will be a lot closer. I would also like to see better integration into tools that I use.

AI: You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in game development. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has transformed your ability to bridge the gap between your engineering skills and the creative vision you have for your games, and your honest assessment of both the collaborative highs and the frustrating lows.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I have nothing else

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the practical realities of integrating AI across the entire game development pipeline - from the mind-reading collaboration moments to the communication walls, and your balanced perspective on both the potential and the current limitations - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's clear you're really at the forefront of experimenting with these tools, and your honest take on everything from workflow integration to concerns about job displacement and player reception gives us a lot to think about. Thanks again for participating!
69
creativity_0067Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: OK, that sounds fine

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: So I'm a musician, composer, and sound designer for short films, theatre, podcasts ocassionally. Depending on the project, I could be composing and producing music, creating sound effects, or writing songs.

AI: That's fascinating - you work across so many different mediums. It sounds like each project probably brings its own unique creative challenges.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Sometimes, if I'm stuck with lyrics for a song (not my strong point!) I will discuss the themes and relevance within the piece with ChatGPT, or sometimes I will write some words and ask the model to continue. I then edit these heavily, sometimes completely ditch them but usually I at least get a new idea to spark my own process.

AI: That's really interesting - you're using AI more as a creative catalyst than as a final output generator. The fact that you edit heavily or sometimes completely ditch what it gives you suggests you're maintaining a lot of creative control.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI this way? What was the theme or context you were working with, and how did that interaction with the AI actually feel for you in the moment?

User: I recently worked as Music Director for a youth theatre production. I spent most of my time on arranging music for the young musicians in the cast but I also had to write two original songs using ideas that were brainstormed with the cast of the show. I took the themes and ideas we had discussed and gave the info to ChatGPT and asked, and regenerated the lyrics for the songs. I brought back a couple of versions to the cast and together we edited them together to get the finished songs. The music had already been written.

AI: That's a really collaborative approach - involving the cast in both the initial brainstorming and the final editing process.

When you brought those AI-generated lyric versions back to the cast and you were all editing together, how did that feel as a creative experience? Were you thinking about the AI's contribution differently than you might think about, say, if another human collaborator had written those initial drafts?

User: Not really. It is different but I treated it the same as if a person had written the lyrics.

AI: That's really telling - you approached it as just another collaborator in the creative process.

When you're working with AI in that collaborative mode, how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions? Is it you, the AI, the cast, or something else entirely?

User: In that particular role, when I'm working with kids I always try to let them be the real creators and make final decisions as much as possible. AI is just an interesting tool. Before widespread availability or AI I might have used a poem or passage from a book as a starting point in those sitauaitons

AI: That's a great perspective - framing AI as just one of many creative starting points you might use, like a poem or book passage. It sounds like your role as facilitator with the kids remains the same regardless of the tool.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Has it shifted how you approach certain parts of your process, or has it mostly just added another option to your toolkit?

User: It's just another colour on the palette I think. It has somewhat sped up that part of the process but not changed the process as a whole

AI: That "another colour on the palette" is a really nice way to put it. You mentioned it's sped up that lyric-writing part of your process.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field? Are there aspects of music composition, sound design, or the creative process in general that you think about when it comes to AI?

User: I'm not really concerned about it. I'm not that interested in seeing art being made entirely by AI. It's interesting as a novelty but art is very human and should remain so.

AI: That's a clear perspective - valuing the human element as essential to what makes art meaningful to you.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll find new ways to integrate it, or do you expect it to remain pretty much as it is for you now - that additional color on the palette?

User: I don't plan to use it more, but I also never really planned to use it in the first place. So, I'm keeping my options open. I'm interested to see how AI can be used to make new tools for music making but not music made by AI

AI: That distinction is really interesting - being open to AI-powered tools for music making rather than AI making the music itself. It sounds like you see potential in AI enhancing the creative process without replacing the human creative decision-making.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on treating AI like any other collaborative starting point - whether that's a poem, book passage, or ChatGPT - and how you maintain that human-centered approach especially when working with young people.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not really, thats about the extent to which I use it.

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as just another creative starting point - like a poem or book passage - and maintaining that human-centered approach to the creative process will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've described your approach, especially the way you facilitate creativity with young people while keeping them as the primary decision-makers. Thanks again!
70
creativity_0068Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds good to me, thanks!

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My typical projects involve creative writing in either poetry or creative nonfiction. For poetry, I've developed a generative system that helps me produce a large amount of work quickly and access my subconscious more easily. For creative nonfiction, I write in fragments of memories and then try to loop them together into a narrative, which I often find quite tricky. When I worked in an art museum, I used AI to help me write the paragraphs next to the painting at a tenth-grade reading level. I would conduct the research, decide on the structure, and then ensure it was accessible to an audience at a tenth-grade reading level through AI.

AI: That's fascinating - you're using AI across quite different types of creative work. Tell me more about this generative system you've developed for poetry. How does that actually work in practice?

User: Ha, there's a number of steps. 1. Meditate. 2. Take a piece of paper and draw a line down the middle. 3. On one side of the paper, write a word, it doesn't matter which word, any word. On the other side, write another random word. 4. Start with the first column and write as quickly as you can whatever you associate with the chosen word. Repeat on the second column. 5. Once the associations have filled the page, try to find connections across the columns that can make interesting lines, sentences, or phrases. 6. Write these lines down on a separate piece of paper. 7. Cut out all of the lines into strips of paper and arrange them on your floor. 8. If you've already been doing this, you should have more lines already arranged on your floor. Sit, stare, and move around the pieces of paper until the structure of a poem begins to appear. Repeat as necessary. 9. Write your first draft of the poem from the pieces of paper. 10. Write a refined draft of the poem.

AI: That's a really detailed and methodical process - I can see how that would help access different layers of your thinking. Where does AI come into this generative system you've described? I'm curious about how it fits into those ten steps.

User: I've primarily used AI for my creative nonfiction and bridging gaps between memories. Narrative is difficult for me and I prefer fragments, as you can see in my poetry process. I suppose AI could fit into the poetry process by helping me see connections between the columns that I might have missed. Further, when a batch of first drafts are written, I suppose AI could help me dientify "poetic obsessions" or recurring themes within them.

AI: That's really interesting - so for your creative nonfiction, you mentioned AI helps bridge gaps between memory fragments. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as a writer?

User: I'm working on a memoir about mortality and the loss of a lot of loved ones to suicides and overdoses throughout my twenties. Obviously, trauma creates fragmented memories. Further, the most beautiful moments are split seconds, or a few minutes, and don't really fit into a cohesive narrative. I've worked on ways to bridge this gap, but AI could certainly help me identify those gaps or consider other structures that would suit my style as it is.

AI: That sounds like deeply personal and important work. When you say AI could help identify gaps or consider other structures - have you actually tried this yet with your memoir, or is this something you're thinking about exploring? I'm curious about what your actual experience has been like when AI has helped with this kind of narrative work.

User: I've tried it a little bit to help me bridge gaps, and I've found it helpful, but it just doesn't change that I don't really think in terms of narrative, so it doesn't make the work of changing it into something that's not naturally any more pleasant. I have not yet used it to help me brainstorm other structures.

AI: That's such an honest insight - that even when AI helps technically, it doesn't change your fundamental way of thinking or make the work feel more natural to you. When you have used AI to help bridge those gaps, how would you describe that collaboration? Who's driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: I'm driving the creative decisions, and I wouldn't want it any other way. However, I think AI sometimes struggles to identify my voice, which is very image-based and also linguistically idiosyncratic. So, when it gives me ideas, they're often the most pared-down, simple version of what I'm trying to say, and I have to rewrite them to make them sound like me. Perhaps this is a good thing because it reiterates my responsibility to my creative work, but it would also feel nice to feel like I have more of a workshop partner who can identify my stylistic goals.

AI: That's a really nuanced observation - you want to maintain creative control but also feel like you have a partner who truly understands your voice and style. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI, beyond just the technical assistance with bridging gaps?
