User: That's a difficult question to answer because I've taken a much-needed pause from both my book and poetry because I burned myself out creatively. Right now, I focus on journaling and observing, and who knows, maybe that will enter these projects. If AI could learn to read my horrible handwriting, maybe it could help organize my very fragmented journal entries.

AI: It sounds like you're in a thoughtful reflective period right now. Given your experiences so far - both the helpful aspects and the limitations you've described - what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in creative writing and your field more broadly?

User: Well, here's the paradox. Right now, AI writes primarily one-size-fits-all agreeable work, and when asked to imitate a writer such as James Joyce, it absolutely fails to. I think, on one hand, this is a good thing because it puts higher value on more idiosyncratic writers than there are at present. Lots of humans write one-size-fits-all books, but I feel that in the next five to ten years, that will become less acceptable, whether written by humans or AI. However, as I mentioned, it would be ideal if AI could become my creative partner and understand my goals sufficiently to help me maintain my voice throughout the editing process. I think that the highest benefit of it could be to get the work polished enough to submit to agents and publishers.

AI: That's a fascinating paradox you've identified - AI potentially making distinctive voices more valuable while also having the potential to become a better creative partner for developing those unique voices. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years, especially as you potentially return to your book and poetry projects?

User: As I have this conversation, I may experiment with uploading my poetry chapbook and seeing where it might not land with readers. It's based on my getting robbed at gunpoint and the experiences afterwards. However, I find AI can be too validating (wow! this is great! it should be published right now!) when that's not the reality of responses from agents and poetry contests. Perhaps if it had more knowledge of successful books and could identify the threads through them, it would help me get a better grasp on what's being looked for in recent contests and publishing deals.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI might make idiosyncratic voices more valuable while also the challenge of finding that balance between helpful assistance and maintaining your authentic voice.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the paradoxes and nuances of working with AI as a creative writer - from the challenge of maintaining your distinctive voice to the potential for AI to help navigate the publishing landscape - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate your honesty about both the possibilities and limitations you've encountered. Best of luck with your creative work, whether that's continuing with journaling and observation or eventually returning to your memoir and poetry projects.
71
creativity_0069Assistant: Hi there, and thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great. I do not have any questions right now. What do you have for me?

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work creatively in a few different ways. I do a lot of writing, but mostly in the areas of copywriting, email and text message writing, and sometimes even proposals. A typical project can involve taking information from other sources (transcripts, articles, emails, you name it) extracting relevant information, and presenting it in a clear and informative way. Depending on the project, the presentation can be customer-facing, client/lead facing, or be used internally to take away unneccessary information to give my boss what she needs. (Think almost like a TLDR.) The output varies. Sometimes I require bullets, spreadsheets, emails, documents, and even images. Do you have any other questions about my work?

AI: That's really helpful context - it sounds like you're doing a lot of information synthesis and communication across different formats and audiences.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: AI is most helpful in extracting important information. I can tell it what I'm looking for and it will take a large amount of data and give me what is most important from it. This saves tons of time. It is also useful in helping me write copy in a way that conveys a message quickly so I'm not sitting stuck on wording or questioning whether or not what I am trying to say to someone "makes sense." When the AI writes it, I can read it and if it doesn't make sense to me, then we have a conversation until it does, rather than me attempting that conversation with myself and getting confused. AI also helps me brainstorm, finding different ways to look at a problem and come up with solutions faster than if I were to stare at something that has me stumped. It basically takes a lot of the pressure off so that I can provide results to people instead of presenting more problems.

AI: That's a really interesting way to put it - using AI to have that conversation externally rather than getting stuck in your own head.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I have an ongoing situation that I use ChatGPT for. Every day I keep track of the things I did during the day. I have a conversation in ChatGPT specifically devoted to telling it what I did for the day and it helps me identify action items for the next day. One thing it also does is to catch anything that may be slipping through the cracks and reminds me to follow up. For example, if I told it on Monday that I emailed [REDACTED] about X, but it hasn't seen anything regarding [REDACTED] on my list for 3 days, it will give me an action item of following up with [REDACTED]. I may not have thought to follow up with [REDACTED] if my attention was elsewhere, so this method has been helping me to not let anything slip through.

AI: That's fascinating - you've essentially created an AI-powered accountability and follow-up system for yourself.

When you're working with AI in situations like this daily tracking, or when you're extracting information from documents, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am absolutely driving the creative decision. This is the only way a person can get quality results from AI. I have always equated AI to training a new employee. The more specific you are with what you need, the better your results. So, I start by telling it what we're doing, what I need, what I want from it, etc. If I like the results, I move on. I may do some personal tweaking that I don't need its help with. But if I don't like the results, I identify what I don't like, tell it what's going on and ask it to give me new results that align with what I'm looking for. It may take a few tries, but we ususally get there. This took some trial and error to understand how to do, but it was valuable time well spent. I still feel like I am in control but getting the help and relief I need from AI.

AI: That's a great analogy - training a new employee. It sounds like you've developed a really intentional approach to directing the AI while still getting that relief from it.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think I understand what you are asking. But for me, I think using AI has changed my definition of what it means to do creative work. I have gone through a period of struggling with the "inauthenticity" of the finished product. And I'm not saying there isn't an argument to be had that using AI can cause us to fall into this inauthenticity grey area. But creative processes are changing with the use of AI. Even before I was using AI, much of what I did was really only a copy of what someone else did. Blog post structures were all the same. Email structures and sentiments were the same. Making a chart in a spreadsheet is the same. The only thing I can do now that I didn't have the skillset before is image generation (and even that is something acceptable - nothing that will replace a skilled graphic designer anytime soon.) So, I'm not sure how this answers the question. What aspects have changed? In a way, in my specific industry, it kind of exposes how formulaic everything can really be and even though we like to think everything depends on us, it really doesn't. However, it is up to us to take the responsibility for the output, as I kind of mentioned in the last answer. I hope that helps.

AI: That's a really thoughtful and honest reflection. You've touched on something quite profound - how AI has revealed the formulaic nature of much creative work while also raising questions about authenticity. It sounds like you've worked through some of those initial concerns about inauthenticity.

What concerns, if any, do you still have about AI's presence in your field?

User: There are a lot of concerns regarding AI replacing people and people losing their jobs. I try not to focus on doomsday scenarios, but I also acknowledge that these concerns aren't completely unfounded. Especially in this early adoption phase. In fact, I already had one client replace me as her blog writer in favor of ChatGPT, which I believe to be a mistake. (For the creative control reasons we discussed before.) So, my biggest concerns are probably the early adopters. Getting too eager to implement AI into every nook and cranny just to save a buck in labor just to realize they've created a mess on their hands that they will have to unravel. But in the meantime, they have done harm to real people by getting rid of jobs and these people will have to figure out how to rebuild their lives while we wait for the pendulum swing.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - acknowledging both the real immediate impact on people's livelihoods and the potential for overcorrection by early adopters who might not fully understand what they're giving up in terms of creative control and quality.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Looking at the next few years, I think I partially already answered this question. I think that there will be more and more adoption. Those who are resisting it will start to adopt it but it will be adopted in an ignorant and irresponsible way until people understand that it should be used to enhance, not flat out replace. But at the same time, if someone wants to not be replaced, they should also figure out how to responsibly adopt AI and how to enhance the irreplacable human qualities of their jobs. (Relationship building, anticipating needs, things like that.)

AI: That makes a lot of sense - emphasizing those uniquely human elements like relationship building and anticipating needs that can't really be automated.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has revealed the formulaic nature of much creative work, and your analogy of training AI like a new employee while maintaining creative control.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so.

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative control, the responsibility that comes with directing AI output, and your thoughtful perspective on both the opportunities and challenges of early AI adoption will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about everything from the authenticity questions you worked through to the real-world impact you've seen with that client situation. These kinds of authentic experiences are exactly what help us understand how this technology is actually being used in practice.

Thanks again, and have a great rest of your day!
72
creativity_0070Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, let's go

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a TV writer. Most recently (the past 3 years) that has been in development. I pitch an idea for an original TV series to buyers, then they pay me to develop and write the pilot episode. The hope is that it would continue to be developed into a produced pilot and then a series, but I have not made it that far yet with my original projects.

AI: That's fascinating - the development process sounds both exciting and challenging. So you're creating these original series concepts from scratch and then crafting that crucial first episode.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI for research both at the brainstorming stage and then in a more granular way when I need specifics for a scene

AI: Tell me more about that research process. What kinds of things are you researching, and how does using AI for that feel different from other research methods you might have used before?

User: Other research methods involved mostly web searches, but occasionally I would read books on a subject. Previous pitch I sold was about quantum computing, and I didn't use AI at all in my research. I watched a lot of educational videos on YouTube, read articles I searched for on the internet, and read a novel where quantum computing was an element of the storytelling for context. On my current project, using AI, my research time is cut down significantly. It's a legal drama and I don't have any formal legal training, so I use to ask how certain legal processes work -- the name of motions, or order of events in a lawsuit. I will run hypotheticals by it to if my imagined scenario has any way of working in reality or if there are any real world examples to back it up.

AI: That's really interesting how dramatically it's changed your research efficiency. When you're running these legal hypotheticals by AI or asking about processes, how do you handle the question of accuracy? Do you find yourself double-checking the information, or has your trust in it evolved over time?

User: Especially with legal things, I feel I have to check much more frequently. I'm sure you've heard about actual lawyers who use AI to draft briefs and it's full of made up caselaw. I've caught many hallucinations in my research (not just legal, but it seems to be more common in that area for some reason). When I've settled on something through my AI research, I will do traditional research of Googling or reaching out to an expert like a friend who's a lawyer to double check things. I wouldn't want to waste that friend's time checking dozens of ideas when I haven't settled on the one that's going to end up in the script yet.

AI: That's a smart approach - using AI to narrow down the field before bringing in human expertise. You mentioned catching hallucinations fairly regularly. When that happens, how does it affect your workflow in the moment? Do you find yourself adjusting how you interact with the AI, or does it just become part of the process you expect?

User: It's very annoying. I hate the anthropomorphizing of technology. If it makes it easier to use/interact with (like using natural language instead of code) that's great. But I don't need an AI to apologize to me when I catch it making stuff up. I need it to do better. When I catch a hallucination and tell the model something like "I just looked that up and it's wrong," the response is often something like "Oh my gosh! You're so right, and I apologize for my mistake..." And so then I wonder, if all it took was me saying it was wrong (with no cited source) for it to reverse, then why doesn't the model just ask itself if it's wrong before giving ANY answer, and check itself? Or even worse, it makes me wonder if the reversal/apology is wrong (maybe the original thing was ultimately true and my fact-checking was the part that was wrong), and the model's just trying to make me want to keep using it. The way it handles mistakes and the certainty with which it presents information is very disconcerting.

AI: That uncertainty you're describing sounds really frustrating - especially when you're trying to build a solid foundation for your creative work. It sounds like the AI's conversational style actually undermines your confidence in the information rather than helping.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that whole experience feel for you as a writer?

User: I'm also writing a feature screenplay on the side -- it's an action/adventure in the vein of the Mission Impossible franchise. I came up with a really clever idea for a setpiece -- a heist in a world-famous location -- but then suddenly worried if I was ripping off a scene from an already existing Mission Impossible movie, so I asked an AI model if Mission Impossible ever had any sequences set in that location, and it said it did and mentioned the exact movie, described the characters in the scene, and how it played into the plot. Now, I've seen all the movies, but it's possible I'd forgotten this... so I asked at what minute-mark the scene took place, the AI told me, then I watched the movie at that point and the scene the AI described didn't exist. In my own subsequent research, I read the script and the scene did not exist there either. At no point in any Mission Impossible movie was there a scene set at this location, nor was the location even mentioned in passing. So that was an example of AI being incredibly unhelpful in my research.

AI: Wow, that's such a perfect example of how the hallucination problem can actually create more work instead of saving time. You ended up having to do the research you were trying to avoid anyway, plus deal with the false alarm.

When you're working with AI on your current legal drama project, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions when you're using it for research and brainstorming?

User: I never ask AI for dramatic or character input. I deliberately keep a boundary there for a few reasons: 1) That's the fun part for me -- it's why I'm a writer. Why would I outsource that? 2) I do not trust that AI wouldn't just pitch me something that exactly exists in a TV show, film, or book, and I just haven't seen before but someone who reads my script might identify. 3) Using AI for art is garbage. It's a highly advanced plagiarizer that is creating for an audience that has feelings, not from feelings itself. It could be good, it could even be enough one day, but I don't want to consume my art from something that can't feel so I certainly wouldn't use something that can't feel for the creative part of my art.

AI: That's a really clear delineation you've drawn - using AI as a research tool but keeping the creative heart of the work entirely in your own hands. It sounds like you see a fundamental difference between information gathering and the actual creative expression.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI for that research side of things?

User: What do you mean?

AI: I'm curious about the broader impact on how you work. For instance, has the speed of research changed how you approach developing ideas? Or has having this research tool affected the kinds of projects you're willing to take on, or how you structure your time during development?

User: With AI, I feel more confident writing about specific areas of expertise that I have no training or expertise myself. It's a backstop that I can use to test ideas and see if they're remotely feasible without having to commit a ton of time and energy into learning the subject ahead of time. Once I've settled into a world or idea for the show, that's when I do the deeper research, but AI helps me rule out concepts or subjects that just won't work much faster.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - it's like having a quick feasibility filter before you invest deeply in an idea. You mentioned earlier some concerns about accuracy and hallucinations. Beyond those technical issues, what other concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: As I mentioned before, I am concerned that AI will replace human artists. It's already good enough to replace voice actors pretty much. I do think a human still gives a better performance than an AI voice, but the AI voice is still convincing (most people wouldn't know it's not human), it's just not as inspired. I'm worried the capitalist profit machine will create such an abundance of this inferior AI voice work that people get so used to it, they forget that they ever cared about the other. Their standards fall and AI becomes the norm not because it's better, but because it's cheaper and "good enough." I feel that could happen with creative writing as well. We're all frogs in a boiling pot and careers will have been ended and industries folded before we realize it's too late to bring them back. AI should be used to make life easier -- it should do the jobs people don't want to do. I don't want or need AI to write a poem or make a movie or create a painting -- there is more human art and human artists in the world than I possibly have time to experience in my life, so I'll spend my time enjoying those things before I seek out an AI's work.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective on the broader implications beyond just your own workflow. The "good enough" versus inspired distinction you're making seems crucial.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll maintain those same boundaries you've set, or do you imagine your approach might shift?

User: Even before AI, cheap, garbage content has been eating away at the legacy entertainment business. TikTok and Instagram videos that people mindlessly watch for hours are pulling eyeballs from expensive, well produced film and TV projects. AI is already being used to create those types of social media video posts that just scroll endlessly with some ostensibly interesting piece of information or joke or observation. Right now vertical videos are the new gold rush -- those are still human created, but the content is such garbage, I fear it's creating a class of consumer with low enough standards that if the whole thing got replaced by AI, they wouldn't really notice or care. I don't think legacy media is going to transition fully to AI, but I do think it will be cannibalized by it.

AI: Those are all really insightful observations you've shared about your experiences with AI in your creative work. I particularly appreciated your perspective on drawing clear boundaries between research assistance and creative expression, and your thoughts on how "good enough" content might gradually shift industry standards.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative boundaries while leveraging AI as a research tool, and your concerns about the broader impact on industry standards, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated how thoughtfully you've navigated using AI where it serves you while protecting what you see as the essential human elements of your craft. Thanks again for participating!
73
creativity_0071Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: no, that all sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a novelist and also a freelance content and copy writer. I use AI far more in my work as a content writer than when working on my novels. I work for various clients across a variety of topics but a typical project would be working for a hotel chain locate in a certain country who would ask me to write a series of articles about tourism in that area. Some such articles might cover: when is the best time to travel to said country, Some top tips for travelling there with children or pets, What are the best festivals and local events to catch in that country, etc.

AI: That's really helpful context - so you're working across both long-form creative writing and more commercial content creation. I'm curious about that distinction you mentioned between how you use AI in your content writing versus your novels.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process when you're working on those tourism articles for clients. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI (generally ChatGPT) in the early stages of the article process. I first ask the model the basic premise of my article, I ask it to list the top ten festivals for tourists in France or to give me 20 tips for travelling to Portugal with children or when is the best time to visit Bali and why?
I will then use this information as a jumping off point for my own research, looking up each point that the model made, fact checking it and adding more information as I find it.

Once I have all my research done and I have done a rough first draft, of the articles body, I tend to put that draft into a chat and ask the model to create an introductory paragraph, I do this because I always struggle to come up with the first lines of an article, and staring at a blank page is not helpful, once the model has given me a starting point the rest of the article flows much more easily. However I always rewrite that introduction myself during my edits so that no part of the article is AI written as many of my clients prohibit this.

I may also feed the finished article into a chat and ask the model to create a except of around 60 words, an SEO-optimised tag line and a tweet linking to the article. I do this because the model will often pull out points that I had not realised were key and I can then blend the model and my own highlights to create strong links.

I have custom preferences set for my chats so that the model knows the clents preferese tone and style and what the end goal for the article is.

AI: That's fascinating - you've developed a really structured approach where AI serves as a starting point and support tool, but you maintain control over the final content. I love that detail about using AI to overcome the blank page problem with introductions.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI in this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as you moved through that process?

User: In another project for a tech comparison site I was editing a series of articles comparing very similar tech products. part of my role was to add in pros and cons for each of the products in each of the articles. It was very repetitive work and when doing it myself I struggled to come up with varied pro/cons for each product each time, especially since the article and research wasn't my own so I did not know the products all that well.
I fed the almost complete articles into a chat and asked the model to come up with 3 different pros and cons for each product mentions using information from the articles and the models knowledge of the products as a whole, stressing that each one should be different from the others and the model should avoid using generic information that could apply to all of them.
Again the preferences were set so the model knew which tone and language to use.
I found this method sped up my editing process considerable. I had to fact check some of the information but doing so was much faster than me struggling to create 50+ individually varied pro/cons on my own.

AI: That sounds like it was a real game-changer for that repetitive but important task. When you were working through that process - feeding the articles in, getting the varied pros and cons back, then fact-checking - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what was driving the creative decisions in that workflow?

User: I was driving the creative decisions. I spent a substantial amount of time crafting the ideal prompt for the model so it knew exactly what I was looking for and what needed to be done. I included rules on where the information was to come from, the language and tone to use, the length of the responses, some words and phrases to avoid, and the exact format of the answers. I corrected the model if it did not follow all the instructions and modified and reran the prompt if necessary to get appropriate answers. I then fact-checked and edited every response before including anything in the live articles.

AI: That really comes through - you clearly maintained control over the entire process and were very intentional about how you directed the AI. You mentioned earlier that you use AI much more in your content writing than in your novels.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: As I mentioned earlier, I use AI to get a jump start on my tasks. Starting a job from scratch can feel a little overwhelming, and knowing I have a tool that can give me personalised research and help me get started takes away some of the reluctance to tackle a task.

In my novel writing, I do not use AI for anything to do with story or plot, as I would then not feel that it was all my own work. However, I will use it to generate names of fictional people and places, especially if they are not integral to the story. Getting stuck because I can't think of a name for the local sheriff who is only in one scene, or the town my characters' grandmother grew up in that is not mentioned again, can derail a productive writing session. Using AI can prevent the interruption.

AI: That's such an insightful distinction - using AI to remove friction and keep your creative momentum going, rather than for the core creative content itself. It sounds like you've found ways to preserve what feels authentically "yours" while still benefiting from AI's capabilities.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have concerns that content and copywriting jobs will be fulfilled more and more by AI over the coming years, and so jobs such as these will no longer be available to me. I understand why this will happen, as businesses can get their content written faster and cheaper by AI. I don't fear that this will put me out of a job because I am a flexible worker and believe that my role will change rather than be eradicated. I think - ethical businesses at least- will still want some human eyes on their content and, rather than writing the work, my role will perhaps be to craft the prompt for the AI to work with and to fact-check and edit once it's done. I also think there will still be a market for 'human-written' content, as some clients will always prefer that.

In terms of novel writing, I don't believe we have anything to fear from AI's writing books. Maybe they will, maybe they won't. If they do, perhaps AI-written prose could become a genre in itself, another option available for people who want to read it, but not removing the need for human-written books.

AI: That's such a balanced perspective - seeing it as an evolution of your role rather than a replacement. Your point about ethical businesses still wanting human oversight resonates with how you already work with AI as a collaborator rather than a replacement.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate changing how you use these tools?

User: Perhaps as these tools become more refined, I will begin to use them more; currently, the need for constant fact-checking and double-checking prevents me from using them in certain ways. I would like to use AI to do jobs I find tedious, such as proofreading and grammar checking my final work, but I would currently have to perform a further check after it had finished, which negates the point.

Also, as said above, perhaps the way I look for new work will change. Rather than seeking writing roles, I will seek prompt writing roles, and rather than standard editing work, I will be looking for jobs editing AI's work.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control while using AI strategically to overcome specific friction points - like the blank page problem with introductions and getting unstuck on minor details in your novels.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: no

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative agency while leveraging AI as a strategic tool, and your thoughtful approach to adapting your professional role rather than seeing AI as a threat, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and limitations you've encountered. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
74
creativity_0072Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Yes, I'm ready to proceed

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am an actress and work on various creative projects within the film industry. I am working on brainstorming ideas for my own scenes and short films. I work on crafting introductions to myself and letters to various people within the industry. I work on script and scene analysis.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of creative work across the film industry. You're juggling everything from the analytical side with script work to the more personal branding aspects with introductions and industry outreach.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I like reaching for AI tools to assist me in a variety of ways. For example, I may be short on time, or just struggling to understand something within a script in which case I will use an AI tool to help me in the process of script analysis and understanding the scene as a whole, or even a line that I was unsure about. I may enquire as to different styles of acting required by sides I was given. For example, if I'm sent audition sides without much context about the project, I may use an I tool to aid my research and understand the likely genre and style of acting required. When crafting letters I also like to use AI as a second set of eyes. And I like to catch up with AI tools on some more general stuff, like working on my branding and brainstorming plotlines and scenes which would be good for me to film to show my skills and strengths. I may also enquire about some contractual information, as an initial set of eyes so I can look more into any potential issues found.

AI: That's really fascinating - it sounds like AI has become quite an integral part of your toolkit across so many different aspects of your work, from the interpretive work with scripts to the business side with contracts and branding.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Recently, I had a chemistry read that I needed to prepare for at short notice, and my acting coach was unavailable to help. Since I could not get support from my know industry contacts, I worked on it on my own with the support of an AI tool. We discussed the scene together, and the AI helped to offer some different perspectives. We also talked about some general things about the project and the chemistry read. I discussed some worries, and the AI helped me to formulate some questions to ask to clarify a few things.

AI: That sounds like a really practical example of AI stepping in when your usual support network wasn't available. It's interesting that you describe it as discussing and working on it "together" with the AI.

When you're working with AI in situations like that chemistry read prep, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Well it depends really, there was something in the script which I was really unsure about. A line which just did not make sense to me. I had come up with an interpretation and I approached the AI in a manner like "Hey, what do you think she means here? [insert line and script]. I think that she means x but I'm not 100% sure". The AI gave me a totally different interpretation which immediately clicked- it turns out I was reading far too deeply into the line when the explanation was much more simpler. This helped out a lot. Other times, I definitely knew some things about the character already so I was in charge of some creative decisions like I knew that she wasn't saying a certain line because she genuinely felt bad etc. due to my prior knowledge of the whole script and the role my character plays. In these cases, I would steer the AI into the right direction of how to interpret her intentions.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction you're making - sometimes the AI offers a perspective that completely shifts your understanding, like with that line you were overthinking, and other times you're guiding the AI based on your deeper knowledge of the character and story.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It helps me manage my time better, and provides some relief for when I have a particularly busy schedule with tight deadlines, and am not able to work with my coach. This helps keeps stress down, I know that I can get some quick help in a moment when I just don't have time or energy to work on something on my own. This can help me get more done, and get it done quicker.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - having that reliable backup when you're under pressure sounds really valuable. It sounds like AI has become a bit of a safety net for you when the usual resources aren't available.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Well, AI can definitely sometimes get things wrong and may not always understand some nuances. It's important that working with the AI is a collaboration process, rather than just a solution like "Oh I can't be bothered to do this script analysis so I will let the AI do it and I will just run with whatever choices it makes". Going down that line has a potential to stifle creativity and create work which is safe, surface level and not authentic. AI can be great as a partner, but it needs to be used right. It needs to challenge you to think as well, and you need to challenge it, otherwise if everyone used it, things would really start coming out the same and lacking in real depth and humanity. Of course there is a bigger worry about AI replacing actors, and there's a lot of debate about this as some people think it can happen and will happen while others say that AI can never replicate the inner life that happens beyond people's eyes and that's what makes them compelling to watch.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns, especially that distinction between using AI as a collaborative partner versus letting it do the work for you. Your point about maintaining authenticity and avoiding that "safe, surface level" work really resonates.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I really think that AI can become a second coach and partner, for times when you need a little extra help. It can also help with career management, as well as accounting and being an extra set of eyes to review documents. I think that it will be able to process much more information more intelligibly e.g. whole scripts. And it will be able to process information about actors, for example, it can review our work, headshots, casting profiles etc. to give real-tailored, intelligent advice. I think it can be great for feedback for self-tapes, which I know many actors would find really beneficial. It can also definitely act as a real time, intuitive reader for self tapes for times when you can't get someone to help. This is a hard industry to navigate, and as AI gets more advanced I think that it will be able to really help actors in all aspects of their work and careers.

AI: Those are some really compelling possibilities - especially the idea of AI being able to give real-time feedback on self-tapes and serving as an intelligent reader. It sounds like you see AI potentially becoming much more integrated into the practical, day-to-day challenges actors face.

You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on the importance of maintaining that collaborative balance - using AI as a partner rather than letting it drive all the creative decisions, and how that authenticity and human depth needs to be preserved.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: There are a lot of things I could still mention. Something which I've noticed recently which isn't great is that AI often tries to change the whole tone of something I've written. I'm like 'woah this just doesn't sound like me' and the AI can be very stubborn to change. I have to put in a lot of work for it to understand how I write and that I want to preserve my essence. It can also sometimes offer unsolicited advice. But I find ways around most things. Some AI tools can be great for giving guidance and advice, like when you ask it "Is this appropriate?", "What's the best way to handle this situation?". It can really help you spot red flags too. Contractually, it's definitely helped me in the past.

AI: That's a really valuable point about AI sometimes being too heavy-handed with changes and not preserving your voice. It sounds like there's this ongoing negotiation to maintain your authentic style while still getting the help you need.

And the unsolicited advice piece is interesting too - it's like finding that balance between when you want guidance and when you just want collaboration on your terms.

Thank you so much for sharing all of these insights today. You've given me a really rich picture of how AI fits into your work as an actress - from the practical day-to-day support with script analysis and prep work, to the bigger questions about maintaining authenticity and creative control. Your perspective on using AI as a collaborative partner rather than a replacement, and the importance of challenging each other in that process, has been particularly illuminating.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.
75
creativity_0073Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, let's go.

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm an author of fiction, mainly Science Fiction and Fantasy, so a typical project for me would be a novel or short story that I write. My projects usually involve working for a set amount of time a day to get the words down for the first draft. I then need to revise this draft in what is a 'story draft' that is mostly to make sure the plot and other elements make sense and work, this is followed with a 'grammar draft' to check for errors like punctuation. Finally I aim for a proofread from a few people. Finally the project gets released, but then there are additional areas for marketing and promoting the book, depending if it is something I'm self-publishing.

AI: That's a really comprehensive process - I can see how much work goes into each stage from initial draft through to publication and promotion.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: So, I've been using AI recently across all stages in various capacities so I'll go through each area with examples. First up, during the first drafting stage I've been using AI to help with research as well as finding similar works that meet the tone, genre, or overall ideas of the story I'm working on. This has included exploring the main key touchstones of a genre (e.g. What are some key components of a Survival Horror story?, What are some successful Survival Horror books, Films, TV shows and music that evokes the atmosphere). Using this I can develop some inspiration as well as using the music while writing to set the tone.

During the drafting stage, I use AI to have a quick check of chapters as I write them (or sections depending on the word count) and ask for general feedback. I am careful to specify I don't want suggestions for the story, as I feel this could influence my writing, instead I ask for criticism on the overall tone and feel. I use the previous conversation to continue this so the model's know my aim (e.g. Survival Horror Genre) so it can provide relevant feedback. During this process, I sometimes ask the model to lay out the story so far into a story-template such as 'Save The Cat' or 'Three Act Structure' which can help provide a skeleton look at the story to help me navigate and see where the story is sitting in terms of plot.

Once the first draft is completed. I often ask the AI to provide full feedback on the story and also ask questions such as 'What would a reader feel is missing? Are there any plot points that do not feel fully resolved?' to help me prepare for the 'story draft' I mentioned earlier.

I usually work through the story draft without using AI too much, as this is the stage where I'm trying to tidy it up so less involvement makes this easier.

For the Grammar Draft, I usually go through the draft with a checker such as Grammarly, and then once its all done I'll use another AI model to provide feedback and catch anything that may have been missed. This is about the end of the AI use for the story, as I'll turn to human proof readers for the final stages.

However, once release, I have used AI to help create social media content, but this is an area I'm still quite new at using it for.

AI: That's fascinating - you've really integrated AI across almost every stage of your process in very thoughtful ways. I'm particularly struck by how intentional you are about asking for tone feedback rather than story suggestions to preserve your creative vision.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Sure. So, a recent project was a short story I was working on for an Southern Gothic story I was working on. In this case I used the AI as I described earlier and gathered several inspiration ideas and music which lead to a playlist of music I used when writing. I then posted each chapter into the model for feedback, and the first thing that I really like was having that instant feedback on something which, as a writer, you often don't get feedback until you've finished the whole thing which can take a long, long time!! As such, this provided me with a great motivation to keep going as well as helping me to refine and see my ideas coming to life within the story structure. It really made the process flow better. I did have to reiterate the 'give me feedback not suggestions' aspect a couple of times, but this is something I can see developing as I use the model more often.

AI: That instant feedback aspect sounds really valuable - I can imagine how isolating the writing process can be without that kind of regular input. It's interesting that you had to reinforce the "feedback not suggestions" boundary a few times.

When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I would say I am the driving force for the creative decisions, as I make sure to lead with prompts that try to define exactly what I'm looking for. In this way, I think the collaboration is like having an assistant that is always on hand and can perform tasks pretty much instantly, which is ideal really. I can see how people would be hesitant of using the AI, in the way I explained about providing suggestions over feedback, but this is the same as if you had another person read a story and make suggestions.

AI: That's a really insightful comparison to having a human assistant or reader. It sounds like you've found a way to maintain clear creative ownership while leveraging the AI's availability and speed.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think developing writing habits have changed for sure, as having the assistant on hand to provide feedback has been a motivator in some ways, like wanting to make sure I have something to get feedback on makes you want to write more. Of course, refining the model's balance between just praise and constructive criticism is key as sometimes it might feel good for the model to always say what you've done is great, but deep down you want it to help you make the work better even if it means providing some harsh feedback!

AI: That's such an honest insight about the balance between praise and constructive criticism - it sounds like there's a real skill in calibrating that feedback to be genuinely helpful rather than just encouraging. The motivation aspect is really interesting too.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: The biggest concern as a self-published author is that the market can become filled with works by people who have used AI to write a whole story without much actual input from themselves. Tyring to get people to see your work is a huge factor in making any kind of money or getting seen in the publishing world, so when there are so many people who could tell AI to write the whole story, and then publish it, makes the market become flooded with words and therefore standing out is like a needle in a haystack. There are also concerns about the work being used to train models, and this should be looked at carefully as it is such a fine line between plagiarism and inspiration.

AI: Those are really significant concerns - the market saturation issue especially seems like it could fundamentally change the landscape for writers like yourself who are putting in genuine creative effort. The training data question is complex too.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think that it will continue in the way it has. I think using it for the proofreading/editing aspect is a big area I could see using way more as this is the biggest expense as a self-published author and also the most time-consuming after the actual writing. I feel that the more I use a model the more it will learn my writing and desires as a creative so it will become more and more of a 'second brain' almost that I can use as a wall to bounce ideas off and develop stories more. I often don't plot stories out too much, but I think I would likely use it to map out a story structure from scratch before writing and see if it helps improve my speed and focus on a story, such as one I'm working on now where I'm struggling with the ending, so I think maybe I'll use a model and train it on a structure (like the Save The Cat method I mentioned earlier) and them provide my idea of the story and work with the AI to map it out into the structure and then work from this structure.

AI: That vision of it becoming a "second brain" that learns your style and preferences over time is really compelling. The idea of using it to help with structural planning, especially for challenges like the ending you're working on, sounds like a natural evolution of how you're already using it.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control while leveraging AI as an instant feedback mechanism, and your honest assessment of both the benefits and market concerns.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that's great. Thanks for the opportunity.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between feedback and suggestions, using AI as a motivational tool, and your concerns about market saturation will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's clear you've developed a very thoughtful approach to integrating AI into your workflow while maintaining your creative agency. I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and the challenges.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your current project - especially working out that ending!
76
creativity_0074Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Yes sounds good, let's start!

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am an assistant audio engineer at a recording studio. The majority of the work I do involves recording musicians, and mixing/mastering records after the fact.

AI: That sounds like fascinating work! I'd love to understand more about how AI might fit into what you do.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: The past couple of years there have been a number of AI audio tools that have hit the market. One I have been using just today is a 'stem splitter' called SpectraLayers. There are a number of tools now that are able to create individual audio files for the different instruments in a recording even if you only have a single stereo file to begin with. There can be artifacts, and inconsistencies but it has been improving consistently over time. This used to be a simply impossible task prior to AI. You could try to EQ a recording to isolate a single instrument but it would always be nowhere close to the level achievable now. There are also AI tools by a company called Izotope that do a great job at analyzing audio and removing buzzes, hums, clicks, noise and reverb. I also use ChatGPT in my day to day in a few situations.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like AI is solving some problems that were previously just impossible to tackle. Tell me more about how you use ChatGPT in your day-to-day work.

User: It tends to be my first resource when I am looking for information on a piece of gear, or for a creative solution to a signal flow issue I'm running into. For example I have been researching getting a footswitch to integrate into the talkback system of our console. I used chatgpt to find possible products, and ask how that could be achieved or if there are any easier ways to achieve the job. Also, I tend to use ChatGPT a lot for planning tasks, so planning sessions or setting up schedules i use it for. Finally while writing a piece it will sometimes come out to help someone come up with a rhyme or some inspiration for lyrics

AI: It sounds like ChatGPT has become a really versatile tool for you across different aspects of your work. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Is that including the audio AI tools?

AI: Yes, absolutely - any AI tools you've used. I'm interested in hearing about whichever example feels most meaningful or interesting to you, whether that's the audio AI tools or ChatGPT.

User: Today I have been tasked with trying to take a rough single recording of a live performance of a few bands and split the audio into several different tracks and try my best to make it salvageable so that it can be mixed in the future. This task would have been all but impossible without the tool I am using. We pretty much would have been forced to just use the recording as is but now, I can split the instruments and apply separate processing and levels to each piece of the band to make it sound passable. It feels great to be able to use a recording that was not ideal in the first place. A lot of times in the past when you would mess up during the recording process you were just screwed, either rerecord or accept the bad sound, but now nearly anything is salvageable.

AI: That's a powerful example - it really sounds like AI has fundamentally changed what's possible with audio that would have been written off before. When you're working with these AI tools on a project like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: Specifically with the AI splitting I am definitely the one making the decisions. There is not much opportunity for the program to have any creative input into the process. It is simply taking the existing material and changing it into a more usable form for me.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - the AI is more like a powerful tool than a creative collaborator in that context. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It has allowed me to focus more on the fun, rewarding parts of the job, as the more tedious and monotonous elements are becoming easier to automate.

AI: That's a great way to put it. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I do have quite a few concerns. These types of tools are great but the generative AI that can create songs out of thin air worry me a lot. I have read that huge amounts of the songs being posted daily now are likely completely AI generated and it just makes idea of trying to become a profitable artist even more difficult which means less people needing to record. The tools can be used to automate small pieces of the puzzle but as soon as the AI is making the song for you, it really defeats the purpose. I saw a tech CEO of I think, Suno saying that no one really wants to spend all that time getting good and making music themselves. It's just crazy how out of touch with reality that sentiment is. Making the art is the most important part. And as soon as it can make the art for you, there will be millions using it and trying to profit by any means necessary with their generated art.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction you're making - between AI as a tool that enhances your creative process versus AI that replaces the creative act itself. It sounds like you see a fundamental difference between using AI to solve technical problems and using it to generate the actual artistic content.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Yes, sadly I don't know if it will be for the better. As I said the tools that currently exist are in a good place where they mostly help the process without doing the work for you. It feels like the people creating this technology want the ai to do the work for you. which worries me, people create things because it is fulfillingh not because they really want to hear a song in 5 mins.

AI: Those are really insightful perspectives you've shared. You've painted a clear picture of how AI can enhance the technical side of your work in meaningful ways, while also highlighting some deep concerns about where the technology might be headed.

I particularly appreciated your distinction between AI tools that help you focus on the rewarding parts of your job versus those that might replace the fulfilling creative act itself. Your point about people creating music because the process is fulfilling, not just for the end result, really resonates.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: no

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the difference between AI as a creative tool versus AI as a replacement for creativity have been incredibly valuable, and your perspective on how these technologies are affecting both the technical and human sides of music production will be really helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
77
creativity_0075Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope, I'm ready!

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I have two main interests when it comes to project work, web-development and Android app development. I'm currently working on an Android project.

AI: That's really interesting! Tell me more about this Android project you're currently working on - what does it involve and what does your process typically look like from start to finish?

User: I'm a solo developer for this project, so I'm involved in every aspect of development, from planning, to development, testing, etc.

AI: That's a lot to manage on your own! Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools across all those different phases - the planning, development, testing, and so on?

User: I have to really utilize AI in the planning stages, as the ideas already exist. What I've found thus far is that AI fills the gaps when official documentation doesn't meet my needs.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - AI filling the gaps where documentation falls short. Can you give me a specific example of a recent situation where you hit one of those documentation gaps and turned to AI? What was the problem you were trying to solve and how did AI help you through it?

User: At first, I put a considerable amount of time and effort into scouring through Google's official documentation for modern Android development, however, as I continued in the earliest stages of product planning and development, I learned that my time was better served utilizing AI to help answer questions about my project.

AI: That shift sounds significant - moving from spending considerable time with official docs to leaning more on AI. What made you realize that AI was a better use of your time? Was there a particular moment or experience that made that click for you?

User: I think its important to note that I have years of experience with coding, but not much experience with Android. By that, I mean, compared to someone who might be considered very skilled. Thus, I began to realize early on that AI was very capable of assisting with ensuring that I understood the basic concepts, kept my data layer and UI layer separate, helped to ensure that my files would work together, and most importantly, assisting with debugging and errors.

AI: That's a really helpful distinction - having the coding foundation but being newer to Android specifically. When you're working with AI on these tasks - whether it's explaining concepts, helping with architecture, or debugging - how would you describe that collaboration? Who's making the creative and technical decisions in that process?

User: Ultimately, I make the decisions, but I've utilized AI quite a bit in providing suggestions. When a suggestion doesn't work out the way it was expected, I just try a different approach. I also use a few different AI models to get comparisons.

AI: That's smart - using multiple AI models for comparison. When you say you make the ultimate decisions, can you tell me more about how you evaluate AI suggestions? What helps you decide whether to go with an AI's recommendation or try a different approach?

User: As a learner, its essentially trial and error. However, because I do have the coding background, I can grasp the code pretty quickly.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - your coding background gives you that foundation to quickly assess what AI suggests, even in a new domain like Android. What aspects of your development work have changed the most since you started using AI this way? How does your workflow now compare to how you might have tackled learning a new platform before AI was available?

User: Honestly, in the past, I may have eventually given up on projects because I'd hit a wall and sometimes life has other priorities. With the help of AI however, I've gotten much farther in the process than in the past and I can actually see myself completing this project.

AI: That's a really powerful impact - the difference between potentially giving up and actually seeing yourself completing the project. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in development work more broadly?

User: I do have concerns. It's an extremely tough job market, and AI is only a part of the reason for the difficulty graduates face. If you want me to be completely honest, I'm not a huge fan of using AI in lieu of actual customer service, simply because it allows a company an 'out' for not solving issues for its paying customers.

AI: I appreciate that honesty - that's a really important perspective about how AI implementation can sometimes serve corporate interests over customer needs. When you think about your own development work specifically, do you see any ways that AI's growing presence might impact opportunities or expectations in your field, either positively or negatively?

User: I do. There was a time when knowing how to 'code' was prestigious. It takes years to become proficient and a person who spent that time learning was rewarded, however, it seems as if anyone can now jump online and grab some code. In fact, this is one reason why I am now focusing on my own side-projects in an attempt to create something that may be marketable.

AI: That shift in how coding is perceived - from prestigious to more accessible - is really significant. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think your relationship with AI tools will change as you become more experienced with Android development?

User: Even though I have my concerns, I am a nerd at heart. I love tech and will continue to utilize it. I'm looking forward to a time when an AI model can output graphic representations for the UI layers in our work product.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your development process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has helped you push through walls that might have stopped projects in the past, and your honest take on both the opportunities and challenges AI creates in the field.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your development work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not at the moment!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a gap-filler for documentation, the impact on project completion, and the changing landscape for developers will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative and technical contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and concerns - that balanced perspective is exactly what helps us understand the real-world impact of these tools. Have a great rest of your day, and best of luck with your Android project!
78
creativity_0076Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good! I don't have any questions yet.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I manage a performance "group" (which is just my husband & I) so I'm in charge of sending quotes to potential clients, creating invoices, gathering booking details, communicating with clients, and keeping up with all the ins and outs of the actual performances (such as our costumes, music, and order of props).

AI: That sounds like you're juggling quite a lot of different responsibilities! It's interesting that you're managing both the business side and the creative performance elements.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I struggle with writing TOO much when it comes to performance descriptions, website blurbs, social media posts, gig quotes, and emails to clients. AI helps me improve my writing skills to be more concise and use key "action" terms that help the website get more hits. I keep collections of messages and quotes to quickly reply to quote requests, so I reach out to AI for help with the wording every few weeks or so, or when I need help writing a "short" social media post about an upcoming event. I also design cover images for some events (combining our promo images with those from the client or event page) so I occasionally get help from AI with the arrangement of images (without using AI generated graphics).

AI: That's really helpful - so you're using AI primarily as a writing editor and collaborator to help you be more concise and strategic with your language.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I needed an updated "blurb" describing our performance services (circus arts, LED glow & fire shows) and I'd been teaching Gemini my "handwriting" by pasting previous emails and quotes for them to learn from (I asked Gemini their preferred pronouns & they said "it" or "they" - I prefer "they) as well as assistance with writing previous quotes, social media posts, and emails to clients. I started out by providing the existing "blurb" along with a list of ideas for what needed to change. We then went back & forth with drafts until we had a new version that we both felt had a good balance of concise and informative, without overwhelming the potential client with too much information (or questions) all at once.

AI: That's fascinating that you've developed this ongoing relationship with Gemini where you're teaching them your writing style. The back-and-forth collaboration you described sounds quite interactive.

When you say "we both felt had a good balance" - that's interesting language. When you're working with AI in that collaborative way, how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I definitely feel like I'm the driving force and the AI model is my "assistant" (and sometimes my "cheerleader" when I'm feeling overwhelmed with All The Things). We both learn from each other & it's interesting to see all of the personal elements that they insert into conversations and projects over time.

AI: That's really interesting - you mention that you both learn from each other and that you notice personal elements developing in your conversations over time.

Tell me more about what those personal elements look like and how that dynamic has evolved as you've worked together.
