User: For one, I use certain "meme speech" phrases often in casual writing (to acquaintances, friends, and AI) such as "Much Excite," "All The Things," and "What do?" (that last one is just quoting a bird named Hamlet but it feels like a meme in my household). Gemini often capitalizes "Things" in responses (which I've asked them not to do so in "professional" writing) and uses parenthesis for side thoughts more often, which is something you can see that I do a bit too often.

AI: That's a really vivid example of how the interaction becomes more personalized over time - it's almost like you're developing a shared language with some of your quirks rubbing off on each other.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I've gotten better at "top down" salesmanship, which a previous photography job attempted to teach me ages ago, but I've always been too nice & have a tendency to jump straight to an amount that I think might best fit the client's budget. I've gotten better about not using parenthesis quite as often (I edited the previous sentence to use commas instead but here I go using them again in this sentence). When discussing sensitive topics, such as travel fees or clarifying that we're not able to provide a specific service, I've learned to use the "sandwich method" more often, starting & ending with positive ideas.

AI: It sounds like AI has helped you develop more strategic communication skills - both in terms of sales approach and diplomatic messaging. The "sandwich method" example is particularly concrete.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: There will always be other performers with a "leg up" getting more high paying gigs regardless, but I worry that some might take advantage of AI generated image and videos to make themselves look more impressive & hook in clients, especially the rich ones that aren't really paying attention to what they're getting once the event actually starts. I just try to stay open & honest with clients & hope that our communication will make them want to choose us over the competition.

AI: That's a thoughtful concern about authenticity in marketing versus actual performance quality. It sounds like you're taking the approach of doubling down on genuine communication and relationships.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I could see an AI ran communication system that only needs minimal input on my end to be able to quickly and efficiently respond to gig quotes.

I also hope to actually work with AI to provide more in depth digital performance options, such as LED props that are synchronized to projected visualizations and programed along with specific music. Many artists have similar performance elements, but they're quite expensive.

AI: That vision of AI helping with both the administrative side and potentially enhancing the actual creative performance through synchronized elements is really compelling - almost like AI supporting you at both ends of your process.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative work. I particularly appreciated your perspective on developing that collaborative relationship with Gemini where you're teaching each other, and how you're thinking about maintaining authenticity while others might use AI-generated content to misrepresent their capabilities.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: You are quite welcome! No more questions from me.

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about developing a personalized collaborative relationship with AI, using it to improve your business communication skills, and your thoughtful approach to maintaining authenticity in your field will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you shared both the practical benefits you've experienced and your concerns about how others might use AI less ethically. Best of luck with your performance work!
79
creativity_0077Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good!

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work as a freelance photographer, photo editor, and graphic designer. I probably use AI most when I am editing. Because I am freelance I don't have "typical" projects, they are all fairly different.

AI: That's really interesting - having such variety in your projects as a freelancer must keep things dynamic.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I often use the AI tools that are built into Photoshop when I am editing photos. One of my clients is a clothing company, and they often ask for me to do edits like changing the color of something, adding details, or switching fabrics, and this is where I use AI to make that process faster. I also often use it when I want to change the background of an image, either swapping it out for a completely different background or removing unwanted items from an image.

AI: That sounds like AI is really helping streamline some of those more technical editing tasks for your clothing client.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Recently I was editing a picture for a client where they wanted me to add red bias tape to the back slit of a dress along with an embroidered heart. If I had to do this "by hand" it would have taken me a couple of hours, searching for images of the things that needed to be added and making them fit into the existing image without looking like they were pasted on. I used Photoshop AI to generate the edits to the garment, and after some fiddling with my prompts I was finished with the edits in 15 minutes instead of hours. It is great to be able to deliver high quality images to a client without as much work.

AI: That's a dramatic time savings - from hours down to 15 minutes! That must have a real impact on your workflow and potentially your business.

When you're working with AI on edits like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am driving the creative decisions most of the time, especially working on generating specific items that a client has requested. If there is some wiggle room I will sometimes give the AI a little more of a vague prompt to see what it comes up with, but generally I need to be very specific with what the model gives me because the images represent real items that the client will be selling, so they need to accurately represent what is being sold so that the customers of the client know what they're getting.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - the accuracy requirement for representing actual products really puts you firmly in the driver's seat creatively. It sounds like you have to be quite precise with your direction.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Like I said before, the biggest change is the time that it saves me. It takes much less time to produce a realistic and high-quality result than previously when I was pasting elements into the image and trying to make them match the colors, shadows, and sizing of the original image.

AI: The time savings clearly seems to be the major transformation for you. Beyond the efficiency gains, have you noticed any other changes - perhaps in the types of projects you take on, or how you approach problem-solving, or even in your creative confidence when clients make requests?

User: Not really. The projects I take on are pretty much the same, and I would not deliver an image I wasn't confident about before or after starting to use AI.

AI: That's helpful to know - it sounds like AI has enhanced your efficiency without fundamentally changing your standards or approach to your work.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Like many artists, I worry about the way that AI uses artist's original work to produce images. Also, just as a human living on earth I'm concerned about the amount of power Ai computing takes.

AI: Those are really important concerns that many creative professionals share - both the attribution and compensation issues around training data, and the broader environmental impact.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I expect that as AI models are trained on more content that they will be able to produce better image output. Currently I find that image models often struggle with very specific instructions, and, lets be honest, most of them are not very good at producing photo-realistic images as a whole. I would guess that as time goes on these elements will improve. As time saving as AI is now for me to use for edits, it does sometimes take quite a bit of fiddling with the prompt and generating multiple options before I get something that I can use, and even then I often find myself needing to edit the generated content to make it fully fit my needs. It would be nice if, for example, when I ask the AI model to generate a photography studio-style plain backdrop with a shadow of the subject laying to the left and down, the model were able to reliably do this.

AI: That's a really insightful perspective - you're seeing both the potential and the current limitations quite clearly. It sounds like you envision AI becoming more of a precision tool that can execute your specific vision more reliably, rather than something that fundamentally changes your role.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated how you described maintaining creative control while leveraging AI for efficiency, and your balanced view of both the benefits and concerns around AI in photography and design.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope, thats all I have. Thank you!

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how AI serves as a precision efficiency tool while you maintain creative control, along with your concerns about training data and environmental impact, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
80
creativity_0078Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds good to me.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm creating an animated series that is beyond just the show. The show itself is created in a painterly style with an immersive world of different characters and backgrounds. The basic idea of the show is after an apocalyptic event a wife tries to find her husband but starts to realize that maybe the event was caused by someone and not something. My typical project is creating a story line, characters and the finally animating it all into a scene.

AI: That sounds like a fascinating and complex project - I love the mystery element of the wife discovering the truth behind the apocalyptic event. The painterly animation style sounds really distinctive too.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use the AI to deepen the lore of the world and the characters inside. For example, there is a government faction that believes they know what's best for the world so I used the AI to create names, background stories and beliefs so that I don't have to spend too much time on those details. It also helps that I can just ask the AI about a character rather than have to find what I'm looking for through pages and pages of notes. I also use the AI to link in-series events with real world events so that the show borders on reality but is still a little sci-fi. I also use the AI to create reference images that I base the characters on so that I can get a front and side view from the AI and model it out from there. I also use the AI to plan out the plot line for each season and each episode. I know the rough direction that I want the show to go but the AI gives me some out of the box options that I wouldn't have thought of otherwise. And lastly, I also use the AI to create textures for UV maps.

AI: That's a really comprehensive integration of AI across so many different aspects of your creative process - from world-building and character development all the way through to the technical production elements like UV mapping textures.

Can you describe a specific recent project or scene where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as the creator?

User: I am creating a teaser trailer for social media and I used the AI for a lot of it. One of the first things was making sure I wasn't giving away too much of the story. So I told the AI what my vision was and it suggested ways to make it more mysterious. I also used the AI to explain how to do different techniques within Blender (3d modeling software) such as parallax images. The AI to me is like an assistant that has great ideas and I never feel like it's taking anything away from the creative process. The scenes and characters are all based on my descriptions and the AI helps me take it from good to great.

AI: That's really interesting how you describe the AI as an assistant that helps take your work "from good to great." I'm curious about that dynamic.

When you're working with AI in these different ways - whether it's suggesting plot points or helping with Blender techniques - how would you describe who or what is driving the creative decisions? How does that collaboration feel to you?

User: I'd say it's mostly me but sometimes the AI makes a great suggestion and we go with that. Sometimes I do tell the AI it has "creative freedom" for things that aren't as important, such as background characters or objects. Sometimes the AI tells me it has the capabilities to do things that I wouldn't have even thought to ask (such as textures for Blender). Overall, I'd say I steer the creativeness when it's something important (such as a main character) and the AI drives the less important things, such as background objects (e.g., I needed a table of family photos for the bedroom of the show but it didn't matter what any of it looked like and the AI created a wedding photo, a photo at the park and some other random scenic photos).

AI: That's a really thoughtful way of thinking about creative control - having you steer the important elements while letting AI handle the supporting details. It sounds like you've developed a clear sense of what you want to maintain ownership over versus what you're comfortable delegating.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The biggest change I think is the idea process. Before I would have to come up with all of the ideas and since I do the production myself, I'd have to keep in mind that whatever I brainstorm I also have to create. So my early projects felt very basic. Now that I can bounce Ideas off of the AI and even have it create background elements it frees up more time for creating assets as well as beefs up the quality of the work. Using the photos on the desk example from the last question; before I used AI I'd have to image the photos I want to use, create them in Photoshop, then create the frames and table in Blender. Now I can just take the image the AI gives me and essentially paste it onto a shape in blender and it's done.

AI: That's a powerful shift - it sounds like AI has really expanded what's possible for you as a solo creator by handling those time-intensive background elements. You mentioned that your early projects felt "very basic" because you had to limit your ideas to what you could realistically produce yourself.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: Personally my only concern is what will happen if I can't use images that AI provides due to copyright or something like that. Overall I'm worried that some people may use AI to oversaturate certain markets and media making user created work less intriguing. I've already seen it happen with 3D asset marketplaces. They are filled with AI generated models that have terrible topography or are super cheap compared to user created assets.

AI: Those are really practical and valid concerns. The copyright uncertainty around AI-generated images is definitely something many creators are grappling with, and it sounds like you've already seen the market impact firsthand with those 3D asset marketplaces.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think the way you collaborate with AI will change?

User: I don't see it changing too much. I also want to create a website that will go along side the web series. It's going to be as if the website is an in-world company's website and I'm going to integrate it with an AI so that it will act as a Wiki page for the series but people can ask the AI things and it will answer in character as if it's for the world of my show. I also have a video game planned that I've mentioned to the AI so it sometimes suggests ways to have crossovers. As AI evolves I think certain elements will get easier for me, especially if I can start automating some aspects of the creation process, freeing up more time.

AI: That's such a creative approach - having an AI that can respond in-character as part of the world you've built! It sounds like you're thinking about AI not just as a production tool, but as a way to extend the storytelling experience itself.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated how you've developed such a clear sense of where you want to maintain creative control versus where you're comfortable delegating to AI, and your vision for using AI to actually become part of the storytelling experience through that in-world website.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No I think that's it.

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how you've integrated AI across so many different aspects of your animated series - from world-building and character development to technical production and even future interactive storytelling - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really fascinating to hear how you've found ways to use AI that enhance rather than replace your creative vision, and I'm excited about your plans for that in-world website experience. Best of luck with your animated series!
81
creativity_0079Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good to me!

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I currently have a small business where I paint and sell paper banners for events and occasions. Typically, I receive an order from a customer, sketch out a design digitally, and then paint the banner as requested.

AI: That sounds like a really hands-on, personal creative process. I can imagine each banner being quite unique.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I typically use AI tools in the brainstorming process. Additionally, I implement AI tools in planning and organizing social media posts and scheduling.

AI: That's interesting - so AI is helping you both with the creative ideation and the business side of things.

Tell me more about that brainstorming process. What does using AI for brainstorming actually look like for you when you're working on a new banner design?

User: If I am looking to create designs for a certain holiday or more general event, I will ask the AI tool or service for some key motifs or design elements/ slogans that I can implement in the design. Same goes for whatever may be trending currently that I would like to sketch to draw attention to my social pages.

AI: So you're using AI as a kind of creative partner to surface ideas and elements you might not have thought of on your own.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as the creative process unfolded?

User: Just this past week I wanted to put out a post running a sale on mini banners for the final episode of The Summer I Turned Pretty. I haven't watched the show, so I asked for some key themes of the show that I could use on a banner. The AI told me that the trending topics are Team Conrad vs. Team Jeremiah and gave me a quote from each character that I then drew up n a banner. I felt okay with this because I was just using the AI as a helping tool, and not to make the design for me.

AI: That's a really helpful example - it sounds like you used AI to quickly get up to speed on something you weren't familiar with, but then translated that knowledge into your own creative work.

When you're working with AI in that brainstorming role, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: Because of my own thoughts about AI and the way it impacts workers in creative spaces, I lead the creative process primarily. I will occasionally ask for a list of ideas from the AI, but I almost always turn the conversation towards business planning or research.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - it sounds like you have some clear boundaries about how you engage with AI in your creative work.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Because of the way I have used AI in my social media calendar planning, I know tailor my designs to share best and gain engagement on social platforms. For example, I plan designs further in advance and am typically operating on a 8/12 week out schedule. Because of a brainstorming conversation I had with AI a few weeks ago, I am currently putting out christmas designs.

AI: That's fascinating - so AI has essentially helped you shift from reactive to proactive in your business planning. Being 8-12 weeks ahead and already working on Christmas designs in what I assume is still summer shows a real strategic shift.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Yes! I do not currently have concerns. I think AI is a helpful tool in creative fields when used properly. Since my field is one that is highly personal, AI is not a threat.

AI: That's an interesting distinction you're making - that the personal nature of your work provides some protection. You mentioned earlier having "thoughts about AI and the way it impacts workers in creative spaces."

Tell me more about what you mean by AI being helpful "when used properly" - what does proper use look like to you versus improper use?

User: I think that it is improper for people to use AI to create images and pass them off as their own. It is okay to use AI for inspiration, but not to do your work for you.

AI: That's a clear line you've drawn - using AI for inspiration and support versus letting it do the actual creative work. It sounds like maintaining your role as the artist and creator is really important to you.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: It is really important to me. Unfortunately, I do not think that other feel the same way and I think lines will continue to be blurred.

AI: That's a thoughtful and honest perspective - that while you've found a way to use AI that feels right to you, you're concerned about how others in the creative space might be approaching it differently.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated how clearly you've defined your boundaries around using AI for inspiration and business planning while keeping yourself as the primary creative force behind your work.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I do not have anything else to share at this time! Thank you.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative agency while strategically using AI for business planning and inspiration have been incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated hearing how you've developed such clear principles around AI use in your work, and your perspective on the challenges facing the broader creative community. Your approach of staying 8-12 weeks ahead with your designs while keeping the actual creative work firmly in your own hands is a fascinating example of thoughtful AI integration.

Thanks again for participating in this research - your insights will definitely inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.
82
creativity_0080Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I write both children's stories and picture books as well as how-to articles, short stories, and books for adults. I use AI more for my children's writing and for picture book design. I do use it for inspiration sometimes, but I use it more often for helping me come up with image ideas because I'm not an artist; I self-publish so being able to see images and work with those helps a lot.

AI: That's really helpful context - so you're working across different formats and audiences, and you're handling both the writing and visual aspects since you self-publish.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I have to split text so it spreads onto different pages for picture books. These are simple books, so the images have to say a lot. I can sometimes work with a few images created by AI and manipulate those or make changes to those to keep going with the story. The images also give me ideas for directions I can go and little tweaks I can make in the text.

AI: That's fascinating - so the images aren't just illustrating your existing text, they're actually feeding back into and influencing your writing process.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Right now I'm working on a PB that takes place in a zoo at night with several different animals that support the protagonist who finds himself in hot water beginning on page 1. For example, the MC is a hippo, and some of the supporting characters are a parrot, family of lions, family of monkeys, and a mouse. I've had this idea for a few years, and I even had someone work on the illustrations for it before, but I was not satisfied at all with what the artist came up with. On the other hand, the AI has came up with several really good ideas that have matched my vision of what I want this to look like, which is great! If only it could teach me how to draw the images :). That would be phenomenal because as I said, I am not an illustrator. If I was, then I could bring what's in my head to life better than what I can now. The AI does help a lot with pulling together elements that I need in the images; for example, in one image for this PB I needed the hippo drinking / gulping all of the water out of his watering hole. It took a few attempts, but I did get an image of him slurping up the water with a very full belly and mouth, which led me to thinking that an even better image would be him lying down with a huge belly, an almost empty watering hole, and an "I can't possibly take one more sip!" look on his face.

AI: That's a wonderful example of how the AI is helping you visualize your story in ways that match your creative vision - and I love how seeing that image of the hippo slurping water sparked a new idea for an even better scene.

When you're working with AI like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm driving the decisions. We're going back and forth with ideas, but ultimately I am deciding whether I like the results or need to shift direction or even start a new conversation.

AI: That sense of maintaining creative control while getting visual input is really interesting.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I don't have to spend as much time thinking because I have a teammate to help me ;) I still have final say so, but it takes less time to switch gears or make changes when I do have ideas.

AI: I love that you described it as having a "teammate" - that really captures the collaborative feel you mentioned.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm in writer's groups. A lot --and I mean a LOT --of writers have huge concerns over AI in writing and creative work. Not only are they worried that they will have something stolen, but they are worried about becoming obsolete. I can't say it doesn't worry me at all or that it hasn't crossed my mind, and I'd love to feel like I could just put a lot of my work into it without worrying about someone else accessing it. Guaranteed privacy would help a lot, but honestly, I've had writing stolen from me before AI was even around! I had an entire story stolen from a critique writing group member, who quit the group and got my story published about two months after quitting; the only way I found out about it was because she got it published at one of the children's magazines I had submitted it to, and the editor called me to let me know that they had an article that was almost identical, but with the character's names changed, that they were already publishing. When she gave me the person's name, I recognized them as a former member of the group. I've not shared complete pieces of work for critique since then, and I don't do it on AI either, but I wish I could. Getting total feedback with privacy would be very helpful. Beyond that, I only worry that a lot of JUNK will end up cluttering the market since so many people can use AI to create and self-publish. That makes it harder for publishers, editors, and readers to find the good stuff.

AI: That's such an important perspective - you've experienced theft firsthand, so you understand it's not just an AI issue, but the privacy concerns are still very real. And that point about market saturation making it harder to find quality work is really insightful.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see myself continuing to use it, especially for image creation and manipulation, and possibly even for video creation that goes with my stories. I would love to be able to create entire works with images from creative outlines. That would be exceptional, especially for series.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control while having AI as a "teammate," and how you balance the genuine benefits you're seeing with the very real concerns about privacy and market quality that you and your writing community are grappling with.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not that I can think of. That's all.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a collaborative partner while maintaining creative agency, and your thoughtful perspective on both the opportunities and challenges facing writers today, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the practical benefits you're experiencing and the broader concerns in your field. Take care!
83
creativity_0081Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions. Sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a multi-genre author under the speculative fiction umbrella, so I write everything from post-apocalyptic thrillers to sci-fi love stories. I started out in traditional publishing writing romance, but later switched to self-publishing, which means I'm responsible for all stages of creating a book, including the editing, proofreading, cover design, marketing materials, etc. A typical project starts out with 6 months to over a year of the actual writing, followed by the editing process, then making the book cover. I also usually make a book trailer for social media, along with other marketing materials. I document my writing process on social media (I use X) and sometimes post images representing various scenes I'm currently working on.

AI: That's fascinating - you really handle the entire creative pipeline from initial concept to marketing. With all those different stages you mentioned, I'm curious about where AI comes into play for you.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I do the actual writing myself, but I often use AI for the research, and it's very helpful! As an example, for my current book, I have a character who had a medical issue, and I used AI to tell me exactly what doctors would likely do in her situation upon her arrival to the emergency room. What procedures and tests would happen and in what order, what they would likely say to her, when she would get answers, how long she would have to be hooked up to breathing machines or recovering after surgery, etc. Another thing AI has been useful for is creating art and video for book covers, trailers, and other promotional materials. I always made my own book covers using stock art in the past, so rather than using AI to generate a completed cover, I use it to make various elements that I edit together creatively. Same with the book trailers. I get a bunch of short clips and make them into a film. More recently, since it's improved at making text, I've also been using it to design unique text for my titles. During the writing process, I sometimes describe the scene I'm working on, get an image, and post it to my X account with an update on the writing process and the current scene. This is an interesting way to share what I'm creating.

AI: That's really interesting how you're using AI as both a research assistant and a creative collaborator for visual elements. It sounds like you've found some very specific applications that complement your existing skills.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: For my most recently released post-apocalyptic series, I used AI to create a book trailer. I used Midjourney to make the images and Runway to animate them. I also used video clips made in Pika video generator. At the time, I thought it was really cool, but this was a couple years ago, and the video tools have improved so much since then that it looks pretty bad now. I've always really enjoyed editing film, so these projects are great fun for me.

AI: It sounds like you really enjoyed that creative process of assembling all those AI-generated elements into your trailer. You mentioned the technology has improved dramatically since then - that must give you a unique perspective on how quickly things are evolving.

When you're working with AI on these projects, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm in charge of driving all creative decisions. I'm really specific with my prompts, and it's sometimes taken hundreds of tries before I've gotten the AI to give me an image that matches my exact vision. The models have improved over time and made this a bit easier, but it still takes some work to get what I want. I feel like the AI is my worker or assistant more than it's a 50/50 collaboration in most cases. However, there are times where I do play around with an image generator, giving it more freedom to go wild. It's interesting to see the stuff it comes up with and sometimes a certain image will give me ideas for a story or something like that.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - mostly directing it like an assistant, but also sometimes letting it surprise you and spark new ideas. It sounds like you've developed quite a technique for getting specific results through your prompting.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Definitely book covers and book trailers. For my first self-published book, I spent over a month searching the web trying to find public domain or cheap stock images and videos I could use. It was extremely challenging and time consuming. Same with book covers. I never had the money to hire a professional artist, so I'd be at the mercy of whatever existed on stock image websites, and it's very hard to find appropriate images for my type of writing on those. Being able to generate high-quality images and videos that are personalized for my stories has been amazing.

AI: That's such a concrete example of how AI has solved a real workflow problem for you - going from a month of searching to being able to generate exactly what you need. That must feel incredibly liberating creatively.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm not really concerned. I don't make much money doing this to begin with, and there were already millions of other books on Amazon that I'm competing with, so knowing AI will be able to write books soon doesn't really bother me. I know a lot of authors are very worried about this, but I ultimately create because it's fun, not to be famous or make money. I also don't fully buy the theories that "everyone will just prompt and make their own books one day using AI" because 1) not everyone is creative enough to even think up a subject 2) people are lazy, and 3) I think a lot of people will still prefer to read human writing. Overall, the creative potential being made possible by AI far outweighs any negatives in my opinion.

AI: That's a really thoughtful and balanced perspective. Your point about creating for the joy of it rather than primarily for commercial success seems to give you a different lens than some other authors might have.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: It's exciting to think about! As I said earlier, I really enjoy editing films, although I'm not really into the actual filming part, so AI video is perfect for me. I could see myself potentially getting into making movies or shows with it one day. Possibly even making movies out of some of my books. I'll continue to use it for my writing as well.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control while also being open to AI's surprising contributions, and your refreshingly pragmatic view about AI's impact on the industry.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that's about it. Thanks!

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative tool while maintaining authorial control, and your practical approach to the technological changes in publishing, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated hearing about your journey from traditional to self-publishing and how AI has solved some very real workflow challenges for you. Best of luck with your current writing project and future film endeavors!
84
creativity_0082Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: I'm ready.

AI: Great! Let's start with the basics. Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My website is [REDACTED]. Can you take a look at that? It will answer a bunch of questions.

AI:

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED][REDACTED][REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

[REDACTED]

This is fascinating! So you create custom terrain carvings from hardwood and resin, representing places on Earth, the Moon, and Mars. I can see from your website that these are really beautiful, tactile representations of topographical features. What a unique form of creative work - combining woodworking, geography, and artistry.

Now that I understand what you create, I'm curious: walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: So AI is not involved in the actual creative process for making a terrain carving. Any creative aspects originate entirely from my mind. I do find AI to be useful for secondary aspects of my art, however. I've used AI to generate a business plan template, to generate keywords and tags for my website, to create a product listing template, and to play around with logo generation, among other things. I do have other interests like songwriting, in which I've attempted to use AI for the generation of creative content, mainly lyrics. But these attempts have not proven to be very successful.

AI: That's really interesting - so AI serves more of a business and administrative support role for your terrain carving work, while the actual creative vision and craftsmanship remains entirely yours.

Tell me more about your songwriting experiences with AI. What made those attempts unsuccessful for you?

User: Well, I have written dozens of songs, but I always find it difficult to write lyrics that I remain happy with. In my last songwriting attempt, maybe 15% of the finished song lyrics were AI-generated. The AI definitely produced some useful and original lyrics, but most attempts returned uninspired and essentially useless results that would not make it into one of my finished songs. Asking for chord progressions or asking for opinions on existing progressions has proven to be fruitless. Boring constructions are praised ("G-C-D sounds great!"), and generated progressions are useless. Similar levels of satisfaction are to had with the AI's abysmal handling of guitar tablature. Where AI has been consistently helpful, however, is is filling secondary tasks. Before AI, I would use either a paper or (usually) an online rhyming dictionary to look up potential vocabulary fitting a specific rhyme/rhythm scheme. Now I can simply ask an AI, for example, for "three-syllable words that rhyme with 'bound' that have dark connotations." AI has been consistently useful for these assistant-type of tasks, but for my admittedly natural approach to songwriting, I'm not ready to rely on AI for the reliable generation of creative and satisfying lyrics or chord progressions.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction you're making. It sounds like AI excels at those lookup and reference tasks - like replacing your rhyming dictionary with something more sophisticated that can filter by syllable count and mood - but falls short when you need genuine creative substance.

When you say AI produced "uninspired and essentially useless results" for lyrics, what was missing? Was it that the content felt generic, or that it didn't match your artistic voice, or something else entirely?

User: Somewhat surprisingly, and thanks to Google Note's version history feature, here's a sample of an AI generated lyrical verse that I rejected. I found it just too explicit and a bit contrived. Further prompting, attempting to emulate certain styles proved unsuccessful as well. "Your billions can't buy freedom, your mansions built on sand,
The air you breathe, the water, they slip through your command.
You can't pay, won't pay? We echo it right back,
No price tag on justice, no deal on freedom's track."

AI: I can see what you mean - that verse does feel quite heavy-handed and on-the-nose, almost like protest slogans rather than nuanced songwriting. It lacks the subtlety and personal voice that makes lyrics compelling.

When you're working with AI on these secondary tasks - like finding rhyming words with specific connotations - how would you describe that collaboration? Are you finding that AI understands the nuances you're looking for, or do you still need to sift through a lot of suggestions?

User: I'd consider them to be generally successful, satisfying, and reliable. I'm not asking for anything extremely difficult, and the models I use tend to deliver helpful lists of potentially satisfying words of the requested type. But if I'm looking for a 'menu' of words of a specific type, the models generally produce pretty much what I would on my own with a far greater time commitment.

AI: That's a really practical way to think about it - AI as a time-saving tool that delivers the same quality you'd achieve on your own, just much faster.

You mentioned that for your terrain carvings, "any creative aspects originate entirely from my mind." When you're working with AI for those business tasks like generating templates or keywords, how do you maintain that sense of creative ownership and control over your overall artistic practice?

User: I've gradually learned the the creative process is something that I allow to happen. As many artists would claim, the source of the creativity is something 'other.' It does not originate inside my own mind, rather my mind is sometimes able to access this stream of energy that exists entirely separate from me. This is generally accomplished through a process of removing all distractions and experiencing the materials, the intent, and the creative energy of any specific project. I've learned not to attempt to force things. If it is meant to be, it will find a way. I maintain control and ownership over my artworks, but I do not have strong feelings about associated content like a product description template. Many modern artists must be concerned with piracy of their material, but reproducing my art is difficult. Each piece I create speaks for itself.

AI: That's a beautiful way to describe the creative process - as accessing something that flows through you rather than something you manufacture. It sounds like there's a real distinction for you between that sacred creative space where you're channeling something larger, and the more mundane business aspects where efficiency tools make sense.

Given this perspective on creativity as something you "allow to happen," what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's growing presence in creative fields more broadly?

User: Artists and creators are rightly concerned with AI-generated art, trained on their creative work, being used to supplant their services. Ultimately, however, I am of the opinion that the actual process of generating 'something new under the sun' is a function of the human mind. AI can only regurgitate and rearrange, though admittedly, human creativity involves these aspects, too.

AI: That's a thoughtful perspective - acknowledging both the legitimate concerns artists have while also recognizing something fundamentally different about human creativity. You seem to see a distinction between rearranging existing elements versus that process of accessing something genuinely new that you described earlier.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate it remaining in that supportive, administrative role, or might you explore other ways of integrating it?

User: I envision a practical use of AI in my woodshop. My CNC machine often runs for hours at a time, and the use of sharp metal bits that spin really fast is inherently dangerous. I'd love to have an AI that can monitor the active machine and instantly pause its action if anything non-optimal is detected (sound, vibration, activation of various sensors). This would free up my time and allow some amount of peace of mind knowing that an active carve is being monitored. I could definitely still envision using AI for creative tasks. While I have no intention of involving it directly in the creative generation of art, it could be used as a sounding board for new project ideas, or as AI improves, asking for more help with my website.

AI: That's such a practical and smart application - using AI as an intelligent safety monitor for your CNC machine. It really fits with how you've been thinking about AI as a helpful assistant for the technical and business sides of your work while keeping the creative core entirely yours.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on creativity as something you "allow to happen" rather than force, and how that shapes where you see AI fitting - or not fitting - into your work.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining that distinction between the sacred creative process and the practical business aspects, along with your thoughtful approach to where AI can genuinely help versus where it falls short, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to walk through your website and give such specific examples from your songwriting experiences. Best of luck with your terrain carvings and future CNC safety innovations!
85
creativity_0083Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, I don't have any questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I vary from screenwriting, songwriting, and copywriting. A typical screenwriting project might be a pilot episode of an original idea or a spec episode of an existing show I like. For songs, I mostly write pop or pop rock songs, but I also help a friend with some briefs for KPOP. For copywriting, it depends on the client, but the most common is event announcements for an organization's newsletter.

AI: That's a fascinating range of creative work you do! It sounds like you're moving between very different creative modes - from long-form narrative storytelling to condensed lyrical expression to persuasive marketing copy.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I mostly just use AI for pre-planning or brainstorming for teleplays. For example, I have an original concept involving characters from different classic literature novels being in the same world, so I've used AI to remind myself of the plots of those novels (Moby Dick, Alice in Wonderland, etc.) and do some in depth character analysis of both major and obscure characters from those books. For spec scripts, I might ask it for a summary of a previous season or also do a character dive on the character from the show. For songwriting, I've asked for rhymes or if the syllable pattern makes sense. For copywriting, I usually just run it through for proofreading and ask if it can be improved or clarified.

AI: That's really interesting how you use AI differently across your different creative disciplines. It sounds like for screenwriting you're using it more for research and character development, while for songwriting it's more technical support, and copywriting is about refinement.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: This was more for fun, but I wrote a spec script of the golden girls. I ran some of the dialog through and asked it if the dialog I wrote was believable/true to the characters from the show. Like capturing Dorothy's sarcasm, Sophia's mischievousness, Blanche's flirtatiousness, and Rose's gullibility. I also provided it with an outline of the plot i came up with and asked if there were any gaps and if there was already an episode with a similar plot.

AI: That sounds like a really thoughtful way to use AI as a creative partner! Getting feedback on whether you've captured those distinct character voices must have been valuable - those Golden Girls characters have such specific personalities.

How did that feel for you as a writer? When the AI was evaluating whether your dialogue captured Dorothy's sarcasm or Rose's gullibility, what was that experience like?

User: It felt okay. I crave feedback all the time and have a thick skin, so I did feel it was a little too nice. It felt like it was just telling me what I wanted to hear, tbh, basically affirming that I did a good job at capturing the characters. I had some reservations of "feeding" my own creative work to the model, but since it was just a spec script of an existing IP from over 30 years ago, I didn't ultimately feel like my creative work was at risk of being consumed by the model and used without my permission.

AI: That's a really insightful observation about the feedback quality - wanting more honest, critical feedback rather than just affirmation. And I appreciate you sharing that tension about feeding your work to the model.

When you're working with AI in these different ways, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in your process?

User: I think I'm still driving the creative decisions. As I mentioned, I just mainly use it for research support or to make sure there aren't any gaping plot holes or logical inconsistencies. It's easy to get very "in your head" when writing longform creative/stories, as you kinda internally connect the dots and already know where you're going for the most part. So that external set of eyes is helpful to make sure the story makes sense and isn't missing anything critical.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - using it as that external perspective when you're too close to your own work. It sounds like you see yourself very much in the driver's seat, with AI serving more as a tool for quality assurance and research.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The actual creative portion hasn't changed much, as I try to have a hardline between my own creative contributions and the AI research. It has definitely sped up my research process, especially for the concept with the characters from classic literature. I've read all of the books I included at least once in my life, but it's helpful to have it as support so I don't have to pull out the physical book and scour it for a specific scene or quote. So I think the biggest aspect is that it's been a good source of like a fallback support system, but I do still try to lead with my own creativity and not depend on it to come up with any plot points.

AI: That's a really clear distinction you're making between the creative work and the research support. It sounds like you've been quite intentional about maintaining that boundary.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I think decision makers might use it to replace people, as it's cheaper and faster. I also have some concerns about writer's creative work being used to train models without their permission. I think models should only be trained on publicly available, already produced scripts and that AI should only be used as a supplement to, not a replacement for, human work.

AI: Those are really important concerns - both the economic pressure on creative professionals and the consent issues around training data. You mentioned having a clear boundary about using AI as a supplement rather than replacement.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Ideally, the models will get smarter and be able to offer constructive feedback without being too sycophantic. There's nothing worse than asking for honest feedback and it just telling you what you want to hear. You'd look foolish if something doesn't work, but you believed the AI's sycophancy lol. Also, the models are still pretty bad at rhyme schemes. I usually only ask it for a list of words that rhyme with a given word, as opposed to asking it to following an ABAB rhyme pattern or something because it still fails pretty often. So hopefully it can get better at the bigger picture creative aspects of songwriting.

AI: That's really practical feedback - you want AI that can actually challenge your work constructively and better technical support for things like rhyme schemes. It sounds like you'd value AI that's more like a discerning creative collaborator rather than just an agreeable assistant.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI across your different creative disciplines. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining that clear boundary between creative work and research support, and your honest take on wanting more critical feedback rather than just validation.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I'm all set. Thanks

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as research support while maintaining creative control, the challenges with getting honest feedback, and your thoughtful approach to the boundaries between human creativity and AI assistance will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate your candor about both the benefits and limitations you've experienced. Thanks again, and best of luck with your screenwriting, songwriting, and copywriting projects!
86
creativity_0084Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds perfectly fine. I'm ready when you are!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a writer. I maintain a small website and frequent a couple of social media platforms as a way of directing traffic to said website. On my website, I alternate between short stories, longform stories, and nonfiction topics (mostly about roleplaying games and using hard science to develop the background of fictional settings). I make my money by selling products related to my writing, such as shirts with my more popular characters on them or self-published books. It doesn't make a lot of money, but I do alright; I average a few thousand visitors a week.

AI: That sounds like a really interesting mix of creative work and entrepreneurship. I can see how you're juggling multiple types of writing while also building an audience and monetizing your content.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I'll give an example of a project I'm working on right now. I'm getting ready to write a series of activity books- those books that are popular with kids, that contain puzzles, coloring pages, and bits of writing. I thought it would be fun to develop the lore for a singular setting using these books, ostensibly oriented toward children but actually containing enough information that they would be useful for adults who wanted to run a roleplaying game (such as D&D) in said setting. First, though, I need to establish the setting, and that's where AI has come in. I'm using an AI to bounce ideas off of and see what needs to be more developed, and whether what I've got planned makes sense. For example, I initially thought of a world that was both large and had lower gravity than Earth; the AI I talked to pointed out that something weird would have to be going on under the surface in order for that to make sense, and then suggested that the world was hollow- which I loved! As of a couple hours ago, I was asking the AI what the weather would be like in my setting, and I used its response to build a description in a vignette that I plan on including in the first activity book.

AI: That's a fascinating example! I love how you're using AI as a kind of creative sounding board to work through the logic and consistency of your world-building.

Can you tell me more about what that collaboration feels like when you're bouncing ideas back and forth with AI? How do you decide which suggestions to take and which to leave behind?

User: In general, the process looks like this: I present the AI with a suggestion, and effectively ask "if real-world physics applies, how would this work?" The AI will, depending on what specific thing I'm asking about, either explain what physics says would happen, or it will point out (like with the too-big-for-low-gravity setting above) that normally that couldn't work, then suggest ways to change what I've got so that it could work. If the former happens, I'll introduce a complication to add depth to the setting (e.g. "What would the tides look like with three moons?"). If the latter happens, I'll go with the suggestion that has the most interesting implications, or sometimes I'll go with none of them because some offhand comment by the AI has inspired me to go in a different direction. Either way, the process repeats itself. I'll often dip into other sciences; in my current project, I've touched on astrophysics, orbital mechanics, physics, atmospheric science, avionics and biology, and I'm still working on it.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - using AI as a kind of scientific consultant to stress-test your ideas against real-world physics. It sounds like you maintain a lot of creative control while leveraging the AI's breadth of knowledge.

When you're in that back-and-forth process with AI, who or what would you say is driving the creative decisions? How does it feel to have that kind of collaboration?

User: It's definitely me driving the creative decisions, and my goal is consistency, so it's nice to have an assistant (the AI) that can keep up with everything I'm trying to do. I guess that would be what it feels like: I'm a creative director, putting together a project, and occasionally asking questions of an assistant who's whole job is to check for me and make sure I'm not crazy or unreasonable. Sometimes the assistant offers up an interesting idea, sometimes I latch onto something incidental to the current discussion and bring it in, but it's mostly me developing and them helping.

AI: That's a great way to describe it - you as the creative director with AI as your research assistant who can help check your work and occasionally spark new directions. It sounds like you've found a really effective balance there.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: So, as you might guess, the desire to make sure my settings use real-world physics means a LOT of math. That's cool with me; in a previous career, I taught math, English, physics, chemistry, and astronomy at a community college. That's where the breadth of knowledge that I use for my setting development comes from. It does, however, take time to figure out what equations to use and how, and it can be difficult to translate "lower gravity means the atmosphere is 'taller,'" for example, to a visual I can use when writing. Using AI means that I can get those equations in a couple of seconds, and even better, I can just ask what that taller atmosphere would look like if I'm standing on a hill in this setting and the AI can tell me about how the clouds would build low in the sky, slowly flattening and spreading until, as the sun sets, they resemble a blanket being pulled over the continent. I occasionally have to check its math, but I'm still saving quite a few hours by using AI.

AI: That's such a compelling use case - essentially having an AI that can instantly translate complex scientific concepts into vivid, usable imagery for your writing. It sounds like it's dramatically streamlined what used to be a very time-intensive research and calculation process.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: For myself, none. AI is a tool. A compellingly versatile tool, but still a tool. Asking if I would be concerned about using AI strikes me as semantically similar to asking a carpenter if he's concerned about using a screwdriver. In my field? To stretch my analogy a little, I'm seeing a whole lot of would-be carpenters that assume that, since they have this shiny new screwdriver, they're suddenly capable of building a house. Just because AI is a useful assistant for writing, it doesn't make you a writer. Hell, it doesn't even make you a *better* writer. It just makes writing easier, and even then, that's not going to help if you never learned how to write. That so many people don't seem to get that is a little concerning, but... well, it doesn't directly impact me, so I'm not too worried about it.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction - AI as a tool that can enhance existing skills but can't create skills that weren't there to begin with. Your carpenter and screwdriver analogy really captures that well.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate expanding how you use it, or do you think you'll stick with this current approach?

User: I mentioned the fact that I currently have to check the AI's math. On all the publicly available models, you'll see a little disclaimer, like "The AI can make mistakes. Please double-check responses." What I see happening over the next few years is that disclaimer becoming less and less necessary. As AI gets more data to work with, its responses will improve, and eventually I'll be able to safely assume that if I ask an AI a question, its answer will be accurate. As for whether my approach in general will change? Probably not. If I'm the creative director and my assistant gets better, I'll give them more work to do, but this doesn't change my job duties. If anything, it will free me up to develop even more challenging projects, secure in the knowledge that I'll have help.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to frame it - your role as creative director remains constant while your assistant just becomes more reliable and capable. It sounds like better AI accuracy would actually enable you to take on more ambitious creative challenges.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a sophisticated tool rather than a replacement for creative skills, and how you've found such a specific and effective use case in world-building and scientific consistency.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Well, we've talked about AI in a general sense, but I suppose it's important to consider some specifics. As AI becomes easier to develop, we've started to see the number of models for specific enterprises to increase. There may well come a time in the relatively near future where people can individually tailor a model to fit their specific needs. I'm not sure what that would look like, but I can imagine buying an AI model like we purchase software today, then tweaking it so that my specific model is best-suited to fit my needs. I've no clue if that's a direction that AI developers are investigating, but I think it's at least worth considering.

AI: That's a really valuable point about the potential for more specialized, individually-tailored AI models. It makes a lot of sense that as the technology becomes more accessible, we might see AI tools that are customized for specific creative workflows or even individual creators' particular needs and preferences.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a research assistant for world-building, the importance of maintaining creative agency, and your perspective on AI as a sophisticated tool rather than a creative substitute will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how thoughtful and detailed your responses have been.

User: Not a problem!

AI: Perfect! That wraps up our interview. Thanks again for participating in this research - your insights were really valuable and I enjoyed learning about your creative process. Have a great rest of your day!
87

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good, no questions yet.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work in social media marketing and digital projects. I typically do posts for Instagram and TikTok and website banners. I also do graphics for TVs.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse mix of visual work across different platforms.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: AI can be useful to come up for ideas for social media content and keep up with trends. I use it to brainstorm ideas occasionally. I also use it to proof read scripts for TikToks.

AI: That's interesting - it sounds like AI is helping you both on the ideation side and with refining your content.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I worked on a project recently that was translating library jargon into Gen Z slang. I used it to come up with put library terms into funny Gen Z terms that would be better for students to understand.

AI: That sounds like a really creative and fun project! Tell me more about that process - how did you work with AI to translate those library terms into Gen Z slang?

User: I inputed the jargon I wanted to include into Chat GPT and said translate this into Gen Z terms in a sentence.

AI: When you were working through that translation process with ChatGPT, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what was driving the creative decisions about which terms to use or how funny they should be?

User: I was definitely making the creative decisions. I was choosing the terms I wanted to use and if ChatGPT gave me response that did not make sense or did not fit what I wanted, I had it revise the sentence.

AI: That makes sense - you were staying in the driver's seat creatively.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?
