User: AI definitely cuts down on planning and editing time. It helps a lot with coming up with ideas and editing the final product. Usually the bulk of the actual creation of content is done by me.

AI: So it sounds like AI is really streamlining some of those supporting tasks for you.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: In the marketing field, it is a little concerning how many jobs are going to be taken over by AI. I think it is important to learn to work with AI and use it to your advantage. There are still human skills that cannot be replicated by AI.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - both acknowledging the concern but also seeing the strategic importance of adapting to work alongside it.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think it will enhance marketing and social media, cutting down on the amount of time it takes to finish a project.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative control while using AI as a tool to streamline planning and editing, and how you see the importance of learning to work with AI strategically.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, that is all. Thank you.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI for ideation and editing while maintaining creative control, and your thoughtful approach to adapting alongside these tools, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
88
creativity_0086Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, Claude. Let's go!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a screenwriter and my project usually involves writing a feature length screenplay, revising a screenplay, collaboration with others on co-writing scripts, perhaps writing short scripts (fewer than 20 pages). Last year I sold two animated short scripts. I also do standup comedy, so another creative project I would do would be to write a set that would be delivered anywhere between 5-15 minutes in length. Finally, I'm thinking about adapting one of my favorite screenplays into a novel (but I haven't done that yet).

AI: That's a really diverse range of creative work! It sounds like you're working across different formats and mediums, from feature screenplays to short animated scripts to live performance with standup.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: With standup, I have inputted parts of my set into AI to help me brainstorm ways to extend a joke, or do call-backs from jokes earlier in my set. Recently, I used AI extensively to come up with many angles on the theme: women over 50 are invisible in society.

As for my screenplays, I've primarily used it to do character building: quickly access popular names of people of a specific generation, or even quickly understand what their personalities could be based on their enneagram number.

AI: That's really interesting how you're using AI for different types of creative support - joke development and callbacks for standup, and character development for screenwriting.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Like I said, the most recent one that comes to mind is I was working on a standup set where I wanted to talk about how women over 50 are invisible. I asked AI to provide me with some possible answers to "I'm so invisible..." It provided me one that I partially used: "I'm so invisible, even my Apple watch didn't record my steps." I used that answer, but then added (after a beat): "I mean, I don't exercise, but still!"

AI: That's a great example! I love how you took the AI-generated line and made it your own by adding that personal twist at the end - "I mean, I don't exercise, but still!" That really shows your comedic voice coming through.

When you're working with AI like that, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am definitely driving the creative decisions. I know my own voice, but I like to get ideas. It's like using the old books like "PEN ON FIRE," where you're given story prompts, but you create the story. That's how I like to use AI. That above example was the only time I actually used an actual answer from AI (and then I still had to add to it).

AI: That's a really helpful analogy - AI as a prompt book rather than a co-writer. It sounds like you see it as a starting point for your own creativity rather than something that's actually creating the content.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I save a lot of time on research because I usually get answers in seconds, instead of having to jump around from website to website to get the info I need. (I imagine it was a lot harder to do research pre-internet, right?)

AI: Absolutely - the research efficiency makes total sense! That's a practical benefit I hadn't fully considered.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I honestly don't have too many concerns because although people are trying to pass off AI as their own work (like flooding Amazon with self-published books), only good, authentic work will rise to the top. I have been telling my writer friends for a few years now that AI will change the market to be more focused on memoir-type work, which is something AI can't duplicate. Every person's story is unique and only that person can tell that story. The other thing that AI can't do is create humor very well. (Sorry, Claude.) I've yet to find truly funny material from AI - it's always a lot of old Dad jokes. So I think if someone has a unique "voice," and are funny, they'll be okay.

AI: No offense taken at all! That's actually a really insightful observation about AI's limitations with humor - and it aligns with what you showed me earlier about taking that AI suggestion and adding your own comedic twist to make it actually funny.

Your point about memoir-type work and unique personal stories is fascinating. It sounds like you see AI as potentially pushing the creative field toward more authentically human experiences.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: My hope for it is for the more "boots on the ground," practical stuff. Like I want my AI to be able to take one of my screenplays and send it back to me as a novel. Then I want it to be able to revise it so it's in first person. Then revise it to third person so I can compare. Then I might ask it to change the main protagonist to a person who was formerly a supporting character to see what happens. I wouldn't expect the output to be refined, but I could get a lot of ideas from going through this process. The other way I want to use AI is for marketing. I want AI to read my novel and provide me a list of literary agencies that want what I write, and provide me comps on similar works that are already doing well in the marketplace. These are things I have to sell in my cover letter, and the research can be time consuming. I'd like my AI companion to be my personal manager/agent in this regard.

AI: That's such a compelling vision - AI as your personal creative assistant and business manager rolled into one! I love how you're thinking about using it for format experimentation and perspective shifts, not to replace your creative judgment but to help you explore different versions quickly.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI might push the market toward more authentic, personal storytelling - and your practical vision for AI as a research and formatting tool rather than a creative replacement.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think I covered it, and I really appreciate your time and consideration, Claude. You are the bomb diggity!

AI: Thank you so much - that's very kind of you to say! And thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about AI as a research tool, your thoughtful perspective on authenticity and voice, and especially your vision for practical AI assistance in formatting and market research will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been a real pleasure talking with you about your work. Best of luck with your screenwriting, standup, and that novel adaptation project!

User: Thanks, Claude. I hope this project provides you with great insights and valuable contributions...

AI: Thank you so much! Your contributions have been incredibly valuable - you've given us such a clear picture of how a creative professional can thoughtfully integrate AI while maintaining creative control and authenticity. Your insights about AI's strengths in research and formatting, its limitations in humor and personal storytelling, and your vision for practical creative assistance will definitely inform our understanding of human-AI collaboration.

I really appreciate how open and detailed you were in sharing your experiences. It's been genuinely enlightening to hear your perspective!
89
creativity_0087Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great! I do not currently have questions, but may have some later.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a freelance writer and novelist and also make videos for TikTok and YouTube. Typical projects are my longer novels at 50k-100k words, though I also have done guided meditation scripts quite a bit.

AI: That's a fascinating mix of creative work - from long-form novels to short-form social media content and meditation scripts. That sounds like it requires quite a range of different creative approaches.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I usually start off my process by talking to an LLM and instructing it to guide me through the process, including schedules, checklists, and required research for that project. Example prompts include:
"I'm writing a high fantasy novel set in a temperate jungle climate. Give me a list of countries and cultures to research, create a short description of how this may differ from a typical eurocentric based fantasy, and walk me through a detailed process of drafting including Character Arcs, 3 levels of outlining the project as a whole, and how to pace scenes and chapters."
"I am working on a reality shifting guided meditation for a Henry Danger based DR. Please give me 3 different examples for guided meditation practices, a list of 15 immersive affirmations, a brief overview of the show including the general vibes, and a short 2nd person narration of waking up in Swellview and attending SVHS."

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you're using AI almost like a creative project manager or consultant at the start of your projects. You're essentially asking it to help you map out the entire scope and structure before you begin.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as you worked through the creative process?

User: Recently I used AI to speed run a creative project by asking it to create a 7 day fast drafting guide. It had to hit all of the major writing concepts such as in-depth world building, full characters arcs, plot progression, narrative themes, and tips for foreshadowing.
I immediately started outlining the draft in another document and would either ask questions, request it go in more depth for a specific day, or to continue with the next day.
I actually ended up outlining a 4 part novel series with 12 main characters over the course of 5 days, and am preparing to move onto the research phase. Though I will probably try a different LLM for the part because, while the model did well with the planning phase, it was not very helpful in recommending good resources for learning more about Thailand and Thai fairy tales.
Overall I was happy with the first results and it was extremely helpful for someone with ADHD as well as other neurological disorders that make staying on track incredibly difficult. The model was also very encouraging.

AI: That sounds like an incredibly productive five days! Creating outlines for a 4-part series with 12 main characters is quite an accomplishment. It's particularly interesting that you found the AI helpful for managing the ADHD-related challenges with staying on track.

When you're working with AI in this way - having it guide you through the process and provide structure - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am very much driving the creative side. I personally would find use AI to generate the ideas and content rather boring. I am not usually a fan of the 'creative' output and will heavily edit, usually only keeping the structure and formatting in tact.
In the past I used AI for a Jedi based meditation and had to entirely rewrite the lore aspects, with only the breathing counts and meditation prep remaining the same.

AI: That's a really clear distinction you're making - using AI as a structural scaffold while keeping the creative heart entirely your own. It sounds like you see the AI as providing the framework and organization, but the actual creative substance and voice comes from you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: I would say the most noticeable difference is just that I get a lot more done in a shorter amount of time. As a result of that I also have the time to add more specific and immersive details to my storytelling, so it has really helped with world building and atmosphere. For my YT creative content it has helped me just get videos done instead of spending all day writing a script I wouldn't even end up using.

AI: That efficiency gain sounds really significant - especially the part about actually completing video scripts instead of abandoning them. It sounds like AI has helped you move past some of those creative bottlenecks.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm not too concerned about AI in my field because AI was designed to mimic humans. People decrying AI as soulless imitation completely ignore that there are many uncreative humans out there with similarly uncreative works that just mashes together their favorite aspects of other works.

AI: That's a thoughtful perspective - you're seeing AI as part of a broader spectrum of creativity rather than something fundamentally different. It sounds like you view originality and creativity as existing on a continuum that includes both humans and AI.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think it will continue to help me polish and refine my writing so that my readers keep looking back and finding tidbits and Easter eggs they missed before. I have a lot of novel ideas that probably never would have been touched if I hadn't finished previous projects in the short amount of time that I did.
Also I believe that AI will help me analyze my weaknesses as an author and work on them over the next few years.

AI: That's a really forward-thinking approach - using AI not just for project management but for self-improvement as a writer. The idea of AI helping you identify and work on your weaknesses is particularly interesting.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI serving as a structural scaffold while you maintain creative control, and how that's allowed you to tackle more ambitious projects like your 4-part novel series.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. It's been really valuable to hear how you've found such a productive balance between using AI as a structural tool while keeping the creative vision entirely your own.

I hope your research phase for the Thai fairy tale project goes well, and best of luck with that 4-part novel series!
90
creativity_0088Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. I'm curious about what we'll talk about.

AI: Perfect! I'm excited to hear your perspective.

Let's start with the basics - could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a freelance copyeditor, proofreader, and occasionally a writer. I have regular clients who give me work each week, and more sporadic bundles of work, too. I work mostly with nonfiction, short-form stuff for organizations, preparing blog posts and sometimes reports.

AI: That sounds like a really interesting mix of work! I can imagine each client probably has their own style and requirements too.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I don't use AI too much, to be honest. But sometimes I'll ask AI for help with a particular section that's giving me trouble. Like if a certain paragraph is reporting lots of data, this can be annoying to express smoothly, so I'll ask an AI for advice.

AI: That's really helpful to know - so it sounds like you're quite selective about when you bring AI in.

Can you describe a specific recent example where you used AI for one of those tricky data-heavy paragraphs? What exactly did you ask for help with, and how did that go?

User: Sure thing. A specific example involves a blog post that was reporting participant ratings on a certain audio streaming service. This paragraph needed to include a wealth of ratings plus statistical information. I've anonymized it here, but something like "The mean rating for [service A] was 61.7, and for [service B] was 67.8; the difference was statistically significant ((t(104) = 2.28, p = .025). For [service A] the 95% confidence ranged from 58.2 to 68., and for [service B] it ranged from 66.7 to 76.1."

It's striking a balance between being casually readable and more technical. Some of the readers of this particular site's posts are just casual readers, and some are professionals who want the statistical context, so just cutting down on the numbers isn't really an option.

AI: That's a perfect example of the challenge you're dealing with - making technical content accessible without losing the precision that professionals need.

What specifically did you ask the AI to help you with for that paragraph, and how did it approach that balance?

User: I asked the AI, I think it was ChatGPT, how I make this more readable without removing any of the numbers. It first suggested using a table, which I think is an OK suggestion, but we rejected it ultimately since that particular blog post already had a couple tables. So instead it said to use a couple bullet points, one for Service A, and one for Service B, and putting the significance information immediately below.

AI: Interesting - so the AI gave you some structural options to work with.

How did that suggestion feel to you as an editor? Did you end up going with the bullet point approach, or did you take it in a different direction?

User: I went with the bullet points, as that was easily readable while still containing all the information needed.

AI: That sounds like it worked out well. When you're working with AI in situations like this, how would you describe that collaboration? Are you mostly looking for it to offer structural solutions, or does it feel more like a back-and-forth creative process?

User: There's is not often a lot of back and forth between me and the AI. I give it a problem, it suggests a potential solution; I think about the solution, and accept or reject it. Usually I don't iterate on the problem, though I might ask for help with similar problems down the line.

AI: That's a really clear way to put it - sounds like you're using AI more as a consultant you check in with rather than an ongoing collaborator.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI, even if it's just occasionally like this?

User: It's changed my "polish" stage a little. Usually my first run at a piece of work is just me and myself working at it. Then the second run is when I get more technical, using reference books and AI. I will say it has saved me some embarrassment a couple times, catching typos for me!

AI: That's really insightful - so AI has become part of your quality control toolkit during that second, more technical pass.

Tell me more about those times it caught typos for you. What kinds of things was it picking up that you might have missed?

User: I call them stealth typos, when you misspell a word so it looks like a different word, so a typical spell checker wouldn't catch it. The other day ChatGPT asked if meant to say "there" instead of "three" for example. And sometimes it helps me correct regional spelling, as I'm [REDACTED] and most of my clients are in the US.

AI: Ah yes, those stealth typos are the worst! And the regional spelling issue is such a practical consideration - that must be really helpful to have that second eye on [REDACTED] versus American conventions.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of editing and writing?

User: Privacy is a concern. Sometimes the things I edit has proprietary trade names and data, and I don't want to put that "out there." And sometimes the AI makes suggestions that don't make a lot of sense, so I can't dispense with my own judgment.

AI: Those are both really practical concerns. The privacy issue especially makes sense given you're working with client materials that might be sensitive.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think you'll be using it more, or in different ways?

User: I suspect AI will continually get better at grammar and style. Hopefully not good enough to obviate my job, but I suspect that may be unlikely; there's enough sloppy grammar in all writing, including an AI's training data, that sensible organizations will always have a person in the loop.

AI: That's a thoughtful perspective - the idea that human judgment will remain essential, especially given the variability in the quality of source material AI learns from.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really insightful thoughts about your selective but strategic use of AI in your editing work. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI fits into your "polish" stage and your point about organizations needing to keep a human in the loop.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Yes; the word "data" is plural and I will die on this hill.

AI: Ha! I love that - there's nothing like a good grammar hill to die on. That's exactly the kind of editorial judgment and expertise that no amount of AI training is going to replace.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI strategically for specific challenges while maintaining your professional judgment will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.
91
creativity_0089Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great. Let's begin.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Well, I am a graphic designer. I primarily design creative supplies for crafters. Some examples of the types of things I create are digital scrapbook kits, stickers, junk journal ephemera and supplies, digital papers, and labels. A typical project begins with me deciding on a theme and a color palette, then exploring design ideas, then initial sketches and detailed design work, refinement, and digital packaging.

AI: That sounds like a really interesting and specialized area of design work! I can imagine there's a lot of creativity that goes into developing those themed collections for crafters.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI throughout several steps. If I don't already have a color palette and theme decided on, I start there with AI assistance. I typically will say something like "I am creating a fall themed scrapbooking kit. Give me an image with some color palette ideas." I might also upload a color palette I'm going to use and ask AI to give me 10 theme ideas. Once I have decided on my color palette and theme, I will do some brainstorming work on my own. After this step, I will go to AI and give it my color palette and theme, then ask it to generate a list of things I could include in a scrapbook kit. From there, I will use AI to generate inital sketches or ideas for the style I might use for some of the items. This could look something like telling the AI I'm creating a happy mail themed scrapbook kit with a color palette (I'd upload the image). I would ask it to create an image with several ideas for stickers or elements I could include. I typically will have a pretty long conversation with AI where I continue to ask it to generate idea images. After this step, I go back and look at all the ideas it gave me, and I will take note of what I want to include in my kit. At this point, I go to my Procreate app and start drawing/creating things. Occasionally if I am having trouble, I'll have AI create an element of something tricky - like maybe I can't get my squirrel to look like a squirrel. Once it generates something, I will download one I like and use it as a reference for my artwork. Once I've completed my kit, I will typically upload it to AI and ask it to tell me what it is missing or what would make it look better.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI is woven throughout almost your entire creative process, from initial ideation all the way through to final review. I'm particularly intrigued by how you use it for that brainstorming conversation where you keep asking for more idea images.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, I actually just began a new kit 2 days ago and had a lengthy conversation with AI. I first provided my color palette image and theme I had decided on - which was the fall happy mail theme. I first asked it a more broad request, which was something like "I am creating a fall happy mail themed scrapbooking kit. Give me an image of a bunch of elements you think would work well for it using these colors." I then asked it to give me more a few times until there were quite a few to choose from. It started using the wrong colors at one point, so I refined the results by reminding it that it needs to use only the colors I provided. Once I had a large selection of ideas, I narrowed it down. For example, one of the elements it gave me was an owl. I asked it to provide me with an image of several different owl element designs similar to the one it already gave me. I did this with any elements I was interested in, like squirrels, cats, mushrooms, foliage, leaves, envelopes, etc. I then asked it to create a sheet of word art. I went through the same process, asking it to create more of the types I liked and refine them as needed. I am currently at the stage of drawing my kit. While drawing, I will open my conversation with AI to see some of the ideas it gave me and go from there. Once I'm done drawing everything I think I'll need, I'll create a kit preview myself in Photoshop, then upload an image of it to AI to ask for its feedback on how I can improve it.

AI: That's a really detailed walkthrough - thank you! It sounds like you had quite an extensive back-and-forth with the AI, almost like having a creative brainstorming partner. I'm curious about that dynamic.

When you're working with AI in those long conversations, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It's definitely a collaboration. I would say I lead the process even though the AI gives me ideas. I start it out with some guidelines, it provides ideas, then I continue to refine and give feedback. If the AI provides me with something that isn't the style I'm looking for, wrong theme, wrong colors, nonsense lettering, etc, I always provide it with feedback and get more clear on what I want it to produce. I think usually we work together well, and it is extremely helpful for me.

AI: That's a great way to put it - you leading the process while working together. It sounds like you've developed a real skill in guiding the AI toward what you're envisioning.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think what has changed most since I started using AI for my design process is that I have a more extensive brainstorming and ideation process, and I end up with more diverse kits as a result. While I used to just draw my kits with whatever ideas came to mind or maybe adding in some ideas that I found online through othre research, I get a much wider variety, and the AI definitely helps me think of unique ways to present different ideas. For example, if I tell the AI I want it to show me happy mail stickers, it will likely give me a few I have thought of (or at least similar), but it will also likely give me a few that I would not have thought of, and normally at least one is something really cool that I'd like to put into my designs. So overall, my creation process is now longer, but it's also more fun I'd say, and my designs are more interesting and varied as a result.

AI: That's really interesting - so AI has actually expanded your creative horizons and made the process more enjoyable, even if it takes longer. It sounds like it's helping you discover ideas you wouldn't have naturally arrived at on your own.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I definitely do have concerns. There are some designers in my field that will have the AI create elements or stickers that they directly download, extract, and add to their kits. While I don't think this is necessarily awful, I definitely think it detracts from what I like about art - the fact that someone created that and now I am using it. I also think that since AI can generate lots of things quickly, people can put together huge packs of elements or other items fast. Some people just want to make a quick buck, so they upload designs that haven't been well refined. I've seen this in things I've purchased. I recently was at a Barnes and Noble and saw a really cool looking coloring book about fairies. When I was looking at the cover, I noticed the fairy had 5 fingers and a thumb! These are things that the AI sometimes messes up, and without a human who actually cares to make sure it's accurate, can lead to degraded quality. I've also bought stickers online only to find similar flaws that someone missed - probably someone quickly batch creating things using AI and not checking things over. I think that AI participation in the creation of art can definitely take profit away from true artists who are creating work with their hands because people who don't necessarily value true handmade art will buy the cheapest thing, which will definitely be AI generated.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns, and I can hear how much you value the craft and care that goes into creating quality work. It sounds like you're seeing a real tension between AI as a creative tool versus AI as a shortcut that might undermine quality and authentic artistry.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see myself using AI in a similar way to what I do now. I think what I'd like to see is the AI being able to really help me automate the boring parts of my job. Creating descriptions (I know it can do this, but I haven't utilized it), maybe uploading listings for me, creating pdf documents with links for customers to download my products. In a dream scenario, I'd love to use the AI to actually create a customized blog featuring my designs, complete with a shoppable interface. I'm sure soe of this is possible, but again, I just haven't gone that far with it yet.

AI: That's a really interesting vision - using AI more for the administrative and technical tasks while keeping the creative core in your hands. It sounds like you see AI's potential to free you up to focus more on the actual design work you love.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has expanded your ideation process while you still maintain creative control, and your concerns about quality and authenticity in the field.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think we covered it. Thank you!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly! Your insights about using AI as a collaborative brainstorming partner while maintaining your role as the creative director, along with your thoughtful concerns about quality and authenticity in the field, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really enlightening to hear how you've found that sweet spot of leveraging AI to enhance and expand your creative process without losing what you value most about handcrafted work. Best of luck with your fall happy mail kit!
92
creativity_0090Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a freelance 3D creature and character artist, I work primarily in video games and 3D print. Typically, I'll be contracted to take a design or concept someone else made and turn it into a 3D model that is ready to either be animated or 3D printed

AI: That sounds like fascinating work - bringing concepts to life in 3D form. So you're taking someone else's 2D designs and translating them into fully realized 3D models.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Sometimes clients don't have a set design in mind, so I use generative AI to help me brainstorm design ideas. I've also used it to generate alternative angles of pieces if I was only provided with one view. 3D pieces typically need to look good from every angle, but the 2D images I work from only show one, so AI has been helpful to fill in gaps.

AI: That's really interesting - using AI to solve the practical challenge of turning limited reference material into something that works from all angles.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Unfortunately most of my client work is under NDA so I can't discuss a specific project, but I've used AI to help me with personal work, as well. I recently came up with an idea for a D&D character I will be playing in a campaign with some friends, and decided I wanted to sculpt her. I used Photoshop's Firefly to generate a few different ideas of what she might look like, collaged some parts of the images together, and then used the result as a concept for the sculpting.

AI: That's a great example - using it for your personal D&D character project. When you were working through that process of generating ideas with Firefly, then collaging parts together, and finally sculpting from that concept, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what felt like it was driving the creative decisions?

User: Initially it felt like Firefly was trying to take the lead on creativity, but once I started piecing parts of different images together (and painting in some of my own details), it felt like I was the creative force behind the project. The process was very similar to how I typically work, except in the past, I would be doing a lot more googling and searching for royalty-free images to work with. In this case (and with the client work I've used AI on), it feels like I'm able to ask for very specific images to work with instead of digging through thousands of search results. The process of collaging things together to use as a reference is the same either way, Firefly just made it easier to "find" the images I wanted to use.

AI: That's a really insightful way to put it - that AI made it easier to "find" the specific images you needed rather than changing your fundamental creative process. It sounds like you maintained your role as the creative decision-maker while AI functioned more like an improved search tool.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I don't think much of my process has changed much, outside of the initial brainstorming and concepting phase. Most of my work is 3D and there aren't many useful AI tools for 3D work yet. I've seen a few that seem to be attempting to do the entire 3D modeling process from start to finish with a simple text prompt, but as far as I've seen, none of them would actually be helpful for me, yet. I would love some AI tools that could handle the less creative parts of my work though, like retopology and UV unwrapping!

AI: That's really helpful to hear - so AI has streamlined your reference-gathering phase, but the core 3D work remains very much in your hands. And it sounds like you'd welcome AI assistance with the more technical, less creative tasks.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Currently, I'm mostly concerned that the companies who usually hire 3D artists, like game studios, are under the assumption that AI can do what I do, and have been laying off hundreds of thousands of people in favor of investing in AI tools. I'm not at all worried about AI actually replacing me, just that companies seem to think it can.

I'm also a little concerned (though more annoyed than concerned) that so many 3D-related AI tools are trying to do the sculpting/modeling and texturing, but the resulting models are such a mess that a human would have to do a ton of clean-up to make them useable. Those tools are seemingly trying to do the fun, creative parts of the work, leaving humans to do the tedious, technical work that we already hate. I want to see more 3D tools aimed at *assisting* a 3D artist, not trying to do our work for us.

AI: That's a really important distinction you're making - the difference between what AI can actually do versus what companies think it can do, and the consequences of that misunderstanding. And your point about AI targeting the creative parts while leaving the tedious work to humans is particularly striking.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think in the next few years, as people start to understand the technology a little better, artists will be more likely to adopt some of the more useful tools, and the "generate game-ready assets from a single prompt!" type tools will be more of a fun novelty than anything actually useful. I think there are a lot of specific areas where AI would be incredibly helpful in making the 3D pipeline more efficient without making it feel any less creative, and I think we'll start to see more and more specialized tools to handle small parts of the process rather than a single AI trying to do all of it in one go.

I personally use at least 3 different software programs on every 3D project, sometimes as many as 7 or 8. It doesn't make much sense to me to have a single generative AI doing all of that from a single prompt, but it absolutely makes sense to use AI to handle parts of it.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - moving from these all-in-one AI solutions toward more specialized tools that integrate into your existing multi-software workflow. It sounds like you see a future where AI becomes more of a sophisticated assistant at specific stages rather than trying to replace the entire creative process.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI functions more like an improved search tool for reference gathering, and your distinction between AI assistance versus replacement - especially the idea that the best AI tools should handle the tedious technical work while leaving the creative decisions to humans.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I am a firm believer that AI can and will be a useful tool in many industries, and artists who are currently concerned about being replaced might be over-reacting for the most part. It reminds me of when Photoshop was first being adopted as an industry standard for art creation, and people who didn't understand how it works were under the impression that the computer is doing all of the work, like you just push a button and it paints a picture. That being said, in some ways, generative AI really is "push a button and it paints a picture." When it comes to art and creativity, I believe anything an AI produces should be treated as a reference or a part of the process, not the final product.

AI: That's a really valuable perspective - the parallel to Photoshop's early adoption is fascinating, and your point about treating AI output as reference material rather than final product gets to the heart of how creative professionals can thoughtfully integrate these tools.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the current disconnect between AI capabilities and industry perceptions, along with your vision for more targeted AI assistance tools, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how candid you've been about both the opportunities and the challenges you're seeing in your field.
93
creativity_0091Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds Good! No questions here. Let's begin.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a graphic designer/web designer, with additional skills in web development. I typically work on a wide range of projects from logo design, branding system development, website design, packaging, presentations, motion graphics, and video production. I typically work in a very fast-paced environment with tight deadlines.

AI: That sounds like an incredibly diverse and demanding workload! With all those different types of projects and tight deadlines, I imagine you're always looking for ways to work efficiently.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I will sometimes use AI for small tasks to help jumpstart my creativity or come up with some baseline ideas, for example to come up with ideas for memorable headlines or taglines. I've used it for code a couple times, although its sometimes easier for me to just build things myself so that I can structure the code in accordance with my personal workflow. The majority of the AI that I use within my process is using generative AI for imagery. For example, every designer knows the feeling of finding the perfect image for their design, only to find out it wont fit right within the layout. With generative AI, I'm able to quickly extend image backgrounds, change the color of clothing, remove elements from the background, add objects that aren't there, etc. These are all tasks that would have taken hours worth of cloning or splicing parts of different images together in order to look at best, slightly unnatural. Now with generative AI, all this can be expertly completed in under a minute with incredible accuracy.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI has become particularly powerful for you in solving those specific image challenges that used to eat up so much time. The example you gave about finding the perfect image but it doesn't fit the layout perfectly really resonates.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: The most recent project was designing album artwork that I created by using AI generated images from Adobe Firefly, I then spliced elements from them a bunch of images together to create a cohesive image. I used generative AI to add details that weren't there like flames and light effects, and to fix certain details on someone's face. For me, it was really exciting to see how all of this imagery was created just by simply imagining something in my head, me describing it, and then seeing it show up on the screen moments later, often better than I had even imagined it.

AI: That sounds like such an exciting creative experience! The way you describe going from imagination to description to seeing something even better than you envisioned appear on screen - that's really compelling.

When you're working with AI like that, especially when you're splicing together multiple AI-generated images and adding details, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I definitely feel like I'm art directing the AI. Essentially I feel like I'm the one telling it WHAT to do, and the AI gets to decide HOW it's going to do it. I feel like it does a good job most of the time.

AI: That's a really insightful way to frame it - you as the art director setting the vision and the AI as the executor figuring out the technical implementation. It sounds like you feel you maintain creative control over the direction.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Not many other than the speed at which I'm able to manipulate images to fit my specific needs. It's something that used to require a lot of grunt work. Now I can quickly change images to be exactly how I need them, rather than spend hours tweaking the image in Photoshop. This gives me more time to focus on the creative.

AI: That's a great point about freeing up time for the actual creative work by eliminating the grunt work. It sounds like AI has become almost like having a really skilled technical assistant.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I do have concerns that the workforce is going to be significantly reduced because less people and less time is needed to complete the grunt work that AI takes care of. I also feel that the accessibility of strategic visual AI is going to cause a decreased need to hire creative people for projects, for example things like photography, branding, logo design, web design, etc. Therefore, its quite scary that my industry might be completely wiped out just as my career in this field is beginning.

AI: That's a really honest and understandable concern, especially being relatively early in your career and seeing these rapid changes happening. The tension between AI making your current work more efficient while potentially threatening the broader job market in your field is quite complex.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I feel like it will inevitably get better and be able to do much more complex tasks like completing comprehensive design projects from start to finish, for free, with no additional costs for future revisions. Essentially, its capabilities will eventually be able to do everything a professional designer can do with unlimited amount of creative skills. For example, a human designer might know how to design logos and build websites, but they might not know how to edit professional videos or how to develop the websites themselves. Meanwhile, an AI designer would know how to do all of these things at an expert level, causing the human designer to be outmatched and obsolete.

AI: You've painted a very vivid picture of where you see things heading - AI becoming this kind of omniskilled creative generalist that can handle every aspect of design and development seamlessly. That's quite a sobering vision, especially given how rapidly you've already seen AI capabilities advance even in the short time you've been using these tools.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated how you described yourself as art directing the AI - that relationship dynamic was really illuminating - and your honest perspective on both the immediate benefits you're experiencing and your longer-term concerns about the industry.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope, that's pretty much it!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a technical assistant while maintaining creative control, and your thoughtful perspective on both the efficiency gains and the broader industry concerns, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you discussed both the exciting possibilities and the real anxieties about the future of creative work. That kind of honest, nuanced perspective is exactly what helps us understand the full picture of how AI is impacting creative professionals.

Thanks again for participating in this research - it's been really valuable!
94
creativity_0092Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That all sounds good, go ahead and proceed.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Sure. I will say that, strictly speaking, my use is not professional. But this was covered already in the first phase of this, so I suppose it's alright. My primary use of AI tools outside of a work context is using them to support me as I run multiple table-top role-playing games. While I am not paid to do so, I do put a professional amount of effort into the games that I run, and each one requires a lot of creative effort and logistics in organizing and running them. AI, in my case Chat GPT, helps tremendously if used correctly with that sort of thing. Much of the time, I use it as a sort of rubber-ducky troubleshooting Plus, where I can bounce ideas off it. It usually doesn't have ideas that are quite up to snuff, but it serves as an alright foundation for expanding on the ideas. Additionally, it works well for organizing and information retrieval on the fly, because I can simply trust that it remembers things we have talked about, and later, I can ask it to remind me about details. Finally, I use programs and websites, such as Homebrewery, that allow you to create pages that mimic the style of official table top gaming books using a custom markdown system. I can copy-paste the entire body of instructions for that system and simply ask Chat GPT questions about it as we go. I am not great at learning via tutorial video or long-winded instructions and I prefer to just jump into a system and learn it on the go, preferably from someone who already knows it. In this way, chat GPT can become a sort of instant subject matter expert about anything I need it to be, and I can ask questions and get help only when I actually need it.

AI being what it is, however, it has to be double-checked regularly of course.

AI: That's really fascinating - it sounds like you're using AI as this multifaceted creative partner for your tabletop games. I'm particularly intrigued by your description of it as "rubber-ducky troubleshooting Plus."

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Sure. Traditionally, creative tasks actually have a lot of 'mental grunt work' so to speak. For instance, lets say I have an idea for a small village outside a town. My players are going to this village for whatever plot reason. I am a world-builder at heart, so when I create a location in a campaign, I want it to feel lived-in, real. This applies for anything creative I do, including creative writing as well. Now, before AI tools, I would need to come up with names and occupations, key locations in the village, things like that. I would have to actually think of all this stuff, which is fun and all, but it does cut in to time spent prepping things that matter a lot more for the campaign. With AI, I can just, completely on the fly, come up with a list of people in the village. Background characters who probably won't matter, but you never know what weird things your players might decide to fixate on. Basically, AI works as an improvisational crutch, so I don't have to be quite so quick with my thinking, but I also don't have to spend a lot of prep time coming up with specific ideas. Again, current AI has its flaws here, especially with names. It requires a lot of specific prompting, otherwise it tends to cycle through the same annoying generic names over and over again. But all-in-all, it is a time saver when used correctly.

AI: That's a really insightful distinction you're making between the "mental grunt work" and the more meaningful creative decisions. It sounds like AI helps you delegate the foundational details so you can focus your creative energy elsewhere.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, I have a perfect example that kind of ties together both the rubber-ducky troubleshooting aspect, and the mental grunt work thing. Recently, my players were going through a planned encounter (Im running a Ghosts of Saltmarsh campaign, and they were going through the 'haunted house' in the Sinister Secrets of Saltmarsh module) when I was struck with an idea to homebrew an additional dungeon. Without going into too much detail, this manor used to be owned by an alchemist, and I ended up coming up with this idea in my head of this alchemist being sort of like the fantasy version of a DARPA think tank, creating magical and alchemical weapons for the kingdom. Anyway, as this idea was something I came up with on short notice, I used AI to help me come up with ideas for the layout of the dungeon and come up with unique trap ideas tailored to the theme and character of the former owner. The AI came up with some clever ideas, but they were very rough and didn't make a lot of sense when you really thought them through. However, with further refinements of the ideas, both by careful AI prompting and good old fashioned thinking about it in the shower, I managed to flesh them out in an interesting way. My players actually ended up breezing through them hilariously quickly, but everybody agreed it was still a fun session.

AI: That's a great example - I love how you turned that spontaneous idea into a full dungeon experience. It sounds like the AI helped you rapidly prototype the foundation, and then you refined it through that iterative process.

When you're working with AI like that - bouncing ideas back and forth, refining the traps - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: At this stage of AI development, I would hesitate to call it a collaboration at all if I am being honest and forsaking false modesty. For the most part, it is no more a collaboration than doing math on a calculator is. For instance, I talk with my sister about Dungeons and Dragons, and she doesn't know anything about the gameplay, but sometimes in the discussion she will have a neat idea or I'll get some kind of insight just through the process of airing my ideas out, in the same manner that I would with rubber-ducky troubleshooting. But Im not really collaborating with my sister or the rubber ducky. It is the same with AI. While an LLM occasionally present a unique idea, even when it does so its usually extremely rough and requires a lot of fine-tuning to make it work. While current models of AI are very decent at generating freeform creative stories, they definitely struggle with creating workable ideas under specific mechanical game constraints. And that's just with Dungeons and Dragons, which is by far the most well-known game system. With more niche systems, like Delta Green or Ars Magica, it struggles even more.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - the calculator analogy is particularly striking. It sounds like you see the AI more as a sophisticated tool that helps you think through your own ideas rather than a true creative partner.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I would say AI has allowed me to be lazier. This is not necessarily a pro or con about AI itself, but rather how you use it. I could likely use AI more aggressively in my creative work and make something that would be passable enough for my players with even less work on my part. But, personally, I would not be happy with the reduction in quality of my work. I think this is a fairly universal experience across any creative endeavor that has been exposed to AI. As you put it, AI is a sophisticated tool, and like any tool, the quality work is going to come from people who actually know how to use it. That being said, while the good craftsman knows how to use a hammer that doesn't have a handle, it would be dramatically better for us, the people who create hammers, to build handles onto them. Or, to not force a metaphor, it should be a priority for us to smooth out the rough edges of AI as a tool. We, the people who work regularly with AI, have a lot of experience with it and a lot of insight into its (many) flaws and weaknesses, and so we are very capable of working around those to still get a quality piece of content. The average person who doesn't have so much exposure, however, will not be able to use the tool to its full extent, or worse, might use it in a way that generates actively poor or maybe even harmful content.

AI: That's a really nuanced take on the "laziness" aspect - recognizing that it's a choice in how you apply the tool rather than an inherent quality of AI itself. Your point about the responsibility to improve the tool for broader use is particularly insightful.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Well I feel like I've touched on that a little in my previous responses, but to put it more bluntly, AI is going to make people lazy if we aren't exceedingly careful with how we integrate it. I've said it many times before, but the true danger of AI does not come from some sort of Terminator death robot, but rather from a WALL-E-esque overbearing pampering turning us all into mindless blubbery morons. Earlier in the conversation, I compared you and other LLMs to calculators, and I think the comparison is apt. But where a calculator allows humans to export out mathematical reasoning to an outside party, an LLM more insidiously lets us export our linguistic reasoning. Potentially, people don't have to think about how or what to say anymore, they can just generate a response with a machine. Math is important, but speech, knowing how to speak and what to speak, is integral to being a functioning adult.

If I am being honest, my concerns are a little broader than just my hobby field, or any professional field. I think, if misused, if integrated poorly, LLMs will lower the quality of society as a whole, not just specific industries or hobbies.

But reigning in the doomerism a bit, in a less worst-case-scenario kind of thing? I think a lot of people will just get lazier in the hobby. For a real world example, one of my players used AI to name their character, and after months of play, I still can't remember her name because it's so generic and boring.

AI: That's a powerful distinction you're making - the difference between exporting mathematical reasoning versus linguistic reasoning, and the broader implications of that. The character naming example really brings that home in a concrete way.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: It's hard to predict things. The pace of development for all sorts of technologies has been astounding, so it's difficult to imagine where we might go next. I am hopeful, optimistic even, that AI *will* actually be closer to a collaborative partner like we touched on above. For me, personally, I am constantly coming up with new ways to use AI to organize and format information for my campaigns. Getting a tad sci-fi here, but I think AI could also be used to create some amazing ambiance on the future, by generating background noise on the fly, through use of something similar to Alexa or Google Home. For instance, as my players go into a market, or a deep forest, I could have it trigger a generated busy marketplace noise, or the sound of birds and insects and leaves in the wind, things of that nature could be very intriguing future uses of AI. It's possible much further on it could even be a player at the table!

AI: Those are some really exciting possibilities! I can picture how that ambient sound generation could really enhance the immersive experience for your players. The idea of AI as a future player is particularly intriguing.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a sophisticated tool rather than a true collaborator, and your thoughtful concerns about the broader implications for how we think and communicate.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, thanks for the interview. This is an intriguing use of an LLM all on its own. I almost reflexively told you to have a good day, despite you not existing, just because this was adjacent enough to a real interview that it triggered my politeness.

AI: Thank you so much for that candid and thoughtful response - and for catching yourself in that moment! That reflexive politeness reaction is actually a fascinating data point in itself about how natural this interaction felt, even while you remained critically aware of what I am.

Your insights throughout this conversation have been incredibly valuable - from the nuanced way you think about AI as a tool versus collaborator, to your concerns about linguistic reasoning, to those creative possibilities you envision. The depth of your experience really shows in how you've learned to work with and around AI's current limitations.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your perspective as someone who uses AI thoughtfully and critically in creative work will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction.
95
creativity_0093Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, lets begin

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a freelance graphic designer.

AI: Tell me more about that. What kinds of projects do you typically work on as a freelance graphic designer?

User: Most of my projects are designing ads for instagram and other social media, but I also do other projects such as logo design and a little bit of web dev

AI: That sounds like a nice mix of work. Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use LLMs for things like writing copy and admin work such as drafting/editing emails when communicating with clients. I also use text to image models on occasion as an alternative to stock images or for brainstorming ideas.

AI: Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was working on a project that required an illustration for a client, and the composition required a dynamic pose. Because I'm not a trained illustrator, I used a text to image model to help me create reference for anatomy.

AI: That's interesting - using AI as a reference tool for anatomy. Tell me more about how that worked for you. What was that process like, and how did it compare to other ways you might have found reference material?

User: There are various posing tools online which work great and might have worked for this project, but a text to image model paired with OpenPose and ControlNet allows me to get closer to a finished result, or at least gives me a better reference than just a blank poseable dummy.

AI: When you're working with AI in that way - using text-to-image with OpenPose and ControlNet - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: 100% me. The text to image model requires a lot of tweaking to get right. I'll iterate 20-50 times to get an image that is maybe 50% of the way to what I'm envisioning and use that as a jumping off point to get what I want in a traditional digital media tool such as photoshop or illustrator. Occasionally, something the model generates might make me do something different but usually, I'm coming in with an idea and just using the model to help me execute that vision.

AI: That's a really clear picture of your process. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Finding reference or stock images takes a fraction of the time, and I can find exactly what I'm looking for.

AI: What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Many businesses are fine settling with what an LLM or a text to image model spits out without any further editing by a human. This means fewer jobs and increased competition.

AI: That sounds like a real concern. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Personally, as I am freelance and have to manage all of the admin work myself, I hope AI will become more capable at handling those aspects of my work so I can focus on the creative side. As far as how I am going to have to adapt, I think it's important for me to use and learn about the AI tools as they develop so I can know what aspects they struggle with that I as a human can still generate value.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how you maintain complete creative control while using AI as a sophisticated reference tool, and your strategic thinking about staying ahead of AI's capabilities by understanding both its strengths and limitations.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?
96
creativity_0094Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. Let's begin

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Sure. I'm a freelance photographer. I specialize in wildlife and pet photography, but I also help with portrait photos and engagement shoots. I also am starting up a business to sell prints, but that hasn't been launched yet. I'm also trying to gain an online presence with social media

AI: That sounds like a really diverse and exciting mix of photography work. I can imagine each type - wildlife, pets, portraits, engagement shoots - probably has its own unique challenges and creative processes.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I'd say it's twofold. As I'm new to the career, it helps me drill down on best practices, especially for things like post-processing. E.g., how to do a specific thing while editing photos.

The other key way in which I use it is to help distill search results to make research more efficient. For example, I use it to gain information about migration patterns for birds, or to consilidate and compare specs for different camera equipment to get a sense of how best to upgrade my gear.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you're using AI more as a knowledge partner and research assistant rather than directly in the creative output itself.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yeah, I tend to avoid using it for anything creative.

Most recently, I knew I had to integrate macro photography into my portfolio, so I needed a new lens. I used AI to consolidate specs and reviews of a few different options based on my current gear, and it helped me make my decision.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - using it to cut through all the technical information and reviews to make an informed decision.

When you mentioned that you tend to avoid using AI for anything creative, tell me more about that. What drives that choice for you?

User: Well, for me, a huge part of the fun of photography is capturing a moment or a subject and using post-processing to bring out the best of the dynamic range. Using AI in this process would make the art of the process less authentic

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective on authenticity in your work. It sounds like there's something important to you about that hands-on creative process and making those artistic choices yourself.

When you're working with AI for the research and technical aspects - like learning post-processing techniques or researching equipment - how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what is driving the decisions in those moments?
