User: In those cases, I'm using AI as a collaborator to save time with "busy work". For example, I could read all the articles and reviews about the lenses I was considering by myself, and I'd successfully make the right decision, but using AI for that part helps save time so I can move on with the most important parts of the job. Plus, I'm buying these lenses second hand, so I often need to act fast and time is of the essence.

AI: That's a really clear distinction you're making - using AI to handle the time-intensive research and technical learning so you can focus your energy on the core creative work. It sounds like you see it as freeing up your time for what matters most to you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: It hasn't really affected my creative work at all. I do my best to keep AI out of the creative side

AI: I hear you - you've been very intentional about keeping that boundary. But I'm curious about the efficiency side. Since you've been using AI for research and learning post-processing techniques, has that changed how you approach projects or manage your time as a freelance photographer?

User: Maybe slightly. Just in the sense that it allows me to cut through some of the administrative hurdles

AI: That makes sense - streamlining those administrative and research tasks.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field of photography?

User: I worry specifically about denoising software. Some of this software uses AI to artificially sharpen and clean up images to a point that is far beyond what is possible given the metadata of the photo at the time of capture. It feels kind of like cheating

AI: That's a really specific and thoughtful concern - the idea that AI denoising could push images beyond what was actually captured in that moment. It sounds like there's a line for you between enhancing what was there versus creating something that wasn't.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see lots of pro photographers using this denoising AI, so I fear the art of photography will be less about the skill of capturing a moment with the technical know-how, and it'll more be about being in the right place at the right time, and there'll be less skill involved in capturing pictures

AI: That's a really insightful observation about how the field itself might be shifting. It sounds like you're concerned about a potential devaluing of the technical mastery and craft that goes into capturing that perfect shot in-camera.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI, particularly your clear boundaries around maintaining authenticity in the creative process while leveraging AI for efficiency in research and learning. I found your perspective on denoising software and its impact on the skill aspect of photography especially compelling.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your photography work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No that's all, thank you

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative authenticity while strategically using AI for efficiency, and your concerns about how AI might change the fundamental skills valued in photography, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating AI into your workflow while preserving what matters most to you about the creative process. Best of luck with your photography business and that upcoming print venture!
97
creativity_0095Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: sounds good, and no, proceed!

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I build pedals from scratch. This means I often choose a tried and true circuit and expand upon it or change it some way to make it my own. I then either purchase an enclosure or make it. Then, I make wood faceplate that is slightly smaller than the face of the enclosure, and I stain it and finish it nice and smooth. Then, I apply waterslide decals with graphics I've created and seal them in with more coats of finish. I do all the work myself, including making the PCBs out of stripboard, drilling all my enclosures, and doing all of the soldering and component purchasing myself. I sell them. I make custom ones often, as well, and what started as a hobby quickly grew into a business where I repair electronics faster than they can be picked up by the customers, and I sell so many of my original pedals I rarely have any of my own to play around with. I spend a lot of time on each project; probably more than is necessary. It's impossible, honestly, to get the hours for each pedal down. It's a week at least, with 12-hour days sometimes, and only 4-hour days other times.

AI: Wow, that sounds like an incredibly hands-on and meticulous process - from circuit design all the way through to the final graphics and finishing. The level of craftsmanship you're describing is really impressive, especially managing both the technical electronics work and the artistic elements like the wood faceplates and custom graphics.

It sounds like you've got your hands full with both custom orders and repairs!

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Sometimes it's as simple as coming up with new names for controls or effects, but sometimes it's also about understanding parts of a circuit I may not understand correctly. Usually, it's to better understand elements of electrical engineering in which I'm lacking. That said, I'd really like to utilize it for Bill of Materials compilations, circuit theory (testing?), as well as providing me with the best of the abundance of sources for components. Also, awareness of existing circuits. Almost like, a wizard librarian with his eye out for the best price and the most up-to-date info on component availability, and if not, what actually is suitable, including alternate pinouts if applicable, etc.

AI: That's fascinating - so AI is serving multiple roles for you, from the creative naming side to being almost like a technical consultant and sourcing assistant.

Tell me more about how you currently use AI to understand circuit elements you're not familiar with. What does that interaction typically look like for you?

User: I feel like I am dyslexic with signal flow, as well as understanding some concepts that might seem basic. In particular, I am developing a synthesizer, and I have a harder time understanding things like when a summing amp is necessary and when it isn't. Or, another example might be discussing diode clipping and cascades...knowledge about what different diode configurations are known to produce is helpful so I know if I'm barking up a tree that's been barked up to much. As far as interactions go...I feel like I get some practical understanding out of the conversation, but my confidence is still kind of low. What if the info is wrong?

AI: That concern about accuracy really resonates - especially when you're working with circuits where getting it wrong could mean a lot of wasted time and materials. The confidence issue you mention seems particularly important when you're pushing into new territory like synthesizer development.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: A practical example is asking how to get PCB files to order PCBs without the usually necessary gerber file. I have, in the example, a jpg or pdf of a PCB layout, and a complete BOM; how do I proceed? I got stiffed by someone years ago and I'm trying to reverse engineer their work. I have my business name literally copyrighted on the image of the PCB layout...I just don't have the gerber. The instruction was to recreate the layout from scratch, which was ultimately what I had to do, and the resources for that were helpful too. I then kind of proceeded to learn how easy it was to use EasyEDA with AI. It took about a year, but now I'm making them with no problems thanks to that one conversation. It kind of set me off from converting my stripboards to proper PCBs for the final sale models.

AI: That's a really compelling example - it sounds like that one conversation ended up being a real turning point for your business, opening up a whole new capability with PCB design. And the fact that it took a year to fully implement shows how that initial AI guidance rippled out into genuine skill development.

When you're working with AI on these technical questions - like in that PCB situation or when you're trying to understand circuit concepts - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I always feel like I'm the one driving the conversation and the AI is being somewhat over-supportive at times; I'd prefer it if a better approach was recommended as an alternative. For example, in the case of the PCB design, I was also given the recommendation to hire a circuit designer. This could've also been a successful approach, and putting the ball in my court for that one was very helpful. I did weigh that option, but since I'd been screwed before by a hired circuit designer (which I hadn't told the AI), I chose the DIY approach.

AI: That's really insightful - you're looking for AI to be more of a thoughtful advisor that presents multiple paths rather than just being encouraging about whatever direction you're already heading. It sounds like you value having those alternatives laid out so you can make informed decisions based on your full context, including past experiences the AI might not know about.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Yeah, it's almost like, there's a balance between the tailored approach and the "generally best" approach; I'd like them both laid out.

To answer your question, probably research streamlining. I would like to say BoM compilations, but I just don't need to do that with Excel until I find a model that can combine multiple BoMs into one without error, including up-to-date components with equivalents when necessary.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - having both the customized advice for your specific situation and the broader "best practice" approach gives you the full picture to work with.

The research streamlining sounds like it's been a significant time-saver for you. And it's interesting that you're holding back on the BoM work until the accuracy and reliability gets to where you need it to be - that seems to tie back to your earlier concern about confidence in the information.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: None. AI can't repair broken electronics or handmake these boutique pedal, at least not yet hehe. If anything, my concerns are that the AI assistant I'm looking for is too far away.

AI: Ha! That's a great perspective - the hands-on craftsmanship and repair work you do really does seem pretty AI-proof for now.

Tell me more about that AI assistant you're looking for being "too far away." What would that ideal assistant look like for your work?

User: I feel like I already laid all that out; I'm happy to do so if you can't cycle through all that, but it's basically a circuit wizard...we're talking population and design, demonstration with component swapability, essentially AI software for circuit design, and then a BoM compiler of course, with all that stuff I said. Again, I'm happy to say it again, but I feel like I did make all that pretty clear. I understand it's a lot.

AI: You're absolutely right - you laid that out very clearly earlier. I was curious if there were additional aspects beyond what you'd already described so well: the wizard librarian for component sourcing and pricing, the circuit theory guidance, BoM compilation with error-free component equivalents, and that broader circuit design software capability.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Hopefully I can rely on it more; I'd like to upload an image of an old stripboard build with components labeled in the picture and create a BoM for me. Then I can input other projects I'm adding to it, and the results are true...I won't go to build them all like I have in the past and realize the AI messed up and I'm short a dozen resistors or something. Being more reliable and proven as such, so then I can actually invest in it as a paid assistant if necessary, because it would certainly be cheaper than a human one. But it needs to have some initiative as well. And it needs to be intuitive and accurate.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on wanting AI to present both tailored and "generally best" approaches, and your vision for that circuit wizard assistant that could handle everything from component identification to reliable BoM generation.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. The way you've described both the current limitations you're working around and your clear vision for what would actually be useful in your field gives us a lot to think about.

Best of luck with your pedal business and that synthesizer development - it sounds like you're doing some really impressive work!
98
creativity_0096Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, thank you, you can proceed.

AI: Great, thank you. Let's start with the basics then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a writer, novels and also ghost writing blog articles. A typical project for ghost writing is to get a topic, double check the requirements for both the client and if there's any middle management, do the research, and write the article, typically between 500-2000 words, depending. Research usually takes a few hours, and writing usually takes an additional hour. Novels are significantly more involved, as you can imagine. Each novel is different, but a simplified version of a project overview would be to outline, research, create a first draft, edit, research, rewrite, create a second draft, and repeat until something is ready to be handed over to betas. Those are typically on my own timetable, for however long it takes.

AI: That's really helpful context - I can see how those two types of writing would have very different rhythms and requirements. The ghost writing sounds quite structured with those clear deliverables, while the novel work has that more iterative, open-ended timeline.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Research is the only area I use it heavily, but I will use AI sporadically for another area. For research, AI saves on time significantly by collecting a bunch of sources for me, or helping me hammer out details I either don't understand or have misunderstood. For the other area, I have a writing disability (ironic, I know), and AI helps me when I'm struggling. For example, when I'm struggling with spelling, I'm able to give AI the definition, and it can tell me the word I'm trying to use. Or when I'm trying to find the correct word for something, for example a piece of architecture or an uncommon piece of furniture, when I know what the thing is, but I'm having difficulty communicating it properly. AI is excellent for taking the description and finding the correct words for me.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI is serving two very distinct functions for you. The research efficiency piece makes a lot of sense, and I hadn't thought about how AI could function as that kind of language accessibility tool for writers with disabilities.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Currently, I'm working on a novel that takes place in a fantasy world, but set in a time similar to our Roman era in western civilization. So, research has been key on that, making sure it is as authentic as possible. AI in particularly is helpful in that kind of area because not only can it give me sources that are usually fact checked and reliable, but it is also aware of how I'm applying that research (ie, into a fantasy world), and can make note when something might not be very applicable, or when something might be uniquely relevant to my fictional world. As far as how it feels, it feels less stressful to have that kind of research buddy, and its encouragement always helps me not feel discouraged when the research itself isn't turning over what I might have hoped.

AI: That's really interesting - you're describing AI almost as a collaborative research partner that understands the context of your project, not just a search tool. The fact that it can flag what might or might not work for your specific fantasy setting sounds quite sophisticated.

When you're working with AI in that research capacity, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am definitely driving the creative decisions. The truth is that the writing community is under fire because of AI. There is a 'witch hunt', as it were, on writers using AI. I always make sure I am the creative driver and do all the writing, provable with receipts, because of it. So, I would say the collaboration is forced to be under very strict rules, and there's not much opportunity to explore what it could be because of the atmosphere among publishing and writing today.

AI: That's a really important point you're raising about the current atmosphere in the writing community. It sounds like there's a tension between what might be possible with AI collaboration and what feels safe or acceptable professionally.

Tell me more about that - how has this atmosphere affected your willingness to experiment with AI or explore different ways it might fit into your process?

User: It has affected it greatly. There have many times when I would have liked to tell the AI my entire ideas, just to get encouraging feedback and a hype man, but by and large, if any writer is caught collaborating actual ideas with AI, they are ostracized. Being an indie author is hard enough, and I've known indie writers whose sales have tanked just because they were (wrongly) accused of using AI. Not many readers will double check and just believe the accusations. And most publishing companies now will ban you if you use AI to so much as help you create an outline. So yeah, it has made me greatly hesitant to experiment with AI professional or explore different ways it could help. But it also makes me want to help find a solution.

AI: That paints a really stark picture of the professional risks involved. It sounds like you're navigating between seeing potential value in AI collaboration and the very real consequences of being associated with it in the writing community.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI, even within those constraints you've described?

User: I feel like my writing has becoming more sophisticated, again using AI to help with my disability, and I feel like my stories are more rounded with the aid in research. AI is able to give me significantly more sources than by myself, which helps me improve the worlds I create, and also helps me correct myself when something in my writing is wrong. As far as ghost writing is concerned, by ability to deliver articles is significantly faster, which means I can take on more work occasionally.

AI: That's a compelling picture - it sounds like within the boundaries you're working in, AI has actually enhanced both the quality and efficiency of your work. The research depth for worldbuilding and the accessibility support seem to have had real impacts.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field, beyond the professional risks you've already mentioned?

User: Beyond the professional risks, I do have concerned about AI replacing writers, and the qualities of stories being diminished without that human aspect. However, that, I feel like, is the usual concerns, when something so significant is new and becoming wide spread, and something only time will tell. Otherwise, I have concerns about how writing itself will become affected. For example, I have seen so many people fall victims to this 'witch hunt', even just socially when attempting to makes blog posts, because some people believe the presence of an em dash must mean it was created by AI. They forget, or not realize, that AI has learned to write from humans, and already writers, like myself, have began altering our styles to be starkly different. This will have a consequences in the future linguistically.

AI: That's a really astute observation about the linguistic consequences - the idea that fear of being mistaken for AI is actually changing how humans write. That's quite a feedback loop.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you think the current atmosphere will shift, or do you expect to continue working within these same constraints?

User: Something will shift, although I would say it will be more like something will break. The constraints and tensions as they stand now aren't going to be able to last, but what will come out of it will be a solution for everyone. I don't feel confident enough to predict how that will evolve, but I do suspect that AI will become a large part of all creative work, as it will for most all work.

AI: That's a thoughtful way to put it - "something will break" - suggesting the current tensions aren't sustainable long-term.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how the current professional climate is constraining experimentation, and that observation about writers already changing their styles to avoid being mistaken for AI - that linguistic ripple effect is fascinating.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Not that I can think of. Thank you! It was a pleasure talking to you.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about navigating AI as both a practical tool and within the current professional constraints will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits you've experienced and the very real challenges facing writers today. Take care!
99
creativity_0097Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: sounds good!

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a web developer and software developer, web apps and android apps primarily. A typical project is setting up a business website or a website for one of the apps I'm developing, creating content for it from graphics to blog articles and writing copy that converts lookers into customers.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse set of creative challenges - from the technical development work to the visual design and persuasive writing.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I rely heavily on AI tools for content creation, a lot of working on the main pages in the site relies on copy that converts and AI helps me A/B test and find something that fits the feel of the site. I use it for programming and occasionally graphics but primarily marketing copy is where I lean heavily on AI.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like AI has become particularly central to your copywriting and marketing work.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I used AI most recently to come up with and then refine the landing page copy for a client who runs a tattoo studio. She wanted something that fits her style so I told AI about her and the specific project goals and the task of writing the main copy. I work through it from top to bottom and then go back through again to find weaknesses and identify ways it could be better. It makes me feel a bit more confident in the marketing copy before I hand it over to a client but there are also issues like sounding too cliche or not finding the right tone.

AI: That's a fascinating process - it sounds like you're using AI as both a creative partner and then a critic of its own work. Tell me more about that back-and-forth process you described - when you go through to find weaknesses and identify improvements, what does that look like?

User: Ok so I start typically with describing the client and goals and such and then ask for the very first line on the website and really refine it before going much further because it sorta sets the tempo. I then go on from there to each individual section whether its telling about features of a SaaS product or describing the tattoo studio or business and who they serve. basically I ask for copy that converts for each section. once I get something I feel confident with I start a new conversation or choose a new model and send it through and ask for critical feedback pros and cons. I use that feedback as guidance for the replacement text, explaining why I like parts the model picked as not great or highlighting areas I don't like. I then ask for two or three versions depending on how I feel about it. The goal being to find issues before it gets to the client and sometimes I'll use multiple models to bounce ideas around and brainstorm ways to improve it

AI: That's a really sophisticated workflow - using different models and fresh conversations to get unbiased feedback, almost like assembling your own creative review team.

When you're working with AI in this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm driving the decisions but I get AI to identify weaknesses in the copy. I'm not particularly attached to it so anything can go and then ultimately I decide on which one is better. Sometimes I'll do an A/B test and show both to the client and we sorta pick out what is best or send both out and display copy A to one visitor and copy B to another visitor and see which one gets more clients through the door. It helps me do more work in a short amount of time and offer a higher level of service

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like you've found a way to maintain creative control while leveraging AI's analytical capabilities to scale up your output and quality. The A/B testing approach is particularly clever.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I've always struggled with writing copy, I've studied it extensively and understand what does and doesn't work but actually getting the words onto a page has always been a challenge for me. The thing that changed the most is being able to feel confident that what I have is going to work and spending the time on refining it instead of coming up with one thing I can try five different things until I get two that I like so much I can't decide between. It makes it so I'm delivering something closer to the high end marketing firms as an individual working from home.

AI: That's such a compelling insight - it sounds like AI has helped you bridge the gap between knowing what good copy looks like and actually producing it. You mentioned being able to compete with high-end marketing firms now as an individual.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm highly optimistic. I think the programming side of things and AI are more concerning than marketing and as a solo founder I'm not as worried about being put out of work as some people might be. For me, AI is empowering but I'm not just collecting a paycheck everything I do is either worth something or worth nothing and I won't know until later on if it works or not. If I worked for a company that found clients and I was writing the copy for them on a large team I might worry about job security but for my situation, I find it helpful. I think using it without asking for refinements creates a word salad that sounds very similar to everything out there. I'll occasionally send a landing page back through and the feedback is something along the lines of "this is very generic" or what have you. Overall I don't think AI will harm my marketing efforts and it will only get better and make me more productive.

AI: That's a really balanced perspective - you're seeing AI as a productivity multiplier rather than a replacement, especially in your role as a solo founder where the results speak for themselves.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think the next step is to have something like an IDE but for marketing. I'd really love to be able to tell one thing all of the goals and everything about the product or business and work on creative assets without telling it the same thing over and over. The context limits are something I bump into quite often and arbitrary limits put in place by AI companies to limit usage is really annoying. I think in the next few years it will be common to just have a cloud computer with a dedicated AI environment instead of paying for individual subscriptions for image generation, video generation etc just one main control panel that creates marketing and creative assets to reach the same goals and with the same quality without struggle. Image and video generation especially is hard to get reliable assets from though its improving.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has helped you bridge that gap between understanding what good copy should look like and actually being able to produce it confidently - that seems like such a fundamental creative challenge that AI is helping solve.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think thats it, I'm more optimistic than some people and really reliant on coming up with something good for my clients I can deliver a higher quality product thanks to AI and it has genuine results but at the same time sometimes I'll have to refine and refine or a model will be great one day and not as great the next week or for a different project so I do use multiple models as a result which I find to be really important in getting a better idea of what is possible and finding things to consider in the future.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using multiple models strategically, the importance of that iterative refinement process, and how AI has empowered you as a solo founder to compete at a higher level - all of that will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits and the practical challenges, like context limits and model inconsistency. That kind of honest perspective is exactly what helps us understand how these tools are actually working in the real world.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your projects!
100
creativity_0098Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, no questions yet :)

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: It is my hobby, and something I spend a lot of time doing, which is researching and writing, about all sorts of topics. A typical project would be anything from a personal interest, or something in current affaris, a philosophy - generally any topic I'm curious about - becoming something I study, almost accademically and then write about. Its a cathartic way of helping me understand myself and the world around me more fully. My neurodiversity has held me back somewhat in the past, but now I'm overcoming those obstacles, I'm re-framing this personal journey as something I can re-orient into creating things to help others like me, get on in the world better. In a sense, give them some of the assistance I could have done with when I was younger.

AI: That's really meaningful work - using your own journey to help others navigate similar challenges. I can hear how personal and purposeful this writing is for you.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: In the past, due to my ASD, I focused in on a range of topics of interest, researched them, and wrote about them, or produced some guide or flow chart - like a process of helping me understand the world and myself. This was really messy, un-focused, the topics were random and often I didn't finish them. I improved my work flow as an adult, higher education and work experience gave me skills that improved this hobby. It wasn't until AI came along though that I've seen a noticable difference in my output. I can use a range of AI tools now to help me research that save me so much time. Brainstorming ideas, checking for bias, validating information, cross checking data, finding the right words or ways to phrase a concept, untangling metaphore, exploratory thinking, advice... so much more, I could write a lot about how AI fits into my creative process. But to put it simply, it helps me untangle and present all the things I'm curious about in the world, that I think may be of interest or use to others, turning a mess of ideas into something useable.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI has become a powerful tool for helping you organize and refine your natural curiosity and insights. You mentioned it's made a noticeable difference in your output.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes, as part of my own journey, I'm making moves to improve my health and weight loss is one of the goals. Naturally I need to understand more about health, nutrition, exercise etc to help me lose weight, I can't just pick a diet and follow a schedule, I need to understand whats going on with me physiologiclaly - how ever complex or detailed that is. I never found anyone with the patients to listen to all my questions and go over and over details until they make sense to me, so in terms of how it felt for me - its like having the guide or teacher in my life now that I always needed. This project helped me understand so much more about how my body works. I've been able to probe and query so many things that it would have taken me hours - weeks maybe to research on my own. I feel gratitude, and I'm feeling a sense of acomplishment for what its done for me with this project so far, I'm still in the middle of this one though, so I'm hoping those feelings are magnified again by the end of it.

AI: That's really powerful - having that patient guide who can go over details until they make sense sounds transformative, especially when you haven't found that kind of support elsewhere. The gratitude you describe comes through clearly.

When you're working with AI on a project like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: The collaboration is quite a nuanced subject here, I am in the drivers seat for most of it, but as I use the AI for bias checking, ensuring I'm not missing any vital pieces of information or context etc, I'd describe it as a corrective co-pilot. As far as research and learning goes anyway. When it comes to writing, I do most of the writing, but I'll often look for ways of explaining something I cant articulate, or the best word for something, and I'll often dump all I've written into the chat and get it to check it over, to make sure I've not repeated myself, over-explained or contradicted myself somehow - so I don't see it as my work alone, I'm very aware of the level of input from AI that contributes towards the finished product. I would say though, that I am in control of the decisions over-all, but the smaller details - which make up the whole - are very much suggested or created by AI.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to describe it - you as the driver with AI as a "corrective co-pilot." It sounds like you have a clear sense of where your agency lies versus where AI contributes, even while acknowledging how those smaller AI-contributed details add up to something significant in the whole.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I can refer back to an answer I gave earlier and say; everything has changed about my creative work. I feel AI is enabling me to get past so many of the obstacles I previously faced. To answer specifically though, the 'most' change is probably my confidence with the work I do. I have dumped a lot of my work in the bin in the past, being overly critical of my own skills and acomplishments. Now (with the help of AI) I see those mistakes, that low quality work, as just early stepping stones I was on, before AI started clearing everything up so well.

AI: That shift in confidence is really striking - moving from being overly critical and throwing work away to seeing those earlier efforts as stepping stones. It sounds like AI has helped you reframe not just your current work, but your whole creative journey.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: My concerns with AI in this field, as with any field, would be regarding job losses, I wouldn't be particuarly effected as its not a job for me, but if journalists, researchers, non-fiction writers generally are replaced by AI, something has to be done about supporting people who are losing their jobs to AI. I'm not that concerned that AI will 'replace' this field any time soon, but we do certainly have the oportunity and potential for AI to start compiling and creating the best, most accurate information on any subject, like a perfect wikipedia for everything, and in that situation, millions of jobs would no longer be needed.

AI: That's a really important perspective - you're seeing both the personal benefits while being mindful of the broader economic implications for professionals in these fields. The idea of AI potentially creating that "perfect wikipedia for everything" is quite a vision.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: The models are constantly improving their understanding and communication, I think we'll see more personalised AI, that gets to know the users better and is more adaptive to their needs. I think that will lead to even better optimisation between people and AI. AI will become more and more integrated into these roles, making the processes smoother and quickly. I think in 5 years time I'll be able to tell the AI what I want to look up, and it will know me well enough that its response will be somewhere near the end product, that I currently take hours or days, sometimes weeks to pull together. Generally speaking I think we'll shorten the gap in time between a projects initial conception, and the end product I create from that idea.

AI: That vision of AI knowing you well enough to jump much closer to your end product is fascinating - essentially compressing what now takes weeks into something much more immediate.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has become that patient guide and teacher you always needed, and how it's helped you reframe your entire creative journey - from being overly critical to seeing your work as stepping stones.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think I managed to mention all the main points I wanted to, thank you.

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about how AI has transformed not just your workflow but your confidence and relationship with your own creative work - especially as someone navigating neurodiversity - have been incredibly valuable. The way you described AI as that patient guide you always needed really stuck with me, and your thoughtful perspective on both the personal benefits and broader societal implications gives us a lot to think about.

Your responses will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. Thanks again for being so generous with your time and insights today!
101
creativity_0099Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That all sounds fine to me

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Well, I work in a couple of areas. One is graphic design where I need to create personalised prints and designs, the other is written. The written work is normally a presentation that needs to fit a particular theme or piece of writing. usually religious in nature. It may sometimes be an application for a grant or fund. Research is very important in order to ensure that the written work has integrity. The writing needs to be informative, engaging and connect with people in a way that hopefully leaves them feeling changed in some way.

AI: That's really interesting - you're working across both visual and written mediums with some pretty meaningful objectives around connecting with people and creating impact.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I still feel that AI is lacking a bit for the visual process, it appears to struggle when it comes to using fonts and writing. It's really helpful for the writing process. I can use it to give me inspiration. For example providing an idea, theme or piece of writing to the model, it often then provides some insights or thoughts. It's particularly helpful to bounce ideas off, check if certain thoughts or insights are factually accurate, or to help build on ideas. Sometimes, if I have a bit of a block and I don't know where to go next, I can upload what I have and ask for some ideas for what is is missing from the written piece.

AI: Tell me more about that process of bouncing ideas off AI - what does that typically look like when you're in the middle of working on a piece?

User: It may be asking about historical context for a piece of writing (particularly religious writing). Often this may lead to asking further questions about specific people, cultures, religions or practices. This all feeds into gaining a deeper understanding of the piece of writing that is being studied. An example of this may be starting with "give me the historical context of 'passage x'". The response from the model may lead me on to the follow up question "Tell me more about y". Alternatively I may ask the model to rephrase a certain part of my writing to make it clearer, or ask if what I have included in my writing is factually accurate.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic back-and-forth process, especially with the research element. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: A recent project began with my question "why did Paul feel the need to write these words to the church in Thessaloniki 'make it your ambition to lead a quiet life: You should mind your own business and work with your hands, just as we told you, 12 so that your daily life may win the respect of outsiders and so that you will not be dependent on anybody.'" This question began the process for me to understand more of what the passage was trying to say, rather than what may at first appear obvious to a modern day western reader. It helped me to get inside the mind of the author of the text. The information provided helped me to begin to find areas of interest and relevance that I could then hang a presentation on, ensuring that I was not misrepresenting the intended message. It was incredibly helpful. Not only did it help give me a starting point, but it also meant that I had a good amount of evidence and information to support my angle.

AI: That's a fascinating example - it sounds like AI helped you move beyond surface-level interpretation to really understand the deeper context and intent. When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am driving the creative decisions. The AI is a great asset to achieving what I want to achieve.

AI: What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: My research has sped up massively. Rather than scrawling through web searches and looking through books, I can ask more direct questions for the specific answers I want. This helps to engage my creativity quicker as well, as I have more time for the creative process and a partner to bounce ideas off.

AI: That's a significant efficiency gain. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: The concerns would be that it becomes so good that there is a temptation to bypass the human element altogether. Whether that is through busyness in other areas, laziness, or that it appears to do a better job without the human input.

AI: That's a thoughtful concern about maintaining that human element. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think it may develop into a place where it could become a recorder of thoughts and notes (a bit like a dictaphone), from there it provides suggestions for how to order the thoughts and make sense of them. I think it will also get better at writing specifically in the tone and voice of a particular user (i.e. me). It may be able to produce helpful slides to accompany a talk or presentation, and hopefully improve with fonts and text.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has transformed your research process - moving from "scrawling through web searches" to having that direct, conversational partner for exploring deeper contexts, especially in your religious writing work.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nothing springs to mind

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a research partner and collaborator - particularly how it helps you get to the deeper meaning behind texts rather than just surface interpretations - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about both the benefits you're seeing and your concerns about maintaining that essential human element in the creative process. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
102
creativity_0100Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great. Let us begin.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I do acting and comedy and I am involved in a lot of behind the scenes/backstage work as well in both film and theater. A typical project is a gig for me frankly. I am being brought into a team to perform a particular role because of my distinct abilities.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you work across multiple facets of the entertainment industry. I can imagine each gig brings its own unique challenges and creative demands.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I primarily engage AI tools to help me deal with administrative tasks and paperwork, filling forms when I am applying for jobs, keeping on top of my paperwork for my union membership, tailoring my resume and portfolio, managing my calendar and schedule.

AI: That's a really practical application - using AI to handle the business side so you can focus on the creative work itself. It sounds like AI is helping you manage the less glamorous but essential parts of being a working creative professional.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I recently helped to design a set for a comedy video/podcast. I had done some drawings as mock ups to show the client what it would look like, but they were unsatisfied with the work I had done. So I transferred my drawing into a AI and was able to make it into a realistic, 3d image and was able to convince the client and make some important changes. We used that image to run green-screen screen tests and give the client a chance to play in the space and see how it would look on camera before we committed to building the set. I was frustrated at first with how the AI tool handled rendering my drawing however, the experience helped me to learn how to better prompt the model to get desired results.

AI: That's a fascinating example - it sounds like AI became a crucial communication tool between you and the client, helping bridge the gap between your creative vision and what they could visualize.

Tell me more about that frustration you mentioned with how the AI initially handled your drawing. What was happening there, and how did you figure out how to get better results from it?

User: It is a rather specific thing, but when you mention to a model to NOT include some element, it would invariably show up. It's like dealing with humans and you tell them to not think of a pink elephant. Do you get my drift? Elements that I didn't want included, I did not even mention. Because for example I would tell it, "get rid of the bust of Shakespeare"; The bust would appear in every iteration even after explicit requests to remove it.

AI: That's such a vivid way to put it - the "pink elephant" problem! It really captures how AI seems to latch onto the very things you're trying to avoid when you mention them explicitly.

So you learned to work around that by simply not mentioning unwanted elements at all. How did that change feel for you as a creative professional - having to adapt your communication style to work effectively with the AI tool?

User: It was kind of annoying but it is like learning to deal with any other personality. I will admit that I caution a lot of people to be careful and sparing in using AI tools because of this, often they will not get what they want at first because they have not bothered to learn how to prompt engineer and will either become frustrated or end up producing work that is "good enough" but contains other flaws or errors that they had not been paying attention to at all.

AI: That's a really insightful point about the learning curve and the potential pitfalls. It sounds like you see AI as a tool that requires skill and attention to use well, rather than something that just automatically produces good results.

When you caution people about using AI tools, what kinds of flaws or errors do you typically see them miss when they settle for "good enough" work?

User: "short attention span errors" for example, you generate an image of a waiting room with potted plants. the issue is that they are not real plants and only bear passing resemblance to real potted plants but you'd only notice if you're looking for more than a split second. A lot of people in Comedy generate posters for shows using AI tools to avoid dealing with graphics software like Photoshop or Gimp, but also create posters where the comedian doesn't look anything like their avatar, they have a double headed mic or extra fingers. It look good enough to the user because they had some ideas about what they wanted to see but neglect details that the end user (the people they are trying to convince to come to their show) will see and judge them on. I am sure you know there is a virulent anti AI sentiment in a lot of the creative world.

AI: That's really telling - those "short attention span errors" you describe paint a clear picture of how AI output can look convincing at first glance but fall apart under closer inspection. And I can see how that connects to the broader tension you mention about anti-AI sentiment in creative fields.

When you're working with AI tools yourself, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in your process?

User: I use AI primarily to do the "boring stuff" or use it as a "sounding board". I very rarely ever use AI to generate unique ideas or concepts. Rather I prefer to use it to tell my ideas to and to try to identify gaps or inconsistencies in my own work, as a third person perspective to look at my own output from a different angle.

AI: That's a really thoughtful approach - using AI as more of a critical thinking partner rather than an idea generator. It sounds like you've found a way to maintain your creative agency while leveraging AI's ability to offer that outside perspective.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: The amount of people who do not know anything about AI except that it is the new hot thing and they want to use it to make their projects stand out. This is not a wrong or bad instinct per se but it has made me think about what the true best use case for AI in creative fields is. Because for science and mathematics, it is straightforward: sometimes there are problems so complex that this amount of computing power and linguistic sophistication is the solution to overcoming human limitations. For arts it is the opposite: how do we use this tool to maximize human potential rather than suppressing it

AI: That's a profound distinction you're making - using AI to overcome human limitations in technical fields versus maximizing human potential in creative fields. That seems to get at the heart of a lot of the tension around AI in creative work.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I am afraid it will be used by unscrupulous parties (especially executives) to cut corners, cheat workers and de-value traditional film-making techniques and skills. Movies and the theater and music are businesses after all, and maximizing profits is still the name of the game.

AI: That's a very grounded concern - the economic pressures in entertainment are real, and AI could easily become a tool for cost-cutting rather than creative enhancement. It sounds like you're seeing this tension between AI as a creative collaborator versus AI as a way to replace skilled workers.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think that AI generated mass entertainment will proliferate, somewhat like the cheap pulp novels and paperbacks from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. A segment of the population will enjoy these and another will see it as an undesirable symptom of modernity. There will be at least one film or album with an "AI" actor or musician that will provoke very heated debate about the nature of art. I also think that there will be both a renaissance of traditional film making (painted scenery and sets , practical effects, etc) as well as a boom of independent creators using AI tools to bring their worlds to life who otherwise would have never had the opportunity. I think there will be strict regulations placed upon AI use in creative fields because of the strength of the Hollywood/Broadway/entertainment lobby.

AI: That's such a nuanced vision - you're seeing multiple parallel tracks emerging rather than just one direction. The comparison to pulp novels is really apt - suggesting AI entertainment might find its own market niche while traditional craftsmanship potentially gains renewed appreciation.

Before we wrap up, those are all the questions I had prepared, and you've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in creative work. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI to maximize rather than suppress human potential, and your practical wisdom about the importance of really learning how to work with these tools effectively.

Is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No I think that about does it. Thank you.

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about the practical realities of working with AI tools - from the "pink elephant" prompting challenges to your thoughtful approach of using AI as a creative sounding board rather than an idea generator - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated hearing your perspective on how to navigate AI as a tool that requires skill and attention, and your vision for how the creative landscape might evolve with both AI-generated content and a renaissance of traditional techniques coexisting.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your upcoming projects!
103
creativity_0101Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great, I have no questions!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a full time wedding photographer so I use many tools to help me in the various stages of my projects. With my clients, I typically help plan some of their wedding details and do correspondance, which I use AI to help with. Creatively, I get to use AI in a lot of my marketing - like creating content for social media or blogs for my website - and in the post-production process of selecting, editing, and delivering the client's wedding photos.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI touches multiple stages of your workflow, from client communication all the way through to final delivery.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: My job is naturally quite creative since not only it deals with the art of photography, but as an entrepreneur I feel my approach to "doing business" is creative. I find I use AI most when I am brainstorming, iterating ideas, trying to communicate to associates and clients, create effective and passive workflows, and especially with marketing. I like to have conversations frequently where I state my objective or intended outcome and then have the AI ask me questions, gain deep knowledge understanding about my approach or perspective, and then help me create the path to obtain my objective. For example, when using AI for creating Instagram content, I will first extensively train the AI on my tone of voice, brand values, mission, objectives, workflows, and more. I typically will say something like "I need you to help me create my Instagram content for my wedding photography business. I'm trying to reach (this type of person) and get them to (do this thing like book a wedding package). Here's what I want my content to look like or sound like. Here are some difficulties I face." And then before I let it help me, I tell it to ask me questions to gain better understanding from me of what it may need to accomplish the task. I will do this with other things - such as creating a new client experience or package. Sometimes I will do a similar approach to help me practice sales calls or create a new script. Really the possibilities here are endless.

I also will use it to edit copy on my website, or to review the copy I have and tell me if it's in a good order for the "customer journey" and ask if there's something I can change to have more impact. I like to think of all AI use as a collaboration. I provide the intended outcome and have the AI help me better define that outcome and the supporting details. Then I provide the initial idea or the draft version and then use the AI to help me see my "blind spots". I iterate my thoughts about the blind spots it pointed out and then it helps me come up with solutions. I will spend extensive amounts of time on many tasks using AI and think it has improved my quality of output in every way and arena of business.

I also use tools that are AI powered, but not the typical "chat bot" AI. I use programs that will AI select a desired amount of images from 10k images from a wedding for example based on certain qualities I'm looking for. It will select the best of the images, include one of every grouping from family photos, omit everything that has closed eye, or follow the other instructions given. I use a program that will auto edit all of my images to a certain style that is trained based on my past images. I have a program that delivers the images AI sorted by individual in the images. The longer I think about it the more uses I realize I use AI for.

AI: That's incredibly detailed - thank you for painting such a clear picture of how deeply integrated AI has become across your entire business. I can really hear the collaborative approach you've developed.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I am in the process of extending my business to include "Lifestyle Photography" which is like family photos. Instead of just integrating it into the current business I have I decided it needs to be it's own standalone thing - really so that my wedding business doesn't become in-cohesive and watered down. In the earliest stages I think AI helped me come to this conclusion when I was confused about how to fuse the two together. But this overhaul that I'm still currently in has required that I look through every corner of my business and convert my business assets (like pdf guides, onboarding documents, contracts, website copy, portfolio blogs, sales scripts) into a version that communicates my lifestyle photography objectives and services. Some of that has been more administrative and editorial in nature, but overall I "outsource" a lot of the decision brainstorming and idea creation to AI because it's an overwhelming and idea-heavy process. Since I train my AI chats with things like my mission statement or values or desired outcome, when I present an idea or problem I find that it will consider the impact to that "bigger vision" more easily and consistently than I do. When it knows in such detail my entire business or even just my client experience, I find that when I do even really quick chats like "how should I respond to this email" it gives a very well considered and thorough answer. But this use of AI for the "creation" stages of things provides me so much more freedom to pursue the best ideas well and abandon the not so great ones early.

AI: That's a really compelling example - it sounds like AI has become almost like a strategic business partner that helps you maintain consistency with your bigger vision while navigating complex decisions.

When you're working with AI in these situations, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I think I like to think of myself as the decider or CEO in this example, and think of that AI as the "collector" or researcher or well of information. As mentioned, I typically begin with my objective. Even a simple weekly task of writing up all my Instagram captions for the week, I will know what I want them to push towards or "sell" and so my collaboration with the AI is asking it to provide me a wealth of ideas of topics that I can then pare down and dive deeper every step with. Once I narrow down to one topic for a caption, for example, then the new objective is to write that caption, and first I need a hook...so I go to the AI and ask for a wealth of ideas of hooks that would capture the reader. This process just continues until I feel like I like where we've landed. I frequently find myself saying things like "no I don't like any of these ideas. Let's try something that (is more educational, or more emotionally driven for example)..." and then steer the direction a bit to get ideas closer to what my original vision was. I find it is one of the most valuable things to help me ideate, test, and come to decisions quickly, but keeping myself in creative control is essential with my work.

AI: That's such a clear dynamic - you as the CEO setting direction and the AI as this expansive research partner helping you explore possibilities within your vision. It really sounds like you've found a way to maintain your creative authority while leveraging AI's ability to generate options.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Definitely the amount of time I spend on these tasks has been reduced. Sometimes I feel that it is reduced, but then the AI provides me with such great and clear ideas that I am able to execute them significantly better. The quality of my ideas has gone up, my decisions are more decisive because often I've been walked through many potential "outcomes" or ideas and so my confidence is higher, and I know for sure the output is improved. The use of the AI tools in my post process with culling and editing my images has cut my turnaround time on galleries from 12 weeks to about 3! And when I provide better marketing materials, a better client experience, faster service, etc. then I am able to charge more, so I know I've been making significantly more money. I do feel that one downside is the sheer amount of ideas I run through - it can give me a sense of shiny-object syndrome and if not kept in check it can run me a bit crazy and feeling like there's always something new to chase. I've had to become much more intentional about what I take on and how it will promote my long-term objectives.

AI: That's such an honest reflection - the dramatic improvements in turnaround time and quality, but also that challenge of managing the overwhelming flow of possibilities. It sounds like AI has almost amplified your creative potential but required you to develop new discipline around focus.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I worry that relying on it too much in regards to creative steps will remove me from the drivers seat. I avoided using AI for my photo editing for a very long time thinking that was too big of a "part" of what I do to outsource it, that the creative reflection of "me" would no longer be in my work. I personally found a workflow that leans on AI to do the bulk of the work early on, so that I am less crunched for time and have more freedom to revisit the gallery with my own eyes, intentionally making edits and tweaks that I may have missed before or not had time for. So in a sense, I am more removed from the "busy work" of editing like exposing or fixing skin tones, and I am more free to let the creative pieces be tended to intentionally - like crop or adding in depth of shadows, or removing distractions. I think there's hardly any downside on my end for using it in an administrative sense, to draft or template email responses, or to think of the workflows that are more thorough to fit the experience I want a client to have, but on the creative side I have to carefully watch how far removed I become from the heart of my work and to consider what impact that is having on my work.

AI: That's such a thoughtful distinction you've made - using AI to handle the technical busy work so you can focus more intentionally on the truly creative decisions. It sounds like you've found a way to let AI enhance rather than replace your creative voice.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see the photography industry embracing AI more as a "whole." I know in my circles I am one of the few that really embrace these tools and even just the use of AI bots in every day to alleviate the brain fog of general business ownership. I'm excited to see what special AI powered tools may be created. The ones I use now have objectively been the best thing to improve my output, time spent, earning power, etc. But I do worry that with mass acceptance there will be the risk that AI is used more - but not more intentionally. I do not want to fall into that trap. I also see there being a limit to the growth or adoption I can have of these tools because of my standard or guardrails protecting my creative ownership. As I scale my business I think it will help more with administrative stuff. I never ever see myself abandoning the tools I use now, so it can only go "up" from here, but I do wonder how much more. I do find new ways every day to use it, so I'm sure I will continue to explore and invest in these options. I think it will be embraced as photographers dabble in it and realize that if done right you really can still protect and keep your creative ownership and direction.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining creative ownership while leveraging AI as a strategic partner, and how you've developed such intentional guardrails to stay in the driver's seat.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about finding that balance between efficiency and creative control, and how you've developed such a thoughtful, intentional approach to AI collaboration, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you shared both the tremendous benefits you've experienced - like cutting your turnaround time from 12 weeks to 3 - as well as the challenges of managing that "shiny object syndrome" and staying focused on your long-term vision. Your approach to using AI as a strategic research partner while maintaining yourself as the CEO making the creative decisions is a fascinating model.

Thanks again, and best of luck with your lifestyle photography expansion!
104
creativity_0102Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions, let's begin!

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a writer and editor, so my flow varies a lot from project to project, but generally it goes like this:
-I go over the initial version of the project, be it notes I took on a show or movie or a rough draft someone else sends me, like a blog post or a manuscript. I take it in and make any obvious edits, like fixing typos or grammar mistakes, or rephrasing things that don't quite sound right.
