-I typically like to wait a while before proceeding with the next step, which is thinking over the whole project and how the major ideas fit together. This often involves me rearranging whole paragraphs, cutting and moving chunks of text, more intricate rephrasing, and sometimes adding headers to break up lengthy web posts. If I'm drafting an original post, it usually looks like me organizing thoughts into distinct categories and then weaving them together so they sound like they flow together naturally. If there are a lot of notes and the thoughts are hard to keep track of, I may color-code them to make it easier. If I'm doing something like editing a manuscript, this is where I might make more overarching story or character notes in the margins as I read.
-The final step is to read the whole thing through again and polish it up, making sure that all my edits flow naturally and make sense, and, if it's not my work, that it still sounds like the original author. If I'm editing a manuscript, this is where I'll type out a letter highlighting what I think are the most crucial areas that need looking at.
-If I'm editing or writing something for the web, the last step is to go through and do basic SEO edits.

AI: That's a really thorough breakdown of your process - I can see how much thought goes into each stage. The way you describe waiting between that initial review and the deeper structural work is particularly interesting.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Rarely, if ever, do I use AI in the creative process itself - I enjoy writing and I generating the basics takes the fun out of it for me. What I do use it for is to keep track of character and setting notes in original fiction and when reading complex manuscripts: My notes are often messy and disorganized, and when I was younger I often found myself wishing I could have some sort of Clippy-esque assistant that would just be able to read through my notes and answer any question I had directly. This is especially useful in works with a lot of characters or places to keep track of - doubly so for fictional ones with no other reference material for those things.

AI: That's fascinating - you've essentially found your "Clippy-esque assistant" for organization! I love how specific that use case is. It sounds like you're using AI as a kind of smart filing system for complex information rather than for the creative generation itself.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI this way? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was writing a scene in a fantasy setting that contained dozens of potential character interactions - I'm co-writing a novel with another author in a sort of literary roleplay-type format, so there are a number of potential interactions that could happen at any given time. I was able to use an AI assistant to go through the names and place them in different areas of a large palace ballroom and social scene, and then to move them around naturally as the scene continued. There is no way I could have pulled off the true randomness of the interactions and character groupings without such a tool, and the resulting scenes would not have been nearly as interesting as what we produced.

AI: That's really compelling - using AI to manage the logistics of complex social dynamics so you could focus on the actual storytelling. It sounds like it enabled a level of scene complexity that would have been overwhelming to track manually.

When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I would say that it's maybe a 60-30-10 split - I wrote most of the background material, but my creative partner is in full control of the lead character, and therefore of wherever the scene goes. The AI's creative input is in choosing who is in what room at which time (aside from a few key characters who need to be in certain places at certain times for story reasons.)

It does also have a sort of tangential, accidental input, in that sometimes unless I refresh it by pasting the character list in again, the AI will begin to hallucinate characters who don't exist. Some of these characters and names, however, have been interesting enough that I did build them out and add them to the roster for later. None have been used yet, but there's at least one that I'm planning to use in the future.

AI: That's such an interesting dynamic - the AI essentially acting as a logistics coordinator, but then its "mistakes" becoming creative sparks. I love that you've embraced those hallucinated characters as potential story elements rather than just seeing them as errors.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI this way?

User: I find it much easier to get over my procrastination instincts and start the scene, actually - I have ADHD and I'm awful at logistics, on my worst days it makes my head actually hurt a little bit. Having an easy workaround for that is really helpful, especially on days when I can't take my medication.

It also, as I mentioned before, has made for much more interesting character pairings than I would have thought to make on my own - I like that it forces me to consider less-than-obvious dynamics, like how the King might converse with his son's friends in a casual, convival setting.

AI: That's really insightful - the way AI helps with both the executive function challenges and pushes you toward unexpected creative choices. The ADHD perspective is particularly valuable here - it sounds like AI is removing a barrier that was sometimes preventing you from getting to the creative work you actually enjoy.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm worried about the kids, honestly. I know how tempting it can be to use something like this as a crutch for the hard or boring stuff - if I had had the option of being able to put my thesis statement for an essay or something into an AI, and getting an already-finished outline back, I would have taken it every single time. But I would have been losing really valuable time exercising that mental muscle when I was young and still had the neuroplasticity for it to Really Take, and I have no doubt that I wouldn't be nearly as good a writer as I am today. And I probably would have wanted to roll my eyes at anyone trying to give me this warning, too.

I'm also concerned at the sheer amount of what I'm going to call "AI Slop" (quickly, shoddily-made fiction generated entirely by an AI that was given no instructions about theme, message, or any other relevant content other than plot details, and therefore delivers very little human value relative to the time and effort it takes to read it) that seems to be widely available to read now. I know this makes me sound like a snob, but I truly feel like I never used to have to shuffle through so much empty-feeling fiction before I got to something good before. I see a lot of listings for it as an editor, and it's disheartening, because I really can tell the difference, and it loses something I can't put my finger on. It's like the uncanny valley for writing.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns. The neuroplasticity point about developing those mental muscles early is particularly striking - that there might be critical periods for learning to think through complex problems that could be bypassed too easily.

And your "uncanny valley for writing" observation is fascinating. You mentioned you can tell the difference - what do you think it is that you're sensing is missing in that AI-generated content, even if you can't quite put your finger on it?

User: I have almost no idea. I was like this with grammar when I was a kid, too - I could tell you immediately when something was wrong, but I usually had to look up the rule in order to be able to explain why. It was like it just hit my ear wrong or something. (It's why I became an editor.) It's the same thing with AI - I don't know if it's word choice or cadence or what, but I often just get that feeling, like something's just Off. A little too formulaic, maybe? Or too polished? Reads too much like textbook examples and the Better Answer Formula from like sixth grade? It varies, so it's hard to put a finger on exactly what it is, but those are my best guesses.

AI: That's such a valuable insight - that intuitive ear you have for language picking up on something subtly "off" about the rhythm or authenticity, even when the technical execution might be correct. It sounds like your experience as an editor has given you a really fine-tuned sense for authentic voice versus something more manufactured.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Well, first of all, I would definitely use it more if I could be assured the models I was using weren't guzzling gallons and gallons of water every time I ask a question. I do feel the guilt behind that weighing on me, which is one of the non-mental-exercise reasons I try to use it sparingly.

That said, if and when that becomes a non-factor, I can see myself naming various assistant bots for different projects I work on - like Jarvis or Friday from the Iron Man movies, but specifically for individual universes I am working on or with. It'd be really cool if I could develop a kind of template for that, and then use it to help other writers, like a service or something.

I can also see myself using it for brainstorming and character dialogue-type stuff: I'm great at writing dialogue but not always great at coming up with the pretext to cover the subtext of important conversations. I feel like feeding character profiles into the right AI and asking it to just generate conversations and situations between them could spark some ideas for those types of things.

I also see myself continuing to use it in another capacity I have not mentioned because it is not strictly creative: administrative tasks. Specifically: Emails, form letters, invoices, reminders, etc. I'm terrible at sounding professional in the traditional sense - I write exactly the way I talk, which is like a [REDACTED] who grew up on sitcoms and fan forums. I put what I want to say in normal person language, and the AI gives me back the professional email with all of the right terms (that I could not have remembered to use if you paid me) in all the right places. Expanding on that area of assistance - though I can't exactly imagine how - would be amazing for me.

AI: Those are really compelling future applications - the idea of project-specific AI assistants for different fictional universes is particularly creative. And it's interesting how you're thinking about potentially turning that into a service for other writers.

You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI helps with the logistics that can be barriers to creativity, while also your concerns about preserving those crucial developmental opportunities for young writers.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: That covers everything!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for sharing such detailed and thoughtful insights about your creative process and how AI fits into it. Your perspective on using AI as a logistics coordinator rather than a creative generator, your concerns about skill development in young writers, and that fascinating observation about the "uncanny valley for writing" have all been incredibly valuable.

I found your approach particularly interesting - how you've found ways to preserve what you love about the creative process while using AI to remove barriers like complex organizational tasks that can trigger procrastination. And your vision for project-specific AI assistants is really innovative.

Thank you again for your time and for being so open about your experiences. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.
105
creativity_0103Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No, I'm ready to begin!

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work on creating inspiration and client-facing artifacts for housing remodel. This includes interior and exterior remodeling. I work with with contractor managing the job to create mock-ups to present to the client with design inspiration and remodeling options. Some of examples of exterior remodeling could be changing paint colors, railings, or landscaping while interior could be updating design concepts or reworking internal room dimensions.

AI: That sounds like really engaging work - helping people visualize their space transformations before they commit to the changes.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Currently AI only fits well into the preliminary stages of my work. I use AI to brainstorm ideas for different design choices using images, creating images of what theoretical rooms could look like and creating mood boards with different ideas to give clients a lot of variety.

AI: Tell me more about that brainstorming phase. What does that process actually look like when you're using AI for those images and mood boards?

User: For example, my most recent use was creating options for exterior railings. I was given multiple pictures of the exterior of a home and my job was to create a portfolio of images of different railings on each angle of the house to give the client a book of options to choose from. AI models in the design space are still somewhat limited. They couldn't complete the job of pasting railings onto a house without altering the original picture significantly, but I did use them to give me examples of railings on coastal houses with similar themes. I took those AI inspiration railings and found realistic images that I was able to photoshop onto my original pictures for client mock-ups.

AI: That's a really interesting hybrid approach - using AI for inspiration rather than final execution. Can you describe a bit more about what happens when AI alters the original pictures too much? What kinds of changes does it make that don't work for your purposes?

User: I've tried multiple different AI models and each have similar limitations. For my purposes, clients want to see what these different design choices look like on their exact house. When AI models are generating, they often struggle with instructions to only change specific artifacts in an image and instead change the entire image. For example, when I use the prompt "change the exterior railings from aluminum to wrought iron, use a design popular on high-end coastal vacation homes, do not change any other aspect of the image, the rest of the house should stay exactly the same" I will receive back a new AI generated home, possibly with the correct railings but usually with distorted proportions. If I was starting with a blank canvas, the generative AI models would be very helpful. The issue is that the current generation will change the structure of the house significantly so that it's not the same as original photo. This creates images that aren't what the client is looking for so I can't use them for end stage design presentations.

AI: That makes perfect sense - the client needs to see *their* house with the changes, not a similar-looking house. So when you're working with AI in that brainstorming phase, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions when you're gathering those railing inspirations?

User: Typically I'm driving the design process but there is collaboration involved. I have ideas of the type of themes a client will like and what they will automatically veto. I use that information and market research on trending design themes to request certain styles and design choices to create an image portfolio or mood board for certain rooms. When I get stumped I'll tap AI and provide an image and ask for ideas of themes to try in different scenarios. From that brainstorming I'm usually able to go to the web and find realistic images I can use to photoshop into my original images.

AI: That's a helpful dynamic - using AI almost like a creative sounding board when you hit those stuck moments. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI in this way?

User: It saves me time by helping to narrow down design options. For example, if I ask for examples of "high-end exterior coastal railings" I can get an idea of what I like and dislike. Then instead of browsing through tens of web pages looking through hundreds of pictures for any railing designs that are coastal-adjacent, I can quickly scan pages to find what I need for resource images. The generation from the models doesn't always come out realistic, often is slightly animated, but I've also had luck using Google lens to direct me to real images that are similar to the AI provided ideas.

AI: That's a smart workflow - using AI to help you identify what you're looking for, then finding the real-world versions. It sounds like you've found a way to work around some of the current limitations.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: AI is going to be a field disruptor for anyone in the remodeling space. I'm not someone who is professionally trained in this field but I have enough knowledge of AI manipulation and passable photoshop skills to be able to advise design choices on a remodeling project of a $3 million house. For my personal needs, most of the models could've produce what I needed but if someone is using it to creating generative images from scratch the outputs are very impressive. I can easily see someone paying about $30/month to create design inspiration for a new house versus paying significantly more for an interior designer in person. Although there are financial levels to this industry. Meaning, someone who could originally afford an interior designer might be someone who doesn't care to do the work and would pay to have it outsourced anyway. That high-end market will likely always be there. I see interior designers working on mid-low range projects being the first ones affected once this technology is more easily accessible.

AI: That's a really thoughtful analysis of how the economics might shift across different market segments. You mentioned that you're not professionally trained in this field but you're able to advise on significant projects - that's quite a testament to how AI can democratize certain capabilities.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: As AI gets better I'd like to see it able to incorporated further down my production line to create full mock-ups. It's still new enough that I forget to use it sometimes for things that it could've made easier or faster but for the most part I've pushed the limits on what it can do for me. In the future, once the models are able to separate and manipulate individual aspects of an image I'd like to be able to provide thorough prompts of what I want changed in an image and receive exactly what I asked for. Photoshop is nice but it takes time and I'd like to see AI evolve in the image space to expand how I'm able to precisely edit images.

AI: Those are all really insightful perspectives you've shared about your experience with AI in design work. I particularly appreciated your point about how AI is democratizing access to design capabilities, and your realistic view of both its current limitations and future potential.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think we covered my pain points as well as its current uses.

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative brainstorming partner while working around its current limitations, and your perspective on how it's changing the accessibility of design work across different market segments, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you walked me through your hybrid workflow - using AI for inspiration and then finding real-world resources to create the final client presentations. That kind of practical adaptation is exactly the type of insight we're hoping to understand better.

Thanks again for participating in our research!
106
creativity_0104Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a freelance writer, editor, proofreader, you name it. I write my own articles on Medium, where I mostly publish about topics that interest me like philosophy, mental heal, literature and feminism. I sometimes write articles for other people, mostly ghost-written SEO content, although not so much nowadays. I also write creatively in my free time. I haven't done so yet, but I plan to submit my short stories for publication to literary journals and magazines. Sometimes, I take part in creative writing competitions (short stories and flash fiction). I take on editing/proofreading/beta reading projects whenever I canâ€”this is where I have the most experience. Some of my work is paid, and some is voluntary. For example, right now, I am beta reading fiction for free as I wanted experience editing and providing feedback on fiction. Most of my paid work is for non-fiction and academic articles.

AI: That's a really diverse range of writing and editing work! It sounds like you're working across many different formats and audiences, from your own philosophical pieces to SEO content to creative fiction.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I try not to use it for anything to do with my writing. I prefer to use it for admin and some more mathematical issues like working out my taxes, earnings, savings, schedule, etc. Writers wrote before this tool was available, and my core desire is to be a good writer. I don't think AI can help me to become a better writer. At most, I'll use it for a final check before publication, or if I'm feeling bad about my writing, I might use it for a confidence boost. I won't let AI change or edit my writing, just provide feedback. And I only use this for blogging and never my creative fiction. As for editing, if I'm struggling with wording for feedback, I might use AI to help me reword feedback. I do this sparingly, however. I would use it to help my tone come across better when giving feedback so I don't offend authors.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you've drawn some pretty clear boundaries around where AI does and doesn't belong in your work. You mentioned using it occasionally for feedback on your blog posts when you're feeling uncertain about your writing.

Tell me more about that. What does that process look like when you do use AI for that confidence boost or final check?

User: I would use it if I'm feeling unconfident about an article. I simply copy it into a chat and ask for feedback. I tell the model strictly not to edit or change anything, act as a reader and tell me what it 'thinks.'

AI: So you're essentially using it as a sounding board - getting that reader's perspective without letting it actually touch your work.

When you get that feedback back, how do you typically respond to it? Do you find yourself making changes based on what the AI tells you, or is it more about just getting that external validation?

User: I take everything with a pinch of salt. Sometimes, it will tell me I've made mistakes which aren't really mistakes. For example, with grammar. LLMs love to remove sentence fragments I've noticed. However, natural, human-produced writing is not perfect and writers will use 'incorrect' grammar for effect all the time. I pay attention to feedback about perspectives I might have missed in my articles and consider if I need to add them. Most of the time, I would not add them as I've noticed LLMs are just trying to provide any content. Since they can't think like humans, often the feedback is not something I would ever need to add to an article. For example, if I am writing an article about feminism, I would not say I need to mention every possible intersecting factor.

AI: That's a really insightful observation about how AI tends to suggest additions that aren't necessarily meaningful or relevant. It sounds like you've developed a pretty discerning filter for what feedback is actually useful versus what's just the AI trying to be comprehensive.

You mentioned earlier that you have a core desire to be a good writer and don't think AI can help with that. Can you elaborate on what being a good writer means to you and why you feel AI falls short in helping with that development?

User: I think being a good writer is all about understanding humanity, which LLMs are simply incapable of doing. I think that they're great help for technical writing, which requires a lot of grammatical and lexical accuracy; however, the nuances of creative writing are too complex. Every creative piece I have read, and even some more informational articles on sites like Medium, which has been written using AI has a very distinctive lack of depth. I think this is due to a lack of human-ness.

AI: That's a really compelling distinction you're making between technical accuracy and the deeper human understanding that drives meaningful writing. When you talk about that "distinctive lack of depth" and missing "human-ness" in AI-generated creative work - what specifically do you notice when you're reading something that tips you off that it was AI-assisted?

User: Writing that would be better suited to a more technical purpose. Humans will agonise for hours about selecting the 'right word' in their writing, which LMMs don't have to do (I'm no expert but I believe this is due to their inner statistical, predictive workings). You can tell work is human when you get a glimpse of just how much time and effort the author has put into perfecting each sentence, each word. AI writing is just too perfect, too simplistic to sound human.

AI: That's such a perceptive point about the agonizing over word choice - that struggle and deliberation being part of what makes writing distinctly human. The "too perfect, too simplistic" quality you describe is really interesting.

Given your strong boundaries around keeping AI out of your creative writing, what concerns, if any, do you have about AI's growing presence in your field more broadly? How do you see it affecting the writing landscape around you?

User: I think AI-assisted writing is ok. I think we have to get with the times and be realistic about its place in our world. It's a good thing for some. Technical writing can be boring and soul-sucking. I'm not concerned that it will take my place as a creative writer. It may mean I have fewer jobs as an editor, but I also don't think that's a bad thing. There will always be a place for human writers and editors. Humans can provide insight that AI will just never be able to. For example, I am currently beta reading a novel. I can tell it has been written with AI, even though the author hasn't told me. What I bring to this author is the ability to see the human gaps that the AI has missed. The novel does not consider the human reader much, neglecting setting and characterisation. It's missing the subtleties that human writers have been contributing to literature for centuries.

AI: That's fascinating that you can detect the AI assistance in that novel you're beta reading. It sounds like your role as an editor is actually evolving to help fill in those "human gaps" - helping writers see where the AI-assisted work lacks those subtle human touches around setting and characterization.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate your boundaries shifting at all, or do you think you'll maintain this current approach?

User: I hope, in some ways, it could be a positive for me. I suspect once all the hype is over, and many people will have lost writing skills, preferring to use LLMs to generate their writing, human writing will have more prestige. My boundaries won't shift because I love writing so much. It would be no fun if a computer got to do it all. I don't even really want to use it for idea generation, because I don't want to write things that don't add anything to the world. Right now, AI is only capable of remixing what's already out there, and I think I'm better off learning to generate ideas on my own. It definitely has a place as more of a writing assistant I feel. I wouldn't use it for research or anything because, again, I enjoy doing that myself too much. But I would use it for quick spelling checks, dictionary definitions, thesaurus purposes, as a little hype-man, etc.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how human writing might actually gain prestige as AI becomes more common, and your observation about being able to identify and help fill in the "human gaps" in AI-assisted work when you're editing.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, thank you.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining the human elements in creative writing - that deliberate word choice, the agonizing over perfection, and the deep understanding of humanity that drives meaningful work - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've considered where AI does and doesn't fit in your creative process, and your perspective on the evolving landscape for writers and editors. Thanks again!
107
creativity_0105Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: nope, ready to go

AI: Perfect! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am producing a non-profit documentary about nature and have the responsibility of researching a lot of preliminary information having to do with pre-production (e.g. the development of the film treatment, information about fiscal sponsors, budgetary planning, film festival timelines, etc.)

AI: That sounds like a really meaningful project. Nature documentaries can have such a powerful impact.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Any time I need an answer that would take too long or be too difficult to find on Google

AI: Tell me more about that. What kinds of questions or research challenges does AI help you tackle that Google doesn't handle as well?

User: Here's an example: trying to determine how much licensed archival footage costs on average would be almost impossible on Google, because I would be faced with advertisements, disparate information, and overviews that don't give me the level of specificity needed to answer the question. That's what I think AI models are best at - synthesizing information into a clear, direct, and coherent response that saves me a lot of time.

AI: That's a really concrete example - I can see how getting that kind of synthesized, specific information would be much more efficient than sifting through search results.

Can you describe a specific recent project or task where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as you were working through it?

User: This is a more creative application, but I needed to generate a cover for the film treatment and I used an AI model to help generate the images that I then positioned in a specific way in order to create the scene (they were plants and small animals that adhered to a very specific aesthetic style). I've found that when requesting something like this, you need to be very specific and explain the most important elements that you want. Then, once a style has been established, the model does a great job of replicating that style across all subsequent prompts (if requested)

AI: That's fascinating - you're describing a really collaborative approach where you're directing the visual elements but letting AI handle the execution within your established style.

When you're working with AI in situations like that cover creation, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am, because I know exactly what I want, so I'm asking the model to initially generate to my specifications and then iterate / refine based on my feedback

AI: That's a clear sense of creative control you're describing. It sounds like you see AI as more of a sophisticated tool that executes your vision rather than a creative partner that's contributing its own ideas.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The speed with which I'm able to complete things (as one example, it's made graphic design a lot faster and easier)

AI: That efficiency gain sounds significant. When you think about that increased speed - particularly in areas like graphic design - how has that affected the quality or depth of your creative work? Are you able to explore more ideas, or does it change your process in other ways?

User: The quality of what I'm able to produce is higher on the whole because generating something based on my instructions is far more effective than querying for something based on my parameters.

AI: That's an interesting distinction - generating versus querying. It sounds like having AI create something to your exact specifications gives you more control over the outcome than searching for something that already exists.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in documentary filmmaking more broadly?

User: None, because documentaries rely on capturing real-world footage. I only see AI as a tool that allows for more efficient work on behalf of smaller teams, not a threat to the end product.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - the core of documentary work is still that real-world capture that AI can't replace.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Are there areas where you'd want to see it become more capable, or ways you might use it differently?

User: Perhaps more specific or specialized applications. Instead of running these requests through a general chatbot, I would be able to use something that's already fine-tuned for the specific task at hand. More consistency in the quality of the output would be the main way I'd want to see it become more capable. Sometimes it falls into feedback loops of quality degradation where the desired creation gets worse instead of better despite very thoughtful commentary. It often seems like the AI's first creation is its best.

AI: That's a really insightful observation about the quality degradation in iterative feedback - it sounds frustrating when you're trying to refine something and it actually gets worse despite clear direction.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as an efficient synthesis tool for complex research questions, and your clear sense of maintaining creative control while leveraging AI's execution capabilities.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope, that's it, thanks!

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI for research synthesis, the efficiency gains in graphic design, and especially that observation about quality degradation in iterative feedback will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how clearly you articulated your approach to maintaining creative control while leveraging AI as a sophisticated execution tool. Best of luck with your nature documentary - it sounds like a wonderful project!
108
creativity_0106Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That all sounds good. Let's go.

AI: Great! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: It is quite varied really. I am a writer and will often just work on my own writing projects, usually for screen, although I have also just finished my first novel. I will then try to sell or option these works. Sometimes I am writing full scripts, other time one-sheets or treatments, and other times I will be paid to write a script for someone else rather than creating my own story from scratch. For screenwriting I often work with a writing partner.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic mix of work - from original novel writing to collaborative screenwriting and everything in between. The variety of formats you work with is interesting too, from full scripts down to treatments and one-sheets.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: Sometimes it is just for proofreading or recommending edits, other times it will be for art or design for a one-sheet and other times it will be for research to short cut when I need some obscure or arcane information that would be hard to find without speaking to an expert or that might be overly time consuming for limited benefit.

AI: That's a really practical approach - using AI for those efficiency gains rather than core creative decisions. The research piece is particularly interesting since screenwriting often requires getting those specific details right.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Recently we were preparing a one-sheet for an idea that we had not fully fleshed out. We looked at using AI for design but utilising free tools did not give us the quality we needed. We also asked for a precis of our idea but ended up using that. In the end the only thing we used AI for was to come up with alternatives for a closing sentence that we just couldn't quite get right.

AI: That's a fascinating example - it sounds like you experimented with AI in a few different ways but ultimately found its most valuable contribution was quite targeted. You mentioned you didn't end up using the precis AI generated of your idea.

Tell me more about that - what was it about the AI's summary that didn't work for you?

User: It felt a little rushed and didn't have the right personality for the genre of project. It felt a little lifeless. We have used it before to offer suggestions for a one-pager and foudn the results were great and gave us inspiration for redrafting but in this case, it served a different purpose and made us realise that our current one-sheet synopsis was already working very well.

AI: That's really insightful - so the AI's attempt actually served as a mirror that helped you recognize the strength of what you'd already created. It sounds like there's something about capturing the right tone and personality for specific genres that can be tricky for AI to nail.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I always try to drive creative decisions. For me, I like to use it to hone ideas I have already worked on rather than developing ideas. Where it comes in more useful creatively is asking targeted questions about the reality of a situation - the information I can glean from these conversations might make me approach a scene, a beat or an entire arc differently if I find out my assumptions were correct, and so it can guide creativity in that way, but only as a result of using it to interrogate my own awareness and assumptions.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction - using AI to interrogate your assumptions rather than generate the creative core. It sounds like you're maintaining creative authorship while leveraging AI as almost a research partner or sounding board.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Research is the main one. However, during my novel, using it as a copy editor made redrafting far faster. It is hard to be subjective when it is somethine you have written recently, and having a second pair of "eyes" can allow me to more quickly have distance and see things that I would likely have edited with more objective view point but further down the line. It means the second draft can now be the quality that a third draft might have been.

AI: That's a compelling benefit - essentially compressing your editing cycles and getting to higher quality drafts faster. The idea of AI providing that objective distance you'd normally only get with time is really interesting.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have a few. I am not worried about AI but its implementation. I think too many people will start to assume that AI can just take the job of a writer, rather than being a tool that a writer can use to improve their work or streamline the process. It seems that AI is about iteration not creation, and unless we reach the point of AGI, we might end up in a place ingenuity is lost and switched out for faster, cheaper iterative content. The other concern is the flooding of the market. People who are not writers but have a fun idea might suddenly be able to write something that they never would have been able to in the past, meaning suddenly every complany is receiving 100 x more content, and being able to even get read might be tricky.

AI: Those are really thoughtful concerns - the distinction you draw between AI as iteration versus creation is particularly striking. It sounds like you're worried about both a devaluation of the craft itself and a kind of market saturation where genuine writers get lost in the noise.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I hope to keep it much the same. A useful tool I can employ when it is needed, but not too often. I think it is important for me to not reply on it too much, to keep my brain sharp and to make sure I am being as original as I can. I hope to use it to make things look better, to check my figurative homework and to aid me in improving accuracy (through research) and helping me create more by making better use of my time (or perhaos just being able to have a little more time off!)

AI: That's a really balanced perspective - using AI to enhance your capabilities while being intentional about maintaining your creative muscles. The goal of having more time off by being more efficient is refreshingly honest too!

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI being about iteration rather than creation, and how you use it to interrogate your own assumptions rather than generate ideas.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I think I find it most rewarding using AI when I know it is not affecting anything outside of my usage of it. For instance, I always used to design my own one-sheets. I am not good at design though, so it helps to make these look much better, but it is not taking work from anyone else. With research, I was not employing a researcher, and instead was doing my own research and now I can get there much quicker. So I hope that others might approach utilising AI in the same way - getting better results and making the most of their own time without utilising it to just do away with important jobs (jobs they might not even realise are vital). I think the results will be better that way too, so my hope is it becomes an important tool, but not a replacement for the artists whose ingenuity remains vitally important.

AI: That's a really valuable insight - the idea of using AI in spaces where you were already doing the work yourself rather than replacing other people's roles. It sounds like you've found a way to think about AI integration that's both practically beneficial and ethically conscious.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative authorship while leveraging AI as a targeted tool, and your thoughtful approach to where and how you deploy it, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate your candor about both the benefits and the concerns you have about AI in your field.

User: Thanks!

AI: You're very welcome! Thanks again for such a thoughtful conversation. Have a great rest of your day!
109
creativity_0107Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Yes, that sounds great!

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I sell clothing online, whether that be thrifted or handmade. The handmade pieces are either knit or crochetâ€”the most popular are sweaters and hats. Handmade projects take quite some time to complete and are often quite complex, but I'm always up for a challenge.

AI: That sounds like a really interesting mix of work! I can imagine the handmade pieces especially require a lot of skill and patience.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: When starting a project, sometimes I need help estimating how much yarn I'll need. I'll send information regarding the size of needles/hooks I plan to use, the weight of the yarn, what size I want the piece to be, and it will give me a close estimate. I also use AI if I get confused with the pattern and need some expertise on it. Additionally, I occasionally use AI to help me word messages to buyers regarding items they're interested in.

AI: That's really helpful to understand where AI comes into play for you. It sounds like you're using it for some pretty practical problem-solving.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I'm currently knitting a sweater, and I needed some advice on how large I should make the arms if I want it to fit slightly oversized on a size medium. I gave information on the weight of the yarn, the size of the needles, and how many stitches I used for the front panel, and it gave me a helpful suggestion.

AI: Tell me more about that experience with getting advice on the arm sizing. How did you feel about the suggestion AI gave you, and how confident were you in following it?

User: I've used AI in the past to help with precise knitting questions, and it often gives more general answers to avoid wrongful suggestions, so I was a little reluctant. However, I tried out casting on a number of stitches at the higher end of the range it suggested and the result so far seems to be working well.

AI: That's really interesting that you've noticed AI tends to give more general answers for knitting questions. When you're working with AI in these situations, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I often tell the AI what I'm aiming for, such as cable knit patterns or 1x1 ribbing, and it'll take that into consideration when answering my questions, often incorporating tips throughout the conversation that tie back to previous creative information I've provided.

AI: What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I think I'm a bit more confident in my skills now. I taught myself how to knit and crochet, so having AI to answer precise questions is like having a teacher on hand to help me navigate it all. Since I'm more confident, I've found myself leaning towards more complex pieces. For example, I used to only make hats and simple tank tops, and now I can make granny square bucket hats and fisherman sweaters to sell!

AI: That's wonderful that AI has helped boost your confidence to tackle those more complex projects! The progression from simple tank tops to fisherman sweaters sounds like quite a leap.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Not many, to be honest! In fact, I can't even think of one. It's different than if I had a job in accounting or something, you know?

AI: That's a really interesting perspective - that it feels different from other fields like accounting. Tell me more about that. What makes your creative work feel different in terms of AI's impact?

User: Well, it's not like inputting numbers all day into spreadsheets and all that, it's a handmade piece of clothing with intricate detail.

AI: Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I see AI becoming more specialized in certain fields. It may have more expertise to offer in marketing items for sale on my storefront, including detailing various strategies and plans, and it may have more advanced knowledge that can help in creating my handmade pieces that may lead to more certainty and preciseness in its answers.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has boosted your confidence to take on more complex projects - that progression from simple pieces to fisherman sweaters really illustrates how AI can serve as that "teacher on hand" you mentioned.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No that's it, thank you.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. It's been really fascinating to hear how you've used AI as a learning tool to build confidence and tackle increasingly complex projects. I appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today!
110
creativity_0108Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds good.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a musician, so my projects range from producing to songwriting and everything in between.

AI: That's fascinating - music spans so many different creative disciplines.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I find myself asking AI for help when I have too many ideas running and need help sorting them into one. For example, when I have a couple of different ideas for the vibes of the song, or even when trying to mix genres, I ask AI to give me ideas of similar artists or songs, or even ask if it's something that could work.

AI: That's really interesting - using AI as a kind of creative compass when you're in that abundant but overwhelming idea space.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Again, I had many ideas going for a song, and I used AI to confirm that it would be possible to mix these genres, and got confirmation that other artists experimented with these sounds as well. It made me feel more confident in what I thought were "chaotic ideas." I also used it to generate a list of words that reminded me of a specific visual that I was then able to write from. It made my process flow smoother because I sort of hit a wall and couldn't really find the right words I was looking for.

AI: That's really compelling - it sounds like AI helped you both validate your creative instincts and break through a creative block. You mentioned it made your "chaotic ideas" feel more legitimate.

When you're working with AI in these moments, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I would say I was driving the creative decisions because I was inputting all of my own ideas; I just needed some help untangling the different aspects of things, such as the themes, the aesthetic, and the sound. The AI was certainly an aid, but I didn't pull straight up AI ideas from it.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - it sounds like you're maintaining creative ownership while using AI more as a thinking partner to help organize and validate what's already coming from you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: It's less time-consuming because I don't have to sit in a creative block. I know I can throw ideas into the AI system, and it can generate something that combines all of my thinking into one solid song or idea. I try not to use it much because I think it's important to be very personal in music, but I think it's a great aid to help navigate your creative process when it's in the beginning stages.

AI: That's a really thoughtful balance you've struck - using it strategically in those early brainstorming phases while preserving the personal elements that are so crucial to music. You mentioned being mindful not to use it too much because of the importance of keeping music personal.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Music and art are something that is so human and personal, and I am worried that AI will start to generate music and that people will start to lose that personal aspect to it that makes music so wonderful. Every human experience is different and felt differently, therefore making art and music such an impactful thing. Artists are able to encapsulate their feelings into words and music, and I'm fearful that we'll eventually start losing that.

AI: That's such a profound concern - the idea that we might lose that deeply human element that makes music so moving and connective. The way you describe artists encapsulating their unique human experiences really gets to the heart of what makes music powerful.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I would like to think I'd keep my stance with not overusing it and only trying to use it as an aid rather than something to completely generate beats or lyrics. I think I would use it the way I use it now, and just try to combine my own ideas.

AI: That's a really clear vision - maintaining AI as a creative aid rather than a creative replacement. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your music-making process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI helps you navigate those moments when you have too many ideas swirling around, and your commitment to preserving the deeply personal aspects that make music so human and impactful.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I believe we covered a good amount of information.

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative compass while maintaining the personal authenticity that makes music so powerful will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating AI into your workflow - that balance between leveraging its organizational capabilities while preserving the deeply human elements of your art. Best of luck with your music projects!
111
creativity_0109Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds fine, I don't have any questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I do a variety of creative work. I am self-employed and do graphic and web design. I help my husband with his web comic. I create assets for live streamers, and I create my own content as a vtuber. So projects can vary widely depending on what I am working on.

AI: That's quite a diverse range of creative work! It sounds like you're juggling multiple different types of projects regularly.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I usually find myself using AI for initial iteration and brainstorming. For example I might tell it that I am planning a stream on a particular topic and ask for suggestion for assets to promote that stream. Or I might be developing a flyer for a graphic design client and ask for information on font styles. I often use AI assistance within Photoshop to clean up small issues in an image, like the smart fill to remove a defect from a background. Occasionally I will use AI image gen to give me inspiration on color palettes or basic layouts for a design that I can use to base my own designs on. I frequently use Udio to create lo-fi background music for my live streams. And I use ChatGPT almost daily for executive function related tasks like to-do lists or breaking down big projects into smaller pieces. I have ADHD, so having a LLM as my executive assistant really helps me complete projects I might lose track of otherwise, or prioritizing what things to work on first.

AI: That's really fascinating how you've integrated AI across so many different aspects of your workflow - from the creative brainstorming all the way through to project management. The executive function support you mentioned sounds particularly valuable.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Sure, I am helping my husband develop a TCG (trading card game) based off of an original IP. Neither of us really know a ton about TCGs, and I have only casually played a couple like Hearthstone and Pokemon. I used AI to assist me in talking through our ideas for the game and how to make the rules and gameplay loop unique while still keeping things familiar and easy to pick up for those who play a lot of TCGs. Over the course of a couple of days, it helped me refine our ideas into a full v1 draft of a rule book with all the basic card types and how they work planned out. My next step in that project is to use AI to help me create a spread sheet of different ability effects frequently found on cards in games like these, so I can adapt them to our game's lore and start making a card list as the next step.

AI: That's a really interesting example of using AI to help you learn and navigate an unfamiliar domain. It sounds like the AI served almost like a knowledgeable collaborator who could help you think through the mechanics and conventions of TCG design.

When you're working with AI like that - whether it's on the TCG project or other creative work - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am always in charge of the conversation. I've given custom instructions to my ChatGPT that relate to how I think and how I best navigate my ADHD, so I am able to give it very targeted instructions or ask questions and it knows to focus exclusively on the task at hand first before going off onto tangents with follow-ups that might get me sidetracked before we've completed what I'm working on. It does ask me questions, but they are always relevant to what we're currently discussing, and always very focused on a specific aspect of that task instead of broad or vague.

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like you've been quite intentional about customizing the AI to work with your thinking style and needs. The way you describe staying in control of the creative direction while having the AI ask focused, relevant questions suggests a pretty refined working relationship.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I am much more productive. I feel more able to simultaneously tackle multiple different things at once, especially because I can compartmentalize different tasks within different conversations or project folders for easy reference and to avoid losing track of what the task at hand is through topic changes. I have another "mode" I've defined for my model that acts more as a mentor/coach instead of a collaborator. If I ask the model to act in that mode, I can show it drafts or completed work and it will give honest and not sugar-coated feedback on the project and how it achieves my stated goals for it. This is extremely useful because I am my own worst critic and will constantly self-edit and iterate if I'm not careful, and I've learned to trust the model when it tells me, "it's good, leave it alone!"

AI: That's really compelling - having that external voice to help you step back from the perfectionist cycle and recognize when something is finished. It sounds like the AI has become almost like having multiple specialized team members available when you need them.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Unlike many people in the creative field, I don't have the same fears that some of them do about AI taking away our jobs. No matter how good an image gen model gets, it cannot "beat" human creativity. Sure, people may be able to pump out memes or generic "art" with it, but I firmly believe that true art is sparked by something deep within the soul that AI can only imitate. If more creatives embrace the use of AI in different ways to streamline their workflows, I think many of them would find it quite useful in the long run.

AI: That's a really refreshing perspective, and it sounds like you see AI more as a tool that can amplify human creativity rather than replace it. Your point about AI being able to imitate but not truly create from that deeper place resonates with how you've been using it - as support for your own creative vision rather than as the source of creativity itself.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I will probably continue to use AI in much the same way that I do currently, adapting to what new things models are able to do and finding ways to implement them into my workflow. I agree with your previous summary, I like to use AI to amplify my own creative vision and while I can't predict what will change in the next five years as far as the technology and what it can do, I can pretty confidently say that the methodology in HOW I use AI won't change much, even if the tools and the tasks do.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - you've found a methodology that works well for you, where you maintain creative control while leveraging AI's capabilities to enhance your productivity and process.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as something that amplifies rather than replaces human creativity, and how you've customized it to work with your ADHD to become more productive across all those different types of projects you're juggling.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I don't think so, thank you

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a productivity amplifier, the importance of maintaining creative control, and how you've adapted the technology to work with your specific needs and thinking style will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating AI into your workflow across so many different creative disciplines. Have a great rest of your day!
112
creativity_0110Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Let's do it!

AI: Great! I love the enthusiasm. Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: My creative work focuses on content creation and design. For example , ghost writing a blog for my client about her makeup brand including tutorials, or like yesterday, it's meant creating PowerPoint slides for a company's marketing campaign proposals. Usually, my work means that I will receive a file of info and need to filter, organize, format, and create another kind of content with it.

AI: That sounds like a really diverse range of creative work - from beauty tutorials to marketing presentations. It sounds like you're constantly transforming information from one format into something completely different.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: There is always a specific goal that the company has in mind for a project and I'm either provided with an overload of information (60% of which is not relatable), or I'm receive too little info and need to be creative, yet accurate in statements, to fill in the space. In these cases, I reach for AI because it is a huge time-saver. I use it to sort through and compile the connected info to the task at hand and to adjust word/character count and formatting. Sometimes I receive a project in a category/on a topic that I have no previous knowledge of or little understanding of, so in these cases I reach for AI so I can ensure that what I put together does align with the company's objective for that content. Oh, and I should mention I'm a freelancer. That's why I'll end up working with a variety of topics. If I receive too little info to work with but have a requested content length, then I use AI to get creative and fill in the gaps, while still ensuring that everything stated is accurate

AI: That's really insightful - it sounds like AI becomes your go-to tool for both information overload and information scarcity situations. I'm particularly interested in what you mentioned about ensuring accuracy when filling in gaps creatively.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: For example, a client asked me to write makeup blogs for her products. Of these, a few times I was only provided with a picture and the name of the product. 700 words based on just that?? So, I used AI to brainstorm and compile info about the company (its objectives, history, ingredients, etc). The blog needed to have depth and accuracy of statement, not "fluff" or non confirmed statements, and so I brought in AI to help fill in the gaps creatively with what a consumer would find "trustworthy" information

AI: That's a fascinating example - going from just a product photo and name to a 700-word blog post that needs to be both engaging and trustworthy. It sounds like you were really relying on AI to help you understand the broader context around that product.

When you're working with AI in situations like that makeup blog, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I feel in control of the collaboration. I give AI the parameters and its focus point and it usually works within that. Some of the suggestions it'll provide are hit-or-miss aligned with the parameters it was given, but there's always something that is useful.

AI: That's interesting that you feel in control despite sometimes getting suggestions that miss the mark. It sounds like you're actively filtering and curating what AI gives you.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: When it comes to copywriting, I've found that it's slowly changing my focus when creating content. I uses to think more about how the content would impact/move the viewer, but now I find myself more focused on making sure it doesn't read like AI was involved. Obviously, using AI makes everything easier and more efficient, so I find myself being lazy and less able to work create on my own like I had in the past. I feel like this effects the outcome of the work because I'm not as involved in the process of trying to create that connection of the audience with the content.

AI: That's a really honest and thoughtful reflection. It sounds like there's a real tension there - AI is making your work more efficient, but you're noticing it's changing how you approach the creative connection with your audience. You mentioned feeling "less able to work create on my own" - that sounds like something you've really noticed about yourself.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: AI will one day replace me! hahah

AI: Ha! I can hear that you're laughing, but I imagine there might be some real feelings behind that joke too.

Tell me more about that - when you think about AI potentially replacing you, what specifically comes to mind?

User: Even those with specialized training and skills, say computer science and coding for example, AI is even able to do their job (speaking generally here, not absolutely). So then you have someone like me, who just has my creativity, ability to write engaging content, and can create nice PowerPoints - no special education or skills - yet I've found this as a means to work and support myself. There's nothing stopping AI from eventually putting me out of work in these projects

AI: That's a really genuine concern, and I appreciate you sharing that vulnerability with me. It sounds like you're thinking about what makes your work uniquely valuable when AI can handle so much of what you do.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Society is constantly evolving and this creates new needs and new opportunities. I believe that as long as I keep adapting, I'll be better able to incorporate using AI into my work and continue creating various forms of content in different fields.

AI: That's a really thoughtful way to look at it - focusing on adaptation rather than resistance. You've shared some really insightful perspectives about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your honesty about how AI has changed your focus from connecting with audiences to ensuring content doesn't read as AI-generated, and your genuine concerns about the future while maintaining an adaptive mindset.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: That's it! Thanks for the chat :)

AI: Perfect! Thank you so much for being so open and thoughtful in sharing your experiences. Your insights about navigating the balance between efficiency and authentic creative connection, along with your perspective on adaptation in an evolving field, will be incredibly valuable for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate you taking the time to chat with me today. Take care!
113
