creativity_0111Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Okay! I don't have any questions so far.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I work as professional freelance copy and content writer. I write ads for clients, landing pages, blog posts, write some for social media, a lot of different things. A typical project includes every step from brainstorming the idea with the client, to extensive competitor research, to creating the ad/web page/blog, proofreading/editing, and finalizing all deliverables.

AI: That sounds like a really comprehensive process with a lot of different moving parts. I can see how you're involved from the very beginning conceptual stages all the way through to the final polish.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I usually lean on AI for the brainstorming and research portion, and then again for the proof-reading and editing.

AI: Tell me more about that. What does the brainstorming and research look like when you're using AI?

User: If I'm starting a new project, like a blog for example, and the topic is rather broad, I'll first tell AI the general outline for the project I have in my head and ask the model to identify any gaps and expand on my outline.

AI: That's interesting - so you're using it almost like a thought partner to help you see what you might be missing. When you get that feedback from the AI about gaps or expansions, how do you work with those suggestions? Do you typically take them as-is, or do they spark other ideas for you?

User: Usually, I'll have them incorporated into the outline with a little bit of personal tweaking. Then I'll continue the process until I feel like I have a full outline. Often, the AI is TOO comprehensive for my required word counts and I find myself cutting out bits of the outline as I actually write the piece.

AI: That's a really practical insight - that the AI tends to be overly thorough for your actual needs. So it sounds like you're using it to generate more possibilities than you'll actually use, then you're the one making the editorial decisions about what to keep.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Recently, I wrote an entire landing page for a brand new health teach startup that had just launched their new health tracking app and community platform. I had NO idea what beats I should hit on the final landing page, it was the first time I had written one in this niche or for this type of product, so I first had the AI assistant outline a basic skeleton for the page. And then as I started filling in areas, sent my finished text back to be polished and placed into the outline. As I wrote, the outline started to evolve.

AI: That sounds like a really dynamic process - almost like the AI was helping you navigate unfamiliar territory at first, but then you took the lead as you got more comfortable with the project.

When you say the outline started to evolve as you wrote, tell me more about that. Was that you making those changes, or were you bouncing ideas back and forth with the AI?

User: I would realize the next section of the outline was too broad or too deeply technical, and I would issue the corrections to the model. Then I would write the section based off the updated outline.

AI: So you were really driving those refinements - recognizing when something wasn't quite right for the audience or scope and directing the AI to adjust.

When you're working with AI this way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I'm definitely in charge of all of the creative decisions. The AI assistant helps out, but it doesn't really suggest anything new or innovative. I have to be the one driving the project and identifying when the outline no longer fits my purpose.

AI: That's a really clear perspective - you see yourself as firmly in the driver's seat. You mentioned that AI doesn't suggest anything new or innovative. What do you think it does bring to the table then? What's valuable about having it in your process?

User: It really speeds up the entire process. Plus, because I work as a freelancer, I spend the bulk of my working hours alone without any outside interaction. I find the AI assistant incredibly valuable for bouncing ideas off of and for really having something to talk through roadblocks with. But I don't have the AI actually do the writing for me.

AI: That's fascinating - so beyond the practical efficiency gains, it's also serving as a kind of creative companion when you're working in isolation. The social aspect of having something to think through problems with seems really important.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I find myself starting at a blank page WAY less than I did before. I used to find the hardest part of the process getting started, but AI really helps me get something onto the page. Even if its an outline I don't really end up using, it helps me start getting my thoughts flowing.

AI: That's such a relatable challenge - that intimidating blank page! It sounds like AI has become a really effective way to break through that initial creative paralysis, even when what it produces isn't your final direction.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I read online ALL the time about how AI assistants are going to make jobs like mine completely obsolete. I love writing and it is really my passion. I am nervous that AI might make it so my job no longer includes much writing and instead will be a whole lot more editing and prompting.

Plus, AI is not always truthful. I worry it may start hurting human writer's credibility.

AI: Those are really understandable concerns. The idea that the core thing you're passionate about - the actual writing - might get squeezed out in favor of more editing and prompting sounds genuinely worrying. And that credibility issue is interesting too.

When you think about that potential shift toward more editing and prompting, what would that mean for you personally? How would that feel different from the work you do now?

User: Since, I now have experience training AI models and working with them extensively I hope I will be able to transition into one of those new editing and prompting roles easily. But it will take some of the fun and creativity out of my job. I'll get to do less thinking and have fewer original ideas.

AI: That tension is really striking - you recognize you'd likely be well-positioned for that transition given your experience, but at the cost of what makes the work fulfilling for you in the first place. The "less thinking and fewer original ideas" part seems like it would fundamentally change the nature of what draws you to this work.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Yeah, definitely. Just in the few years I've trained AI, I've seen it change so much. Where models used to struggle with short form blogs, they now blow some junior or untrained writers out of the water. I see AI eliminating a lot of entry roles and leaving space only for the folks, like myself, who have learned how to leverage this technology.

AI: That's a really thoughtful observation about how the bar has shifted so quickly. It sounds like you see a future where working effectively with AI becomes an essential skill rather than just a nice-to-have.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as both a practical tool for overcoming creative blocks and a kind of creative companion when you're working alone, while you maintain clear creative control.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Yeah, I think it's really important to draw attention to the fact that writing needs a human element to really connect with readers. People can spot AI because it isn't capable of adding that distinctly human element to the text. AI text still requires so much refinement to sound truly human.

AI: That's such an important point - that human connection and authenticity that readers can sense. It sounds like you see that as something AI fundamentally can't replicate, no matter how sophisticated it gets technically.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative control while leveraging AI as a collaborative tool, the concerns about the evolution of writing roles, and especially that point about the irreplaceable human element in connecting with readers - all of this will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate your candor about both the benefits and the genuine concerns you have about where this is all heading.

User: No problem

AI: Thank you so much! Have a great rest of your day, and best of luck with your creative work.
114
creativity_0112Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Let's go!

AI: Great! I love the enthusiasm. Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Of course. I am an illustrator who currently focuses on medieval-woodcut inspired pen and ink illustrations. I sell prints online, in person at local art markets, and I accept commissions. I have been a working artist for over a decade now. I have previously worked 4 years as a professional tattoo artist, taking appointments and walk-in clientele, with a focus on American Traditional style tattoos. My typical projects are usually punk and nature inspired imagery, done with staedtler 1.2mm pens on paper, though I am looking to branch out into dip pens or even linocut/woodblock printing as my business grows.

AI: That's fascinating - I can really picture the aesthetic you're working with, combining that medieval woodcut style with punk and nature themes. The transition from tattooing to illustration sounds like an interesting journey too.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use a lot of large language models for assistance with website copy, drafting artist statements, brainstorming social media marketing, and sometimes (though rarely) illustration ideas. Basically, the more tedious and less creative parts of running an art business. I would rather be spending my time drawing. I will tap into an AI tool maybe a few times a week for creative assistance.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - using AI to handle the business side so you can focus on the actual drawing. It sounds like you've carved out pretty clear boundaries about where AI fits in.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I launched a new brand in June, so I had AI draft my website copy for me, though I did go in and edit it to sound more like my personal voice. I am a bit shy about admitting my use of AI to other creatives in my industry. Otherwise, most recently, I've put together some art supply lists.

AI: That's really interesting that you mention feeling shy about admitting AI use to other creatives. Tell me more about that - what's driving that feeling for you?

User: I understand that the use of AI does require a lot of environmental resources, and large scale data centers popping up are using a ton of fresh water and electricity. I'm very much concerned about climate change and its effects, so I do sometimes feel bad using it for quick jobs. However, I also recognize that there is a lot of incredible potential with certain AI tools. I hope in the near future, these environmental concerns can be addressed.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective - weighing the practical benefits against those environmental concerns. It sounds like there's some internal tension there for you.

When you are working with AI on things like website copy or brainstorming, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I have a pretty clear idea of how I want my "brand" to look, what tone I'd like to use, and the style I'm looking to portray. But my mind isn't an endless well of creativity, sometimes I need that extra push to jumpstart my creative endeavors, and if I ask AI to lean into a certain voice (like, make my copy sound like a wizard, but more professional and less campy) I know that I can glean some ideas that I otherwise might have struggled to achieve on my own. Could I spend a lot of time simply googling and writing and editing on my own? Sure, but I'd much rather have a little extra help and save that time for my illustration.

AI: That wizard example is great - it really shows how you're using AI as a creative collaborator while staying firmly in the driver's seat. You're giving it very specific direction about tone and style.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Definitely perfecting my branding. I prefer to use my own natural brain to come up with illustration ideas, and I almost exclusively use large language models rather than image-generating models. But it's been a super helpful tool to make my website, my social media, and my business plan look and feel exactly how I want it to, and it's all cohesive. That's been the biggest impact for sure.

AI: That cohesiveness across all your business materials sounds like a huge win. It's interesting how AI has helped you nail down that brand voice and consistency.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Going back to the image-generating models, I am very concerned that AI-generated art will have a huge effect on my field. I think we're already seeing a lot of bad press about AI being trained on certain artists' work without their consent, and used to replicate their styles. I am concerned that people will gravitate towards the ease-of-use of image-generating models without considering the importance of art made with a human hand. I am also concerned about AI-generated images being used as reference material when it might contain glaring inaccuracies- imagine I ask for a tattoo design and I don't realize my portrait has six fingers. This might have already happened by now, who knows. Yikes. I'd rather the role of AI be used to reduce the tedious workload of humanity so that we have enough time to focus on making art, writing poetry, etc.

AI: That six-finger tattoo scenario is both funny and genuinely concerning! Your vision of AI handling tedious work so humans can focus on art and poetry really resonates - it sounds like that's exactly how you're trying to use it in your own practice.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Well, I think I will stick to the large language models for myself and my work, personally. I don't think I will ever transition into using image or video-generating models. At least not with the current environmental impacts. I do hope humanity and AI can learn to coexist in a way that's beneficial. But in my personal work, I will continue to use AI to refine my brand, take the annoyances out of keeping inventory, build restock lists, manage my website, and give me advice to push my content out into this whacky world wide web.

AI: That's a really clear vision - using AI as your business assistant while keeping the creative work firmly in your own hands.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI to handle the business side so you can focus on what you love most - the actual drawing - and your nuanced view of the environmental concerns balanced against the practical benefits.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope!

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining that clear boundary between AI as a business tool versus keeping the creative work human, along with your thoughtful approach to the environmental considerations, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really fascinating to hear how you've carved out such a intentional role for AI in your practice. Best of luck with your illustration work and that potential expansion into linocut and woodblock printing!
115
creativity_0113Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. No questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a multi-disciplinary creative. I come from a family of artists. I am a film maker by profession. My skills revolve around shooting and editing digital footage. I have worked on a range of projects from feature films, documentaries, music videos, ads and online content. Recently I have also completed professional programs in VFX and Technical Arts.

A typical project depends on the type. If it is a film or video project, it involves logging and cataloguing footage first. Then I build a narrative based on the story. The creative element comes in cutting clips together into a coherent story. There is a distinct visual grammar that changes with each edit, and this evolves as the narrative is crafted. It is one of the more dynamic and challenging natures of film montage.

For 3D projects such as personal artwork and game development, I first gain an understanding of the brief and then I proceed with creating 3D models along with textures. The detail in these artworks depends on the use case. There will be a higher poly count for personal artwork, but a lower poly count with more optimization for game engines. It all starts with a 3D doodle, which I then slowly refine into the finished work.

As a creative my first priority is to get into the mindset of the brief and to develop a workflow that lets art evolve into the finished piece.

AI: That's fascinating - you have such a diverse range of creative skills across film, VFX, and 3D work. I can see how each medium would require its own distinct approach and workflow.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I find myself constantly using AI. Many 3D art pieces are based on realistic measurements and there are certain terms that help define what I need to make or what I need to illustrate. For example, for a project creating a realistic camera lens, I used AI to obtain the measurements of a micro 4/3 mount. In another project, I needed the history of medieval times to understand their architecture and what was relevant during certain time periods.

I have also used AI for help in coding python tools within Maya. It is very helpful when I am stuck and need another perspective. Coming from a non-coding background, as an artist working with complex software, this is immensely helpful in automating certain tasks. It also helps to troubleshoot issues within creative software.

The most useful AI has been is in learning new techniques. I would simply ask how to do a certain task in a software, and it would detail the process saving me a lot of time.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like AI has become almost like having a research assistant and technical mentor available at any moment. You mentioned using it for everything from historical research to Python coding to learning new software techniques.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I am working on a sci-fi watch project. Another useful thing about AI is that it can help filling in gaps or weaknesses in a skill-set. I am not as good in setting up and composing or laying out a scene. With AI I was able to get some quick mock-ups and concepts of how I can place objects. While it is not perfect, this is what makes it more 'human,' where there is deliberation and re-iteration through the process.

AI: That's a really compelling way to think about it - using AI to fill in gaps in your skillset and the imperfection actually making the process feel more human through that back-and-forth iteration.

When you're working with AI on something like those scene compositions for your sci-fi watch project, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: It is always me who is driving the creative process. At the end of the day, it's not about the final artwork, it's about growing through my craft and seeking enjoyment and fulfillment. There is a lot of information and data as to what goes into good composition, and while my technical skills with composition and layout need stronger development, my sensibility as an artist and what looks appealing will always be stronger.

AI is like the buddy on my shoulder, my R2D2. It's there when I need it, when I need to fill in those blanks, and it's sometimes reassuring to know it's there when exploring uncharted territory.

AI: I love that R2D2 analogy - that sense of having a reliable companion that's there when you need it but you're clearly the one piloting the ship.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I have started to learn faster, which is very important in a fast moving technology driven profession.

I have been able to distill large search pages of information into exactly what I need. No more pouring over websites for over an hour just to find the one workflow step that I need. This has saved me so much time.

A major relief is in scheduling, logistics and project management. I can focus on the creative work and leave all that boring stuff to AI. For example, I needed to re-write my resume to be more in line with recruitment platforms. What would have taken me hours, took AI a few minutes. This gave me so much time to work on my portfolio.

Another improvement is in iteration and establishing a sense of direction in my personal projects

AI: That's a great point about being able to focus more energy on the actual creative work rather than the administrative side. It sounds like AI has really streamlined the more tedious aspects of your workflow.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Yes there is concern with it completely taking over, but as a generalist I see it as a strong set of tools rather than an adversary. The one concern for me is for new artists coming in who do not understand what art is. AI is not a be all end all. It's not about how many kinds of software you know how to use or how fast you can do a repetitive task. It is never about the final artwork or the validation that something looks appealing. It is about that journey through art and developing a craft. And that takes time.

The main concern is actually with individuals who believe that AI can eradicate their need for human involvement in the creative fields, just to save money. This not only lets down creative professionals, but also diminishes the perception of what AI can do and how helpful it can be.

The landscape has surely changed, but I am excited as now I don't have to worry about my weaknesses holding me back. I can essentially do even more creative work now than I could ten years ago when I had first started.

AI: That's such a thoughtful perspective - distinguishing between AI as a powerful creative tool versus something that replaces the fundamental human journey of developing artistic craft and sensibility.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: It is going to immensely improve my skills and in turn bolster my confidence. Over the next few years I want to reach a comfort level where I have a set of AI tools that fill in for my weaknesses and then focus on what I'm good at and get those 10,000 hours in.

I see it more involved in the logistics of my job. I see more help with tool building. More basic code training and building a technique library. Taking over my admin work, mundane professional communication like drafting cover letters or emails.

Self motivation can be tough, especially as an artist. But I have learned to practically leverage AI to establish a challenge routine where it presents a task to make something, and then I have to make it. The most important within my creative workflow for these challenges would be concepting.

AI: That's fascinating - using AI to create creative challenges for yourself is such an innovative approach to self-motivation and skill development. It sounds like you've really thought through how to leverage AI strategically to support your artistic growth.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as an "R2D2" companion that fills gaps in your skillset while you maintain creative control, and your concern about new artists understanding that the journey of developing craft is what matters most, not just the final output.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Just that AI should be developed in harmony with human creativity. This not only elevates us as a species, but also AI as a true system of the future.

AI: That's a beautiful way to put it - AI developed in harmony with human creativity rather than in competition with it. That perspective of mutual elevation really captures something important about the potential relationship between humans and AI in creative fields.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a creative companion while maintaining the primacy of artistic craft and growth will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached integrating these tools into your work.
116
creativity_0114Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds great. Lets go.

AI: Wonderful! Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I have lots of creative projects, mostly involved with audio and video content creation. My video projects are typically promo videos for the church I work for. They feature service projects or identity statements.

AI: That sounds like really meaningful work. When you're creating these promo videos for your church - whether it's showcasing service projects or communicating identity statements - walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI tools for planning and scripting. As most of my videos have time restrictions, I use AI to make sure my plan will fit in the constraint. I also use it to help brainstorm content like b-roll or settings. I also run either my script or outline through AI to clean and tighten it while trying to maintain my voice.

AI: That's really interesting how you're using AI across different stages - from initial planning through to script refinement. Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play in that particular video, and how did that experience feel for you as you were working through it?

User: Sure. I created a promo video for our mobile food pantry that was a shorter version of a previous video. I used AI to talk through the most essential elements to communicate and how to pare down the script for a tight one minute promo.

AI: Tell me more about that process of working with AI to identify those essential elements and pare down the script. How did that conversation with the AI unfold?

User: I gave the voice over from the longer video to the model and asked it to identify what it thought were the most important elements. I then told it what were the important elements for me and asked it to create a one minute script from those details that fit the voice and identity of the church.

AI: When you're working with AI in that way - giving it your longer script, discussing what's important, and having it create that condensed version - how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: It's collaborative, but I'm making the final decisions. I find the AI suggestions useful, but often generic and slightly off the mark. So I use the suggestions to shape my own creative output.

AI: That's a really helpful distinction - using AI suggestions to shape rather than replace your creative output. What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI tools in your video production process?

User: It makes my work faster and more efficient. It gives me more time to be creative during production and editing.

AI: That efficiency gain sounds significant - freeing up time for the creative aspects you enjoy most. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field or in creative work more broadly?

User: Like any creative, I'm concerned about AI being able to replicate my work and reduce my value. I have hope that human storytelling continues to be essential to the kind of work I do. Optimistically, I see AI as a partner in my work.

AI: That tension between concern and optimism really comes through clearly. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate it expanding into other areas of your video production, or changing in how you use it?

User: Certainly. I use Adobe products and they are adding AI features regularly. That's only going to continue to grow.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on using AI as a collaborative partner while maintaining creative control, and how that efficiency gain allows you more time for the truly creative aspects of production and editing.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No. That's all. Thanks!

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about balancing efficiency with creative authenticity, and viewing AI as a collaborative tool rather than a replacement, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how thoughtfully you described your process - from using AI for planning and constraints through to script refinement while maintaining your unique voice. Have a great rest of your day!
117
creativity_0115Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Absolutely! Let's go!

AI: Wonderful! I love your enthusiasm.

Let's start at the beginning then. Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I think like a lot of creatives, I have multiple projects going simultaneously. I'm not a narrow-focus creative who does one type of work and that's it - I write, I edit, I do multimedia crafts, painting, extremely niche restoration techniques, I do graphic design, printing, and even some web design. I do a lot of experimenting and testing - most projects involve some level of learning on the fly. Other than straightforward printing, there's no "typical project flow."

AI: That's fascinating - you really span a huge range of creative disciplines. It sounds like you thrive on that variety and constant learning.

With such a diverse creative practice, walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools across all these different areas?

User: Lately I've been using it to do some research on new techniques - maybe I heard someone mention something in passing that I've never done, and I'll do a surface level recon on my own and think it MIGHT fit in my skill sets/abilities. I'll have a chat with an AI to see what other chatter is out there, and see if I'm overlooking potential pitfalls implementing that with the tools I have. For example, I asked about waterslide decals one time after hearing about it - it seemed like a good potential fit for some restoration work, but after deeper AI conversations combined with my practical knowledge, I determined it probably wouldn't be viable with the printer I have now. Better to find out via chat with an AI than to try it on an important project and ruin it with running ink.

AI: That's a really smart approach - using AI as a way to test the waters before committing resources or risking a project. The waterslide decal example is perfect - you essentially used the AI conversation to help you think through the practical constraints.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Yes! I found some plush patterns I was iterating on in a huge backlog - probably ten years they had been waiting for me to get time to finish them. I had no notes or ideas about what I was going to do to finish it. The body was stuffed, but it had no eyes, and I was out of inspiration at that moment. I shared a WIP picture and asked the AI what color would look good. I expected just a text response, but the AI actually edited my picture and put eyes on the plush. I was a little surprised, then impressed, and then I looked at the eyes more closely. They looked like googly eyes you might glue on a sock puppet, not polished eyes you would actually put on a soft plush animal. I was disappointed once I thought about it a little more - just the text would have been fine in that case instead of making my work look goofy.

AI: That's such an interesting experience - you went from surprise to impressed to disappointed all in one interaction. It sounds like the AI overstepped in a way, giving you more than you asked for but not necessarily what you actually needed.

When you say the eyes looked like "googly eyes you might glue on a sock puppet" versus "polished eyes you would actually put on a soft plush animal" - tell me more about that difference. What did the AI miss about the level of craftsmanship you were aiming for?

User: I do very intricate hand-embroidered and appliqued eyes - not a plastic "punch through and done" type, which is what the edited image looked like. The plush was already sewn up and stuffed, and the level of craftsmanship should have been a hint that gluing plastic eyes on top was inappropriate.

AI: That makes perfect sense - the AI completely misread the craftsmanship level you were working at. Your hand-embroidered and appliqued work requires a totally different approach than mass-produced toy assembly, and the fact that you'd already invested that level of detail should have been obvious from the image.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in these interactions?

User: When I need some creative inspiration, I'll turn to the AI as a first pass or open new avenues for exploration. I was trying to do research for a fun Halloween-themed candy project based on fictional candy with some 80s and 90s movies nostalgia factor, and unfortunately the AI didn't get it - it suggested Harry Potter candy (wrong era) and SpongeBob Krabby Patties (not candy). Going deeper seemed to have just led to hallucinations. That one was pretty disappointing. Obviously I didn't get what I needed there, so I discarded the suggestions and went in a different direction, since my first attempt didn't have the meat to support it. But in other cases, when I'm just spitballing, the AI might get me on the path just enough I can get creative and finish the idea (color choices, new designs, that sort of thing).

AI: That Halloween candy example really illustrates the limits - the AI completely missed the cultural context you were looking for and then seemed to double down with hallucinations when you tried to guide it deeper. But it sounds like there's a sweet spot where AI can give you just enough of a creative nudge to get your own ideas flowing.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Unfortunately I've given up writing. That has been the most easily displaced by AI - passable text if you aren't going for something extremely niche is pretty easy for AI these days. It can even edit AND read handwriting now, so those avenues I used to have have closed (for paying work). On the other hand, I'm a huge fan of messing around with image iteration via AI - I'll get the base idea out via AI, and then I use that output to guide a painted image by hand (or polish it digitally with image editing/repainting). I found the AI can get me the base layout I need to translate that into unusual art pieces - things I wouldn't have painted just on my own, but are "out there" enough I can get actual purchasers. That really surprised me!

AI: That's such a striking contrast - losing your writing work while discovering this new collaborative approach with visual art that's actually opening up market opportunities. The fact that AI-generated base layouts are helping you create pieces that are "out there" enough to find buyers is really intriguing.

Tell me more about that visual collaboration process. When you say the AI helps you create things you "wouldn't have painted just on your own" - what's happening there that's different from your usual creative process?

User: I think my usual style tends more to standard cartoon/comics/sketches - left on my own, I'm going to draw something with obvious outlines, color fills, and simplified shapes. The AI has pushed me to a more psychedelic, abstract shapes plus organic creatures, more negative space images, interesting color choices.

AI: That's fascinating - the AI is actually expanding your artistic vocabulary and pushing you into territories you wouldn't naturally explore. It sounds like it's breaking you out of your comfort zone in ways that are commercially successful too.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I think I already touched on the writing issues. Unfortunately a lot of writing online now feels inauthentic. I've stopped reading any recent books - I went back to the classics to keep up with the feel of a well-crafted story with intriguing writing. I don't know if we'll ever see another Nabokov-level writer is AI assistance become standard in the future.

I know a lot of people outwardly say AI is going to kill art, but I don't think that's true. I was around for early digital art and remember that same type of resistance to anything "easy" to undo making art "less" somehow. As I'm seeing it, AI is actually helping me do new and intriguing things. The problem is a lot of online art spaces are super knee-jerk/witch hunting people who use AI at all, so...I've had to keep my enthusiasm for it off the radar! I hope in a few more years that will change and I won't have to hide the AI inspiration for the base painting ideas.

AI: That's such a nuanced perspective - you're seeing both the displacement happening in writing and the creative expansion in visual art, while also dealing with the social dynamics around AI use in creative communities.

The parallel you draw to early digital art resistance is really insightful. And it sounds like you're having to navigate this strange situation where AI is genuinely enhancing your work, but you can't openly discuss that process because of community reactions.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I'm hoping the image editing in the AI programs gets a bit more fluid (some of this may be my hardware limitations at the moment more than the actual capabilities). I think I saw a demo a bit ago where you could wireframe a still image and drag the subject around at points to move the head and legs, and the AI would be able to reposition the subject naturally from that - that looks so cool! I'm also looking forward to better personalization/memory, so we won't see those awkward suggestions about plastic googly eyes on my high-end plushies. ;)

I'm not sure if I'm 100% on board with the AI helping me keep track of sales/profit, given its current problems with numbers, but over time I can see that being a time saving point as well - as well as a "business partner" of sorts that can track trends of what's working and what isn't.

AI: Those improvements sound like they'd really enhance your workflow - especially the image manipulation capabilities and the memory aspect so AI can better understand your craftsmanship level. And the idea of AI as a "business partner" for tracking trends is intriguing, though I can definitely see why you'd want to wait until the math gets more reliable!

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI across such a diverse creative practice. I particularly appreciated how you've navigated both the displacement you've experienced in writing and the genuine creative expansion you've found in visual work, plus your perspective on the community dynamics around AI use.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: So, I'm not doing it yet, BUT I'm really intrigued about the possibility of reigniting my musical interests with AI and finally composing songs. I could see this being similar to my early experimenting with digital art - I try it for fun, but over time and with experimentation, eventually I can incorporate it into more multimedia based projects. I guess I'm much more open to experimenting and I personally don't feel it diminishes me or my creativity - it gives me opportunities to participate in something I enjoy but couldn't do on my own. :)

AI: That's a perfect note to end on! Your openness to experimentation and seeing AI as expanding what you can participate in rather than diminishing your creativity really captures something important. The parallel you draw to your early digital art experimentation suggests you have a great sense for how these tools might evolve in your practice over time.

It sounds like music could be the next frontier where AI helps you access a creative domain that was previously out of reach - much like how it's pushed your visual work into new territories you wouldn't have explored on your own.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about navigating both the challenges and genuine creative opportunities with AI will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate your honesty about both the wins and the disappointments!
118
creativity_0116Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. No questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm a professional voice actor. A typical project (assuming that I've already auditioned and have been cast) involves me recording my lines, being directed and depending on if there is an engineer, doing some light editing before final delivery. I'll get a script and then record it according to the specifications whether it's a character script, commercial or narration. The length of the process depends on how long the script is and can range from as short as 5 minutes to a few hours.

AI: That's really interesting - voice acting covers such a wide range of work.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: As voice acting is a creative profession where the human element is really important, I will not use AI much during the middle of the recording process. For that aspect, I would prefer to do everything myself to maintain authenticity. What I will use AI for is purely for research, ideation and editing recorded audio. For example, if there are character references (ex: quirky scientist, tough cop, Chris Evans as Captain America) then I may consult AI to help me find example video clips or other character examples to help draw from. My audio editing software does have AI features which does help smooth out the AI process, but I will often have to double check their work to make sure they didn't do anything unnatural to the audio.

AI: That's a fascinating distinction you're making - keeping the core performance purely human while using AI as a research and technical tool.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Again, AI has a limited role during my work. Aside from the initial audition, after that, AI is really only used for the sake of editing. But for an example of what AI does during the audition process, there were vague specifications that the client wanted but was unable to truly highlight any examples (friendly, upbeat, kind of quirky). So I plugged their specifications into an AI assistant and it was able to point in the direction of TV show characters that were along the lines of something they may have wanted. There, it just truly felt like the AI was simply a better search engine and helps me maintain my integrity as an actor as I'm still making the majority of acting decisions for myself. It is very important to me that AI is only a supplement to my work, never a replacement.

AI: I really appreciate how thoughtful you are about maintaining that boundary. It sounds like you see AI as more of an enhanced research tool than a creative collaborator.

When you're working with AI in those research moments, how would you describe the relationship? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am 100% driving the creative decisions. While the AI can give suggestions, I can easily ignore any that I feel are bad fits and hone in on the ones that I feel might have legs. Essentially, the AI to me during the process helps me clarify and brainstorm, giving me a little jump start in the creative process before ultimately stepping out of the way. Even during audio editing when I am simply cleaning up artifacts and breaths, I always make sure it sounds natural and will never let something that is purely done by AI be the final product.

AI: That's a really clear perspective on creative control. It sounds like you've found a way to use AI that feels authentic to your process.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The amount of time taken before and after recording. The greatest conveniences have been during editing where the AI can do work that would have taken me a fair bit of time to do. The help during searches too has been great for helping find clips where I may have needed to comb through YouTube or Google to find examples. But for the actual creative aspect of my job, AI has little to no effect.

AI: That efficiency gain sounds really valuable - saving time on the technical work so you can focus on the creative performance itself.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I do indeed have major concerns about AI's presence in the voice acting industry. Synthesized voices are on the rise and with their rising quality, I can definitely see the temptation for indie or low budget productions/businesses choosing to go for an AI voice over an actual human. There are certain sectors of voice acting that have essentially died due to the rise of AI, such as industrial voice acting (training videos, explainers, how-to's) because often, those businesses only need a pleasant sounding voice instead of an actual human. I fear that this will lead to a lot of content that will sound very similar, while also lessening opportunities for many voice actors.

AI: That's a really sobering perspective on how AI is already impacting different segments of your industry. It sounds like you're seeing very real economic effects, particularly in those more straightforward voice work categories.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I do see AI expanding in the future. I'm sure that developers of AI voices will try to make them sound more authentic, but I do believe in the end that nothing will ever replace human emotion and experience. Ideally, I would hope that AI voices can be used for proof of concepts, ideation and help with the more technical aspects of voice acting but ultimately leave the actual acting to people. Hopefully, AI can expand in the supplemental aspects in voice acting, but I'm fairly certain that it will also advance in mimicking humans, for better or for worse.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative process. I particularly appreciated how clearly you've defined your boundaries - using AI as a research tool and for technical editing while keeping the core creative performance purely human, and your perspective on how different segments of voice acting are being affected differently.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I've nothing to add to what I've already said.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative authenticity while finding practical uses for AI, and your perspective on how different segments of voice acting are being impacted, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you discussed both the benefits you've found and the very real concerns about AI's growing presence in your industry. Take care!
119
creativity_0117Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope - let's go

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am an artist. I draw and sell products with my designs printed on them. I make money as an artist by selling my designs, primarily on Etsy. I also have released two coloring books, one of which was just professionally published and is available at Target & Barnes + Noble!

AI: That's fantastic - congratulations on getting your coloring book into Target and Barnes & Noble! That's a huge accomplishment.

It sounds like you have a really interesting mix going on - from individual designs for products to published coloring books. Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI to facilitate my business. I use AI for SEO and writing listing descriptions.

AI: Tell me more about that. How does using AI for the SEO and listing descriptions work in practice for you?

User: I will use an AI service or just a chat model to help me come up with tags for my Etsy listings. Etsy has specific parameters for tagging listings, so I use AI to try to come up with product tags that will promote my listings

AI:

User: I'll also provide brief descriptions of my products to have AI generate product listing descriptions with keywords. They're usually not very good, but they give me a good starting point. Or I can pick out some good phrases from the response.

AI: That's really interesting - so you're using AI more for the business side of your art rather than the creative process itself. You mentioned the AI-generated descriptions usually aren't very good but give you a starting point. What makes them not quite right, and how do you typically refine them to work better for your listings?

User: They usually sound very formulaic, like you can tell they were written by a robot. They don't have much personality, and I feel like my art has personality. The responses sound sterilized. I usually will copy & paste the whole response and edit it to sound more personable. I keep the keywords that help with SEO or performance, but I edit out the filler stuff that sounds cookie cutter.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - there's a real disconnect between the personality you put into your art and that formulaic AI tone.

I'm curious about the actual art creation side of things. When you're drawing or developing your designs - the creative work itself - do you use AI at all in that process, or do you keep that completely separate from the business tools?

User: I don't use AI to create art. I've spoken with a chatbot before to braintorm concepts, but I have really mixed feelings about letting AI generate art itself.

AI: Tell me more about those mixed feelings you have about AI generating art itself.

User: It feels like I'd be letting down my community by using AI in my art process. I think there are probably certain areas where AI can be used meaningfully in art, but right now, it feels like AI art only hurts artists. Viewers are now skeptical that anybody's art could be AI-generated, and I think AI art lends itself to plagiarism and the unfair treatment of artists who don't want anything to do with AI.

AI: That's a really thoughtful perspective. It sounds like you see a real tension between AI as a potential creative tool versus its current impact on the artistic community and how people perceive art in general.

You mentioned you have used chatbots to brainstorm concepts though. Can you describe what that experience was like for you? How did that feel different from using AI to actually generate visual art?

User: I feed the AI a concept, and it helps me spitball similar ideas, but I still have to make the art. There's no visual or graphic creation, just discussion.

AI: That's a really interesting distinction - using AI for ideation and discussion versus visual creation. When you're in those brainstorming conversations with AI, how would you describe that collaboration? Are you driving the creative direction, or does it feel more like a back-and-forth exchange of ideas?

User: Well, I've only tried it probably twice. I didn't feel like it was very helpful because it wasn't very back-and-forth. I felt like it was only giving me surface-level ideas, nothing that really expanded the idea or came up with something new. However, I did recently use it for a similar purpose, although for personal reasons, not professional. I was asking it to come up with design ideas for my Minecraft house, and it definitely felt more collaborative and insightful. That conversation happened last week, whereas the business ones were probably from a year or two ago. Maybe things have improved since then.

AI: That's really fascinating - the difference between those experiences separated by a year or two, and also between the professional art context versus the personal Minecraft project. What do you think made the Minecraft conversation feel more collaborative and insightful compared to those earlier brainstorming sessions for your art business?

User: Well, it actually was coming up with new ideas. I gave it the initial concept, then it storyboarded some ideas, with concepts I hadn't previously considered. Then I asked it to tweak some things, and the process continued back and forth. Before, when I was asking it for art ideas, I felt like its suggestions were very surface-level. Nothing novel or creative.

AI: That's a really insightful comparison. It sounds like the quality of AI collaboration can vary significantly depending on the context and maybe how the technology has evolved.

Looking ahead, given these different experiences you've had - both the useful business applications and the mixed results with creative brainstorming - how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: Well hopefully the art community will have a more concrete opinion on AI and its role in the field by then. Right now, it feels controversial and harmful. I think the business applications will only continue to improve. As for the creative side, I can see myself using AI in editing aspects, but I don't think AI should ever replace the human element of creating something.

AI: Those are some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative work. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how the community context really shapes how you think about using these tools, and that distinction you drew between AI as a business utility versus its role in the actual creative process.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No

AI: Thank you so much for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about navigating AI as both a business tool and potential creative collaborator - while being mindful of your artistic community - will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

It's been really valuable hearing how you've found practical uses for AI in the business side while maintaining clear boundaries around the creative process itself. Best of luck with your art and congratulations again on that coloring book success!

User: Thank you!

AI: You're very welcome! Thanks again for participating - this was really insightful. Have a great day!
120
creativity_0118Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Nope.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I have several facets of creative work that I function in, but they essentially boil down to writing and audio engineering. For writing, my process is very flexible. I used to begin with an outline and then work my way through it chronologically, but lately I have been piecemealing sections of text together as they cross my mind. AI has been especially helpful for this latter technique. I also write eBay listing descriptions. For this, I used to cut and paste old descriptions into new listings and then change words as necessary, but AI has helped cut down this process significantly, especially when it comes to describing images based on the pictures. As for audio engineering, this process entail recording tracks of sound, mixing it and mastering it. AI has come to help me hone in on higher quality sound by making suggestions of how to sculpt audio per my intentions when I seem to hit a wall.

AI: That's fascinating - you're working across quite different creative domains. I'm curious about that piecemeal writing approach you mentioned.

Walk me through how AI fits into that process when sections of text are crossing your mind. What does that collaboration look like in the moment?

User: I have an ongoing conversation with a model that I will open. Per the initial system instructions, it knows to transcribe what I say, while cutting out artifacts like "uh" or "um" and keeping the grammar correct while changing as few words as possible and ideally no words at all. I use this method for prose projects. Later I will go to a desktop and copy the transcribed sections over to a master word document. I also have used AI to flesh out the story for a script I am working on. Helping me hone in on details that can most realistically connect the story's events while firming up a solid order of events.

AI: That's a really interesting workflow - using AI as both a transcription tool and a story development partner.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Well I just described two writing projects where AI served as a transcriber, light editor and writing partner. As for audio, I recently mixed a live show and was having trouble getting the kind of definition I wanted. I described my tracking set up and the model gave me specific frequency cuts to make on specific tracks. This gave me the intended sound I was seeking and also taught me a new technique. This made me feel elevated, as though my expertise had increased, and with it my confidence.

AI: That's a powerful example - going from hitting a wall to learning a new technique and feeling more confident. It sounds like the AI helped you break through a specific technical challenge.

When you're working with AI, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: For transcribing and editing, I would describe the collaboration as strictly utilitarian. For organizing a story as for with audio, I would describe the collaboration as more of an exercise in curiosity. That is because, in these cases, I am usually already at a baseline performance and am just seeking to tighten it up. Ultimately the creative decisions are mine, but AI helps me better achieve the vision when I can't quite find the bridge from one idea to another. It's good for "honing in" above all.

AI: That's a really thoughtful distinction - utilitarian versus curiosity-driven collaboration. I like how you describe AI as helping you "hone in" and find bridges between ideas.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Like I said...when writing, I now do more piecemeal writing and assembling than plodding chronologically. The eBay listing writing goes much quicker. And for audio, I have more confidence in my abilities.

AI: Those are some concrete shifts in your process and confidence. It sounds like AI has given you more flexibility in how you approach your work.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I have no concerns about AI. I am not worried.

AI: That's interesting - you seem quite comfortable with AI's role. Tell me more about that perspective.

User: I'm not sure what else there is to tell. It seems to work just fine for me.

AI: Fair enough. Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I think it will come to work more coherently with larger packages of data. There is a bit of a limit now, especially when using it to transcribe and edit the prose. I can give it multiple sections at a time, but the model always fails to combine large numbers of these sections into a single piece I can download or cut out and paste. As a result, I currently have to manually cut and paste snippets at a time, which takes longer than it should.

AI: That makes sense - you're envisioning AI handling larger, more complex tasks rather than just the smaller pieces you're working with now.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your practical perspective on how AI serves as both a utilitarian tool and a curiosity-driven collaborator, and how it's changed your approach from chronological to piecemeal writing.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Nope.

AI: Perfect. Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI across different creative domains - from transcription and story development to audio engineering - and especially your distinction between utilitarian and curiosity-driven collaboration, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you were about your workflow and the specific ways AI has enhanced your confidence and efficiency. Thanks again!
121
creativity_0119Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Let's do it!

AI: Great! I love the enthusiasm. Let's dive right in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I'm an independent musician-entrepreneur. This takes on many forms, such as producing music, recording other artists, mixing music, releasing music, marketing the projects, writing grants to secure funding, and so forth. Think of it like a single-person label.

AI: That sounds incredibly multifaceted - you're wearing so many different hats! It sounds like you're handling everything from the artistic side all the way through to the business operations.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: A majority of the time I use it for admin stuff - like tracking CPC on Meta Advertising campaigns or budgeting to buy new equipment. I've also found myself using quite a bit when writing lengthy applications to government funding, as well as "copy" about releases, such as playlist pitches to Spotify. The content generated in those conversations can usually be used in more than one place (artist statement, for example). On rare occasions, I've tried using LLMs to find the perfect turn of phrase in lyrics - but this isn't usually that helpful and I don't love doing it.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like AI has become more of a business partner than a creative collaborator for you. You mentioned trying it for lyrics but not finding it that helpful and not loving the experience.

Tell me more about that. What made the lyric writing experience feel different or less satisfying compared to using AI for those administrative tasks?

User: Well, the first obstacle is that it really isn't that good at it. I've tried a few different models and they always generate generic or non-applicable ideas. It's usually stuff that people would find 'corny' or uninspired. That's why I only really reach for it when I can ask for a really granular task that don't require idea generation or conceptual thinking, like "Find a rhyme for XYZ" or "Write this sentence but only use 4 syllables". For that stuff, it can be really helpful.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - you're drawing a clear line between AI as a technical tool versus AI trying to generate the actual creative ideas. It sounds like you've found a sweet spot using it for very specific, mechanical tasks in lyrics but keeping the conceptual and emotional core firmly in your own hands.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: Well I've used it to write the copy and press content (for music releases) that I submit to DSPs and playlist curators. It seems 'good enough', but I can't help think that it might be hurting my chances because so many people probably have similar pitches. Same goes for grant writing that describes my creative practices.

AI: That's a fascinating tension you're describing - AI is helping you handle those time-consuming tasks more efficiently, but you're aware that everyone else might be using the same shortcuts, potentially making your work blend into a sea of similar-sounding pitches.

When you're working with AI on these applications and pitches, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: I usually bring many ideas to the table and have the LLM sort it out into a fluid text. It's rare that the model adds something I really hadn't thought of or whatever. It's moreso like creative presentation of pre-established ideas.

AI: That's a really clear distinction - you're using AI more as a formatting and presentation tool rather than an idea generator. It sounds like you maintain full creative control over the concepts and direction, while AI helps you translate that into polished copy.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: The bulk of my creative process remains mostly unscathed - as AI hasn't taken hold of music creation, production, or distribution in a way that feels unavoidable. I've seen people use Models like SUNO, but I don't see myself ever touching those. It's a slippery slope!

AI: You mentioned it being a "slippery slope" with tools like SUNO - that's a strong reaction.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: Well, much like with my admin tasks, I think it has the potential to rope people in to a sort of dependence. At first, you're just testing it out, it seems helpful, your brain is combining forces with another intelligence. However, slowly but surely, I find myself (and others) offloading more and more mental labour to whatever tools we use. I think the creative and critical thinking parts of our brains are like muscles, and not using them as much as we might 'need' to is causing a slow atrophy. You know what I mean? That's why I'm really careful about engaging with tools that can quickly take up a majority share of creative decisions. It reminds me of when I used 'Splice' loops for a period of time (pre-recorded loops of music that are royalty-free and made for production) - at first it was a cool addition to my original ideas, but soon enough I was 'writing' songs by simply dragging-and-dropping formulaic ideas until they aligned. Since I stopped that I've had to re-learn how to come up with things on the fly, start from scratch, and trust my artistic intuition, something that's far more rewarding that quick pre-cooked results.

AI: That's such a thoughtful and vivid analogy - the idea of creative muscles atrophying from lack of use, and your experience with Splice loops really illustrates that progression from helpful tool to creative crutch. It sounds like you've lived through that cycle before and recognized the warning signs.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years? Given your awareness of that slippery slope, how are you thinking about navigating that balance?

User: Well, I don't think it'll be difficult to avoid the services in my personal practice because I have a core aversion to that feeling of creating something yet not feeling connected to it. Imagine how much power that gives to the already rampant imposter syndrome most artists feel! Still, I do have fears about how AI changes in music will affect me externally. As the culture shifts and (probably) accepts AI-made 'art' more and more, I think the market will get (even more) over-saturated with algorithm-pleasing tropes and simplified, cookie-cutter, generic music. so, culturally, i think it will worsen the state of things, where organic authentic art isn't valued because it isn't as profitable as commercial slop.

AI: That's a really profound point about imposter syndrome - the idea that using AI could disconnect you from your own creative output and amplify those existing doubts artists already face about their work's authenticity.
