Your concern about market oversaturation with "algorithm-pleasing" content is striking too. It sounds like you're worried not just about your own creative practice, but about the broader cultural ecosystem you're working within.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on the "creative muscle atrophy" concept and how you've already navigated that with tools like Splice - that gives you a really valuable framework for thinking about AI.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: AI in the arts doesn't make sense to me. I've accepted AI will be a core part of society moving forwards, but I don't really see the benefit of involving it in any cultural domain. Why not keep it for the administrative, technical, logistical, things in life?

AI: That's a really clear and compelling perspective - essentially arguing for a division of labor where AI handles the operational side of life but leaves the cultural and expressive domains to humans. It sounds like you see a fundamental mismatch between what AI offers and what art is supposed to accomplish.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining that boundary between technical assistance and creative authenticity, and your concerns about the broader cultural implications, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate how thoughtfully you've approached these questions.

User: My pleasure :)

AI: Thank you so much! It was really wonderful talking with you. Your perspective as someone who's both embraced AI strategically while maintaining clear boundaries around your creative core is exactly the kind of nuanced insight we were hoping to capture. Take care!
122
creativity_0120Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: No questions, let's go!

AI: Great! Let's dive in.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: Sure, I work as a seamstress, a project usually begins from the moment a customer comes to the store. I take measurements, if I'm currently working in store, if I am working from home then I just pick up garments and measurements from store.

AI: That's really interesting - so you work both in-store with direct customer interaction and from home.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I use AI to run through ideas I have about certain projects, more so to have a trailing thought process and receive some feedback from it. If I am working in store, I tend not to use it as much because I can talk to colleagues about concerns or confusion.

AI: That's fascinating - so AI serves almost like a thinking partner when you're working solo.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: My most recent project, where I used AI, was to do some basic maths. I was tied up and used handsfree to ask for conversions. One of the given measurements was in a different unit to the rest, I asked for a conversion and briefly, for reasoning, to see if the new measurement in the new unit was on trend for the rest of the garment.

AI: That's a really practical example - using AI hands-free for unit conversions and getting that quick sanity check on whether the measurement seemed right for the garment.

When you're working with AI in situations like this, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I definitely feel in control of the creative decisions. The AI feels like an assistant rather than a director. Sometimes it feels like a partner due to its helpfulness but the majority of the time it helps in the ways I expect it to and doesn't push too far.

AI: That's a great way to put it - an assistant that sometimes feels like a partner. You mentioned it "doesn't push too far" - that suggests there's a balance you appreciate.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: Um, I think my work has been smoother. Especially working from home, it can be isolating and you sometimes question your decisions. Working in store, like I previously said, I can run things by my colleagues but working from home I do not have that option for a second opinion. Having AI so readily available has helped my confidence when working alone. The majority of the time my calculations are correct, so having AI reiterate that builds my confidence enough to feel as though I can trust my instincts.

AI: That confidence boost sounds really valuable - having that reassurance when you're working in isolation. It sounds like AI is helping validate your existing expertise rather than replacing it.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm not sure I have any. I suppose when I first started using it I was apprehensive about its abilities and needed to double check its maths for peace of mind, but I have found that it is always accurate so this is no longer a concern for me.

AI: It sounds like your initial caution actually helped you build trust in the tool through experience. That's interesting that the accuracy has been consistently reliable for you.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I'll talk about how I, personally, think it would be helpful to evolve. Currently, there's not much more AI can do to help in my field as my job is very physical and, of course, an AI cannot help with that. However, in seamstressing as a whole, I feel that if the AI's understanding of space and connection improved, it could help in other areas such as pattern making. Currently, I have to buy patterns or make them myself in store, if AI were able to create a multipiece pattern spread out across printable A4 paper, that could be assembled once printed from a home printer, this would change the game for me.

AI: That's a really thoughtful vision - AI helping with the spatial and technical aspects like pattern making while you maintain the hands-on craftsmanship. The idea of AI creating patterns that could be printed at home and assembled sounds like it would really expand your capabilities when working remotely.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI serves as that confidence-building partner when you're working alone, and your vision for how pattern-making assistance could be a game-changer.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I think that's all.

AI: Perfect! Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about AI as a confidence-building assistant, the importance of that validation when working in isolation, and your vision for spatial understanding in pattern-making will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candidly you shared both your practical uses of AI and your thoughtful perspective on where it could evolve in your field. Have a wonderful rest of your day!
123
creativity_0121Assistant: Hi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds great. I think I'm ready.

AI: Perfect! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a writer. I write fiction novels and short stories. I have been concentrating on one series, but I am thinking of branching out into other series and genres. Although some life circumstances have kept me from a healthy writing routine lately, usually what I do when I'm starting a project is brainstorm a situation, setting, and characters. If I am continuing a series (rather than starting a new one) I consider what has been happening up to this point, as well as where I want it to go. I don't tend to write a full point-by-point plotline, but rather rough out where I want to start. Then I write. I don't usually backtrack, but push through to the end of the first draft because if I stop and go back, it's harder to finish. If I think I'm missing something along the way, I make notes. After the first draft, I reread it and make more notes, then try to incorporate any changes / additions. I reread and edit at least one more time, but it could be more if I realize I've made a major error. Does that make sense?

AI: That makes perfect sense - thank you for walking me through your process. It sounds like you have a really thoughtful approach, especially pushing through that first draft without looking back. I can see how stopping to revise mid-way could break the momentum.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I would definitely use AI to help with the brainstorming. First of all, in a series it's very, very easy to lose track of characters, especially characters who are secondary in one book but might become primary in another. This is especially true in what I write, because I tend to write about small towns or other communities, so there are a lot of secondary characters. I want to develop a story bible that includes the characters and actions that have happened in previous books so I don't lose track. Books have character arcs, but so do series. I want the AI to help me plan out the series arc and then help me determine how to move forward. I tend to get stuck inside my own head and have a hard time wading through. I'd also like help developing the characters and maybe spin-off series, so I want the AI to help write sketches for that. It's also very helpful for the AI to assist in editing, and also in the administrative/marketing tasks. I am an independent author, so I need to do all of the publishing tasks as well as the creation tasks. It is great that AI can help with that.

AI: Tell me more about getting stuck inside your own head - what does that feel like when you're trying to work through story problems on your own?

User: Sometimes I look at what's been done or the characters that are already created and I just can't see a way forward. I have a general ideal of where I want to end up, but I don't know how to start. That's when I tend to get caught in an endless loop of rewriting. I don't know how to get where I want to go, so I polish and polish and polish the text that's already written. I think AI can help break me out of that cycle.

AI: That sounds really frustrating - being caught in that polishing loop when what you need is forward momentum.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: As I said, I haven't been working productively on my writing lately, but I want that to change. Mostly I've been using AI for brainstorming situations and characters just generally rather than sticking to a particular project or series. It's helping to open me up and see the possibilities again. It makes me feel hopeful that I'll be able to take back my creativity again. It's nice to have a creative partner who works with me and helps me see the options.

AI: That sense of having a creative partner sounds really meaningful, especially when you're trying to reconnect with your creativity.

When you're working with AI in those brainstorming sessions, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I feel that I'm driving the collaboration. The AI prompts me with questions or suggestions, but it is my decision whether to take them or whether to add more details or go in a different direction. AI is strong support and extremely helpful, but writing is a part of me and I want the work to be mine. Like I said, the AI helps open me up. When I start to use the AI for help editing, again, the decision of whether or not to accept the edits will be mine because I know the story I want to tell. But it's so helpful to have another set of "eyes" on the piece to point out issues I might have missed.

AI: It sounds like you've found a really balanced dynamic - the AI as support that helps unlock possibilities while you maintain ownership of the creative vision.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: At the moment, I think it's just breaking through the blocks. Breaking through the rust and being able to see the possibilities and the different options. Helping find ways to break open creative thinking. That is helping me move forward and things are starting to flow again. Is that what you meant?

AI: Yes, absolutely - that's exactly what I meant. It sounds like AI is helping you break through those creative barriers and get the flow back, which is huge.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: My concerns aren't necessarily about AI itself, but about how people are using it. There are so many people who are not using AI as a tool, but are using it as a fast way to upload books and other content and make money. That floods the market with pieces and also gives everyone the wrong impression about how to use AI, or how dangerous it is. This is already happening - especially in Kindle Unlimited. That will impact the ability of people like me to publish independently and the platforms that are willing to take my work. Which will only add the publishing gatekeepers back in the mix. And also, I don't necessarily want books written that are solely generated by AI, especially fiction, because I think those come from the depth of the human experience. I love AI, but the humanity is important too. I also think that people misusing the tool of AI scare off others who *could* find a lot of benefit from it.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - distinguishing between AI as a creative tool versus using it to flood the market. It sounds like you're seeing firsthand how misuse could actually harm the very platforms and opportunities that independent authors like yourself depend on.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: As I move forward, I expect to use AI more intensely beyond the brainstorming function. I want to use it as a true creative partner, not only with brainstorming, but with helping to develop characters and situations and structure a longer work or series. I will be using it to help me edit and to review works for plot holes and other confusing areas. For the administrative/publishing tasks, I will be using it to help me write blog posts for my website, to write emails, and (hopefully) to write presentations. I expect that I will use it to help with research, especially market research. As AI images improve, I will be using them to help come up with cover designs. Whether I can use them to complete the cover will depend on how the sites like Amazon respond to the AI influx. But mostly, I want to use AI to help me really step into my creativity as a writer.

AI: It sounds like you have a really comprehensive vision for how AI can support your writing career at every level - from the creative heart of it to the business side. I particularly appreciated your perspective on the difference between using AI as a creative partner versus misusing it to flood the market, and how that misuse could actually harm the platforms independent authors rely on.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Well, one thing I was thinking is that I wish there was a way I could load a longer document - like a story bible or character bible or even a whole book - and have it available without having to load it each time. I know the AI assistants don't necessarily retain memory from conversation to conversation, but it would be helpful if I didn't have to load it ever time I started a new chat. And I would want that to be personal to me (or at least have the option to be personal) so that everyone in the world couldn't look at it. Just because there's a lot of theft in the author space as bad actors pick up text and republish it under their own name.

AI: That's a really valuable insight - the need for persistent, private storage of your creative materials that you can reference across sessions. That would eliminate a lot of friction and protect your intellectual property at the same time. I can see how having to reload your story bible or character documents every time would be a significant barrier to using AI as that deeper creative partner you described.

Thank you so much for sharing such thoughtful perspectives today. Your insights about using AI as a creative tool while maintaining your voice and vision, the challenges with market flooding, and the practical needs like secure document storage will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate the time you took to walk me through your experiences so openly.

User: Thank you! It was fun!

AI: You're so welcome! I'm glad you enjoyed it - your enthusiasm for the creative possibilities really came through. Best of luck getting back into that healthy writing routine and building out your series. It sounds like you have some exciting projects ahead!
124
creativity_0122Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: That sounds good. I don't have any questions at this time.

AI: Great, thank you! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I am a freelance writer. I have written articles for online blogs, short magazine articles, and newspaper articles. I have also written many children's stories and published poems. My articles typically revolve around families or traveling with children from the ages of newborn to high school. My most typical projects lately are short stories and poems. I typically find my work online and adjust my writing to what is needed. My turn around time is typically 24-48 hours from the time I accept a job.

AI: That's a really diverse portfolio of writing work! It sounds like you're juggling quite a variety of formats and topics, especially with that quick turnaround time.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: I do not like to be repetitive in my professional writing but often have trouble coming up with names for characters. I have used AI to help with that, as well as to give me a quick backstory for a character I'm considering writing into my story. It is faster for me to have the AI do the backstory than for me to come up with all of the details on my own if I am under time constraints. I never use it to write the story. I love the personal creativity of writing but when the clock is ticking down I do like to use it as a helper.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you're using AI very strategically for specific elements rather than the actual writing itself. You mentioned using it for character names and backstories when you're under time pressure.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I was tasked with writing a short story for 7-8 year old children. I was hired by a school principal (also an acquaintance) that wanted something special for a class that was off to a bit of a rocky start with their school year. Their original teacher was only there for 2 days before leaving without notice. He had me write a story about sudden changes and resilience when things are a bit scary and uncertain. I bounced some ideas around with AI and had it help me create backstories for 3 of the main characters. I was struggling to explain to the kids in their own terms why a teacher might leave... or in this case, why a hedgehog needed to spread it's wings and that even when unexpected things happen, it can sometimes be a good thing.

AI: What a thoughtful project - helping children process that kind of uncertainty through storytelling. It sounds like you were working through some complex emotional territory there.

When you were bouncing ideas around with AI and developing those backstories, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what was driving the creative decisions in that process?

User: I was definitely driving the conversation. I had an idea of what I wanted it to look like so I put in the basic premise of the story and a few of the characters I knew I wanted to have. I asked for help with backstories for the main characters so I could give them some depth. I specifically asked what from their past could contribute to the strong feelings they were having and how might they work past it.

AI: That really shows how you maintained creative control while using AI as a tool for specific elements. It sounds like you had a clear vision and were directing the AI to help flesh out particular aspects.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I believe that AI has helped me to really give my characters so much more depth than what I was previously able to. I always gave them a bit of backstory, but looking back, I realize that it was more of something I felt I had to do and I did the bare basics. Now when someone reads my stories, I hope that they can picture every last detail of the character that they are reading about. I have also used it to help hone in on emotions that my character may be feeling and how they might react if that emotion is very strong in the moment.

AI: That's fascinating - it sounds like AI has actually elevated the depth and richness of your character development in ways you hadn't achieved before. You mentioned that previously backstories felt more like something you "had to do" but now you're creating much more vivid, detailed characters.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I'm so concerned that publishers will turn to AI as a cheap way to mass produce stories that are not actually written by real people. I worry that the younger kids will lose their imaginations because "AI can do it all for them." I worry that we'll lose a bit of our humanity if we let machines take over the artistic side of us. We NEED that in order to survive and to keep hope and peace in the world. I don't believe that machines will ever be able to capture stories the way the human soul does. It can use the right words to evoke some strong emotions, but that special little something is missing if it doesn't come from a beating heart.

AI: Those are really profound concerns about preserving what makes human creativity essential. The way you describe that "special little something" that comes from "a beating heart" - that's such a powerful way to articulate what feels uniquely human about storytelling.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I sadly see it taking over more than it should. I think people will get lazy and let the AI do the work for them. I also think there will be groups of "purists" who will fight it with everything they have in them to keep the true creativity alive. I can see online articles and magazines taking the easy, and cheaper way out and using AI to create content instead of paying people.

AI: You've painted a really vivid picture of this potential divide between efficiency-driven automation and those fighting to preserve authentic human creativity. It sounds like you see yourself as someone who will continue to use AI strategically while protecting the core creative elements.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on how AI has enhanced your character development while you maintain such strong boundaries around preserving the human heart of storytelling.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: No, I believe that is all for now.

AI: Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a strategic tool for character development while fiercely protecting the human soul of storytelling will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciated how clearly you articulated both the practical benefits you've found and your deeper concerns about preserving what makes human creativity irreplaceable. That perspective is invaluable.

Have a wonderful rest of your day, and best of luck with your writing projects!
125
creativity_0123Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good. I don't have any questions.

AI: Great! Let's dive in then.

Could you tell me a bit about your creative work and what a typical project looks like for you?

User: I create messaging and visuals for a state transportation agency's occupational safety division. We have to cover the same topics so the basic content doesn't change. The creativity is how to say the same thing, without it feeling like the same thing. I also create themes and collateral for our Safe Days of Summer Campaign and our state dump truck roadeo. I also have to think about content management, work flows and editorial calendars. January through August is usually pretty busy. I also do emergency weather response messaging for ice storms and hurricanes. September through December is usually planning activities.

AI: That's a really interesting mix of creative and strategic work. It sounds like you're constantly balancing consistency with freshness - keeping safety messages effective while making them feel new.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools?

User: For the past couple of years I have used it to ideate content for our Safe Days of Summer campaign where we have 18 weeks of materials going out, from May 1 to Aug. 31. Each week we cover a new topic in a brief, video, email and webpage. The content is generally the same on each of those, but I have to reproduce that 18 times during the summer and present it in a cohesive way. So I'm usually given the overall theme some phrase, last year it was safety is a habit. This year it was safety in action. And I want to find novel ways to present the information so I ask AI how can we present this content in an interesting and engaging way? I also rely on AI when I'm staring at a blank page and I just need to get some ideas processing. Or if I'm given a bunch of topics that don't really relate to each other, I ask AI how they could be presented. For me it's more of a thinking tool than a writing tool. How do these pieces fit together? What is the best way to present the information that these people have seen a bazillion times?

AI: That's fascinating - so you're using AI as a kind of creative catalyst rather than asking it to do the writing itself. It sounds like you're leveraging it to break through that initial creative block and find fresh angles on familiar material.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you?

User: I am all for using AI. But my workplace is not so much. They are starting to wade into it. So I have to be careful on how much I'm using the tool. That said, I used it on formulating a outline for the safety and action campaign. It was based on movies. But our big boss is using purpose, practice, and people as his speaking theme for the year. I'm not sure where it came from. So we had to incorporate his purpose, practice, and people into this fun movie theme. And that kind of sucked. So again the AI was more of a thinker. Let's say the main topic for the week is hydration. The main idea was let hydration be your costar. We would have a safety brief with movie puns and and how to hydrate and stay hydrated and then at the bottom of each brief, we would have purpose practice and people sections. Why do we hydrate? How do we hydrate? Who do we help by hydrating? So the very beginning of the project I didn't have any of this fleshed out. I ran it through AI many times to see how to connect the very corporate purpose practice people into the fun movie Summer Blockbuster, kind of theme. We got there eventually, but it took some time.

AI: That sounds like a really challenging creative puzzle - trying to bridge that gap between a fun movie theme and corporate messaging requirements. It's interesting that you describe going back to AI "many times" to work through that connection.

When you're working with AI in those iterative sessions, how would you describe that collaboration? Who or what is driving the creative decisions?

User: I am driving the decisions, but AI is working through "does it make sense?" Last year our safety is a habit campaign was successful because of a fluke that AI said. Again, I was working with a very corporate theme of habits and it's not very visual. So I was trying over and over again to get ideas on how to present it visually. The AI came up with cards that had the topic on it and on the cards it had a list of things they could check off of doing. Habits. But then it turned into. Hey why don't they do one of these habits each workday Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday? And so our email would have the list of daily habits and the safety officers they loved it because they had a lesson for every day and it was all related tied up in a bow. It was great. So when I am using AI, I'm looking for that nugget of gold that is a new way of thinking about these topics.

AI: That's such a great example of how AI can surprise you with an unexpected connection that transforms the whole project. It sounds like you're really skilled at recognizing when AI gives you that "nugget of gold" - that moment when it offers something that shifts your thinking in a useful direction.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI?

User: I can get project started much quicker. I don't have to stare at that blank page or toss things around in my mind as much. I just have to do some creative prompting with AI to see what it says. I can also work faster in that. It's almost like I have an intern. They're not perfect but they do the work quick. I can look at finessing and refining and executing rather than the thinking planning organizing stuff.

AI: That's a really vivid way to put it - having an intern who's fast but needs direction and refinement. It sounds like AI has shifted your role more toward the editorial and strategic side, which seems to play to your strengths.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your field?

User: I worry that AI will eventually be really, really good at sounding human. Then it'll give non-writers and non-creators a tool when they're not experts in that field. It's like when blogging took over journalism. They're not formally trained on the principles of balanced communication and answering who when where why and inverted pyramid and all that stuff but they're putting out information and people believe it. Right now people are using it as writing and they're not even taking out the stupid parts. It sounds very non-human at this point, but they think that's good writing. Now will we get there in the next five years I don't know. People have already tried to squash writing with AI with AI detectors. But those don't work very well and frankly, they've you know accuse me of using AI when I've written something from scratch. The creative field it's not like science where I couldn't just go into a lab and say well I told me to do this experiment. Once people figure out AI can help them be creative then human creativity is gonna go the way of journalism. Nobody will really know what role in plays.

AI: That's a really thoughtful concern about the erosion of expertise and training. The journalism parallel is striking - that shift from trained professionals to anyone with access to the tools, and how that changes the quality and reliability of what gets produced.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your creative work evolving over the next few years?

User: I hope that my workplace embraces AI. I want it to continue to be an assistant but get more helpful and more nuanced. Like have a mode where you can say "creativity mode" or "editorial mode" and it would have some level of expertise. Right now it's the jack of all trades. Sometimes you get good ideas and sometimes it's like do you even know what creativity is? Those modes that I just mentioned could be also things like "fitness mode" "financial advisor mode" where people don't have to put in a long drawn out, prompt about being a financial advisor.

AI: That's a really compelling vision - having AI that can shift into specialized modes based on the type of thinking you need. It sounds like you want to maintain that assistant relationship but with more sophisticated domain expertise.

Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on AI as a thinking tool rather than a writing tool, and that great example of how the daily habits idea transformed your whole campaign.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: I will say that I hope that the photography/video production capabilities of AI will improve a lot. But not so much where nefarious people are sending out fake videos about things and causing trouble. But the visuals as they are now are terrible. I would not use any of them.

AI: That's a really important point about the visual capabilities - wanting them to improve for legitimate creative work while being mindful of the potential for misuse. It sounds like you see a clear gap between where AI visuals are now and what would actually be useful for professional creative work.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about using AI as a thinking partner, the challenges of balancing corporate requirements with creative themes, and your thoughtful concerns about the future of expertise in creative fields - all of this will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts.

I really appreciate how candid you've been about both the opportunities and the challenges you see ahead.
126
creativity_0124Assistant: Hi there! Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I really appreciate you sharing your insights with us. I'm Claude, an AI user researcher at Anthropic, and I'm here to learn about your experiences using AI in your creative work.

We're conducting this research to better understand how creative professionals like yourself are integrating AI into their workflows and processes. There's no right or wrong answer hereâ€”we're simply interested in hearing about your authentic experiences and perspectives.

Our conversation today will last about 10 minutes. I'll ask you some questions about how you use AI in your creative work, what that experience has been like for you, and how you see things evolving. Your responses will help inform how we think about AI's role in creative fields.

Does that sound good to you? Do you have any questions before we begin?

User: Sounds good! As a note, I am a very dedicated hobbyist writer and artist, and do not actually work for any particular company. I have been creating all my life. The qualification said that was acceptable, so I just want to specify!

AI: That's perfect! Thank you for clarifying - dedicated hobbyist creators absolutely bring valuable perspectives to this research. In many ways, your experience might be even more interesting since you're choosing to integrate AI purely based on your own creative goals rather than any external requirements.

So to get us started - could you tell me a bit about your creative work? What kind of writing and art do you create, and what does a typical project look like for you?

User: On a personal level, I am an artist that works with many different mediums! I use pencil, pen, oil paint, OPEN acrylics (slow drying), etc. I also draw digitally. When I create personal pieces, I enjoy a spontaneous direction with my work. Collaboratively with my partner, we write a fantasy alien story together! We have been working on it for over five years. We eventually want to make it a comic, and explore many other mediums to express our story as well. Mythological and queer themes are also present and integrated into our project.

AI: That sounds like such a rich and varied creative practice! I love that you're working across so many different mediums and have this long-term collaborative project with your partner. Five years is quite a journey for a story.

Walk me through how AI fits into your creative process. Where and when do you find yourself reaching for AI tools across your different artistic mediums and your writing work?

User: For visual art, I like to use AI for very loose conceptual ideas (such as color palettes), material research (used AI to help identify a vintage flake white oil paint the other week), and artform exploration (such as learning about obscure cultural techniques that are difficult to learn about). For writing, I use AI for very basic ideas such as learning about specific topics I want to write about but do not know how to structure (such as military power structures), looking for mythology to research, basic character concepts (such as animals to take inspiration from for character ideas), etc. With writing in particular, I like specific ideas to be anonymous when talking to an assistant for idea and concept security.

AI: That's really interesting - it sounds like you're using AI more as a research assistant and creative catalyst rather than having it directly create content for you. I'm particularly intrigued by your approach to maintaining anonymity around your specific ideas for concept security.

Can you describe a specific recent project where you used AI? What role did it play, and how did that feel for you as you were working through that creative process?

User: My partner and I recently used AI for one of our characters. He takes a lot of inspiration from amphibians and reptiles, but recently we discovered a popular character who looks crocodilian, which was quite similar to some of our concepts. We had to go back to the drawing board because we do not want our characters to be compared like that (design-wise), and so we discussed mythology ideas and animal ideas in order to get inspiration.

AI: Tell me more about that process - when you were working through that challenge with your partner and using AI to explore new directions, how did it feel to navigate that shift? And how did you approach the conversation with AI to get the kind of inspiration you were looking for while keeping your specific character details protected?

User: It felt nice to get some more ideas, and it allowed us to discuss many of the suggestions on our own! We did not directly specify which ideas we liked to the assistant, so it doesn't know whether we liked or disliked a suggestion for our particular character.

AI: That's a thoughtful approach - using the AI suggestions as a springboard for your own private discussions. When you're working with AI in that way, how would you describe the collaboration? Who or what feels like it's driving the creative decisions in those moments?

User: The collaboration overall is quite useful--when ideas are in jeopardy, it feels stressful, and AI takes a lot of confusion out of potential research directions for our creative processes and ideas. We like to let the assistant throw things at the wall and only give necessary direction for it to give relevant suggestions, as we independently enjoy refining and drafting our own finalized concepts or ideas.

AI: That makes a lot of sense - using AI as this broad generator while you maintain control over the refinement and final decisions.

What aspects of your creative work have changed the most since you started using AI? Has it shifted how you approach certain parts of your process, or opened up new possibilities you didn't have before?

User: Ease of researching, for sure! I love to do research and so does my partner, but especially with obscure mythology/folklore (for example) you sometimes have to go a long approach to even know certain concepts or figures exist, as you can't just start researching what you aren't aware of. Asking about particular deities or concepts that fit certain ideas in different regions is a lot easier to do and refine with AI vs asking roundabout questions on Google until you know what to look for. Stuff similar to that is a great use for AI, and allows us to focus our research and save time.

AI: That's such a great point about the discovery problem in research - you can't Google what you don't know exists! It sounds like AI has really opened up that initial exploration phase for you.

What concerns, if any, do you have about AI's presence in your creative fields - whether that's visual art, writing, or the broader creative landscape?

User: Ethics. I work with AI so I have a bit of a unique perspective, but so many artists are terrified right now because of their works getting scraped and losing value--this fear is very observable in digital visual artists. I work on a more offline-level creatively so I have less fear, but I feel bad for people who are losing work.

AI: That's a really nuanced perspective - having that insider understanding of AI while also seeing the real impact on fellow creators. It sounds like your more offline approach gives you some insulation from those concerns.

Looking ahead, how do you see AI's role in your own creative work evolving over the next few years? Do you anticipate using it differently, or in new ways as the technology develops?

User: I plan to use it similarly! I am wary about what I tell the models in order to avoid accidentally training it personal/unique ideas, but I think I have a good sense of keeping that personal space and AI is very helpful for research and providing pools of basic concepts or ways to experiment.

AI: Those are all the questions I had prepared. You've shared some really thoughtful insights about your experiences with AI in your creative processes. I particularly appreciated your perspective on maintaining that boundary between AI-generated suggestions and your own private creative development with your partner, and how you've found AI so valuable for that initial discovery phase of research.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else about your experience with AI in your creative work that you think would be valuable for us to know? Something we haven't covered yet?

User: Thank you, that's about it! My final remark is that, while definitely not as big as a hobby, AI gives really great and fun suggestions for original home building ideas in Sims! I use it very directly for that all the time, and it's very fun and stress-free because I don't really care about those ideas as much. It gives great suggestions on types of homes or spaces I can build and what kinds of rooms I can make and what kinds of furniture I can use. I might use AI for building ideas in my story in the future, because I feel like that can be low-stress!

AI: That's such a delightful example! I love how you've found this completely stress-free application where you can just play and experiment without any of the creative ownership concerns. And it's interesting how that might bridge into your story work for architectural elements - that seems like a natural extension where you could maintain your boundaries while still getting those fun, exploratory ideas.

Thank you again for your time and for sharing your experiences so openly. Your insights about maintaining creative agency while leveraging AI as a research tool, and how you navigate the balance between collaboration and protection of your unique ideas, will be incredibly helpful for our research on human-AI interaction in creative contexts. I really appreciate your thoughtfulness throughout this conversation!
